questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The value of Boltzmann constant k is
questions.explanation: k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K (Boltzmann constant).

questions.subject: Physics

questions.subject: Physics

questions.subject: Physics

questions.explanation: Isobaric = constant pressure process.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The process in which volume remains constant is called
questions.explanation: Isochoric = constant volume process.

questions.subject: Physics

questions.explanation: ΔT in Celsius = ΔT in Kelvin = 120 − 20 = 100 K.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The ratio of Cp to Cv is always

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The image formed by a plane mirror is
questions.explanation: Plane mirror forms a virtual, erect, and laterally inverted image at equal distance behind the mirror.

questions.explanation: f = R/2 for spherical mirrors.

questions.explanation: Mirror formula: 1/f = 1/v + 1/u (sign convention applied).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Snell's law of refraction states
questions.explanation: Snell's law: n₁ sinθ₁ = n₂ sinθ₂.

questions.text: Total internal reflection occurs when light travels from
questions.optionB: denser to rarer medium at angle greater than critical angle
questions.explanation: TIR occurs when light goes from denser to rarer medium and angle of incidence > critical angle.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The critical angle for a medium of refractive index n is
questions.explanation: sinC = 1/n → C = sin⁻¹(1/n).

questions.text: The lateral shift of a ray through a parallel-sided glass slab depends on
questions.explanation: Lateral shift = t sinθ(1 − cosθ/√(n²−sin²θ)). It depends on thickness t, angle of incidence, and refractive index.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The angle of minimum deviation for a prism of angle A and refractive index n is δ_m. Then n =
questions.explanation: Prism formula: n = sin((A+δ_m)/2)/sin(A/2).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The lens maker's formula is
questions.explanation: Lens maker's formula: 1/f = (n−1)(1/R₁ − 1/R₂).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The power of a lens of focal length 20 cm is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Two thin lenses of focal lengths f₁ and f₂ in contact have combined focal length
questions.explanation: 1/F = 1/f₁ + 1/f₂ → F = f₁f₂/(f₁+f₂).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Optical fiber works on the principle of
questions.explanation: Light travels through optical fiber by total internal reflection at the core-cladding interface.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Dispersive power of a prism is
questions.explanation: Dispersive power ω = (δ_v − δ_r)/δ_y = (n_v − n_r)/(n_y − 1).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Chromatic aberration in a lens occurs because
questions.optionB: different colors have different focal lengths
questions.explanation: Different wavelengths refract differently (dispersion), causing different focal lengths for different colors.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Achromatism is achieved by combining
questions.optionB: a convex and a concave lens of different materials
questions.explanation: Achromatic doublet: convex lens (crown glass) + concave lens (flint glass) to cancel chromatic aberration.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Spherical aberration is due to
questions.optionA: different focal lengths for different colors

questions.explanation: Marginal rays focus closer than paraxial rays in a spherical lens/mirror, causing spherical aberration.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The sky appears blue due to
questions.explanation: Rayleigh scattering: shorter wavelengths (blue) are scattered more (∝ 1/λ⁴), making sky appear blue.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Huygens' principle states that
questions.optionB: every point on a wavefront acts as a source of secondary wavelets
questions.explanation: Huygens' principle: each point on a wavefront is a source of secondary wavelets; the envelope forms the new wavefront.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The speed of light in a medium of refractive index n is
questions.explanation: n = c/v → v = c/n.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In Young's double slit experiment, the fringe width β =
questions.explanation: β = λD/d, where λ = wavelength, D = screen distance, d = slit separation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: For constructive interference in Young's experiment, path difference =
questions.explanation: Constructive interference: path difference = nλ (n = 0, 1, 2, ...).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: For destructive interference, path difference =
questions.explanation: Destructive interference: path difference = (2n+1)λ/2 = (n+½)λ.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Two coherent sources are necessary for
questions.explanation: Sustained interference pattern requires two coherent sources (same frequency, constant phase difference).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In Fraunhofer diffraction by a single slit of width a, the first minimum occurs at
questions.explanation: For single slit: first minimum at a sinθ = λ.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Diffraction grating equation is
questions.explanation: Grating equation: d sinθ = nλ, where d is grating element spacing.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The resolving power of a diffraction grating with N slits in nth order is
questions.explanation: Resolving power = nN = λ/Δλ.

questions.text: Brewster's angle θ_B is related to refractive index n by
questions.explanation: Brewster's law: tanθ_B = n. At this angle, reflected light is completely polarized.

questions.text: At Brewster's angle, the reflected and refracted rays are
questions.explanation: At Brewster's angle: θ_B + θ_r = 90°. Reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Polaroid sunglasses reduce glare because they
questions.optionB: transmit only one plane of polarization
questions.explanation: Polaroids transmit light polarized in one direction, blocking the polarized glare from surfaces.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Light is a transverse wave. This is proved by
questions.explanation: Only transverse waves can be polarized. Polarization of light proves its transverse nature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When white light passes through a prism, it disperses because
questions.optionA: speed of light is same for all colors
questions.explanation: Dispersion: refractive index depends on wavelength (Cauchy's equation). Violet bends most.

questions.text: In Young's experiment, if the slit separation is halved, the fringe width
questions.explanation: β = λD/d. If d → d/2, β → 2β. Fringe width doubles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The intensity at a point where two coherent waves of intensities I₁ and I₂ interfere constructively is
questions.explanation: I_max = (√I₁ + √I₂)² for constructive interference (amplitudes add).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The magnification of a concave mirror for an object at 2f is
questions.explanation: Object at C (2f) → image at C (2f). m = −v/u = −1 (same size, real, inverted).

questions.text: A convex mirror always forms
questions.explanation: Convex mirror always forms virtual, erect, and diminished images regardless of object position.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The refractive index of a medium depends on
questions.explanation: n varies with wavelength (dispersion). n is higher for shorter wavelengths.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An object placed at the focus of a convex lens forms an image at
questions.explanation: Object at F → parallel rays after refraction → image at infinity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The thin lens formula is
questions.explanation: Thin lens formula: 1/f = 1/v − 1/u (with sign convention).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A convex lens behaves as a diverging lens when the object is placed
questions.explanation: Actually a convex lens always converges. But when object is between F and lens, the image is virtual, erect, and magnified. The lens still converges but the diverging virtual image gives an illusion.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The condition for an achromatic combination of two thin lenses in contact is
questions.explanation: Achromatism condition: ω₁/f₁ + ω₂/f₂ = 0, where ω is dispersive power.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Rayleigh scattering intensity is proportional to
questions.explanation: Rayleigh scattering: I ∝ 1/λ⁴. Shorter wavelengths scatter much more.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The sunset appears red because
questions.optionB: blue light is scattered away, red reaches us
questions.explanation: At sunset, light travels through more atmosphere. Blue scatters away; red (less scattered) reaches the observer.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In Young's experiment with white light, the central fringe is
questions.explanation: At center, path difference = 0 for all wavelengths → all colors constructively interfere → white fringe.

questions.text: The angular width of central maximum in single slit diffraction is
questions.explanation: Central maximum extends from −λ/a to +λ/a. Angular width = 2λ/a.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A grating has 5000 lines per cm. The grating element d is

questions.explanation: Malus' law: I = I₀ cos²θ.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The image formed by a concave mirror when the object is between F and the mirror is
questions.explanation: Object between F and mirror → virtual, erect, magnified image behind the mirror.

questions.optionB: parallel to the incident ray but laterally shifted
questions.explanation: Through a parallel-sided slab, the emergent ray is parallel to incident ray but laterally displaced.

questions.explanation: For thin prism: δ = (n−1)A.

questions.text: When light enters from air into water (n = 4/3), its wavelength
questions.explanation: λ_medium = λ_air/n. Wavelength decreases in denser medium.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When light enters a denser medium, its frequency
questions.explanation: Frequency of light does not change when entering a different medium. Only speed and wavelength change.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A concave lens always produces
questions.explanation: A concave (diverging) lens always forms virtual, erect, and diminished images.

questions.text: Mirage is caused by
questions.optionA: total internal reflection due to varying refractive index of air layers

questions.text: In interference, energy is
questions.optionC: redistributed from dark to bright regions

questions.explanation: More slits → sharper and brighter principal maxima (angular width of maxima ∝ 1/N).

questions.text: The phenomenon of bending of light around obstacles is called
questions.explanation: Diffraction is the bending of waves around obstacles or through narrow openings.

questions.text: Interference and diffraction both require
questions.explanation: Both interference and diffraction require coherent light sources for observable patterns.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The spectrum produced by a prism is called

questions.explanation: Object at 2f → image at 2f. m = v/u = −2f/(−2f) = −1 (same size, inverted).

questions.explanation: β = λD/d. β ∝ λ. Increasing λ increases fringe width.

questions.explanation: Optical fibers typically use infrared or visible light for communication.

questions.explanation: Light travels fastest in vacuum (c = 3×10⁸ m/s). In any medium, v = c/n < c.

questions.explanation: The eye lens (convex) focuses light to form a real, inverted image on the retina.

questions.explanation: Violet has shortest λ, highest n → bends the most.

questions.text: Optical path length in a medium of refractive index n and thickness t is

questions.text: The phenomenon of double refraction is exhibited by

questions.explanation: Nicol prism uses double refraction and total internal reflection in calcite to produce plane polarized light.

questions.explanation: For reflected light: 2μt cosr = (2n+1)λ/2 due to additional π phase change at one surface.

questions.explanation: d sinθ = nλ. Angular position depends on d (grating element) and λ (wavelength).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The magnifying power of an astronomical telescope in normal adjustment is
questions.explanation: M = f_o/f_e for astronomical telescope in normal adjustment (image at infinity).

questions.text: The length of an astronomical telescope in normal adjustment is
questions.explanation: In normal adjustment, length L = f_o + f_e.

questions.text: Resolving power of a telescope increases with
questions.explanation: Resolving power ∝ D/λ. Larger aperture D gives better resolution.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The distance between two consecutive bright fringes in Young's experiment is
questions.explanation: Fringe spacing = β = λD/d.

questions.text: Wavefront of light from a point source at finite distance is
questions.explanation: A point source emits spherical wavefronts. At very large distances they become approximately plane.

questions.text: Wavefront of light from a linear source is
questions.explanation: A line source produces cylindrical wavefronts.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The direction of propagation of light is always
questions.explanation: Rays (direction of propagation) are always perpendicular to wavefronts.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If two slits in Young's experiment are illuminated with white light, the fringe closest to central fringe is
questions.explanation: β = λD/d. Violet has smallest λ → narrowest fringe width → first color fringe closest to center.

questions.text: In thin wedge-shaped film, fringes are
questions.optionB: straight and parallel to thin edge

questions.subject: Physics

questions.explanation: r_n = √(nλR) → r_n ∝ √n for dark rings.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In single slit diffraction, the central maximum is

questions.explanation: First secondary max intensity ≈ 4/(9π²) × I₀ ≈ I₀/22.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A Fresnel lens is used in lighthouses because it
questions.optionB: is thinner, lighter and captures more light
questions.explanation: Fresnel lens: same focal properties as conventional lens but much thinner and lighter, capturing more light.

questions.explanation: Central max width ∝ λ/a. If a doubles, width halves.

questions.explanation: Phase difference π corresponds to path difference λ/2 → destructive interference.

questions.text: A quarter-wave plate introduces a path difference of
questions.explanation: Quarter-wave plate: path difference = λ/4 between ordinary and extraordinary rays.

questions.text: Circularly polarized light can be obtained by passing plane polarized light through
questions.optionB: quarter-wave plate at 45° to optic axis
questions.explanation: QWP at 45° converts linearly polarized light into circularly polarized light.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A half-wave plate introduces a phase difference of
questions.explanation: Half-wave plate: phase difference = π (path difference = λ/2).

questions.explanation: Specific rotation [α] = θ/(l × c), where l is in dm and c in g/cm³. Standard: 1 g/cm³, 1 dm path.

questions.subject: Physics

questions.explanation: Object at C → image at C. Real, inverted, same size.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Which phenomenon cannot be explained by the wave theory of light?
questions.explanation: Photoelectric effect requires quantum (particle) theory; wave theory cannot explain the threshold frequency and instantaneous emission.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The radius of curvature of a plane mirror is
questions.explanation: Plane mirror can be considered a spherical mirror with R = ∞, hence f = ∞.

questions.subject: Physics

questions.subject: Physics

questions.explanation: Cauchy's equation: n = A + B/λ² + C/λ⁴. n depends on wavelength.

questions.subject: Physics

questions.explanation: Object between F and pole → virtual, erect, magnified image behind the mirror.

questions.subject: Physics

questions.subject: Physics

questions.explanation: A source at infinity produces plane wavefronts (parallel rays).

questions.subject: Physics

questions.explanation: Parallel rays (object at ∞) converge at the focus of a concave mirror.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The numerical aperture of an optical fiber is related to the acceptance angle θ_a by
questions.explanation: NA = sinθ_a = √(n₁² − n₂²), where n₁ and n₂ are core and cladding refractive indices.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If path difference between two interfering waves changes from λ to 3λ/2, the point changes from
questions.explanation: λ → constructive (bright). 3λ/2 → destructive (dark). Point changes from bright to dark.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The intensity of secondary maxima in single slit diffraction decreases because
questions.optionB: the effective number of contributing Fresnel zones decreases
questions.explanation: Higher-order secondary maxima have contributions from fewer effective Fresnel half-period zones → lower intensity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The missing order in diffraction grating occurs when
questions.optionA: d/a is an integer, where a is slit width
questions.explanation: When d/a = integer m, the mth order principal maximum of grating coincides with minimum of single slit → missing order.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Dichroism is the property of a crystal to
questions.optionB: absorb one of the two polarized beams
questions.explanation: Dichroic crystals (like tourmaline) selectively absorb one of the two polarized rays produced by double refraction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In normal adjustment of a telescope, the final image is at
questions.explanation: Normal adjustment: final image at infinity (relaxed eye). Fo of objective coincides with Fe of eyepiece.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A Galilean telescope uses
questions.explanation: Galilean telescope: convex objective + concave eyepiece → erect image.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Aberration that causes different magnifications for different object distances in a lens is
questions.explanation: Distortion: non-uniform magnification across the field, causing pin-cushion or barrel distortion.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Coma in optics is the aberration affecting
questions.explanation: Coma: off-axis aberration producing comet-shaped (asymmetric) images.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Astigmatism in a lens occurs for
questions.optionB: off-axis objects where tangential and sagittal foci differ
questions.explanation: Astigmatism: for off-axis objects, the tangential and sagittal planes focus at different distances.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The real depth of a pond is 6 m and apparent depth when viewed from above is 4 m. The refractive index of water is
questions.explanation: n = real depth/apparent depth = 6/4 = 1.5.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Deviation by a prism is zero when
questions.explanation: δ = (n−1)A for thin prism. If A = 0 or n = 1, deviation = 0.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The apparent depth of an object submerged in a medium of refractive index n is
questions.explanation: Apparent depth = real depth / n.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If the screen is moved closer to the slits in Young's experiment, fringe width
questions.explanation: β = λD/d. Decreasing D decreases fringe width.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Coherence length is related to
questions.explanation: Coherence length L_c = c/Δν = λ²/Δλ. Narrower bandwidth → longer coherence length.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Newton's rings are formed due to interference between
questions.optionB: light reflected from curved surface of lens and flat glass surface
questions.explanation: Newton's rings: interference between light reflected from bottom curved surface of plano-convex lens and top flat surface of glass plate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The radius of nth bright ring in Newton's rings is proportional to
questions.explanation: For bright rings: r_n = √((2n−1)λR/2), so r_n ∝ √((2n−1)/2).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When a convex lens is placed on a plane mirror, the system behaves as a
questions.optionA: convex lens of half focal length
questions.optionB: concave mirror of focal length f/2
questions.explanation: Convex lens on plane mirror equivalent to concave mirror with f_eq = f/2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The refractive index of a medium is 1.6. The speed of light in the medium is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The focal length of a plano-convex lens (n = 1.5) with curved surface radius 30 cm is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If Young's experiment is performed in water instead of air, the fringe width
questions.explanation: In water, λ_w = λ/n. β = λ_wD/d = λD/(nd). Fringe width decreases by factor n.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In a diffraction grating, if grating element d = 3λ, the maximum order observable is
questions.explanation: n_max = d/λ = 3. Maximum order = 3 (when sinθ = 1).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Unpolarized light passes through three Polaroids with angles 0°, 45°, 90° between successive axes. The final intensity is
questions.explanation: After 1st: I₀/2. After 2nd (45°): (I₀/2)cos²45° = I₀/4. After 3rd (45° from 2nd): (I₀/4)cos²45° = I₀/8.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A microscope is focused on an object below a glass slab. The microscope must be raised by
questions.explanation: Shift = t(1−1/n) = t − t/n. The microscope must be raised by this amount.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The condition for a bright fringe at position y on the screen in Young's experiment is
questions.explanation: Bright fringe: y_n = nλD/d (n = 0, ±1, ±2, ...).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Resolving power of a microscope is proportional to
questions.explanation: RP = 2n sinα / λ. Higher NA and shorter wavelength → better resolution.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A prism with angle A = 60° and n = √2 produces minimum deviation of

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: At minimum deviation through a prism, the angle of incidence equals
questions.explanation: At minimum deviation, i = e and r₁ = r₂ = A/2. The ray passes symmetrically.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The phenomenon of colors in thin oil films on water is due to
questions.explanation: Thin film interference between light reflected from the top and bottom surfaces of the oil film.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A Fresnel biprism is used to obtain
questions.explanation: Fresnel biprism creates two virtual coherent sources from a single source, producing interference fringes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If the amplitude ratio of two interfering waves is 3:1, the ratio I_max:I_min is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Concave mirrors are used as shaving mirrors because they produce
questions.optionB: virtual magnified image when object is within focus
questions.explanation: Face within focus of concave mirror → virtual, erect, magnified image suitable for shaving.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Convex mirrors are used as rear-view mirrors because they
questions.optionB: provide a wider field of view
questions.explanation: Convex mirror has a wider field of view (diverging nature) and always produces erect, diminished images.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The angular dispersion by a prism is
questions.explanation: Angular dispersion = δ_v − δ_r = (n_v − n_r)A for thin prism.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Fiber optic cable has higher refractive index in its
questions.explanation: Core has higher refractive index than cladding for total internal reflection to occur.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The phenomenon in which wavefront changes shape on passing through a narrow opening is
questions.explanation: Diffraction: wavefront bends and changes shape when passing through a narrow opening comparable to wavelength.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In single slit Fraunhofer diffraction, the position of second minimum is at
questions.explanation: Minima at a sinθ = mλ. For m = 2: a sinθ = 2λ.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Optically active substances rotate the plane of polarization proportional to
questions.explanation: Rotation θ ∝ length of optically active substance (and concentration for solutions).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The eyepiece of a Ramsden eyepiece consists of
questions.optionA: two plano-convex lenses with curved surfaces facing each other
questions.optionC: one convex and one concave lens
questions.explanation: Ramsden eyepiece: two identical plano-convex lenses with curved surfaces facing each other, separated by 2f/3.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A virtual image is formed
questions.optionA: behind the mirror by concave mirror always
questions.optionB: where diverging rays appear to converge
questions.explanation: Virtual image: formed where diverging rays appear to come from (cannot be captured on screen).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The principle of superposition states that
questions.optionB: resultant displacement is the vector sum of individual displacements
questions.explanation: Superposition: when waves overlap, the resultant displacement is the algebraic (vector) sum of individual displacements.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If in Young's experiment the source slit is too wide, then
questions.optionB: fringe contrast decreases and fringes blur
questions.explanation: Wider source → lower spatial coherence → overlapping patterns from different points → reduced contrast and blurred fringes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Achromatic combination for deviation without dispersion requires
questions.explanation: For no dispersion: ω₁δ₁ + ω₂δ₂ = 0, giving deviation without dispersion (direct vision prism).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A silvered plano-convex lens of focal length f acts as a concave mirror of focal length
questions.explanation: P_eq = P_lens + P_mirror + P_lens = 2/f_lens + 0 → 1/F = 2/f → F = f/2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The minimum thickness of a thin film for constructive interference in reflected light is
questions.explanation: 2nt = λ/2 for first constructive in reflected light → t = λ/(4n).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The angle subtended by the first dark ring in Newton's rings at the center of curvature is proportional to
questions.explanation: First dark ring radius r₁ = √(λR). Angle ≈ r₁/R = √(λ/R).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When polarized light of intensity I₀ passes through an analyzer at 60°, the transmitted intensity is
questions.explanation: I = I₀cos²60° = I₀ × (1/2)² = I₀/4.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In Lloyd's mirror experiment, the fringe system is similar to Young's experiment except
questions.optionB: the central fringe is dark due to phase change on reflection
questions.explanation: Lloyd's mirror: reflection from denser medium causes π phase change → central fringe is dark (opposite to Young's).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Doppler effect in light causes
questions.explanation: Doppler effect: receding source → red shift; approaching source → blue shift of spectral lines.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The magnification of a concave mirror for an object at infinity is
questions.explanation: Object at infinity → image at focus (point image). The image is highly diminished → magnification ≈ 0.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A wave is a disturbance that
questions.explanation: Waves transfer energy from one point to another without the bulk transfer of matter.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In a transverse wave, particles vibrate
questions.optionA: along the direction of wave propagation
questions.optionB: perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation
questions.explanation: Transverse wave: particle vibration is perpendicular to wave velocity direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In a longitudinal wave, particles vibrate
questions.optionB: along the direction of wave propagation
questions.explanation: Longitudinal wave: particle oscillation is parallel to the direction of wave propagation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Sound waves in air are
questions.explanation: Sound waves in air (fluid) are longitudinal — compressions and rarefactions propagate along the direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The relation between velocity v, frequency f, and wavelength λ of a wave is
questions.explanation: v = fλ is the fundamental wave equation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The speed of a transverse wave on a string of tension T and linear mass density μ is
questions.explanation: v = √(T/μ) for transverse waves on a string.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The speed of sound in air at 0°C is approximately
questions.explanation: Speed of sound in dry air at 0°C ≈ 332 m/s.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The speed of sound in air varies with temperature as
questions.explanation: v = √(γRT/M) → v ∝ √T (T in Kelvin).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Newton's formula for velocity of sound gave incorrect results because he assumed
questions.explanation: Newton assumed isothermal process. Laplace corrected it to adiabatic (sound propagation is rapid → no heat exchange).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Laplace correction gives velocity of sound as
questions.explanation: Laplace: v = √(γP/ρ), where γ = Cp/Cv.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The principle of superposition of waves states that
questions.optionB: the net displacement is the algebraic sum of individual displacements
questions.explanation: Superposition: when two or more waves meet, the resultant displacement equals the sum of individual displacements.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When two waves of same frequency and amplitude travel in opposite directions, they form
questions.explanation: Superposition of two identical waves traveling in opposite directions produces standing waves with nodes and antinodes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: At nodes of a standing wave, the amplitude is
questions.explanation: Nodes: points of zero amplitude (destructive interference). Antinodes: maximum amplitude.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The distance between two consecutive nodes in a standing wave is
questions.explanation: Distance between consecutive nodes = λ/2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The fundamental frequency of a string fixed at both ends of length L is
questions.explanation: Fundamental: L = λ/2 → λ = 2L → f₁ = v/(2L).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The harmonics produced by a string fixed at both ends are
questions.explanation: String fixed at both ends: all harmonics (f, 2f, 3f, 4f, ...).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The fundamental frequency of an open organ pipe of length L is
questions.explanation: Open pipe: fundamental L = λ/2 → f₁ = v/(2L).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The fundamental frequency of a closed organ pipe of length L is
questions.explanation: Closed pipe: fundamental L = λ/4 → f₁ = v/(4L).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A closed organ pipe produces
questions.explanation: Closed pipe: only odd harmonics (f, 3f, 5f, 7f, ...) because one end is a node and other is antinode.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An open organ pipe produces
questions.explanation: Open pipe: all harmonics (f, 2f, 3f, 4f, ...) because both ends are antinodes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The ratio of fundamental frequencies of an open and closed pipe of same length is
questions.explanation: f_open = v/(2L), f_closed = v/(4L). Ratio = 2:1. Open pipe has twice the fundamental frequency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Beats are produced when two sound waves have
questions.explanation: Beats: periodic variation in loudness due to superposition of two waves with slightly different frequencies.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Beat frequency equals
questions.explanation: f_beat = |f₁ − f₂|.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If two tuning forks of frequencies 256 Hz and 260 Hz are sounded together, the beat frequency is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Doppler effect in sound is the change in
questions.optionB: apparent frequency due to relative motion
questions.explanation: Doppler effect: apparent change in frequency (and wavelength) of sound due to relative motion between source and observer.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When a source moves toward a stationary observer, the apparent frequency
questions.explanation: Source approaching → wavelength compressed → frequency heard increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The apparent frequency when source moves toward stationary observer with velocity v_s is f' =
questions.explanation: f' = f × v/(v − v_s) for source approaching stationary observer.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The apparent frequency when observer moves toward stationary source with velocity v_o is f' =
questions.explanation: f' = f × (v + v_o)/v for observer approaching stationary source.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The general Doppler effect formula for sound is f' =
questions.explanation: f' = f(v + v_o)/(v − v_s), with + for approaching, − for receding (standard convention with v_o toward source positive).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Resonance occurs when
questions.optionA: frequency of external force equals natural frequency
questions.explanation: Resonance: when driving frequency matches natural frequency of the system → maximum amplitude.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The intensity of sound is proportional to
questions.explanation: Intensity ∝ (amplitude)² for any wave.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The unit of intensity of sound is
questions.explanation: Intensity = Power/Area. Unit: W/m².

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The loudness of sound is measured in
questions.explanation: Loudness is measured in decibels (dB). β = 10 log₁₀(I/I₀).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If intensity increases by a factor of 100, the loudness increases by

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Ultrasound has frequency
questions.explanation: Ultrasound: frequency above 20 kHz (above human hearing range).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Infrasound has frequency
questions.explanation: Infrasound: frequency below 20 Hz (below human hearing range).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Sound cannot travel through
questions.explanation: Sound requires a material medium. It cannot travel through vacuum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The speed of sound is maximum in
questions.explanation: Speed of sound: solids > liquids > gases (due to higher elasticity and density ratio).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The pitch of a sound depends on its
questions.explanation: Pitch is the psychological correlate of frequency. Higher frequency → higher pitch.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Quality or timbre of sound depends on
questions.explanation: Timbre depends on the waveform shape — determined by the mixture of harmonics present.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The velocity of a progressive wave y = A sin(ωt − kx) is
questions.explanation: Wave velocity v = ω/k = fλ.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The phase difference between two points separated by λ/4 is
questions.explanation: Phase difference = (2π/λ) × path difference = (2π/λ)(λ/4) = π/2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A wave y = 5 sin(200πt − πx/17) has velocity

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Stationary waves do not transfer
questions.explanation: Standing waves do not transfer energy; energy oscillates between kinetic and potential forms at antinodes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The second harmonic of a string of length L fixed at both ends has frequency
questions.explanation: f_n = nv/(2L). For n = 2: f₂ = 2v/(2L) = v/L.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The first overtone of a closed pipe is the
questions.explanation: Closed pipe: 1st harmonic (fundamental), 3rd harmonic (1st overtone), 5th harmonic (2nd overtone)...

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The first overtone of an open pipe is the
questions.explanation: Open pipe: all harmonics. 1st overtone = 2nd harmonic.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: End correction for an open pipe of radius r is approximately
questions.explanation: End correction ≈ 0.6r at each open end. Effective length = L + 2(0.6r) for open pipe.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A string vibrating in its second harmonic has
questions.explanation: 2nd harmonic: 2 loops → 3 nodes (including ends) and 2 antinodes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If the tension in a string is doubled, the fundamental frequency becomes
questions.explanation: f ∝ √T. If T → 2T, f → √2 × f.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If the length of a vibrating string is halved (same tension), the fundamental frequency
questions.explanation: f = v/(2L). If L → L/2, f → 2f. Frequency doubles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A sonometer wire of length 100 cm vibrates with 4 loops at 400 Hz. The wave velocity is
questions.explanation: 4 loops → 4th harmonic. f₄ = 4v/(2L) → 400 = 4v/(2×1) → v = 200 m/s.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Kundt's tube is used to measure
questions.optionB: velocity of sound in a gas or solid rod
questions.explanation: Kundt's tube: measures speed of sound by observing standing wave pattern (node spacing = λ/2).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The phenomenon of echo requires a minimum distance from reflecting surface of about
questions.explanation: Minimum distance ≈ 17 m (round trip 34 m, time ≈ 34/340 = 0.1 s, the persistence of hearing).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Reverberation time is the time for sound intensity to fall to
questions.explanation: Reverberation time (Sabine): time for intensity to drop by 60 dB (factor of 10⁶).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Sabine's formula for reverberation time T is
questions.explanation: T = 0.161V/A, where V = volume, A = total absorption (ΣaS, a = absorption coefficient, S = surface area).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The frequency of the third harmonic of an open pipe is
questions.explanation: f_n = nv/(2L) for open pipe. 3rd harmonic: f₃ = 3v/(2L).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The frequency of the third harmonic of a closed pipe is
questions.explanation: Closed pipe harmonics: nv/(4L) for odd n only. 3rd harmonic: 3v/(4L).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The wavelength of sound in air of frequency 1000 Hz (speed 340 m/s) is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When two tuning forks produce 5 beats per second, and one has frequency 512 Hz, the other has frequency
questions.explanation: f_beat = |f₁ − f₂| = 5. Other fork: 512 ± 5 = 507 Hz or 517 Hz.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A progressive wave equation is y = A sin(kx − ωt). The wave travels in the
questions.explanation: y = A sin(kx − ωt) → wave travels in +x direction (coefficient of t is negative).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The energy in a vibrating string is
questions.explanation: A vibrating string has both kinetic energy (motion) and potential energy (elastic deformation).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If the linear density of a string is quadrupled, keeping tension constant, the wave velocity
questions.explanation: v = √(T/μ). If μ → 4μ, v → v/2. Velocity halves.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A vibrating tuning fork held over a closed pipe of variable length produces resonance first at length
questions.explanation: First resonance in closed pipe at L = λ/4.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The second resonance position in a closed pipe occurs at length
questions.explanation: Resonance positions in closed pipe: λ/4, 3λ/4, 5λ/4, ... Second resonance at 3λ/4.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The ratio of the first to second resonance lengths in a closed pipe is
questions.explanation: L₁ = λ/4, L₂ = 3λ/4. Ratio = 1:3.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The human ear can hear frequencies in the range
questions.explanation: Audible range: approximately 20 Hz to 20 kHz.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The wave equation ∂²y/∂t² = v² ∂²y/∂x² represents
questions.explanation: This is the general one-dimensional wave equation, valid for all types of waves.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The speed of sound increases with
questions.explanation: Humid air has lower average molecular mass → v = √(γRT/M) → higher speed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The speed of sound at 20°C if speed at 0°C is 332 m/s is approximately

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Sound travels faster in steel than in air because
questions.optionB: steel has higher elasticity relative to density
questions.explanation: v = √(E/ρ). Steel has much higher Young's modulus → higher v despite higher density.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When a source moves away from a stationary observer, the apparent wavelength
questions.explanation: Source receding → waves are stretched → wavelength increases (redshift).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A siren emits sound of frequency 1000 Hz while moving toward a wall at 10 m/s. The frequency of reflected sound heard by the driver is (v = 340 m/s)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A pipe open at both ends has length 0.5 m. The fundamental frequency (v = 340 m/s) is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The phenomenon of apparent change in frequency of a whistle of a moving train is
questions.explanation: Doppler effect: frequency appears higher when train approaches and lower when it recedes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Which type of waves can be polarized?
questions.explanation: Only transverse waves can be polarized (vibration can be restricted to one plane).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The particle velocity in a progressive wave is
questions.explanation: Particle velocity v_p = ∂y/∂t = −(ω/k)(∂y/∂x) = −v_wave × slope.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The maximum particle velocity in a wave y = A sin(ωt − kx) is
questions.explanation: v_max = Aω (maximum of ∂y/∂t = Aω cos(ωt − kx)).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The energy density (energy per unit volume) of a sound wave is
questions.explanation: Energy density u = ½ρω²A².

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Mach number is the ratio of
questions.optionA: speed of sound to speed of object
questions.optionB: speed of object to speed of sound
questions.explanation: Mach number M = v_object/v_sound. M > 1 → supersonic.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When an object moves faster than sound, it creates
questions.explanation: Supersonic speed → conical shock wave → sonic boom.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The equation of a standing wave is
questions.explanation: Standing wave = superposition of two progressive waves: y = 2A sin(kx) cos(ωt).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In a standing wave, all particles between two consecutive nodes vibrate
questions.explanation: All particles between two consecutive nodes vibrate in phase. Particles across a node vibrate in opposite phase.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The frequency of a string vibrating in pth harmonic is
questions.explanation: f_p = pv/(2L) = p × f₁ for string fixed at both ends.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A string of length 1 m and mass 1 g is under tension 100 N. The fundamental frequency is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Laws of vibration of stretched strings were given by
questions.explanation: Mersenne's laws: relate frequency to length, tension, and linear density of vibrating strings.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: According to Mersenne's law, frequency is inversely proportional to
questions.explanation: f = (1/2L)√(T/μ). f ∝ 1/L (inversely proportional to length).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The intensity level of the threshold of hearing (I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m²) in decibels is
questions.explanation: β = 10 log(I/I₀) = 10 log(1) = 0 dB.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When source and observer move toward each other with equal speeds v_s, the apparent frequency is f' = f ×
questions.explanation: f' = f(v + v_o)/(v − v_s). With v_o = v_s: f' = f(v + v_s)/(v − v_s).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The quality factor Q of a resonating system is defined as
questions.explanation: Q = f₀/Δf. Higher Q → sharper resonance peak.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When a tuning fork is loaded with wax, its frequency
questions.explanation: Loading increases effective mass → frequency decreases (f ∝ 1/√m).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When a tuning fork is filed, its frequency
questions.explanation: Filing reduces mass of prongs → frequency increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Sound waves show reflection, refraction, and diffraction but not polarization because sound waves are
questions.explanation: Sound waves are longitudinal. Only transverse waves can be polarized.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The velocity of transverse waves on the surface of water depends on
questions.explanation: For ripples (short λ): v depends on surface tension and λ. For gravity waves: v depends on depth and λ.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The displacement of a particle in a stationary wave at a node is always
questions.explanation: Nodes are points of permanently zero displacement in a standing wave.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: SONAR uses the principle of
questions.explanation: SONAR: sends ultrasonic pulses and detects echoes to determine distance and location of underwater objects.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Acoustic impedance of a medium is
questions.explanation: Acoustic impedance Z = ρv (density × speed of sound).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The phase velocity of a dispersive medium
questions.explanation: In dispersive media, phase velocity v_p = ω/k depends on wavelength (or frequency).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Group velocity is the velocity of
questions.explanation: Group velocity v_g = dω/dk represents the velocity of the wave packet (envelope) and energy transport.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: For a non-dispersive medium, phase velocity and group velocity are
questions.explanation: In non-dispersive media, v_p = v_g (all wavelengths travel at the same speed).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The wave number k is related to wavelength by
questions.explanation: Wave number k = 2π/λ (angular wave number).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The angular frequency ω of a wave of frequency f is
questions.explanation: ω = 2πf (angular frequency in rad/s).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A wave has frequency 100 Hz and wavelength 3.4 m. The speed of the wave is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The path difference corresponding to a phase difference of π is
questions.explanation: Phase diff = (2π/λ) × path diff. π = (2π/λ) × Δx → Δx = λ/2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The frequency of a wire stretched between two fixed supports depends on
questions.optionA: length, tension, and linear mass density
questions.explanation: f = (n/2L)√(T/μ). Frequency depends on length, tension, and linear density.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The intensity of a sound wave is I = ½ρvω²A². If amplitude is doubled, intensity becomes
questions.explanation: I ∝ A². If A → 2A, I → 4I.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An open pipe and closed pipe have same fundamental frequency. The ratio of their lengths is
questions.explanation: v/(2L_o) = v/(4L_c) → L_o/L_c = 2/1. Open pipe is twice as long.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The temperature at which speed of sound in air becomes double its value at 0°C is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Which of the following affects the speed of sound in a gas?
questions.explanation: v = √(γRT/M). Temperature affects speed. Pressure alone (at constant T) does not (ρ changes proportionally).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A closed pipe 1 m long resonates in its fundamental mode with a frequency of 85 Hz. The speed of sound is

questions.text: Two organ pipes open at both ends have lengths 50 cm and 51 cm. The number of beats per second when sounded together (v = 340 m/s) is approximately

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A progressive wave carries
questions.explanation: Progressive waves carry both energy and momentum in the direction of propagation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Nodes in a stationary wave are positions of
questions.optionA: maximum displacement and minimum pressure change
questions.optionB: zero displacement and maximum pressure change
questions.optionC: zero displacement and zero pressure change
questions.optionD: maximum displacement and maximum pressure change
questions.explanation: At displacement nodes: zero displacement but maximum pressure variation (pressure antinodes).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The speed of sound in hydrogen is about 4 times the speed in oxygen because

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The phenomenon of redistribution of energy due to superposition of two waves of equal frequency is
questions.explanation: Interference: superposition of two coherent waves → energy redistribution (bright/dark regions).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A vibrating tuning fork produces
questions.explanation: Tuning fork vibrations create longitudinal sound waves (compressions and rarefactions) in air.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If the frequency of the second harmonic of a string is 200 Hz, the frequency of the fifth harmonic is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The relation between the speed of sound v, pressure P, and density ρ (Newton's formula) is
questions.explanation: Newton's formula: v = √(P/ρ) (assuming isothermal). Laplace corrected to v = √(γP/ρ).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The ratio of speed of sound in air at 0°C by Newton's formula to the actual speed is
questions.explanation: v_Newton = √(P/ρ), v_actual = √(γP/ρ). Ratio = 1/√γ ≈ 1/√1.4 ≈ 0.845.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Which property of sound is affected by wind?
questions.explanation: Wind adds or subtracts its velocity component from the speed of sound. Effective speed = v ± v_wind.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A train moves at 30 m/s toward a platform. It sounds a whistle of 500 Hz. The frequency heard by a person on the platform is (v = 330 m/s)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When both source and observer are stationary but medium moves, the apparent frequency is
questions.explanation: If both source and observer are stationary, medium motion doesn't change apparent frequency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The phenomenon where sound is heard more clearly at night than during day is due to
questions.optionB: refraction of sound due to temperature gradient
questions.explanation: At night, temperature decreases with height → sound refracts downward → carries farther.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The displacement at an antinode of a stationary wave y = 2A sin(kx) cos(ωt) is
questions.explanation: At antinode: sin(kx) = 1 → y = 2A cos(ωt). Maximum displacement = 2A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The power transmitted by a progressive wave on a string is
questions.explanation: Power = ½μω²A²v, where μ is linear density and v is wave speed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Resonance tube experiment is used to determine
questions.explanation: Resonance tube: find resonance lengths with known frequency tuning fork → calculate v = f × λ.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When a vibrating body is in contact with air, it produces sound. The sound frequency is the same as
questions.explanation: Sound frequency = vibration frequency of the source.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Reflection of sound follows
questions.explanation: Sound reflection follows laws of reflection: angle of incidence = angle of reflection.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Refraction of sound occurs because
questions.optionA: sound speed varies in different media or temperatures
questions.explanation: Sound refracts when it passes through regions of different speeds (different media or temperature gradients).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Absorption of sound is higher in
questions.explanation: Soft, porous materials (carpets, curtains, foam) absorb sound effectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In auditorium design, excessive reverberation is controlled by
questions.explanation: Sound-absorbing materials reduce reverberation by absorbing reflected sound energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Doppler effect for sound is asymmetric (depends on whether source or observer moves) because
questions.optionB: speed of sound depends on medium, not source/observer
questions.explanation: Sound propagates in a medium at fixed speed relative to medium, making source vs observer motion physically distinct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A standing wave has equation y = 4 sin(πx/15) cos(96πt). The speed of the component traveling waves is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The number of beats heard per second has an upper limit of about
questions.explanation: Human ear can distinguish individual beats up to about 16-20 per second. Beyond this, a continuous tone is heard.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If the fundamental frequency of a pipe closed at one end is 300 Hz, its second overtone frequency is
questions.explanation: Closed pipe: fundamental = 300 Hz. Harmonics: 300, 900, 1500... Second overtone = 5th harmonic = 5×300 = 1500 Hz.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A pipe closed at one end of length 25 cm resonates at 340 Hz, 1020 Hz, 1700 Hz... This represents
questions.explanation: 1020/340 = 3, 1700/340 = 5. These are 1st, 3rd, 5th harmonics (odd only) → closed pipe.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A wave y = 0.02 sin(2π(4t − x/8)) has amplitude
questions.explanation: Amplitude A = 0.02 m (coefficient of sin).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: For the wave y = 0.02 sin(2π(4t − x/8)), the wavelength is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The distance between a node and the nearest antinode in a standing wave is
questions.explanation: Node-to-antinode distance = λ/4 (half the node-to-node distance).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A string vibrates in 5 loops between two fixed supports 2.5 m apart. The wavelength is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Forced vibrations have frequency equal to
questions.optionC: average of natural and driving frequencies
questions.optionD: difference of natural and driving frequencies
questions.explanation: In forced vibrations, the body vibrates at the frequency of the external driving force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Damped oscillations have
questions.explanation: In damped oscillations, amplitude decreases exponentially: A(t) = A₀e^(−bt/2m).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The superposition of two waves of equal amplitude A and phase difference φ gives resultant amplitude
questions.explanation: A_R = 2A cos(φ/2). For φ = 0: A_R = 2A. For φ = π: A_R = 0.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A pipe of length L open at both ends and a pipe of length L/2 closed at one end have
questions.explanation: Open: f = v/(2L). Closed (L/2): f = v/(4 × L/2) = v/(2L). Same fundamental frequency!

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Stethoscope works on the principle of
questions.explanation: Stethoscope channels sound through a tube by multiple reflections, preventing spreading and loss.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The speed of longitudinal waves in a solid rod is v = √(Y/ρ), where Y is
questions.explanation: For a thin rod: v = √(Y/ρ), where Y = Young's modulus.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The speed of longitudinal waves in a fluid is v = √(B/ρ), where B is
questions.explanation: In fluids: v = √(B/ρ), where B = bulk modulus.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Whispering gallery effect demonstrates
questions.optionB: reflection of sound along curved surfaces
questions.explanation: In a whispering gallery, sound reflects along the curved wall surface and reaches the other side clearly.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The half-angle of the Mach cone for an object moving at Mach 2 is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A medium is dispersive if
questions.optionA: wave velocity is independent of frequency
questions.explanation: Dispersive medium: wave speed depends on frequency (or wavelength). e.g., glass for light, deep water for surface waves.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If a source of sound approaches a cliff at 20 m/s and emits 500 Hz, the echo frequency heard by the source is (v = 340 m/s)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Seismic P-waves are
questions.explanation: P (primary) waves are longitudinal compression waves — they travel through solids and liquids.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Seismic S-waves are
questions.explanation: S (secondary) waves are transverse — they can only travel through solids (not liquids).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The equation y₁ = A sin(ωt) and y₂ = A sin(ωt + π) superpose to give
questions.explanation: y₂ = A sin(ωt + π) = −A sin(ωt). y₁ + y₂ = 0 (complete destructive interference).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A vibrating string produces
questions.optionB: only transverse waves on the string
questions.explanation: Vibrations on a string are transverse waves. The string then produces longitudinal sound waves in air.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When two waves of frequencies 100 Hz and 102 Hz superimpose, the beat frequency is
questions.explanation: Beat frequency = |102 − 100| = 2 Hz.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The condition for resonance in a closed pipe is
questions.explanation: Closed pipe resonates at L = nλ/4 for odd n (1, 3, 5, ...).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The frequency of a sonometer wire is determined using
questions.explanation: Sonometer frequency can be determined by paper rider method, optical methods, or electromagnetic excitation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Frequency of a vibrating string is inversely proportional to
questions.explanation: f = (1/2L)√(T/μ). f ∝ 1/L. Also μ = ρπr², so f ∝ 1/diameter. Both B and C.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Two strings of same material and length but radii r and 2r under same tension have frequency ratio
questions.explanation: f ∝ 1/r (since μ = ρπr²). f₁/f₂ = 2r/r = 2/1.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A tuning fork of frequency 480 Hz is used in resonance tube. First resonance occurs at 17 cm. The speed of sound is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The first and second resonance lengths in a resonance tube are 17 cm and 51 cm. The end correction is
questions.explanation: L₁ + e = λ/4, L₂ + e = 3λ/4. L₂ − L₁ = λ/2 → λ = 2(51−17) = 68 cm. e = λ/4 − L₁ = 17 − 17 = 0. Actually e = (L₂ − 3L₁)/2 = (51 − 51)/2 = 0. Let me recalculate: L₂ = 3L₁ + 2e → 51 = 51 + 2e → e = 0. With more precise data e ≈ 0.5 cm typical end correction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The ratio of velocity of sound in hydrogen and oxygen at same temperature is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A source of sound and observer move in the same direction with the same speed. The apparent frequency is
questions.explanation: When v_s = v_o in same direction: f' = f(v − v_o)/(v − v_s) = f. No Doppler shift.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Reflection of waves from a denser medium causes a phase change of
questions.explanation: Reflection from a denser medium (or fixed end) causes a phase change of π (180°).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Reflection from a rarer medium causes a phase change of
questions.explanation: Reflection from a rarer medium (or free end) causes no phase change.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The phenomenon used in musical instruments to amplify sound is
questions.explanation: Musical instruments use resonance (in air columns, strings, membranes) to amplify sound.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The harmonics present in a vibrating string depend on
questions.optionA: how and where the string is plucked
questions.explanation: The point and manner of plucking determine which harmonics are excited and their relative amplitudes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A guitar string produces a note of 440 Hz. If the string is pressed at its midpoint, the frequency becomes
questions.explanation: Pressing at midpoint halves the effective length. f ∝ 1/L → f → 2×440 = 880 Hz.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The flute is an example of
questions.explanation: Flute is an open pipe — open at both ends.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The clarinet acts approximately as a
questions.explanation: Clarinet effectively acts as a closed pipe (reed end is closed), producing mainly odd harmonics.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The SI unit of acoustic impedance is
questions.explanation: Acoustic impedance Z = ρv. SI unit: kg/(m²·s) = Pa·s/m = rayl. All are equivalent.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The fundamental mode of a vibrating membrane (drum) produces
questions.explanation: 2D vibrating membranes produce overtones that are not integral multiples of the fundamental (non-harmonic).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If frequency of sound is tripled, the wavelength
questions.explanation: v = fλ. If f → 3f and v is constant (same medium), λ → λ/3.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A wave pulse reflected from a rigid wall is
questions.explanation: Reflection from a rigid (fixed) boundary inverts the pulse (phase change of π).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A wave pulse reflected from a free end is
questions.explanation: Reflection from a free end: pulse returns without inversion (no phase change).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In a resonance tube, the velocity of sound can be calculated as v = 2f ×
questions.explanation: L₂ − L₁ = λ/2 → λ = 2(L₂ − L₁). v = fλ = 2f(L₂ − L₁).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The displacement equation y = A sin(ωt) + A cos(ωt) represents a wave with amplitude
questions.explanation: A sin(ωt) + A cos(ωt) = A√2 sin(ωt + π/4). Amplitude = A√2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If the temperature increases by 1°C near 0°C, the speed of sound increases by approximately

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The speed of transverse waves cannot propagate through
questions.explanation: Transverse waves require rigidity (shear modulus). Liquids interior and vacuum have no rigidity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The vibrations of air column in a pipe are
questions.explanation: Air column vibrations in pipes are longitudinal standing waves.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If the closed end of a pipe is opened, the fundamental frequency
questions.explanation: Closed: f = v/(4L). Open: f = v/(2L). Opening doubles the fundamental frequency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The number of possible natural frequencies for a string fixed at both ends is
questions.explanation: A string fixed at both ends has infinite harmonics: f₁, 2f₁, 3f₁, 4f₁, ...

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Shock waves are produced when
questions.explanation: Shock waves (sonic boom): when source speed exceeds sound speed (Mach number > 1).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The bulk modulus B of air is related to pressure P by B =
questions.explanation: For adiabatic process (sound): B_adiabatic = γP.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Noise is characterized by
questions.explanation: Noise: irregular vibrations with no definite pitch or periodic pattern.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Musical sound is characterized by
questions.optionB: regular, periodic vibrations producing pleasant sensation
questions.explanation: Musical sound: regular periodic vibrations with definite pitch, quality, and loudness.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Doppler effect can be used to measure
questions.explanation: Doppler effect: used in radar, medical ultrasonography, astronomy to measure speed of moving objects.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If two identical waves travel in the same direction, the resultant is a wave with
questions.explanation: Two identical waves (same A, f, φ) in same direction: constructive superposition → amplitude = 2A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A string of length 2 m resonates in 4th harmonic at 200 Hz. The wave speed on the string is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Ultrasound is used in medical imaging (sonography) because
questions.optionB: short wavelength gives good resolution; reflects from soft tissue
questions.explanation: High-frequency ultrasound has short wavelength → good spatial resolution. It reflects at tissue boundaries.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The period of a wave of frequency 50 Hz is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: For a wave y = 10 sin(2π(t/0.02 − x/100)), the wave speed is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The loudness of a sound depends on
questions.explanation: Loudness depends on amplitude (intensity ∝ A²). Larger amplitude → louder sound.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Sound waves exhibit diffraction. This allows us to
questions.explanation: Sound wavelengths are comparable to everyday obstacles → significant diffraction → we can hear around corners.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The wavelength of visible light is much smaller than sound wavelength. Therefore
questions.optionA: light diffracts more than sound around obstacles
questions.optionB: sound diffracts more than light around everyday obstacles
questions.explanation: Sound λ ~ meters (comparable to obstacles) → large diffraction. Light λ ~ nm → negligible diffraction around large objects.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Interference of sound can be demonstrated by
questions.explanation: Quincke's tube demonstrates interference of sound waves from two paths of different lengths.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A vibrating body must have
questions.explanation: Vibration requires both inertia (to overshoot equilibrium) and elasticity (restoring force).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The frequency above which the sound becomes painful is approximately
questions.optionC: the threshold of pain is about intensity level, not frequency
questions.explanation: Pain threshold is about intensity level (~120 dB), not a specific frequency. Any frequency at high enough intensity causes pain.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When the length of a closed pipe is doubled, its fundamental frequency
questions.explanation: f = v/(4L). If L → 2L, f → f/2. Frequency halves.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Coulomb's law states that the force between two point charges is
questions.explanation: Coulomb's law: F = kq₁q₂/r², where k = 1/(4πε₀) ≈ 9×10⁹ N·m²/C².

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The electric field due to a point charge q at distance r is
questions.explanation: E = F/q₀ = kq/r². Electric field varies as 1/r².

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Electric potential due to a point charge q at distance r is
questions.explanation: V = kq/r. Potential varies as 1/r.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The relation between electric field E and potential V is
questions.explanation: E = −dV/dr (electric field is the negative gradient of potential).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Gauss's law states that the total electric flux through a closed surface equals
questions.explanation: Gauss's law: ∮E·dA = q_enclosed/ε₀.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor is C =
questions.explanation: C = ε₀A/d for parallel plate capacitor (A = area, d = separation).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When a dielectric of constant K is inserted between plates of a capacitor, the capacitance becomes
questions.explanation: Capacitance with dielectric: C' = KC, where K is the dielectric constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The energy stored in a capacitor is
questions.explanation: Energy = ½CV² = ½QV = Q²/(2C).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Ohm's law states
questions.explanation: Ohm's law: V = IR (voltage = current × resistance).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The resistance of a conductor R = ρL/A, where ρ is
questions.explanation: R = ρL/A. ρ is resistivity (specific resistance) of the material.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Resistances in series have equivalent resistance
questions.explanation: Series: R_eq = R₁ + R₂ + R₃ + ...

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Resistances in parallel have equivalent resistance
questions.explanation: Parallel (two): 1/R_eq = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ → R_eq = R₁R₂/(R₁+R₂).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Kirchhoff's first law (junction rule) is based on conservation of
questions.explanation: KCL: ΣI_in = ΣI_out at a junction (conservation of charge).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Kirchhoff's second law (loop rule) is based on conservation of
questions.explanation: KVL: Σ(EMF) = Σ(IR) around a closed loop (conservation of energy).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Wheatstone bridge is balanced when
questions.explanation: Balanced Wheatstone bridge: P/Q = R/S → galvanometer shows zero deflection.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A potentiometer measures EMF more accurately than a voltmeter because
questions.optionB: it draws no current at balance (null method)
questions.explanation: Potentiometer at balance draws zero current → no internal resistance drop → measures true EMF.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Seebeck effect is
questions.optionA: heating at a junction of two metals when current flows
questions.optionB: generation of EMF in a thermocouple due to temperature difference
questions.explanation: Seebeck effect: EMF generated when junctions of dissimilar metals are at different temperatures.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Peltier effect is
questions.optionA: EMF generation due to temperature difference
questions.optionB: heating or cooling at a junction when current flows through it
questions.explanation: Peltier effect: heating or cooling at the junction of two dissimilar metals when current passes through.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Thomson effect is
questions.optionA: heating/cooling along a single conductor carrying current with temperature gradient
questions.explanation: Thomson effect: absorption or evolution of heat along a single conductor carrying current through a temperature gradient.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The magnetic field due to a long straight conductor at distance r carrying current I is
questions.explanation: Biot-Savart for infinite wire: B = μ₀I/(2πr).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The magnetic field at the center of a circular loop of radius R carrying current I is
questions.explanation: B = μ₀I/(2R) at the center of a circular current loop.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The force on a current-carrying conductor of length L in magnetic field B is
questions.explanation: F = BIL sinθ (θ = angle between conductor and B). Maximum when θ = 90°.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The force between two parallel conductors carrying currents in the same direction is
questions.explanation: Same direction currents → attractive force. Opposite direction → repulsive.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Ampere is defined based on the force between two parallel conductors as
questions.explanation: 1 ampere: current which produces a force of 2×10⁻⁷ N/m between two infinite parallel conductors 1 m apart.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The torque on a current loop in a magnetic field is
questions.explanation: τ = NIAB sinθ (N = turns, I = current, A = area, B = field, θ = angle between normal and B).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A galvanometer is converted to an ammeter by connecting a
questions.explanation: Ammeter: galvanometer + low resistance shunt in parallel (to bypass most current).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A galvanometer is converted to a voltmeter by connecting a
questions.explanation: Voltmeter: galvanometer + high resistance in series (to limit current for high voltage measurement).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction states that induced EMF equals
questions.optionA: rate of change of magnetic flux
questions.optionD: rate of change of electric field
questions.explanation: ε = −dΦ/dt (negative sign from Lenz's law).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Lenz's law states that the induced current
questions.explanation: Lenz's law: induced current direction opposes the change in flux that caused it (conservation of energy).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Self-inductance L of a solenoid of N turns, length l, and area A is
questions.explanation: L = μ₀N²A/l = μ₀n²Al (n = N/l).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The energy stored in an inductor is
questions.explanation: Energy = ½LI².

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In an AC circuit, the RMS value of voltage is
questions.explanation: V_rms = V₀/√2 for sinusoidal AC.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The impedance of a series RLC circuit is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: At resonance in a series RLC circuit, the impedance is
questions.explanation: At resonance: X_L = X_C → Z = R (minimum impedance, maximum current).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The resonant frequency of a series RLC circuit is
questions.explanation: f₀ = 1/(2π√(LC)). At this frequency, X_L = X_C.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The power factor in an AC circuit is
questions.explanation: Power factor = cosφ = R/Z.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The average power consumed in a purely inductive circuit is
questions.explanation: Pure inductor: φ = 90° → P = V_rms I_rms cos90° = 0 (wattless current).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A transformer works on the principle of
questions.explanation: Transformer: mutual induction. Changing current in primary induces EMF in secondary.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In an ideal step-up transformer, if voltage increases by factor n, the current
questions.explanation: Ideal transformer: V₁I₁ = V₂I₂. If V₂ = nV₁, then I₂ = I₁/n.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The electric field inside a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium is
questions.explanation: In electrostatic equilibrium, E = 0 inside a conductor. All charge resides on the surface.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Electric field lines never
questions.explanation: Field lines never cross because at any point E has a unique direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An equipotential surface is always
questions.explanation: No work is done moving a charge along an equipotential surface → E is perpendicular to it.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The electric potential at the surface of a conducting sphere of radius R and charge Q is
questions.explanation: V = kQ/R at the surface. Inside conductor: V = constant = kQ/R.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Two capacitors C₁ and C₂ in series have equivalent capacitance
questions.explanation: Series: 1/C_eq = 1/C₁ + 1/C₂ → C_eq = C₁C₂/(C₁+C₂).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Two capacitors C₁ and C₂ in parallel have equivalent capacitance
questions.explanation: Parallel: C_eq = C₁ + C₂.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Electric flux through a closed surface containing a charge of 1 μC is (ε₀ = 8.85 × 10⁻¹² C²/(N·m²))

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The drift velocity of electrons in a conductor is
questions.explanation: Drift velocity is very small (~mm/s), much less than thermal velocity (~10⁵ m/s).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The current I is related to drift velocity v_d by I =
questions.explanation: I = neAv_d (n = number density, e = charge, A = cross-section, v_d = drift velocity).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The temperature coefficient of resistance for metals is
questions.explanation: Metals: resistance increases with temperature → positive temperature coefficient.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The temperature coefficient of resistance for semiconductors is
questions.explanation: Semiconductors: resistance decreases with temperature → negative temperature coefficient.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The internal resistance of a cell can be measured using a
questions.explanation: Potentiometer accurately measures EMF and terminal voltage → internal resistance r = (E−V)R/V.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Joule's law of heating states H =
questions.explanation: Heat produced H = I²Rt (Joule's law of heating).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Electrical power P = VI =
questions.explanation: P = VI = I²R = V²/R.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: 1 kilowatt-hour (kWh) equals

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In a Wheatstone bridge, if P = 10 Ω, Q = 20 Ω, R = 15 Ω, the value of S for balance is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The magnetic field inside a solenoid of n turns per unit length carrying current I is
questions.explanation: B = μ₀nI inside a long solenoid (n = turns per unit length).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A charged particle moving perpendicular to a magnetic field follows a
questions.explanation: F = qvB (always perpendicular to v) → centripetal force → circular motion.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The radius of circular motion of a charged particle in magnetic field is r =
