questions.explanation: qvB = mv²/r → r = mv/(qB).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Lorentz force on a charge q moving with velocity v in fields E and B is
questions.explanation: Lorentz force: F = q(E + v × B).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A magnetic dipole moment of a current loop is m =
questions.explanation: Magnetic moment m = NIA (N = turns, I = current, A = area).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Diamagnetic materials have susceptibility
questions.explanation: Diamagnetic: χ is small and negative (slightly repelled by magnetic field).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Paramagnetic materials have susceptibility
questions.explanation: Paramagnetic: χ is small and positive. Weakly attracted to magnetic field.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Ferromagnetic materials have susceptibility
questions.explanation: Ferromagnetic: χ is very large and positive (strongly attracted, can be permanently magnetized).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Curie's law states that susceptibility of a paramagnetic material is proportional to
questions.explanation: Curie's law: χ = C/T (susceptibility inversely proportional to temperature).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Hysteresis loop area represents
questions.explanation: Area of B-H hysteresis loop = energy lost per unit volume per cycle (heat dissipated).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Retentivity is the
questions.optionA: ability to retain magnetism after removing magnetizing field
questions.explanation: Retentivity: residual magnetism when the magnetizing field is reduced to zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Coercivity is the
questions.optionB: reverse field needed to reduce magnetization to zero
questions.explanation: Coercivity: the reverse magnetic field required to reduce residual magnetism to zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: For permanent magnets, we need materials with
questions.explanation: Permanent magnets: high retentivity (strong residual field) and high coercivity (hard to demagnetize).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The EMF induced in a coil of N turns when flux changes by dΦ in time dt is
questions.explanation: Faraday's law: ε = −NdΦ/dt. Negative sign indicates opposition to change (Lenz's law).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An EMF of 10 V is induced when current changes at 5 A/s. The self-inductance is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The time constant of an LR circuit is
questions.explanation: τ = L/R for an LR circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The time constant of an RC circuit is
questions.explanation: τ = RC for an RC circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In AC, the reactance of an inductor X_L is
questions.explanation: Inductive reactance X_L = ωL = 2πfL.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In AC, the reactance of a capacitor X_C is
questions.explanation: Capacitive reactance X_C = 1/(ωC) = 1/(2πfC).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In a purely resistive AC circuit, voltage and current are
questions.explanation: In a pure R circuit, V and I are in phase (φ = 0).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In a purely inductive AC circuit, current lags voltage by
questions.explanation: In pure L: current lags voltage by 90° (π/2).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In a purely capacitive AC circuit, current leads voltage by
questions.explanation: In pure C: current leads voltage by 90° (π/2).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Q-factor of a series RLC circuit at resonance is
questions.explanation: Q = ω₀L/R = 1/(ω₀CR). Higher Q → sharper resonance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The transformer ratio is
questions.explanation: Transformer: V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Eddy currents can be reduced by using
questions.explanation: Laminated cores (thin insulated sheets) reduce eddy current paths → less energy loss.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The direction of force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is given by
questions.explanation: Fleming's left-hand rule: thumb (force), forefinger (B), middle finger (I).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The direction of induced current is given by
questions.explanation: Fleming's right-hand rule for generators: thumb (motion), forefinger (B), middle finger (induced current).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An electric dipole in a uniform electric field experiences
questions.optionB: net torque but no net force
questions.explanation: Uniform field: equal and opposite forces on +q and −q → zero net force but non-zero torque τ = pE sinθ.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The electric dipole moment is p =
questions.explanation: p = q × 2a = qd (magnitude of charge × distance between charges), directed from −q to +q.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The electric field of a dipole on the axial line at large distance r varies as
questions.explanation: Dipole field on axis: E = 2kp/r³ (at large r). Falls off as 1/r³.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Superconductors have zero resistance below their
questions.explanation: Below the critical temperature T_c, a superconductor has zero electrical resistance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Meissner effect in superconductors is the expulsion of
questions.explanation: Meissner effect: complete expulsion of magnetic flux from the interior of a superconductor (perfect diamagnet).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A moving coil galvanometer uses a radial magnetic field so that
questions.optionB: deflection is proportional to current (linear scale)
questions.explanation: Radial field: θ is always 90° → τ = NIAB sin90° = NIAB → linear relationship between deflection and current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The equivalent resistance of n identical resistors R in parallel is
questions.explanation: n identical R in parallel: R_eq = R/n.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The equivalent resistance of n identical resistors R in series is
questions.explanation: n identical R in series: R_eq = nR.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The neutral temperature of a thermocouple is the temperature at which
questions.explanation: Neutral temperature: dE/dT = 0 → EMF is maximum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The inversion temperature of a thermocouple is the temperature at which
questions.explanation: At inversion temperature, thermoelectric EMF reverses direction (becomes zero and changes sign).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An ideal ammeter has
questions.explanation: Ideal ammeter: zero resistance (doesn't alter the circuit current).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An ideal voltmeter has
questions.explanation: Ideal voltmeter: infinite resistance (draws no current from the circuit).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The mutual inductance between two coils depends on
questions.optionB: geometry, number of turns, and medium
questions.explanation: M depends on geometry (shape, size, orientation), number of turns, separation, and permeability of medium.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An AC generator converts
questions.explanation: AC generator: mechanical energy (rotation) → electrical energy (induced AC EMF).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The maximum EMF generated by an AC generator is ε₀ = NBAω, where ω is
questions.explanation: ε = NBAω sin(ωt). ε₀ = NBAω (peak EMF).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Choke coil is preferred over resistance for reducing AC current because
questions.explanation: Choke (inductor): current lags by ~90° → very little power loss. Resistance would waste energy as heat.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The skin effect in AC conductors causes current to flow mainly
questions.explanation: Skin effect: AC current tends to flow near the surface of the conductor. Increases effective resistance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The displacement current was introduced by
questions.explanation: Maxwell added displacement current (ε₀ dΦ_E/dt) to Ampere's law, completing electromagnetic theory.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Maxwell's equations unify
questions.explanation: Maxwell's four equations provide a complete description of electromagnetic phenomena.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The electric field just outside a charged conductor is
questions.explanation: E = σ/ε₀ just outside the surface, directed perpendicular to it.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The potential inside a charged hollow conducting sphere is
questions.explanation: Inside hollow conductor: E = 0 → V = constant = kQ/R (same as surface).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The capacitance of a spherical conductor of radius R is
questions.explanation: C = 4πε₀R for an isolated sphere (other plate at infinity).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When a dielectric slab is inserted in a charged capacitor (battery disconnected), the voltage
questions.explanation: Battery disconnected: Q constant. C increases. V = Q/C decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When a dielectric slab is inserted in a capacitor connected to a battery, the charge
questions.explanation: Battery connected: V constant. C increases. Q = CV increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The energy density in an electric field E is
questions.explanation: Energy density u = ½ε₀E² (energy per unit volume stored in electric field).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The energy density in a magnetic field B is
questions.explanation: Energy density u = B²/(2μ₀).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The SI unit of electric flux is
questions.explanation: Electric flux Φ = E·A. Unit: V·m = N·m²/C.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The SI unit of magnetic flux is
questions.explanation: Magnetic flux Φ = B·A. Unit: Weber (Wb) = T·m².

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: 1 Tesla equals
questions.explanation: 1 T = 1 Wb/m² = 1 kg/(A·s²).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The force per unit length between two parallel wires carrying currents I₁ and I₂ separated by distance d is
questions.explanation: F/L = μ₀I₁I₂/(2πd).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A cyclotron is used to accelerate
questions.explanation: Cyclotron accelerates heavy charged particles like protons and deuterons using oscillating electric field and magnetic field.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The cyclotron frequency is f =
questions.explanation: Cyclotron frequency f = qB/(2πm). Independent of radius and speed (non-relativistic).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A charged particle enters a uniform magnetic field parallel to the field direction. Its path is
questions.explanation: v ∥ B → F = qv×B = 0. Particle continues in a straight line.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A charged particle enters a magnetic field at angle θ to the field. Its path is
questions.explanation: Component parallel to B → straight line. Perpendicular component → circular. Combined → helical path.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The magnetic field at a point on the axis of a circular coil at distance x from center is B =
questions.explanation: B = μ₀IR²/(2(R²+x²)^(3/2)). At center (x=0): B = μ₀I/(2R).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Ampere's circuital law states ∮B·dl =
questions.explanation: ∮B·dl = μ₀I_enclosed for any closed loop.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The magnetic field outside a long solenoid is approximately
questions.explanation: Outside an ideal infinite solenoid, B ≈ 0.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The magnetic field of a toroid with N turns, mean radius r, and current I is B =
questions.explanation: B = μ₀NI/(2πr) inside the toroid (using Ampere's law).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Electromagnetic waves were predicted by
questions.explanation: Maxwell predicted electromagnetic waves from his equations. Hertz experimentally verified them.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The speed of electromagnetic waves in vacuum is c =

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In electromagnetic waves, E and B are
questions.optionB: perpendicular to each other and to direction of propagation
questions.explanation: EM waves: E ⊥ B ⊥ direction of propagation. They oscillate in phase.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Biot-Savart law gives the magnetic field due to
questions.explanation: Biot-Savart law: dB = (μ₀/4π)(Idl × r̂)/r² for a current element.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A metre bridge is based on the principle of
questions.explanation: Metre bridge is a practical form of Wheatstone bridge using a uniform resistance wire.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In a potentiometer, the fall of potential across the wire is
questions.optionB: uniform if wire has uniform cross-section
questions.explanation: Uniform wire → uniform resistance per unit length → uniform potential gradient.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The sensitivity of a potentiometer can be increased by
questions.optionA: increasing the current through the wire
questions.optionC: using a longer wire with same total EMF
questions.explanation: Longer wire → smaller potential gradient → can detect smaller EMF differences → higher sensitivity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The thermoelectric EMF of a thermocouple is given by E = aT + bT², where the neutral temperature T_n =
questions.explanation: dE/dT = a + 2bT = 0 → T_n = −a/(2b).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The inversion temperature T_i of a thermocouple is related to the neutral temperature T_n and cold junction temperature T_c by
questions.explanation: T_n = (T_c + T_i)/2 → T_i = 2T_n − T_c.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The electric field between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor (neglecting edge effects) is
questions.explanation: Between plates: E = σ/ε₀ (contributions from both plates add up: σ/(2ε₀) + σ/(2ε₀) = σ/ε₀).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The work done in moving a charge q through a potential difference V is
questions.explanation: W = qV (work = charge × potential difference).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: 1 electron volt (eV) equals

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The potential due to an electric dipole at a point on the equatorial line is
questions.explanation: On the equatorial line of a dipole, the potential contributions from +q and −q cancel → V = 0.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The capacitance of a cylindrical capacitor of inner radius a and outer radius b, length L is
questions.explanation: C = 2πε₀L/ln(b/a) for a cylindrical capacitor.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Kirchhoff's laws are applicable to
questions.explanation: Kirchhoff's laws apply to both AC and DC circuits.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The maximum power is transferred from a source to load when
questions.explanation: Maximum power transfer theorem: P_max when R_load = r (internal resistance).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Two cells of EMFs E₁ and E₂ and internal resistances r₁ and r₂ connected in parallel give equivalent EMF
questions.explanation: Parallel cells: E_eq = (E₁r₂ + E₂r₁)/(r₁ + r₂), r_eq = r₁r₂/(r₁ + r₂).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The magnetic susceptibility χ is related to relative permeability μ_r by

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Earth's magnetic field has both horizontal (H) and vertical (V) components. The angle of dip δ satisfies
questions.explanation: tanδ = V/H, where δ is the angle of dip.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: At the magnetic equator, the angle of dip is
questions.explanation: At magnetic equator: vertical component V = 0 → tanδ = 0 → δ = 0°.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: At the magnetic poles, the angle of dip is
questions.explanation: At magnetic poles: horizontal component H = 0 → tanδ = ∞ → δ = 90°.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The EMF induced in a rod of length L moving with velocity v perpendicular to magnetic field B is
questions.explanation: Motional EMF ε = BLv (for rod moving perpendicular to both its length and B).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In an LC oscillation, the frequency of oscillation is
questions.explanation: f = 1/(2π√(LC)). Energy oscillates between electric (capacitor) and magnetic (inductor).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A step-down transformer has
questions.explanation: Step-down: fewer secondary turns → lower voltage.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Transformers do not work with DC because
questions.optionB: DC produces constant flux, so no EMF is induced in secondary
questions.explanation: DC: constant current → constant flux → dΦ/dt = 0 → no induced EMF in secondary.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The electric flux through a closed surface enclosing no charge is
questions.explanation: By Gauss's law: Φ = q_enclosed/ε₀ = 0/ε₀ = 0 when no charge is enclosed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The magnetic flux through any closed surface is always
questions.explanation: ∮B·dA = 0 (Gauss's law for magnetism). No magnetic monopoles exist → net flux through closed surface = 0.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An electric charge at rest produces
questions.explanation: A stationary charge produces only electric field. Moving charges produce both E and B fields.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The condition for maximum current in a series RLC circuit is
questions.explanation: At resonance X_L = X_C → Z = R (minimum) → I = V/R (maximum).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Wattless component of current in an AC circuit is
questions.explanation: Wattless (reactive) component = I sinφ. Active component = I cosφ.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The color code for a 470 Ω ± 5% resistor is
questions.explanation: 4 = Yellow, 7 = Violet, ×10 = Brown, 5% = Gold → 470 Ω ± 5%.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The drift velocity of electrons is related to current density J by
questions.explanation: J = I/A = nev_d.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The relaxation time in Drude model is the average time between
questions.explanation: Relaxation time τ: average time between successive collisions of conduction electrons with lattice ions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Conductivity σ in terms of Drude model is σ =
questions.explanation: σ = ne²τ/m = 1/ρ.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Magnetic field lines form closed loops because
questions.explanation: No magnetic monopoles → ∮B·dA = 0 → B lines have no beginning or end → closed loops.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The coefficient of coupling k between two coils of self-inductances L₁ and L₂ with mutual inductance M is
questions.explanation: k = M/√(L₁L₂). k = 1 for perfect coupling, k < 1 otherwise.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In a purely capacitive AC circuit, the average power consumed is
questions.explanation: Pure capacitor: φ = −90° → P = V_rms I_rms cos(−90°) = 0. Wattless current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The power consumed in an AC circuit is P =
questions.explanation: P = V_rms × I_rms × cosφ (true/active power).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The apparent power in an AC circuit is
questions.explanation: Apparent power = V_rms × I_rms (in VA). True power = apparent power × power factor.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: At resonance in a series RLC circuit, the phase angle between voltage and current is
questions.explanation: At resonance: X_L = X_C → tanφ = 0 → φ = 0°. V and I are in phase.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The magnetic moment of a bar magnet of length 2l and pole strength m is
questions.explanation: Magnetic moment M = m × 2l (pole strength × magnetic length).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A compass needle aligns along the
questions.explanation: Compass needle aligns with the horizontal component of Earth's magnetic field → along the magnetic meridian.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The angle between geographic north and magnetic north is called
questions.explanation: Declination: angle between geographic and magnetic meridians at a location.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Eddy currents are induced in
questions.optionB: bulk conductors in changing magnetic fields
questions.explanation: Eddy currents: circulating currents induced in bulk conductors by changing magnetic flux.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Applications of eddy currents include
questions.explanation: Eddy currents used in EM braking, induction heating, speedometers, and many other applications.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The back EMF of a motor
questions.explanation: Back EMF opposes the applied voltage (Lenz's law), limiting current through the motor.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The principle of a DC motor is based on
questions.optionB: force on a current-carrying conductor in magnetic field
questions.explanation: DC motor: torque on current loop in magnetic field causes rotation (F = BIL sinθ).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The principle of a DC generator is based on
questions.explanation: DC generator: rotating coil in magnetic field → changing flux → induced EMF (Faraday's law).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The bandwidth of a series RLC circuit is
questions.explanation: Bandwidth Δω = R/L = ω₀/Q. They are equivalent expressions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Van de Graaff generator is used to generate
questions.explanation: Van de Graaff generator: accumulates charge on a hollow sphere → very high voltages (millions of volts).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The dielectric strength of a material is the maximum electric field it can withstand without
questions.explanation: Dielectric strength: maximum E field before breakdown (insulation failure, current flows).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A capacitor blocks DC because
questions.optionB: after fully charging, no current flows (gap between plates)
questions.optionC: it has infinite resistance at all times
questions.explanation: DC → capacitor fully charges to source voltage → no more current flows. AC → continuous charging/discharging → current flows.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The unit of self-inductance is
questions.explanation: L is measured in Henry (H). 1 H = 1 V·s/A = 1 Wb/A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The current through an inductor cannot change instantaneously because
questions.optionB: changing current induces EMF opposing the change (self-induction)
questions.explanation: Self-induced EMF opposes rapid change in current (ε = −L dI/dt). Instantaneous change would require infinite EMF.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In an RL circuit, the current grows as I = I₀(1 − e^(−t/τ)), where τ =
questions.explanation: Time constant τ = L/R for RL circuit. At t = τ, current reaches ~63% of maximum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In an RC circuit, the voltage across the capacitor during charging is V = V₀(1 − e^(−t/τ)), where τ =
questions.explanation: τ = RC for an RC circuit. At t = τ, V reaches ~63% of V₀.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Hall effect is used to determine
questions.optionB: type (n or p) and concentration of charge carriers
questions.explanation: Hall effect: charge carriers deflected by magnetic field → Hall voltage reveals carrier type and density.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Hall voltage V_H =
questions.explanation: V_H = BI/(net), where n = carrier density, e = charge, t = thickness.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Electromagnetic induction is the basis of
questions.explanation: EM induction (Faraday's law) is fundamental to generators, transformers, induction coils, and many devices.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The electric field at the center of a uniformly charged ring is
questions.explanation: By symmetry, all E field contributions cancel at the center of a uniformly charged ring → E = 0.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The potential energy of a dipole in a uniform electric field E is
questions.explanation: U = −p⃗·E⃗ = −pE cosθ. Minimum when p ∥ E (θ = 0).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A parallel plate capacitor with plate area A = 100 cm² and separation d = 1 mm has capacitance (ε₀ = 8.85 × 10⁻¹² F/m)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A cell of EMF 2 V and internal resistance 1 Ω is connected to a 3 Ω external resistance. The current is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The terminal voltage of a cell during discharge is
questions.explanation: During discharge: V = E − Ir (internal resistance causes voltage drop).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Cells in series: total EMF =
questions.explanation: Series cells: E_total = E₁ + E₂ + ... and r_total = r₁ + r₂ + ...

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The shunt resistance S needed to convert a galvanometer (resistance G, full deflection current Ig) to an ammeter of range I is
questions.explanation: S = IgG/(I − Ig). Shunt carries (I − Ig) current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The high resistance R needed to convert a galvanometer to voltmeter of range V is
questions.explanation: R = V/Ig − G (in series with galvanometer).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Electromagnetic waves are produced by
questions.explanation: Accelerating charges produce electromagnetic waves (changing E → changing B → propagating EM wave).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Poynting vector S⃗ represents
questions.optionB: rate of energy flow per unit area in EM wave
questions.explanation: S⃗ = (1/μ₀)(E⃗ × B⃗). It gives the energy flux (power per unit area) and direction of EM wave propagation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The ratio E₀/B₀ in an electromagnetic wave equals
questions.explanation: E₀/B₀ = c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A charged particle in a uniform electric field (no B) follows a
questions.explanation: If initial v ∥ E → straight line (acceleration). If v has component ⊥ E → parabolic path (like projectile in gravity).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The net force on a charged particle in crossed E and B fields is zero when
questions.explanation: qE = qvB → v = E/B. Used in velocity selector.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Faraday's ice pail experiment demonstrated that
questions.optionB: charge resides on outer surface of a conductor
questions.optionD: magnetic field is zero inside ice
questions.explanation: Faraday showed that induced charge appears only on the outer surface of a hollow conductor.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The principle of a capacitor microphone is based on
questions.optionB: change in capacitance due to change in distance between plates
questions.explanation: Sound vibrations change plate separation → capacitance changes → voltage/charge changes → electrical signal.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Corona discharge occurs at
questions.optionB: sharp points of conductors at high voltage
questions.explanation: Sharp points: very high E field → ionizes air → corona discharge (visible glow, crackling sound).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The capacitance of Earth (radius ≈ 6400 km) is approximately

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: For a series RLC circuit below resonance (ω < ω₀), the circuit is
questions.explanation: Below resonance: X_C > X_L → circuit is capacitive → current leads voltage.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: For a series RLC circuit above resonance (ω > ω₀), the circuit is
questions.explanation: Above resonance: X_L > X_C → circuit is inductive → current lags voltage.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The relation between electric displacement D, electric field E, and polarization P is
questions.explanation: D = ε₀E + P = εE (in a linear dielectric).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The number of electric field lines originating from a charge of 1 C (in SI) is
questions.explanation: By Gauss's law: total flux = q/ε₀ = 1/ε₀ for q = 1 C.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The photoelectric effect was explained by
questions.explanation: Einstein explained the photoelectric effect using the quantum theory of light (photon concept) in 1905.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The energy of a photon is E =
questions.explanation: E = hf = hc/λ, where h is Planck's constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The photoelectric equation is
questions.explanation: Einstein's photoelectric equation: hf = φ + ½mv²_max, where φ is work function.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The threshold frequency in photoelectric effect is the minimum frequency
questions.optionB: below which no photoelectrons are emitted
questions.explanation: Threshold frequency f₀ = φ/h. Below f₀, photon energy < work function → no emission.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The stopping potential V₀ in the photoelectric effect depends on
questions.explanation: eV₀ = hf − φ. Stopping potential depends on frequency, not intensity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Increasing the intensity of incident light (above threshold) increases
questions.explanation: Higher intensity → more photons → more photoelectrons → higher current. KE_max unchanged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Planck's constant h =
questions.explanation: h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s (Planck's constant).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The de Broglie wavelength of a particle of momentum p is
questions.explanation: λ = h/p = h/(mv). de Broglie hypothesis: matter has wave properties.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through potential V is
questions.explanation: KE = eV = p²/(2m) → p = √(2meV) → λ = h/√(2meV).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Bohr's first postulate states that electrons revolve in
questions.explanation: Bohr: electrons in certain allowed orbits do not radiate energy (stationary states).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Bohr's quantization condition is
questions.explanation: Angular momentum: L = mvr = nℏ = nh/(2π) for n = 1, 2, 3...

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The energy of an electron in the nth orbit of hydrogen atom is E_n =
questions.explanation: E_n = −13.6/n² eV for hydrogen. Negative sign indicates bound state.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The radius of the nth Bohr orbit is r_n =
questions.explanation: r_n = n²a₀ = n² × 0.529 Å. Radius ∝ n².

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Bohr radius a₀ is approximately
questions.explanation: a₀ = 0.529 Å = 0.529 × 10⁻¹⁰ m (radius of first orbit of hydrogen).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: When an electron transitions from n=3 to n=2 in hydrogen, the spectral line belongs to
questions.explanation: Balmer series: transitions to n=2. 3→2 is the first line (Hα) of Balmer series (visible light).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Lyman series of hydrogen corresponds to transitions ending at
questions.explanation: Lyman: transitions to n=1 (ultraviolet region).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Rydberg formula for hydrogen spectral lines is 1/λ =
questions.explanation: 1/λ = R(1/n₁² − 1/n₂²), where R = 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹ (Rydberg constant).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The maximum number of spectral lines from transitions in n levels is
questions.explanation: Maximum spectral lines = n(n−1)/2 (number of possible downward transitions).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The charge-to-mass ratio of an electron was determined by
questions.explanation: J.J. Thomson measured e/m of electrons using cathode ray deflection in E and B fields.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The charge of an electron was measured by
questions.explanation: Millikan's oil drop experiment: measured elementary charge e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Cathode rays consist of
questions.explanation: Cathode rays are streams of high-speed electrons emitted from the cathode in a discharge tube.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: X-rays were discovered by
questions.explanation: Wilhelm Roentgen discovered X-rays in 1895.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: X-rays are produced when
questions.optionA: fast electrons strike a heavy metal target
questions.explanation: X-rays produced by decelerating high-energy electrons hitting a metal target (Bremsstrahlung) and inner-shell transitions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The minimum wavelength of X-rays produced at voltage V is λ_min =
questions.explanation: λ_min = hc/(eV) (Duane-Hunt limit). All KE converts to photon energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Moseley's law relates the frequency of characteristic X-rays to
questions.explanation: Moseley's law: √f = a(Z − b). Frequency depends on atomic number Z.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Compton effect demonstrates
questions.explanation: Compton effect: X-ray photon scatters off electron, losing energy. Proves particle nature of EM radiation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Compton wavelength shift Δλ = λ' − λ =
questions.explanation: Compton shift: Δλ = (h/(m_e c))(1 − cosφ). Maximum at φ = 180°.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Radioactivity was discovered by
questions.explanation: Henri Becquerel discovered radioactivity in 1896 while studying phosphorescence of uranium salts.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Alpha particles are
questions.explanation: α particles = helium-4 nuclei (2 protons + 2 neutrons), charge +2e, mass 4u.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Beta particles are
questions.explanation: β⁻ = electrons, β⁺ = positrons emitted during nuclear decay.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Gamma rays are
questions.explanation: γ rays: high-energy photons emitted from excited nuclei. No charge, no mass.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The penetrating power order is
questions.explanation: Penetrating power: γ > β > α. Ionizing power: α > β > γ (reverse order).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The radioactive decay law is N =
questions.explanation: N = N₀e^(−λt), where λ = decay constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The half-life of a radioactive substance is T₁/₂ =

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The mean life of a radioactive substance is τ =
questions.explanation: Mean life τ = 1/λ = T₁/₂/ln2 = 1.443 × T₁/₂.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: After 3 half-lives, the fraction of radioactive atoms remaining is
questions.explanation: After n half-lives: N/N₀ = (1/2)ⁿ = (1/2)³ = 1/8.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In α-decay, the atomic number decreases by
questions.explanation: α decay: Z → Z−2, A → A−4 (emits ²He⁴).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In β⁻ decay, the atomic number
questions.explanation: β⁻ decay: n → p + e⁻ + ν̄. Z increases by 1, A unchanged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In γ decay, the atomic number and mass number
questions.explanation: γ decay: nucleus goes from excited to ground state by emitting photon. Z and A unchanged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Nuclear fission is the
questions.optionB: splitting of a heavy nucleus into lighter nuclei
questions.explanation: Fission: heavy nucleus (e.g., U-235) splits into two medium-mass fragments + neutrons + energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Nuclear fusion is the
questions.optionB: combination of light nuclei to form heavier nuclei
questions.explanation: Fusion: light nuclei combine (e.g., H → He in stars), releasing enormous energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The mass-energy equivalence relation is
questions.explanation: Einstein's mass-energy equivalence: E = mc² (c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: 1 atomic mass unit (u) is equivalent to energy of

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The binding energy of a nucleus is the energy required to
questions.optionB: separate all nucleons from the nucleus
questions.explanation: Binding energy: energy needed to completely disassemble a nucleus into its constituent protons and neutrons.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The binding energy per nucleon is maximum for
questions.explanation: BE/A is maximum (~8.8 MeV) around Fe-56. Most stable nucleus.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A p-type semiconductor is formed by doping silicon with
questions.explanation: Boron (group III) in silicon → 3 valence electrons → creates holes → p-type semiconductor.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An n-type semiconductor is formed by doping silicon with
questions.explanation: Phosphorus (group V) → 5 valence electrons → extra electron → n-type semiconductor.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In a p-n junction diode, the depletion region is formed at the
questions.optionC: junction of p and n regions
questions.explanation: Depletion region: thin layer at the junction where mobile charge carriers have diffused away, leaving fixed ions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A p-n junction diode allows current mainly in
questions.explanation: Forward bias: p connected to +, n to − → depletion region narrows → current flows easily.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The barrier potential of a silicon p-n junction is approximately
questions.explanation: Silicon: ~0.7 V. Germanium: ~0.3 V barrier potential.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A Zener diode is used for
questions.explanation: Zener diode: operates in reverse breakdown → maintains constant voltage → voltage regulator.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In a transistor, the base region is
questions.explanation: Base is lightly doped and very thin so most charge carriers pass through to the collector.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The current gain β (or h_FE) of a transistor in common emitter configuration is
questions.explanation: β = I_C/I_B (collector current / base current). Typically 50-300.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The relation between α (current gain in CB) and β (current gain in CE) is
questions.explanation: β = α/(1−α) and α = β/(1+β). Both are equivalent.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A transistor can be used as
questions.explanation: Transistor applications: amplifier (active region), switch (cutoff/saturation), oscillator (with feedback).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The truth table with output 1 only when both inputs are 1 represents
questions.explanation: AND gate: output = A·B. Output is 1 only when both A and B are 1.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The truth table with output 0 only when both inputs are 1 represents
questions.explanation: NAND: output = NOT(A·B). Output is 0 only when both inputs are 1.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: NAND and NOR gates are called universal gates because
questions.optionB: any logic gate can be built using only NAND or only NOR gates
questions.explanation: NAND and NOR are universal: any Boolean function can be implemented using only NAND or only NOR gates.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Rutherford's alpha scattering experiment proved that
questions.optionB: atom has a small, dense, positively charged nucleus
questions.explanation: Most α particles passed through, few scattered at large angles → atom has a tiny dense positive nucleus.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The nuclear radius R is related to mass number A by R =
questions.explanation: R = R₀A^(1/3), where R₀ ≈ 1.2 fm. Nuclear volume ∝ A → constant nuclear density.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Nuclear forces are
questions.optionB: short range, very strong, and charge-independent
questions.explanation: Nuclear (strong) force: short range (~1-2 fm), very strong, charge-independent, saturated.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Isotopes have the same
questions.explanation: Isotopes: same Z (protons), different A (mass number). Same element, different neutron count.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Isobars have the same
questions.explanation: Isobars: same mass number A, different Z. E.g., ¹⁴C and ¹⁴N.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Isotones have the same number of
questions.explanation: Isotones: same number of neutrons, different Z and A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The energy source of the sun is
questions.explanation: Sun: proton-proton chain fusion → 4H → He + energy (26.7 MeV per cycle).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In a nuclear reactor, the moderator is used to
questions.explanation: Moderator (heavy water, graphite) slows fast neutrons to thermal energies for sustained fission.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Control rods in a nuclear reactor are made of
questions.explanation: Control rods (Cd, B) absorb excess neutrons to control the chain reaction rate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The mass defect of a nucleus is the difference between
questions.optionA: mass of nucleus and atomic mass
questions.optionB: sum of masses of constituent nucleons and actual nuclear mass
questions.explanation: Δm = (Zm_p + Nm_n) − M_nucleus. This mass is converted to binding energy (E = Δmc²).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The photoelectric work function is the
questions.optionA: maximum kinetic energy of emitted electrons
questions.optionB: minimum energy needed to eject an electron from the metal surface
questions.explanation: Work function φ: minimum energy required to remove an electron from the metal surface.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The photocurrent in the photoelectric effect is proportional to
questions.explanation: More intensity → more photons → more photoelectrons → proportionally larger photocurrent.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Davisson-Germer experiment confirmed
questions.explanation: Davisson-Germer: electron diffraction from nickel crystal → confirmed de Broglie's wave hypothesis.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle states
questions.explanation: Uncertainty principle: ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2. Cannot simultaneously know exact position and momentum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The electron microscope uses the
questions.optionB: wave nature of electrons (short de Broglie wavelength)
questions.explanation: Electron microscope: electrons have much shorter wavelength than visible light → much higher resolution.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In Bohr model, the speed of electron in the nth orbit varies as
questions.explanation: v_n = e²/(4πε₀nℏ) ∝ 1/n. Speed decreases with increasing orbit number.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The ionization energy of hydrogen from ground state is
questions.explanation: Ionization energy = |E₁| = 13.6 eV (energy to remove electron from n=1 to n=∞).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: LED works on the principle of
questions.explanation: LED: forward biased p-n junction → electrons recombine with holes → emit photons (electroluminescence).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Solar cell is based on
questions.explanation: Solar cell: light creates electron-hole pairs in depletion region of p-n junction → photovoltaic EMF.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A rectifier converts
questions.explanation: Rectifier: converts AC to pulsating DC using diodes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A full-wave rectifier uses minimum
questions.explanation: Full-wave rectifier: minimum 2 diodes (center-tap) or 4 diodes (bridge rectifier).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The energy gap of a semiconductor is typically
questions.explanation: Semiconductors: E_g ≈ 0.7-1.1 eV (Ge: 0.67, Si: 1.12 eV). Insulators: >5 eV.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: At absolute zero temperature, a pure semiconductor behaves as
questions.explanation: At 0 K: no thermal energy to excite electrons across band gap → behaves as insulator.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The majority carriers in an n-type semiconductor are
questions.explanation: n-type: electrons are majority carriers (from donor impurity atoms).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The majority carriers in a p-type semiconductor are
questions.explanation: p-type: holes are majority carriers (from acceptor impurity atoms).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The output of an OR gate is 0 only when
questions.explanation: OR gate: Y = A + B. Output is 0 only when both A = 0 and B = 0.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The NOT gate has
questions.explanation: NOT (inverter): single input, single output. Y = Ā (complement of input).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: De Morgan's first theorem states
questions.explanation: De Morgan: (A+B)' = A'·B' and (A·B)' = A'+B'. Both are De Morgan's theorems.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Paschen series in hydrogen spectrum lies in the
questions.explanation: Paschen: transitions to n=3 → infrared region.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The wave function ψ in quantum mechanics is related to probability by
questions.explanation: |ψ|²dV gives the probability of finding the particle in volume element dV (Born interpretation).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The energy of a photon of wavelength 6600 Å is (h = 6.6 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The critical mass in a nuclear reactor is the minimum mass of fissile material required for
questions.explanation: Critical mass: minimum mass at which chain reaction becomes self-sustaining (k = 1).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Carbon dating uses the isotope
questions.explanation: C-14 (radioactive, T₁/₂ ≈ 5730 years) used for dating organic remains up to ~50,000 years.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: LASER stands for
questions.optionA: Light Amplification by Simulated Emission of Radiation
questions.optionB: Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation
questions.optionC: Light Absorption by Stimulated Emission of Radiation
questions.optionD: Low Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation
questions.explanation: LASER: Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Stimulated emission produces photons that are
questions.explanation: Stimulated emission: incoming photon triggers emission of identical photon → coherent, same f, φ, direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Population inversion is necessary for laser action. It means
questions.optionA: all atoms are in ground state
questions.optionB: more atoms in excited state than ground state
questions.explanation: Population inversion: N_upper > N_lower. Required for stimulated emission to dominate over absorption.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The photodiode is operated in
questions.explanation: Photodiode: reverse biased. Light creates electron-hole pairs → photocurrent proportional to light intensity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The de Broglie wavelength of a particle at temperature T is λ =
questions.explanation: Thermal de Broglie: KE = 3kT/2. λ = h/p = h/√(2m × 3kT/2) = h/√(3mkT).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The transistor was invented by
questions.explanation: Transistor invented in 1947 by Bardeen, Brattain, and Shockley at Bell Labs.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An XOR gate gives output 1 when
questions.explanation: XOR: Y = A⊕B. Output is 1 when inputs are different (A=0,B=1 or A=1,B=0).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The wavelength of matter waves depends on
questions.explanation: λ = h/(mv). Depends on mass and velocity (momentum) of the particle.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Pair production occurs when a photon of sufficient energy creates
questions.explanation: Pair production: γ → e⁻ + e⁺ (near a nucleus). Minimum photon energy = 2m_e c² = 1.022 MeV.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Pair annihilation produces
questions.explanation: e⁻ + e⁺ → 2γ. Two photons each of 0.511 MeV emitted in opposite directions (momentum conservation).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The rest mass energy of an electron is approximately

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Compton wavelength of electron h/(m_e c) is approximately

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The fine structure constant α is approximately
questions.explanation: α = e²/(4πε₀ℏc) ≈ 1/137. Dimensionless constant characterizing EM interaction strength.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In β⁻ decay, along with the electron, the nucleus also emits
questions.explanation: β⁻: n → p + e⁻ + ν̄_e (antineutrino). Neutrino was postulated by Pauli to conserve energy and momentum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In β⁺ decay, the nucleus emits
questions.explanation: β⁺: p → n + e⁺ + ν_e (positron and neutrino).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Electron capture is the process where
questions.optionA: nucleus captures an orbital electron, emitting a neutrino
questions.optionD: proton captures an electron to form a neutron in free space
questions.explanation: Electron capture: inner orbital electron captured by nucleus. p + e⁻ → n + ν_e.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The activity of a radioactive sample is defined as
questions.explanation: Activity A = |dN/dt| = λN. Measured in Becquerel (1 Bq = 1 disintegration/s) or Curie.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: 1 Curie (Ci) equals
questions.explanation: 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ dps (activity of 1 g of Ra-226).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The SI unit of radioactivity is
questions.explanation: 1 Becquerel (Bq) = 1 disintegration per second (SI unit of activity).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The absorbed dose of radiation is measured in
questions.explanation: Gray: 1 Gy = 1 J/kg of absorbed radiation energy. Older unit: rad (1 Gy = 100 rad).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The dose equivalent (biological effect) of radiation is measured in
questions.explanation: Sievert = Gray × quality factor. Accounts for different biological effects of different radiations.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Nuclear fusion requires extremely high temperatures because
questions.optionB: electrostatic repulsion between nuclei must be overcome
questions.explanation: Nuclei are positively charged. High KE (high T ~10⁷ K) needed to overcome Coulomb barrier for fusion.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The chain reaction in nuclear fission is sustained when the multiplication factor k =
questions.explanation: k = 1: critical (sustained chain reaction). k < 1: subcritical (dies out). k > 1: supercritical (explosive).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The four fundamental forces in nature are
questions.optionA: gravity, electromagnetic, strong nuclear, weak nuclear
questions.explanation: Four fundamental forces: gravitational, electromagnetic, strong nuclear, and weak nuclear.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The strongest fundamental force is
questions.explanation: Relative strengths: strong (1) > EM (10⁻²) > weak (10⁻⁶) > gravitational (10⁻³⁹).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The particle responsible for mediating the strong nuclear force is
questions.explanation: Gluons mediate the strong force between quarks. Pions mediate nuclear force between nucleons.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The antiparticle of an electron is
questions.explanation: Positron (e⁺): same mass as electron, opposite charge (+e). Predicted by Dirac, discovered by Anderson.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Quarks come in how many flavors?
questions.explanation: Six quark flavors: up, down, charm, strange, top, bottom.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A proton consists of
questions.optionB: two up quarks and one down quark (uud)
questions.optionC: one up quark and two down quarks (udd)
questions.explanation: Proton = uud. Charge: (2/3 + 2/3 − 1/3)e = +e.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A neutron consists of
questions.optionA: two up quarks and one down quark
questions.optionB: one up quark and two down quarks (udd)
questions.explanation: Neutron = udd. Charge: (2/3 − 1/3 − 1/3)e = 0.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Higgs boson is responsible for
questions.explanation: Higgs boson (discovered 2012 at LHC) → Higgs field gives mass to fundamental particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Nanotechnology deals with structures of size
questions.explanation: Nanotechnology: manipulation of matter at nanoscale (1-100 nm = 10⁻⁹ m).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Carbon nanotubes are
questions.explanation: Carbon nanotubes: cylindrical nanostructures of sp² carbon atoms with extraordinary strength and electrical properties.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Graphene is a single layer of
questions.optionB: carbon atoms arranged in hexagonal lattice
questions.explanation: Graphene: single layer of carbon atoms in a 2D hexagonal lattice. Excellent conductor, very strong.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The working principle of a nuclear bomb is based on
questions.explanation: Nuclear (atomic) bomb: uncontrolled chain fission reaction (supercritical mass, k >> 1).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A hydrogen bomb works on the principle of
questions.explanation: H-bomb: fission trigger creates conditions for fusion of hydrogen isotopes → much more energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The specific charge (e/m) of cathode rays is approximately
questions.explanation: e/m = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹/9.1×10⁻³¹ = 1.76 × 10¹¹ C/kg for electrons.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Thermionic emission is the emission of electrons from
questions.explanation: Thermionic emission: electrons escape from metal surface when heated sufficiently (overcome work function).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The work function of a metal for thermionic emission is typically
questions.explanation: Work function for metals: typically 1-5 eV. Lower φ → easier thermionic emission.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The momentum of a photon is p =
questions.explanation: p = E/c = hf/c = h/λ. Photon has momentum despite zero rest mass.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In the Bohr model, the total energy of the electron is
questions.explanation: E_n = −13.6/n² eV < 0. Negative energy indicates a bound state.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The kinetic energy of the electron in the nth Bohr orbit is
questions.explanation: KE = −E_n = 13.6/n² eV. KE = |E_n|. Also KE = −PE/2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The potential energy of the electron in the nth Bohr orbit is
questions.explanation: PE = 2E_n = −27.2/n² eV. PE = −2KE. Total E = KE + PE = −13.6/n² eV.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The wavelength of the first line of the Lyman series is (R = 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The series limit of the Balmer series corresponds to transition from n = ∞ to n =
questions.explanation: Series limit: n → ∞ to n = 2 for Balmer. λ = 4/R ≈ 3646 Å.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: For hydrogen-like atoms (He⁺, Li²⁺), the energy levels are E_n =
questions.explanation: E_n = −13.6Z²/n² eV for hydrogen-like ions. Z = atomic number.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Bohr model correctly predicts the spectrum of
questions.explanation: Bohr model works only for one-electron systems: H, He⁺, Li²⁺, etc.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through 100 V is approximately

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Tunnel effect (quantum tunneling) allows particles to
questions.optionB: pass through potential barriers greater than their kinetic energy
questions.explanation: Quantum tunneling: particle has non-zero probability of penetrating a classically forbidden potential barrier.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Alpha decay involves quantum tunneling because
questions.optionB: α particles tunnel through the nuclear Coulomb barrier
questions.explanation: α particle inside nucleus has less energy than the Coulomb barrier but can tunnel through it.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A photoelectric cell converts
questions.explanation: Photoelectric cell: light strikes photocathode → photoelectrons emitted → photocurrent.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In the Bohr model, the angular momentum of an electron in the 3rd orbit is
questions.explanation: L = nℏ = nh/(2π) = 3h/(2π) for n = 3.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The concept of nuclear energy levels explains
questions.optionB: emission of gamma rays after α or β decay
questions.explanation: After α or β decay, daughter nucleus may be in excited state → drops to ground state by emitting γ rays.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Radioactive series end with a stable isotope of
questions.explanation: All three natural radioactive series end with stable isotopes of lead (Pb-206, Pb-207, Pb-208).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A half-wave rectifier has output frequency
questions.explanation: Half-wave: one pulse per cycle → output frequency = input frequency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A full-wave rectifier has output frequency
questions.explanation: Full-wave: two pulses per cycle → output frequency = 2 × input frequency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The ripple factor of a half-wave rectifier is approximately
questions.explanation: Half-wave rectifier ripple factor ≈ 1.21. Full-wave ≈ 0.48.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The input impedance of an ideal operational amplifier is
questions.explanation: Ideal op-amp: infinite input impedance, zero output impedance, infinite gain.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Superconductivity was discovered by
questions.explanation: Kamerlingh Onnes discovered superconductivity in mercury at 4.2 K in 1911.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The BCS theory explains superconductivity through
questions.explanation: BCS theory: electrons form Cooper pairs via lattice vibrations (phonons). Pairs move without resistance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: High-temperature superconductors are typically
questions.explanation: HTS materials are ceramic oxides (e.g., YBa₂Cu₃O₇) with T_c above liquid nitrogen temperature (77 K).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The photoelectric effect is instantaneous because
questions.optionB: energy transfer is quantized (one photon ejects one electron)
questions.explanation: Photon energy is absorbed in a single quantum event → instantaneous emission (no time lag).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The graph of stopping potential V₀ vs frequency f in the photoelectric effect is a
questions.explanation: eV₀ = hf − φ → V₀ = (h/e)f − φ/e. Linear with slope h/e and intercept −φ/e.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons does not depend on
questions.explanation: KE_max = hf − φ. Depends on frequency and work function, NOT on intensity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The particle exchange model explains forces through virtual particles. The electromagnetic force is mediated by
questions.explanation: Virtual photons mediate the electromagnetic force between charged particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The weak nuclear force is mediated by
questions.explanation: W and Z bosons mediate weak interactions (responsible for β decay).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: According to the Standard Model, matter is composed of
questions.explanation: Standard Model: fundamental matter particles are 6 quarks and 6 leptons (plus their antiparticles).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Leptons include
questions.optionC: electrons, muons, taus, and their neutrinos
questions.explanation: 6 leptons: electron (e⁻), muon (μ⁻), tau (τ⁻), and three corresponding neutrinos (ν_e, ν_μ, ν_τ).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Hadrons are particles that experience the
questions.explanation: Hadrons (baryons + mesons) are composed of quarks and experience the strong force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Baryons are composed of
questions.explanation: Baryons: three quarks (e.g., proton = uud, neutron = udd).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Mesons are composed of
questions.explanation: Mesons: quark-antiquark pair (e.g., pion π⁺ = ud̄).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The transistor in saturation mode acts as a
questions.explanation: Saturation: both junctions forward biased → V_CE ≈ 0 → acts as closed switch (ON state).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The transistor in cutoff mode acts as
questions.explanation: Cutoff: both junctions reverse biased → no collector current → open switch (OFF state).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The voltage gain of a common emitter amplifier is
questions.explanation: Voltage gain A_v = −β(R_C/R_B) = β × R_L/R_in (magnitude).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An oscillator circuit uses
questions.explanation: Oscillator: positive feedback with loop gain ≥ 1 (Barkhausen criterion) → self-sustained oscillations.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The frequency of Hα line of hydrogen (n=3 to n=2) corresponds to wavelength

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Planck's quantum hypothesis states that energy is emitted or absorbed in discrete quanta of
questions.explanation: Planck (1900): electromagnetic radiation is emitted/absorbed in quanta of energy E = hf = nhf (n = integer).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The photoelectric effect cannot be explained by wave theory because wave theory predicts
questions.optionB: time delay for low intensity and no threshold frequency
questions.explanation: Wave theory predicts: (1) time delay for electron to accumulate energy, (2) no threshold frequency. Both contradict experiment.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The dual nature of matter (wave-particle duality) was proposed by
questions.explanation: Louis de Broglie (1924) proposed that all matter has wave properties: λ = h/p.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The electron volt is a unit of
questions.explanation: 1 eV = energy gained by an electron through 1 V potential difference = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The distance of closest approach of an alpha particle to a gold nucleus is related to
questions.explanation: At closest approach: KE = PE = kZe(2e)/r_min → r_min = 2kZe²/KE. Depends on initial KE.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Geiger-Marsden experiment showed that most α particles
questions.optionB: passed straight through the gold foil
questions.explanation: Most α particles passed through undeflected → atom is mostly empty space. Few scattered at large angles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Impact parameter b in Rutherford scattering is the
questions.optionB: perpendicular distance from nucleus to initial path of α particle
questions.explanation: Impact parameter b: perpendicular distance between initial trajectory and parallel line through nucleus center.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In a p-n-p transistor, the current carriers from emitter to collector are mainly
questions.explanation: PNP transistor: holes are injected from p-type emitter through thin n-type base to p-type collector.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In an n-p-n transistor, the current carriers from emitter to collector are mainly
questions.explanation: NPN: electrons from n-type emitter through thin p-type base to n-type collector.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The forbidden energy gap in insulators is
questions.explanation: Insulators: E_g > 5 eV. Too large for thermal excitation at room temperature → very few charge carriers.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Conductors have
questions.optionB: overlapping conduction and valence bands (no gap)
questions.explanation: Conductors: valence and conduction bands overlap → electrons move freely → good conduction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Intrinsic semiconductor has equal concentrations of
questions.explanation: Pure (intrinsic) semiconductor: n_e = n_h. Equal electron and hole concentrations from thermal excitation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Fermi level in an intrinsic semiconductor lies
questions.optionC: approximately in the middle of the band gap
questions.explanation: For intrinsic semiconductor: E_F ≈ (E_C + E_V)/2, approximately at the center of the band gap.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: In an n-type semiconductor, the Fermi level shifts toward
questions.explanation: n-type: more electrons → Fermi level moves up toward conduction band.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A photoelectric cell with cesium surface has work function 2.14 eV. The threshold wavelength is approximately

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: X-ray diffraction from crystals confirms that X-rays are
questions.explanation: Laue/Bragg diffraction of X-rays from crystal planes → X-rays are electromagnetic waves with wavelength ~ atomic spacing.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Bragg's law for X-ray diffraction is
questions.explanation: Bragg's law: 2d sinθ = nλ, where d = crystal plane spacing, θ = glancing angle.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Continuous X-ray spectrum is produced by
questions.explanation: Bremsstrahlung (braking radiation): decelerating electrons produce continuous spectrum of X-ray wavelengths.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Characteristic X-rays are produced when
questions.optionB: inner shell vacancies are filled by outer shell electrons
questions.explanation: Characteristic X-rays: electron from outer shell fills inner shell vacancy → emits X-ray photon of specific energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The de Broglie wavelength of a proton is smaller than that of an electron at the same speed because
questions.optionA: proton has larger mass → larger momentum → shorter λ
questions.explanation: λ = h/p = h/(mv). Proton mass >> electron mass → larger p → smaller λ.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Which hydrogen spectral series lies in the visible region?
questions.explanation: Balmer series (transitions to n=2): visible light (400-700 nm). Lyman: UV. Paschen and beyond: IR.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The shortest wavelength of the Balmer series is
questions.explanation: Series limit (n=∞ → n=2): 1/λ = R(1/4) → λ = 4/R ≈ 3646 Å.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Nuclear density is approximately
questions.explanation: Nuclear density ≈ 2.3 × 10¹⁷ kg/m³. Nearly constant for all nuclei (nuclear matter is incompressible).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A radioactive element has half-life of 10 days. After 30 days, the fraction remaining is
questions.explanation: 30 days = 3 half-lives. Remaining = (1/2)³ = 1/8.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Artificial radioactivity was discovered by
questions.explanation: Irène and Frédéric Joliot-Curie discovered artificial radioactivity in 1934 by bombarding aluminum with α particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The neutron was discovered by
questions.explanation: James Chadwick discovered the neutron in 1932 by bombarding beryllium with α particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Schrödinger's equation is the fundamental equation of
questions.explanation: Schrödinger equation describes how the quantum state (wave function) of a system evolves in time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The Bohr model fails for multi-electron atoms because
questions.optionB: it ignores electron-electron interactions and quantum mechanical effects
questions.explanation: Bohr model: no electron-electron repulsion, no wave mechanics, no spin → fails for Z > 1.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Black body radiation spectrum was explained by
questions.explanation: Planck (1900) explained black body spectrum by quantizing energy: E = nhf.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Wien's displacement law states λ_max × T =
questions.explanation: Wien's law: λ_max T = b = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K. Higher T → shorter peak wavelength.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Stefan-Boltzmann law states that total power radiated by a black body is proportional to
questions.explanation: P = σAT⁴ (Stefan-Boltzmann law). σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The photomultiplier tube works on the principle of
questions.explanation: PMT: photon hits photocathode → electron → cascading secondary emission at dynodes → amplified signal.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The uncertainty in energy and time are related by
questions.explanation: Energy-time uncertainty: ΔEΔt ≥ ℏ/2. Complementary to position-momentum uncertainty.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The lightest element in the periodic table is:
questions.explanation: Hydrogen (H, Z=1) is the lightest element with atomic mass ≈ 1 g/mol. It is the most abundant element in the universe.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The preparation of hydrogen in the laboratory by reacting dilute sulphuric acid with:
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄(dil) → ZnSO₄ + H₂↑. Zinc reacts with dilute H₂SO₄ to produce hydrogen gas. Metals below hydrogen in activity series (Cu, Ag, Au) do not displace hydrogen from acids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Ortho hydrogen and para hydrogen are:
questions.explanation: Ortho-H₂ (spins parallel, ↑↑) and para-H₂ (spins antiparallel, ↑↓) are nuclear spin isomers (not chemical isomers) of molecular hydrogen.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Heavy water (D₂O) is used as a moderator in nuclear reactors to:
questions.explanation: Heavy water (D₂O) is an excellent moderator — it slows down fast neutrons to thermal speeds without absorbing them, enabling sustained nuclear fission chain reaction in CANDU reactors.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of hydrogen with nitrogen in the Haber process produces:
questions.explanation: Haber process: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃. Conditions: 450°C, 200 atm, Fe catalyst. Produces ammonia for fertilizers and other chemicals.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Nascent hydrogen (H) differs from molecular hydrogen (H₂) in that nascent hydrogen:
questions.optionB: Is more reactive as it is an atomic species
questions.explanation: Nascent (nascent = just born) hydrogen is atomic hydrogen (H) released in the reaction flask. It is highly reactive compared to H₂ because it hasn't combined into stable H₂ molecules yet.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following is NOT an oxide of nitrogen?
questions.explanation: N₂O₆ is not a known stable oxide of nitrogen. Known nitrogen oxides include: N₂O (nitrous oxide), NO (nitric oxide), N₂O₃ (dinitrogen trioxide), NO₂/N₂O₄, N₂O₅.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Ostwald process is used for the industrial manufacture of:
questions.explanation: Ostwald process: 4NH₃ + 5O₂ → 4NO + 6H₂O (Pt catalyst, 850°C). Then: 4NO + 3O₂ + 2H₂O → 4HNO₃. Manufactures nitric acid industrially.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Aqua regia is a mixture of:
questions.explanation: Aqua regia (royal water): concentrated HNO₃:HCl in 1:3 ratio. It can dissolve noble metals (gold, platinum) that resist individual acids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The brown ring test is used to detect:
questions.explanation: Brown ring test for NO₃⁻: FeSO₄ + H₂SO₄ + NO₃⁻ → brown ring of [Fe(H₂O)₅(NO)]²⁺ at the junction. The ring is brown-colored ferrous nitrosyl complex.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Oxygen is obtained industrially by the fractional distillation of:
questions.explanation: Industrial O₂: fractional distillation of liquid air (air is cooled, liquefied, then separated). O₂ boils at -183°C, N₂ at -196°C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Ozone is formed in the stratosphere by:
questions.optionA: UV radiation splitting O₂ into atomic oxygen (O), which then reacts with O₂
questions.explanation: Stratospheric ozone: UV photons (λ < 240 nm) split O₂ → 2O. Then O + O₂ + M → O₃. The ozone layer (15-35 km altitude) absorbs harmful UV-B and UV-C radiation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The hybridization of oxygen in ozone (O₃) is:
questions.explanation: O₃: central O has 1 lone pair + 2 bond regions = sp² hybridization → bent geometry with O-O-O angle ≈ 117°. Ozone has one σ and one delocalized π bond.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Hydrogen peroxide is used as:
questions.optionC: Both an oxidizing and reducing agent, and as a bleaching agent
questions.explanation: H₂O₂ acts as: (1) oxidizing agent (acidic/neutral media): H₂O₂ + 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → 2H₂O, (2) reducing agent (alkaline/with strong oxidants), (3) bleaching agent for hair, paper.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The structure of water (H₂O) is:
questions.explanation: H₂O: sp³ O, 2 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs. VSEPR: bent shape, H-O-H = 104.5°. The two lone pairs reduce the bond angle below tetrahedral (109.5°).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Hard water contains dissolved:
questions.optionB: Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions (sulphates, chlorides, bicarbonates)
questions.explanation: Hard water contains Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions. Temporary hardness: Ca(HCO₃)₂, Mg(HCO₃)₂ (removed by boiling). Permanent hardness: CaSO₄, MgCl₂ (removed by adding Na₂CO₃ or ion exchange).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which nitrogen compound is used as a fertilizer?
questions.explanation: Ammonia and its derivatives (urea CO(NH₂)₂, ammonium nitrate NH₄NO₃, ammonium sulphate (NH₄)₂SO₄) are major nitrogenous fertilizers.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Laughing gas (used in anesthesia) is:
questions.explanation: Nitrous oxide (N₂O) is known as 'laughing gas.' It's used as a mild anesthetic/analgesic in dentistry and minor surgeries. It causes euphoria at low concentrations.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Halogens are called 'salt-forming' elements because they:
questions.optionB: React with metals to form salts (metal halides)
questions.optionC: Are found in seawater as salts
questions.explanation: Halogen (Greek: hals = salt, genes = forming): halogens readily react with metals to form ionic halide salts. Example: 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl; Mg + Br₂ → MgBr₂.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The correct order of reactivity of halogens is:
questions.explanation: Reactivity decreases down Group 17: F₂ > Cl₂ > Br₂ > I₂ (or equivalently I₂ < Br₂ < Cl₂ < F₂). Fluorine is most reactive halogen (strongest oxidizing agent).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The chlorine gas is prepared in the laboratory by oxidation of HCl with:
questions.explanation: Lab preparation of Cl₂: MnO₂ + 4HCl(conc) → MnCl₂ + 2H₂O + Cl₂. MnO₂ oxidizes HCl to Cl₂ (Mn: +4 → +2).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which is the most electronegative element among the halogens?
questions.explanation: F is the most electronegative element of all (EN = 4.0). Its small size, high nuclear charge, and strong tendency to attract electrons make it the most electronegative halogen.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Iodine dissolves in KI solution to form:
questions.explanation: I₂ + KI → K[I₃] (KI₃). Iodine dissolves in KI solution by forming the triiodide complex ion [I₃]⁻, which gives the characteristic brown/deep amber color of iodine solution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The allotropes of carbon include:
questions.explanation: Carbon allotropes: diamond (sp³, hard, insulator), graphite (sp², soft, conductor), fullerene (C₆₀, sp², hollow cage), and more recently graphene (single graphite layer) and carbon nanotubes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Why is graphite a good electrical conductor while diamond is not?
questions.optionB: Graphite has delocalized π electrons; diamond has all sp³ localized bonds
questions.explanation: Graphite: each C is sp² hybridized. The unhybridized p-electron forms a delocalized π system across graphite layers → electrical conductor. Diamond: all sp³, no free electrons → insulator.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Carbon dioxide turns lime water milky because:
questions.optionA: CO₂ dissolves in water to form H₂CO₃
questions.explanation: CO₂ + Ca(OH)₂ → CaCO₃↓ + H₂O. CaCO₃ is a white insoluble precipitate, making lime water milky. Excess CO₂ dissolves CaCO₃: CaCO₃ + H₂O + CO₂ → Ca(HCO₃)₂ (soluble) → turns clear again.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The water gas reaction (producer gas) involves:
questions.optionA: C + H₂O(steam) → CO + H₂ at high temperature
questions.explanation: Water gas: C + H₂O(g) → CO + H₂ (at ~1000°C). The mixture of CO and H₂ is called water gas or synthesis gas. It's used as a fuel and in industrial synthesis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Phosphorus is most commonly available as:
questions.explanation: White phosphorus (P₄) is the most reactive form, spontaneously ignites in air (stored under water), and is produced commercially. Red phosphorus is more stable and used in match heads.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The common allotropes of sulphur are:
questions.explanation: Sulphur allotropes: rhombic (α, stable at room temp, S₈ crown rings) and monoclinic (β, stable above 96°C, also S₈). Both convert to each other at the transition temperature (96°C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Contact process is used to manufacture:
questions.explanation: Contact process: 2SO₂ + O₂ ⇌ 2SO₃ (V₂O₅ catalyst, 450°C). SO₃ + H₂SO₄ → H₂S₂O₇ (oleum) → diluted with H₂O → H₂SO₄. Main industrial route to H₂SO₄.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Sulphuric acid is known as the 'king of chemicals' because:
questions.optionB: It is the most widely produced industrial chemical globally
questions.explanation: H₂SO₄ is called 'king of chemicals' because it's the most widely produced industrial chemical (~250 million tons/year). Used in fertilizers, petroleum refining, metal processing, and numerous chemical syntheses.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Noble gases (Group 18) have very low reactivity because:
questions.optionB: They have completely filled outer shells (ns²np⁶ or 1s²)
questions.explanation: Noble gases have completely filled valence shells (octet rule fulfilled). They have no tendency to gain, lose, or share electrons. Thus very low chemical reactivity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
