questions.text: The first noble gas compound was synthesized by Neil Bartlett in 1962. It was:
questions.explanation: Neil Bartlett (1962) first synthesized Xe⁺[PtF₆]⁻ by reacting Xe with PtF₆. This disproved the belief that noble gases could not form compounds. Xenon forms XeF₂, XeF₄, XeO₃, etc.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which noble gas is used to fill light bulbs and electric discharge tubes for orange-red light?
questions.explanation: Neon is used in neon lights/discharge tubes to produce the characteristic red-orange glow. Argon is used to fill incandescent light bulbs (inert atmosphere prevents filament oxidation).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The greenhouse effect is mainly caused by:
questions.optionB: CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, and water vapor absorbing infrared radiation
questions.explanation: Greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, H₂O, N₂O, O₃) absorb outgoing infrared radiation from Earth's surface, trapping heat and warming the planet. CO₂ is the main anthropogenic greenhouse gas.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Acid rain is caused by:
questions.optionB: SO₂ and NOₓ reacting with water in atmosphere to form H₂SO₄ and HNO₃
questions.explanation: Acid rain: SO₂ + H₂O + ½O₂ → H₂SO₄; NO₂ + H₂O → HNO₃. These strong acids make rainwater pH < 5.6, causing damage to ecosystems, buildings, and forests.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Ozone depletion is caused by:
questions.optionB: Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) releasing Cl atoms that catalytically destroy O₃
questions.explanation: CFCs (Cl-F-C compounds) in stratosphere release Cl· under UV: CCl₃F → CCl₂F· + Cl·. Then: Cl· + O₃ → ClO· + O₂; ClO· + O → Cl· + O₂ (catalyst). Each Cl atom destroys ~100,000 O₃ molecules.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Hydrogen cyanide (HCN) is extremely toxic because:
questions.optionB: CN⁻ inhibits cytochrome oxidase, blocking cellular respiration
questions.explanation: CN⁻ (cyanide) binds to Fe³⁺ in cytochrome c oxidase, blocking electron transport chain and cellular respiration. Cells cannot use oxygen, leading to rapid cell death.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The rusting of iron is an example of:
questions.optionB: Electrochemical corrosion involving both oxidation and reduction
questions.explanation: Rusting: electrochemical process. Anode: Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ (oxidation). Cathode: O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻ (reduction). Fe²⁺ and OH⁻ form Fe(OH)₂ → oxidized to Fe₂O₃·xH₂O (rust).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: White phosphorus is stored under water because:
questions.optionD: It is highly soluble in water
questions.explanation: White phosphorus (P₄) is pyrophoric — it spontaneously ignites in air (oxidizes rapidly). Stored under water to prevent contact with O₂. It glows in the dark (chemiluminescence).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The molecular formula of boric acid is:
questions.explanation: Boric acid = H₃BO₃ (orthoboric acid) or B(OH)₃. It is a weak, monobasic acid. B(OH)₃ acts as Lewis acid (accepts OH⁻ from water): B(OH)₃ + H₂O → [B(OH)₄]⁻ + H⁺.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The test for hydrogen gas is that it:
questions.optionA: Turns damp starch iodide paper blue
questions.optionB: Ignites with a 'pop' sound with a glowing splint
questions.optionC: Produces a brick-red precipitate with NaOH
questions.explanation: Test for H₂: brings a burning (or glowing) splint to the gas. H₂ ignites with a characteristic 'pop' (squeaky pop) sound. The burning confirms the gas is hydrogen (2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Chlorine water contains:
questions.optionB: Cl₂, HCl, and HOCl (hypochlorous acid) in equilibrium
questions.explanation: Cl₂ + H₂O ⇌ HCl + HOCl. Chlorine water contains dissolved Cl₂, HCl, and HOCl in equilibrium. HOCl is the active bleaching and disinfecting agent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The brown gas produced when copper reacts with concentrated HNO₃ is:
questions.explanation: Cu + 4HNO₃(conc) → Cu(NO₃)₂ + 2H₂O + 2NO₂↑. The brown fumes of NO₂ are characteristic of the reaction of metals with concentrated HNO₃.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Sulphur dioxide acts as a reducing agent toward:
questions.optionB: Strong oxidizing agents like KMnO₄ and K₂Cr₂O₇
questions.explanation: SO₂ is a reducing agent with strong oxidants: SO₂ + 2KMnO₄ + 2H₂O → K₂SO₄ + 2MnSO₄ + 2H₂SO₄. S in SO₂ (+4) is oxidized to +6 in SO₄²⁻.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Fluorine cannot be prepared by oxidation with chemicals because:
questions.optionB: It is the strongest oxidizing agent and has no stronger oxidant to oxidize F⁻
questions.optionC: It is too soluble in water
questions.optionD: It has too high a melting point
questions.explanation: F₂ is the strongest oxidizing agent (highest reduction potential). No chemical oxidizing agent is strong enough to oxidize F⁻ to F₂. F₂ is produced only by electrolysis of KF in liquid HF (Moissan process, 1886).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Nitrogen gas can be fixed (converted to useful compounds) by:
questions.optionB: Biological fixation by bacteria, lightning, and industrial Haber process
questions.explanation: Nitrogen fixation: (1) Industrial: N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃ (Haber process). (2) Biological: N₂ → NH₄⁺ by Rhizobium bacteria (in root nodules of legumes). (3) Lightning: N₂ + O₂ → 2NO → HNO₃ in soil.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The oxidation state of S in H₂SO₄ is:
questions.explanation: In H₂SO₄: 2(+1) + S + 4(-2) = 0. 2 + S - 8 = 0. S = +6. Sulphuric acid has sulphur in +6 oxidation state.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The oxoacids of phosphorus in +3 oxidation state include:
questions.explanation: Phosphorous acid (H₃PO₃): P is +3. Hypophosphorous acid (H₃PO₂): P is +1. Phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄): P is +5.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Carbon tetrachloride (CCl₄) is used as a:
questions.optionB: Fire extinguisher and solvent for non-polar compounds
questions.explanation: CCl₄ was historically used as a fire extinguisher and is a non-polar solvent (dissolves fats, oils, waxes). Its use as a fire extinguisher is now banned due to toxicity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The most abundant metal in the earth's crust is:
questions.explanation: Aluminum (Al) is the most abundant metal in the earth's crust (~8.1% by mass). It is found mainly as bauxite (Al₂O₃·2H₂O). Silicon is the most abundant non-metal (second overall element).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The process of extracting metals from their ores is called:
questions.explanation: Metallurgy: science and technology of extracting metals from their ores, purifying them, and making alloys. Steps include: concentration of ore, reduction, and refining.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The gangue (matrix) in an ore is:
questions.optionB: The unwanted earthy material mixed with the ore
questions.optionC: The reducing agent used in extraction
questions.explanation: Gangue (or matrix): unwanted earthly/rocky material associated with the ore. Removed during concentration/dressing of ore before extraction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The process of heating an ore strongly in the presence of air to convert sulphide ores to oxides is called:
questions.explanation: Roasting: heating a sulphide ore in excess air/O₂. Example: 2ZnS + 3O₂ → 2ZnO + 2SO₂. Converts metal sulphides to oxides (easier to reduce).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Calcination is the process of heating an ore:
questions.optionB: In absence or limited supply of air
questions.explanation: Calcination: heating ore in limited/absence of air. Drives off volatile impurities (CO₂, H₂O). Example: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂; ZnCO₃ → ZnO + CO₂.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which ore is used to extract aluminium?
questions.explanation: Bauxite (Al₂O₃·2H₂O) is the principal ore of aluminium. Al is extracted by Bayer's process (purification) + Hall-Héroult process (electrolytic reduction of Al₂O₃ in molten cryolite).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The most common ore of iron is:
questions.explanation: Haematite (Fe₂O₃) is the most important/abundant iron ore globally. Magnetite (Fe₃O₄) is richer in iron but less common. Both are used in blast furnaces.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the blast furnace, the role of coke (C) is to act as:
questions.explanation: Coke in blast furnace: (1) Fuel: C + O₂ → CO₂ (provides heat). (2) Reducing agent: CO₂ + C → 2CO; then Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂ (reduces iron oxide to iron).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The flux used in the smelting of iron ore (haematite, Fe₂O₃) with a limestone flux is:
questions.optionA: CaCO₃ (limestone), which reacts with SiO₂ gangue
questions.explanation: Limestone flux (CaCO₃) decomposes: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂. CaO (basic) reacts with acidic SiO₂ gangue: CaO + SiO₂ → CaSiO₃ (slag), which floats on top of molten iron.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Pig iron from the blast furnace is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Low carbon content (<0.2%) and high malleability
questions.explanation: Pig iron (cast iron from blast furnace): contains 3-4% C (+ Si, Mn, S, P). Very hard and brittle. Not malleable. Further processed to remove C → wrought iron (<0.2% C) or steel (0.2-2.0% C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Alkali metals are stored in:
questions.optionB: Mineral oil (kerosene) or dry inert atmosphere
questions.explanation: Alkali metals (Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs) react vigorously with air and water. Stored under mineral oil (kerosene). Na, K react immediately with water; Li oxidizes slowly in air.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The characteristic flame colour of sodium is:
questions.explanation: Flame test: Na → golden yellow (589 nm). K → violet/lilac. Li → crimson red. Ca → brick red. Ba → apple green. Cu → blue-green.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃) is manufactured by the:
questions.explanation: Solvay process: NaCl + NH₃ + CO₂ + H₂O → NaHCO₃ + NH₄Cl. Heat NaHCO₃: 2NaHCO₃ → Na₂CO₃ + H₂O + CO₂. Economical and widely used for Na₂CO₃ production.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Potassium superoxide (KO₂) is used in:
questions.optionB: Self-contained breathing apparatus (generates O₂ from CO₂ and H₂O)
questions.explanation: KO₂ + CO₂ → K₂CO₃ + O₂ and 4KO₂ + 2H₂O → 4KOH + 3O₂. KO₂ absorbs CO₂ and H₂O while releasing O₂ — ideal for self-contained breathing apparatus used by firefighters.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Plaster of Paris is:
questions.explanation: Plaster of Paris = CaSO₄·½H₂O (or 2CaSO₄·H₂O). Made by heating gypsum: CaSO₄·2H₂O → CaSO₄·½H₂O + 1½H₂O (at 120°C). Sets hard by reabsorbing water to form gypsum.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The main ore of zinc is:
questions.explanation: Main zinc ore: sphalerite (zinc blende, ZnS). Also calamine (ZnCO₃) and smithsonite. ZnS is roasted to ZnO, then reduced with C (coke) in retort furnaces.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Mercury (Hg) is unique among metals because:
questions.optionB: It is liquid at room temperature (melting point -39°C)
questions.optionC: It is the most reactive metal
questions.explanation: Mercury is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature and standard pressure (mp = -38.8°C). It forms amalgams (alloys) with many metals and is used in thermometers, barometers.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Cinnabar (HgS) is the main ore of mercury. Mercury is extracted by:
questions.optionB: Roasting: HgS + O₂ → Hg + SO₂ (direct thermal decomposition)
questions.explanation: HgS + O₂ → Hg(l) + SO₂. Mercury oxide and sulphide can be reduced just by roasting (heating in air) because Hg is low in activity series. Hg vapour condenses on cooling.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Coinage metals (Group 11: Cu, Ag, Au) are found in nature as:
questions.explanation: Coinage metals occur both as free (native) metals (especially Ag, Au) and as sulphide ores (Cu₂S, chalcopyrite CuFeS₂, argentite Ag₂S). They are less reactive than other transition metals.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The extraction of copper from copper pyrites (CuFeS₂) involves:
questions.optionC: Roasting (to Cu₂S), further roasting (self-reduction), then electrolytic refining
questions.explanation: Copper extraction: (1) Roast: 2CuFeS₂ + 4O₂ → Cu₂S + 2FeO + 3SO₂. (2) Slag FeO. (3) Self-reduction: Cu₂S + O₂ → 2Cu + SO₂. Blister copper (98-99% pure) purified by electrolytic refining.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Silver tarnishes because it reacts with:
questions.optionA: Oxygen in air to form Ag₂O
questions.optionB: H₂S or sulphur compounds in air to form Ag₂S (black)
questions.explanation: Silver tarnishes due to H₂S in air: 4Ag + 2H₂S + O₂ → 2Ag₂S + 2H₂O. Ag₂S is black, forming the tarnish layer on silver objects.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Gold dissolves in aqua regia because:
questions.optionA: HNO₃ oxidizes gold while HCl provides Cl⁻ to form stable complex [AuCl₄]⁻
questions.optionD: The mixture is a very strong acid
questions.explanation: In aqua regia: HNO₃ oxidizes Au (Au → Au³⁺). HCl provides Cl⁻ which forms stable [AuCl₄]⁻ complex, driving the dissolution forward. Neither acid alone dissolves gold.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Slaked lime is:
questions.explanation: Slaked lime = Ca(OH)₂. Produced by adding water to quicklime: CaO + H₂O → Ca(OH)₂ (exothermic). Used in construction, water treatment, and to neutralize soil acidity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The alloy bronze is composed of:
questions.explanation: Bronze = Cu + Sn (typically 88% Cu, 12% Sn). Brass = Cu + Zn. Bronze is harder than pure copper and was the basis of the Bronze Age civilization (~3000 BCE).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The process of zone refining is used to obtain:
questions.explanation: Zone refining: a narrow molten zone passes along a bar. Impurities (with distribution coefficient <1) concentrate in the molten zone and are swept to one end. Used for ultra-pure Si, Ge for semiconductors.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Thermite welding (alumino-thermic process) uses:
questions.explanation: Thermite reaction: 2Al + Fe₂O₃ → Al₂O₃ + 2Fe + heat (3000°C). The intense heat melts iron for welding railway tracks. Aluminum reduces Fe₂O₃ because Al has greater affinity for O₂ than Fe.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Baking soda (sodium bicarbonate) is:
questions.explanation: Baking soda = NaHCO₃ (sodium bicarbonate/sodium hydrogen carbonate). When heated or mixed with acid: NaHCO₃ + H⁺ → Na⁺ + H₂O + CO₂. CO₂ causes leavening in baking.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The electrolytic process used to coat objects with a layer of metal is called:
questions.explanation: Electroplating: object to be plated = cathode. Plating metal = anode. Electrolyte = metal salt solution. Metal deposits on cathode. Used to coat with Cr, Ag, Au, Ni for protection/appearance.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Stainless steel contains primarily:
questions.explanation: Stainless steel: Fe + 10-30% Cr + some Ni. Chromium forms a passive oxide layer (Cr₂O₃) on the surface, preventing further corrosion. Used for cutlery, surgical instruments.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of sodium with water produces:
questions.explanation: 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂↑. Sodium reacts vigorously with water (exothermic), producing sodium hydroxide solution and hydrogen gas. Potassium reacts even more vigorously.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Cement is a mixture of:
questions.explanation: Portland cement: mainly calcium silicates (3CaO·SiO₂ and 2CaO·SiO₂) and calcium aluminates (3CaO·Al₂O₃). Made by heating limestone + clay in a rotary kiln at 1450°C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The ore galena (PbS) is used to extract:
questions.explanation: Galena (PbS) is the main ore of lead (Pb). Extraction: Roast 2PbS + 3O₂ → 2PbO + 2SO₂. Reduce: PbO + C → Pb + CO or PbO + CO → Pb + CO₂.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: An amalgam is:
questions.optionA: An alloy of iron with other metals
questions.optionB: An alloy of mercury with other metals
questions.optionC: A compound of silver and gold
questions.explanation: Amalgam: an alloy of mercury (Hg) with another metal. Examples: Zn amalgam (used in Daniell cell), Na amalgam (reducing agent), dental amalgam (Ag-Hg-Sn).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Duralumin is an alloy of:
questions.explanation: Duralumin: Al (93-95%) + Cu (~4%) + Mg (~0.5%) + Mn (~0.5%). Much stronger than pure aluminum while remaining lightweight. Used in aircraft construction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The mineral 'malachite' Cu₂(OH)₂CO₃ is an ore of:
questions.explanation: Malachite Cu₂(OH)₂CO₃ (or Cu(OH)₂·CuCO₃) is a green copper ore. Azurite (Cu₃(OH)₂(CO₃)₂) is blue. Both are carbonate ores of copper.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Aluminium is extracted from bauxite by the Hall-Héroult process, which involves:
questions.optionC: Electrolysis of molten Al₂O₃ in cryolite (Na₃AlF₆)
questions.explanation: Hall-Héroult process: Al₂O₃ dissolved in molten cryolite (lowers melting point from 2050°C to ~970°C) and electrolyzed. Cathode: Al³⁺ + 3e⁻ → Al(l). Anode: 2O²⁻ → O₂ + 4e⁻.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Iron is purified from pig iron by:
questions.optionB: Oxidizing impurities (C, Si, S, P) by basic oxygen process (BOF)
questions.explanation: Steel making: Basic Oxygen Furnace (BOF) blows pure O₂ through molten pig iron. O₂ oxidizes C (→CO₂), Si (→SiO₂), S (→SO₂), P (→P₂O₅), reducing their content to produce steel.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The van Arkel method for purification of metals involves:
questions.optionB: Formation and thermal decomposition of volatile metal iodides
questions.explanation: Van Arkel method: metal reacts with I₂ to form volatile metal iodide: Ti + 2I₂ → TiI₄(g). TiI₄ then thermally decomposes on hot tungsten filament: TiI₄ → Ti(pure) + 2I₂. Gives very pure metals.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Bleaching powder (chlorinated lime) is:
questions.explanation: Bleaching powder = CaOCl₂ or Ca(OCl)Cl. Prepared: Ca(OH)₂ + Cl₂ → CaOCl₂ + H₂O. In water releases HOCl (active bleaching agent). Used for bleaching textiles and disinfection.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The hardest natural substance known is:
questions.explanation: Diamond is the hardest natural substance (10 on Mohs scale). Each carbon is bonded to 4 others in a tetrahedral (sp³) network. The strong 3D covalent network gives extreme hardness.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Hypo (sodium thiosulphate, Na₂S₂O₃) is used in photography to:
questions.optionB: Fix the photograph by dissolving unexposed silver halide
questions.explanation: Fixer (hypo): Na₂S₂O₃ + AgBr → Na₃[Ag(S₂O₃)₂] + NaBr. Dissolves unexposed AgBr from photographic film, 'fixing' the image permanently.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reagent used to detect Cu²⁺ ions in solution is:
questions.explanation: Cu²⁺ + 4NH₃ → [Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺ (deep blue/violet). Adding excess NH₃ to Cu²⁺ solution gives the characteristic deep blue tetraamminecopper(II) complex.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The type of bonding in metals is best described as:
questions.optionC: Metallic bonding — delocalized electron sea model
questions.explanation: Metallic bonding: metal atoms release valence electrons into a 'sea' of delocalized electrons. Metal cations are held in lattice by attraction to this electron sea. Explains conductivity, malleability, lustre.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The process of separating silver from its ore using NaCN solution is called:
questions.explanation: Cyanide/MacArthur process: 4Ag + 8NaCN + O₂ + 2H₂O → 4Na[Ag(CN)₂] + 4NaOH. Silver complex Na[Ag(CN)₂] is dissolved, then Ag recovered by displacement: 2Na[Ag(CN)₂] + Zn → Na₂[Zn(CN)₄] + 2Ag.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Calcium reacts with water to produce:
questions.explanation: Ca + 2H₂O → Ca(OH)₂ + H₂↑. Calcium reacts with cold water (less vigorously than Na/K) to form calcium hydroxide (milky precipitate if excess Ca(OH)₂) and hydrogen gas.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Epsom salt is:
questions.explanation: Epsom salt = MgSO₄·7H₂O (magnesium sulphate heptahydrate). Used as bath salt and laxative. Glauber's salt = Na₂SO₄·10H₂O. Gypsum = CaSO₄·2H₂O.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Fool's gold (iron pyrites) is:
questions.explanation: Fool's gold = iron pyrites = FeS₂ (iron(II) disulphide). Has golden metallic lustre resembling gold but is much lighter and harder. Also spelled 'pyrite' or 'fool's gold'.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction used to test for the presence of Al³⁺ ions in solution is:
questions.optionB: Adding NaOH (excess) — Al(OH)₃ dissolves to give [Al(OH)₄]⁻ (amphoteric)
questions.explanation: Al³⁺ + 3OH⁻ → Al(OH)₃↓ (white gelatinous precipitate). With excess NaOH: Al(OH)₃ + OH⁻ → [Al(OH)₄]⁻ (soluble). This amphoteric behavior distinguishes Al³⁺ from Fe³⁺ (Fe(OH)₃ doesn't dissolve in excess NaOH).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: What is the product when Na₂O₂ reacts with water?
questions.explanation: 2Na₂O₂ + 2H₂O → 4NaOH + O₂. More precisely: Na₂O₂ + H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂O₂ (intermediate). H₂O₂ decomposes: 2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂. Sodium peroxide is a source of both NaOH and H₂O₂.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Frasch process is used to extract:
questions.optionB: Sulphur from underground deposits using superheated water
questions.explanation: Frasch process (1891): Superheated water (170°C, 10 atm) is pumped underground to melt native sulphur. Hot compressed air forces liquid S to surface as 99.5% pure sulphur.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The most reactive metal (highest in the electrochemical series) is:
questions.explanation: Lithium (Li) has the most negative standard electrode potential (E° = -3.05 V) in aqueous solution, making it the strongest reducing agent and most reactive metal in the activity series.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Brass is an alloy of:
questions.explanation: Brass = Cu (60-80%) + Zn (20-40%). Used for decorative items, coins, musical instruments. Bronze = Cu + Sn.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which metal is used as a catalyst in the Haber process for ammonia synthesis?
questions.optionC: Iron with K₂O and Al₂O₃ as promoters
questions.explanation: Haber process catalyst: finely divided iron (Fe) with K₂O as promoter (electronic promoter) and Al₂O₃ as structural promoter. Platinum is used in the Ostwald process for HNO₃.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of iron with hot concentrated H₂SO₄ produces:
questions.explanation: Fe + 4H₂SO₄(conc, hot) → Fe₂(SO₄)₃ + SO₂ + 4H₂O. Hot concentrated H₂SO₄ is oxidizing; Fe is oxidized to Fe³⁺ while H₂SO₄ is reduced to SO₂.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the electrolytic refining of copper, pure copper deposits at:
questions.explanation: In electrolytic refining: Anode (impure Cu): Cu → Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻. Cathode (pure Cu blank): Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (deposits as pure copper). Less active impurities fall as 'anode mud'.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Froth flotation is used to concentrate:
questions.explanation: Froth flotation: used mainly for sulphide ores. The ore is mixed with water and pine oil frother. Air is blown through; sulphide minerals (hydrophobic) attach to froth bubbles, oxide gangue settles.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following metals does NOT react with cold dilute HCl?
questions.explanation: Copper (Cu) is below hydrogen in the activity series (E°Cu²⁺/Cu = +0.34V). It does not displace H₂ from dilute HCl. Metals above H (Zn, Fe, Mg) displace H₂.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The process of forming a protective layer of ZnO on zinc to resist further corrosion is called:
questions.explanation: Passivation: formation of thin, adherent oxide layer (like ZnO on Zn, Al₂O₃ on Al, Cr₂O₃ on stainless steel) that protects the metal from further corrosion.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: German silver (nickel silver) is an alloy of:
questions.explanation: German silver (nickel silver): Cu + Zn + Ni (approximately 60:20:20). Contains NO silver. Used for cutlery and decorative items. The name refers to its silvery appearance.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The gold standard for purity is measured in:
questions.explanation: Gold purity: measured in carats. 24 carats = pure gold (99.9%+). 22 carat = 22/24 × 100 = 91.7% Au. 18 carat = 75% Au. The remainder is typically Cu or Ag.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The element that forms the most chloride compounds with different oxidation states is:
questions.explanation: Iron forms FeCl₂ (iron(II) chloride, Fe²⁺) and FeCl₃ (iron(III) chloride, Fe³⁺), demonstrating variable oxidation states characteristic of transition metals.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The thermite reaction is used for:
questions.optionB: Welding of railway tracks and electrical cables
questions.explanation: Thermite: 2Al + Fe₂O₃ → Al₂O₃ + 2Fe + 3000°C heat. The extremely high temperature melts iron for in-situ welding of railway tracks (field repairs) and pipelines.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The corrosion of aluminium is less severe than iron because:
questions.optionA: Al is less reactive than Fe
questions.optionB: Al forms a thin adherent Al₂O₃ layer that prevents further corrosion
questions.explanation: Al₂O₃ forms a thin (3-4 nm), transparent, adherent passivating layer on aluminium surface. This self-healing layer prevents further oxidation, even though Al is more reactive than Fe.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) is converted to Plaster of Paris by:
questions.explanation: CaSO₄·2H₂O → CaSO₄·½H₂O + 1½H₂O at ~120°C. This partial dehydration gives Plaster of Paris. At higher temperatures, anhydrite (CaSO₄) forms, which sets much more slowly.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The metal that protects iron from corrosion even when the zinc coating is scratched (sacrificial protection) is:
questions.explanation: Zinc is higher in activity series than iron. When Zn coating breaks, Zn acts as sacrificial anode (Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻), protecting Fe cathode. Tin forms a passive layer but doesn't protect sacrificially.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Sodium is extracted industrially by electrolysis of:
questions.explanation: Down's process: Electrolysis of molten NaCl (mp 801°C, lowered to 600°C by adding CaCl₂). Cathode: Na⁺ + e⁻ → Na. Anode: 2Cl⁻ → Cl₂. Both useful products obtained.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound that causes the blue color of copper sulphate (CuSO₄·5H₂O) crystals is:
questions.optionB: The hydrated Cu²⁺ ion [Cu(H₂O)₄]²⁺ with d-d transitions
questions.explanation: Blue color of CuSO₄·5H₂O: due to d-d electronic transition in [Cu(H₂O)₄]²⁺ complex. Anhydrous CuSO₄ is white because Cu²⁺ in a different environment has different absorption.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which reducing agent is used to reduce iron oxide to iron in the blast furnace?
questions.explanation: In blast furnace, CO is the primary reducing agent: Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂. CO is produced by: C + O₂ → CO₂, then CO₂ + C → 2CO (Boudouard reaction at ~1000°C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The formula of alum is:
questions.explanation: Potash alum = KAl(SO₄)₂·12H₂O. Alums are double salts of general formula M⁺M³⁺(SO₄)₂·12H₂O. Used as mordant in dyeing, water purification, baking powder.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Mond process is used to purify:
questions.explanation: Mond process for Ni: Ni + 4CO(g) → Ni(CO)₄(g) at 50-60°C; Ni(CO)₄ → Ni(pure) + 4CO at 230°C. The volatile nickel carbonyl can be separated, then decomposed to yield pure Ni.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The main function of the flux in metallurgy is to:
questions.optionA: Reduce the metal oxide to metal
questions.optionB: React with gangue to form fusible slag
questions.explanation: Flux reacts with gangue (impurities) to form low-melting slag that floats on molten metal. Limestone (CaCO₃ → CaO) reacts with silica (SiO₂) gangue: CaO + SiO₂ → CaSiO₃ (slag).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: An ore of zinc that yields ZnO directly on roasting is:
questions.explanation: ZnCO₃ + heat → ZnO + CO₂ (calcination). ZnS must first be roasted: 2ZnS + 3O₂ → 2ZnO + 2SO₂. ZnO is then reduced to Zn: ZnO + C → Zn + CO (at ~1100°C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The alloy solder contains:
questions.explanation: Solder: Pb + Sn alloy (traditionally 60% Sn + 40% Pb, mp ~183°C). Used for joining electrical components. Lead-free solders (Sn-Ag-Cu) now used in electronics due to environmental concerns.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The formula of washing soda is:
questions.explanation: Washing soda = Na₂CO₃·10H₂O (sodium carbonate decahydrate). Used in washing as a water softener (precipitates Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ as carbonates). Baking soda = NaHCO₃.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The property of metals to be beaten into thin sheets is called:
questions.explanation: Malleability: ability to be hammered/rolled into thin sheets without breaking. Ductility: ability to be drawn into wires. Gold is most malleable; platinum is most ductile.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The process of magnetic separation is used to separate:
questions.optionB: Magnetic ore (like magnetite Fe₃O₄) from non-magnetic gangue
questions.explanation: Magnetic separation: used when ore (e.g., magnetite Fe₃O₄, chromite FeCr₂O₄) is magnetic and gangue is not (or vice versa). A magnetic field separates the two. Also used to separate iron fillings from sand.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: What is the role of cryolite (Na₃AlF₆) in the Hall-Héroult process?
questions.optionB: It dissolves Al₂O₃ and lowers its melting point from 2050°C to ~970°C
questions.optionD: It reacts with Al₂O₃ to form AlF₃
questions.explanation: Cryolite (Na₃AlF₆) serves as a solvent for Al₂O₃ in the Hall-Héroult process, reducing the operating temperature from 2050°C (mp of pure Al₂O₃) to ~970°C, making the process economically viable.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Chromium is added to steel to make it stainless because:
questions.optionB: Cr forms a protective Cr₂O₃ passivation layer
questions.explanation: Chromium (>10.5%) forms a thin, invisible Cr₂O₃ layer on steel surface (passivation). This layer prevents O₂ and H₂O from reaching the iron, preventing rust (stainless steel).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The process of liquation is used to purify:
questions.optionA: Metals with higher melting points than impurities
questions.optionB: Metals with lower melting points than impurities (e.g., Bi, Sn, Pb)
questions.explanation: Liquation: used for low-melting metals (Sn, Bi, Pb). The impure metal is heated on a sloped hearth; the pure metal melts and flows away, while higher-melting impurities remain solid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Magnesium is used in flashbulbs and incendiary bombs because:
questions.optionB: It burns with an intensely bright white light in O₂
questions.explanation: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO + 1200°C. Magnesium burns with an intensely bright white light (used in photography, flares, incendiary bombs). The high temperature and brightness make it useful.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The bleaching action of Cl₂ is due to:
questions.optionB: Formation of HOCl, which releases nascent oxygen [O]
questions.optionD: Cl₂ acting as a reducing agent
questions.explanation: Cl₂ + H₂O → HCl + HOCl. HOCl → HCl + [O]. Nascent oxygen (atomic O) oxidizes the coloring matter (chromophores), destroying its color. Bleaching by Cl₂ is permanent (oxidative bleaching).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Phosphine (PH₃) is a:
questions.explanation: PH₃ is a much weaker base than NH₃. The lone pair on P is in a larger, more diffuse orbital → poorer overlap with H⁺. PH₄⁺ (phosphonium ion) is unstable in water (unlike NH₄⁺ which is stable).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The strongest acid among halogen acids (HX) in aqueous solution is:
questions.explanation: Acid strength of hydrohalic acids: HF < HCl < HBr < HI. Bond strength decreases H-X: HF > HCl > HBr > HI. Weaker H-I bond means HI dissociates more completely. HF is weak acid (H-bonding).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The interhalogen compounds contain:
questions.optionA: Two molecules of the same halogen
questions.optionB: One halogen bonded to a different halogen
questions.explanation: Interhalogen compounds: formed between two different halogens. Examples: ClF, BrF₃, IF₅, ICl, BrF₅. General formula: XX'n where n = 1, 3, 5, or 7. More reactive than pure halogens.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The shape of XeF₄ according to VSEPR theory is:
questions.explanation: XeF₄: Xe has 4 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs = 6 electron pairs (sp³d² hybridization). VSEPR: 2 lone pairs occupy axial positions → square planar molecular geometry.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Carbon monoxide is poisonous because:
questions.optionB: CO binds to hemoglobin 200× more strongly than O₂, forming carboxyhemoglobin
questions.optionC: It forms carbonic acid in blood
questions.optionD: It prevents O₂ from entering lungs
questions.explanation: CO + Hb → COHb (carboxyhemoglobin, bright cherry red). CO has 200× higher affinity for hemoglobin than O₂, displacing O₂ and preventing oxygen transport. Causes cellular hypoxia.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Nitrogen forms N₂O₄ which is a dimer of:
questions.explanation: 2NO₂ ⇌ N₂O₄. Nitrogen dioxide (NO₂, brown, paramagnetic) dimerizes to dinitrogen tetroxide (N₂O₄, colorless, diamagnetic). The equilibrium depends on temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Silica (SiO₂) is used in:
questions.explanation: SiO₂ (quartz, silica): main component of glass (Na₂SiO₃, CaSiO₃ network). Also used in semiconductors (after purification to ultra-pure Si), optical fibers (SiO₂ glass), and as abrasive.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Ozone decomposes into:
questions.explanation: 2O₃ → 3O₂ (spontaneous decomposition). Or: O₃ + hν → O₂ + O (photolytic). The stratospheric ozone balance involves: O₃ + O → 2O₂ (catalyzed by Cl, NO, OH radicals).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of nitrogen with oxygen at very high temperatures (like in lightning) produces:
questions.explanation: N₂ + O₂ ⇌ 2NO (at ~3000°C, e.g., in lightning or internal combustion engines). This NO then oxidizes: 2NO + O₂ → 2NO₂ → HNO₃ in rain → contributes to acid rain and natural nitrogen fixation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The molecular formula of oleum (fuming sulphuric acid) is:
questions.explanation: Oleum (fuming H₂SO₄) = H₂S₂O₇ (pyrosulphuric acid), formed when SO₃ is absorbed in H₂SO₄: H₂SO₄ + SO₃ → H₂S₂O₇. Diluting with water gives H₂SO₄: H₂S₂O₇ + H₂O → 2H₂SO₄.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which oxide of nitrogen is found in the atmosphere as a greenhouse gas and is also implicated in ozone depletion?
questions.explanation: N₂O (nitrous oxide): greenhouse gas (~300× more potent than CO₂ per molecule over 100 years). In stratosphere: N₂O + O(¹D) → 2NO. NO then catalytically destroys ozone.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The oxidation state of iodine in KIO₃ is:
questions.explanation: In KIO₃: K = +1, O = -2. +1 + I + 3(-2) = 0. I = +5. Iodate ion IO₃⁻ has I in +5 oxidation state.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Hydrofluoric acid (HF) is stored in:
questions.explanation: HF etches/dissolves glass (SiO₂): SiO₂ + 4HF → SiF₄ + 2H₂O. Therefore, HF is stored in polyethylene or PTFE (Teflon) containers. HF is also used commercially to etch glass.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The smell of garlic is due to:
questions.explanation: Garlic smell is due to diallyl disulfide [(CH₂=CH-CH₂)₂S₂] and allicin [(CH₂=CH-CH₂)₂S(O)], organic sulfur compounds released when garlic is cut or crushed.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Dry ice is:
questions.optionA: Frozen water in very dry conditions
questions.explanation: Dry ice = solid CO₂. At atmospheric pressure, CO₂ sublimes at -78.5°C (no liquid phase). Used as refrigerant and in theatrical fog effects. 'Dry' because it leaves no liquid residue.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The main component of natural gas is:
questions.explanation: Natural gas is mainly CH₄ (70-90%). Also contains ethane, propane, butane in smaller amounts. LPG (liquefied petroleum gas) is mainly propane + butane.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: When sulphur burns in oxygen, the primary product is:
questions.explanation: S + O₂ → SO₂ (primary burning product). Further oxidation to SO₃ is slow without a catalyst: 2SO₂ + O₂ → 2SO₃ (requires V₂O₅ catalyst in Contact process).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Phosphorous acid (H₃PO₃) is a:
questions.optionB: Diprotic acid (only 2 OH groups are acidic)
questions.explanation: H₃PO₃ structure: one P-H bond (not acidic), two P-OH bonds (acidic). So H₃PO₃ is diprotic, not triprotic, despite the formula suggesting 3 H atoms. It forms only 2-series of salts.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Carborundum (used as an abrasive) is:
questions.explanation: Carborundum = SiC (silicon carbide). Prepared: SiO₂ + 3C → SiC + 2CO at ~2000°C. Used as abrasive (hardness 9.5 Mohs) for grinding wheels, sandpapers, and as a semiconductor.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The oxoacids of chlorine in increasing order of oxidative power are:
questions.explanation: Oxidative power of oxoacids of Cl: HClO > HClO₂ > HClO₃ > HClO₄. As the number of O atoms increases, Cl is more oxidized (higher oxidation state) and Cl-O bond is more stable, making it a weaker oxidizing agent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) are responsible for:
questions.explanation: CFCs (e.g., CCl₂F₂, Freon-12): used as refrigerants and aerosol propellants. UV radiation in stratosphere releases Cl·: Cl· + O₃ → ClO· + O₂; ClO· + O → Cl· + O₂. Catalytic cycle depletes ozone.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The triple point of water is:
questions.explanation: Triple point of water: temperature and pressure at which all three phases (solid, liquid, gas) coexist in equilibrium. T = 273.16 K (0.01°C), P = 611.7 Pa (6.12 × 10⁻³ atm).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Silicon dioxide is an acidic oxide because:
questions.optionA: It dissolves in water to form an acid
questions.optionB: It reacts with bases like NaOH to form silicates
questions.optionC: It has a positive oxidation state of Si
questions.explanation: SiO₂ is acidic oxide: SiO₂ + 2NaOH → Na₂SiO₃ + H₂O. (SiO₂ dissolves very slowly in water; its acidic nature is better shown by its reaction with basic oxides at high temperatures).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Hydrogen sulphide (H₂S) is:
questions.optionB: A weak diprotic acid and good reducing agent
questions.explanation: H₂S: weak diprotic acid (Ka₁ = 1×10⁻⁷, Ka₂ = 10⁻¹⁴) with rotten egg smell. Good reducing agent: 2H₂S + SO₂ → 3S + 2H₂O; H₂S + Cl₂ → 2HCl + S.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The position of hydrogen in the periodic table is controversial because:
questions.optionB: It can be placed in Group 1 (like alkali metals) or Group 17 (like halogens) due to its dual nature
questions.explanation: H can be placed in Group 1 (has 1 valence e⁻, can lose e⁻ to form H⁺) OR Group 17 (needs 1 e⁻ to complete its shell, can form H⁻). It has properties of both groups.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Nessler's reagent is used to detect:
questions.explanation: Nessler's reagent = K₂[HgI₄] in NaOH. Test: NH₄⁺ + Nessler's reagent → yellow-brown precipitate (or brown color) of [Hg₂N]I·H₂O. Used to detect NH₃/NH₄⁺ in water.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The element that exists as a diatomic molecule at room temperature with the highest bond dissociation energy is:
questions.explanation: N₂ has bond dissociation energy 945 kJ/mol (triple bond N≡N), the highest among common diatomic molecules. O₂ = 498 kJ/mol (double bond). This makes N₂ very unreactive.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The chemical formula of slaked lime is:
questions.explanation: Slaked lime = Ca(OH)₂. Made by adding water to quicklime (CaO). Used in mortar/plaster, water treatment, paper making, sugar refining, and neutralizing soil acidity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The most common allotrope of phosphorus used in safety matches is:
questions.explanation: Red phosphorus (used in match heads/striking surface): P_n polymer, non-toxic, not spontaneously flammable. White P₄ is too dangerous (spontaneously ignites). Red P ignites only by friction with match head.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The oxoacid of sulfur with sulfur in +6 oxidation state is:
questions.explanation: H₂SO₄: S = +6 (highest oxidation state for S in oxoacids). H₂SO₃: S = +4. H₂S₂O₇ (oleum): S = +6.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound formed when excess ammonia reacts with chlorine is:
questions.explanation: Excess NH₃ + Cl₂: 8NH₃ + 3Cl₂ → N₂ + 6NH₄Cl. With excess Cl₂: NH₃ + 3Cl₂ → NCl₃ + 3HCl. Limiting Cl₂ gives N₂ + NH₄Cl; excess Cl₂ gives explosive NCl₃.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Sulphur trioxide (SO₃) is absorbed in H₂SO₄ (not water directly) because:
questions.optionA: SO₃ does not react with water
questions.optionB: Direct absorption in water produces mist of fine H₂SO₄ droplets hard to handle
questions.optionD: Water would dilute H₂SO₄ too much
questions.explanation: SO₃ + H₂O → H₂SO₄ produces a dense white mist of fine H₂SO₄ droplets (aerosol). This is difficult to condense. Instead: SO₃ + H₂SO₄ → H₂S₂O₇ (oleum), then diluted carefully.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Lewis structure of N₂O (nitrous oxide) shows:
questions.optionA: All atoms with zero formal charge
questions.explanation: N₂O has two main resonance structures: N≡N⁺-O⁻ and N⁻-N⁺=O (or ⁻N=N⁺=O). The actual structure is between N≡N-O (more contribution) and ⁻N=N=O.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following is used to test for starch?
questions.explanation: Starch + I₂ → blue-black complex (amylose helix traps I₂ molecules). Used to test for starch or as endpoint indicator in iodometric titrations (blue color disappears at endpoint when I₂ is consumed).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Carbon forms more compounds than any other element because:
questions.optionA: It is the most abundant element
questions.optionB: C-C catenation (chain-forming) ability and sp³ hybridization allow unlimited chain length
questions.explanation: Carbon's unique properties: (1) Self-linking (catenation): C-C bonds are strong and stable → chains/rings of any length. (2) Tetravalency: bonds to 4 atoms. (3) Multiple bonding (sp², sp). Results in millions of organic compounds.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The pH of rainwater contaminated by sulphur dioxide (acid rain) is approximately:
questions.explanation: Normal rainwater pH ≈ 5.6 (due to dissolved CO₂). Acid rain (contaminated with SO₂ and NOₓ → H₂SO₄ and HNO₃) has pH < 5.6, sometimes as low as 3-4.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound used as an antiknocking agent in petrol (historically) is:
questions.explanation: Tetraethyl lead (TEL): Pb(C₂H₅)₄ was used as antiknock additive in leaded petrol. Pb radicals terminate the combustion chain reaction, preventing engine knock. Now banned due to lead poisoning.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The chemical used to remove permanent water hardness is:
questions.optionB: Adding Na₂CO₃ (washing soda) or using ion exchange
questions.explanation: Permanent hardness (CaSO₄, MgCl₂, CaCl₂) cannot be removed by boiling. Removed by: (1) Adding Na₂CO₃: Ca²⁺ + CO₃²⁻ → CaCO₃↓. (2) Ion exchange resin. (3) Adding sodium hexametaphosphate.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The element selenium (Se) is used in:
questions.explanation: Selenium is a photoconductor (conductivity increases with light). Used in: solar cells (thin-film Se), xerography (photocopiers use Se-coated drums), and glass manufacturing (to remove greenish tint).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Phosphorous pentachloride (PCl₅) reacts with water to give:
questions.explanation: PCl₅ + 4H₂O → H₃PO₄ + 5HCl. PCl₅ is completely hydrolyzed. (PCl₃ + 3H₂O → H₃PO₃ + 3HCl).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Sodium chloride (NaCl) is purified from saturated solution by:
questions.optionB: Passing HCl gas (common ion effect, NaCl precipitates)
questions.explanation: Purification of NaCl: pass HCl gas through saturated NaCl solution. Excess Cl⁻ (common ion) decreases NaCl solubility → NaCl precipitates. Filtered and dried.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The oxidation state of oxygen in peroxide ion (O₂²⁻) is:
questions.explanation: In peroxide O₂²⁻: total charge = -2, with 2 oxygen atoms. Each O = -2/2 = -1. Peroxide has oxygen in -1 state, not -2. Examples: H₂O₂, Na₂O₂, BaO₂.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reagent used to distinguish between SO₂ and CO₂ is:
questions.explanation: SO₂ decolorizes acidified KMnO₄ (acts as reducing agent: SO₂ → SO₄²⁻, Mn⁷⁺ → Mn²⁺). CO₂ does NOT decolorize KMnO₄. Both turn lime water milky.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Sodium peroxide (Na₂O₂) reacts with CO₂ to produce:
questions.explanation: 2Na₂O₂ + 2CO₂ → 2Na₂CO₃ + O₂. This reaction is used in submarines and breathing masks: Na₂O₂ absorbs CO₂ and releases O₂ simultaneously.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The aluminothermic process (thermite reaction) shows that aluminium:
questions.optionA: Is a weaker reducing agent than iron
questions.optionB: Is a stronger reducing agent than iron (displaces Fe from Fe₂O₃)
questions.optionC: Has lower melting point than iron
questions.explanation: 2Al + Fe₂O₃ → Al₂O₃ + 2Fe. Al reduces Fe₂O₃ because Al has higher affinity for O₂ than Fe. This demonstrates Al is a stronger reducing agent than Fe.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The formula of magnetite (iron ore) is:
questions.explanation: Magnetite = Fe₃O₄ = FeO·Fe₂O₃ (mixed iron(II)/iron(III) oxide). It is magnetic and contains both Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺. Haematite = Fe₂O₃ (contains only Fe³⁺).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Aluminium is more electropositive than iron but aluminium resists corrosion because:
questions.optionB: Al forms a passive Al₂O₃ layer immediately on exposure to air
questions.explanation: Paradox: Al is very reactive (high in activity series) but very corrosion-resistant due to the instant formation of a thin, adherent, transparent Al₂O₃ layer (passivation) that prevents further reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The process of converting iron to steel involves:
questions.optionB: Removing excess carbon (3-4%) and other impurities from pig iron
questions.explanation: Steel making: Remove excess C (3-4% in pig iron → 0.2-2% in steel) and impurities (Si, S, P) by oxidation (blast of O₂ in BOF or open-hearth furnace). Different carbon content gives different steel grades.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Quicklime is:
questions.explanation: Quicklime = CaO (calcium oxide). Made by strongly heating limestone: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ (calcination). CaO reacts vigorously with water: CaO + H₂O → Ca(OH)₂ (slaked lime).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following metals tarnishes in air due to formation of PbCO₃ and Pb(OH)₂?
questions.explanation: Lead tarnishes slowly in air to form a surface layer of PbCO₃ (ceruse) and Pb(OH)₂. This layer prevents further corrosion. Gold doesn't tarnish at all.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The atomic radius of tungsten (W) and molybdenum (Mo) are nearly equal due to:
questions.optionB: Lanthanide contraction making 5d elements similar in size to 4d elements
questions.explanation: Lanthanide contraction: the 4f electrons in lanthanides (58-71) provide poor shielding → extra nuclear charge effect → 5d transition metals (period 6) are almost the same size as 4d metals (period 5).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound responsible for the green patina on copper monuments is:
questions.explanation: Green patina on copper (like Statue of Liberty): Cu₂(OH)₂CO₃ (malachite-like). Formed by: 2Cu + H₂O + CO₂ + O₂ → Cu₂(OH)₂CO₃. Also contains CuSO₄·Cu(OH)₂ if SO₂ is present.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The catalyst used in the Contact process for H₂SO₄ manufacture is:
questions.explanation: Contact process: 2SO₂ + O₂ ⇌ 2SO₃. Catalyst: V₂O₅ (vanadium pentoxide) at 450-500°C. V₂O₅ is oxidized/reduced in catalytic cycle: V₂O₅ + SO₂ → V₂O₄ + SO₃; V₂O₄ + ½O₂ → V₂O₅.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The anode mud in electrolytic refining of copper contains:
questions.optionB: Noble metals (Au, Ag, Pt) that don't dissolve
questions.explanation: Anode mud: during electrolytic refining, more noble impurities (Au, Ag, Pt) don't dissolve from the anode. They accumulate below the anode as 'anode mud' and are collected as valuable by-products.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Galena (PbS) is the main ore of:
questions.explanation: Galena = PbS (lead(II) sulphide). Main ore of lead. Roasted: 2PbS + 3O₂ → 2PbO + 2SO₂. Reduced: PbO + CO → Pb + CO₂. Lead used in batteries, soldering, radiation shielding.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The valence electrons of transition metals are in:
questions.explanation: Transition metals have valence electrons in both (n-1)d and ns subshells. This allows them to exhibit variable oxidation states by losing different numbers of d and s electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Copper is obtained from blister copper by:
questions.explanation: Blister copper (98-99% pure) → electrolytic refining → pure copper (99.9%+). Anode: impure Cu dissolves; Cathode: pure Cu deposits. Precious metal impurities (Au, Ag) collect as anode mud.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Lithopone (white paint pigment) is a mixture of:
questions.explanation: Lithopone: ZnS + BaSO₄ mixture. Prepared: ZnSO₄ + BaS → ZnS + BaSO₄. White pigment used in paints. More opaque than ZnO alone.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The most commonly used ore to extract zinc is:
questions.explanation: Sphalerite (ZnS, zinc blende) is the most important zinc ore. Process: Roast ZnS → ZnO → reduce with coke at 1100°C → Zn vapour → condensed to liquid zinc.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Ferrous sulphate (FeSO₄·7H₂O) is commonly known as:
questions.explanation: FeSO₄·7H₂O = green vitriol or copperas. Blue vitriol = CuSO₄·5H₂O. White vitriol = ZnSO₄·7H₂O. Oil of vitriol = concentrated H₂SO₄.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The metal used as a catalyst in the Wilkinson catalyst for hydrogenation is:
questions.explanation: Wilkinson's catalyst = RhCl(PPh₃)₃ (chlorotris(triphenylphosphine)rhodium(I)). Used for homogeneous catalytic hydrogenation of alkenes at room temperature. Nobel Prize winning organometallic chemistry.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: When iron reacts with cold dilute HCl, the products are:
questions.explanation: Fe + 2HCl(dil) → FeCl₂ + H₂↑. With dilute acids, iron gives Fe²⁺ (iron(II)). With strong oxidizing acids (conc. HNO₃, conc. H₂SO₄), iron gives Fe³⁺.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Calamine (ZnCO₃) ore is first heated in the metallurgy of zinc because:
questions.optionB: Calcination converts ZnCO₃ → ZnO + CO₂ (easier to reduce)
questions.explanation: Calcination of ZnCO₃: ZnCO₃ → ZnO + CO₂. ZnO is then reduced by coke: ZnO + C → Zn + CO. Zinc vapour (bp 907°C) is collected by condensation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The iron ore siderite has the formula:
questions.explanation: Siderite = FeCO₃ (iron(II) carbonate). It's a low-grade iron ore. Haematite = Fe₂O₃. Magnetite = Fe₃O₄. Pyrite (fool's gold) = FeS₂.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The element used in nuclear reactors as a control rod material to absorb neutrons is:
questions.explanation: Control rods in nuclear reactors: made of Cd or B (or Hf) which have high neutron absorption cross-sections. Inserting rods slows/stops the chain reaction by absorbing neutrons. Zirconium cladding is used for fuel rods (low neutron absorption).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The process used to concentrate gold-bearing ore is:
questions.explanation: Gold extraction: (1) Gravity/panning: gold is denser (19.3 g/cm³) than rock gangue; (2) Cyanide process: Au + 2NaCN + ½O₂ + H₂O → Na[Au(CN)₂] + NaOH. Gold recovered by Zn displacement.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: When sodium burns in excess oxygen, it forms:
questions.explanation: 2Na + O₂ → Na₂O₂ (sodium peroxide) — in excess O₂. In limited O₂: 4Na + O₂ → 2Na₂O. Potassium in excess O₂ gives superoxide KO₂.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound used in photography as a photosensitive material is:
questions.optionB: AgCl, AgBr (silver halides in gelatin coating)
questions.explanation: Photography: AgBr (and AgCl, AgI) in gelatin on film. Light causes 2AgBr + hν → 2Ag + Br₂. Dark areas: little reduction. After development (amplifies image) and fixing (Na₂S₂O₃ removes unexposed AgBr).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Dry cell batteries typically use:
questions.optionA: Zn anode, carbon cathode, NH₄Cl/MnO₂ electrolyte paste
questions.explanation: Leclanché dry cell: Anode: Zn case (Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻). Cathode: C rod surrounded by MnO₂. Electrolyte: NH₄Cl/ZnCl₂ paste. MnO₂ + H₂O + e⁻ → MnOOH + OH⁻. Voltage ≈ 1.5 V.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Nichrome (Ni-Cr alloy) is used in:
questions.optionB: Heating elements in electric appliances (high resistivity, oxidation-resistant)
questions.explanation: Nichrome (Ni 80% + Cr 20%): high electrical resistivity, high melting point, and oxidation resistance at high temperatures. Used in heating elements of toasters, heaters, ovens, hair dryers.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The first metal to be used by ancient humans was:
questions.explanation: Copper was one of the first metals used by humans (~8000 BCE), found native. Gold was also found native and used early. The Bronze Age (Cu-Sn) preceded the Iron Age (~1200 BCE).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Zone refining is used to purify:
questions.optionB: Semiconductors like Si, Ge (ultra-high purity needed)
questions.explanation: Zone refining: used for ultra-pure Si, Ge for semiconductors. A moving heater melts a narrow zone; impurities preferentially stay in the melt and move to one end. After many passes, most pure Si is at one end.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Amalgam is an alloy of:
questions.optionB: Mercury with another metal (Hg-Ag, Hg-Au, Hg-Sn)
questions.explanation: Amalgam: alloy of mercury with another metal. Dental amalgam = Hg-Ag-Sn-Cu. Gold-mercury amalgam used in historical gold extraction. Some metals (Fe, Pt) do not form amalgams.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The composition of bronze is:
questions.explanation: Bronze = Cu + Sn (typically 90% Cu + 10% Sn). Harder than pure copper. Used for statues, bells, coins, springs. Brass = Cu + Zn. German silver = Cu + Zn + Ni.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The ore chalcopyrite has the formula:
questions.explanation: Chalcopyrite = CuFeS₂ (copper iron sulphide). Most common copper ore (~70% of global Cu production). Other copper ores: chalcocite (Cu₂S), malachite (Cu₂(OH)₂CO₃), azurite.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product formed when concentrated HNO₃ reacts with copper is:
questions.explanation: Cu + 4HNO₃(conc.) → Cu(NO₃)₂ + 2NO₂ + 2H₂O (brown NO₂ gas). Dilute HNO₃ + Cu: 3Cu + 8HNO₃(dil.) → 3Cu(NO₃)₂ + 2NO + 4H₂O (colourless NO).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Calcium carbide (CaC₂) reacts with water to produce:
questions.explanation: CaC₂ + 2H₂O → Ca(OH)₂ + C₂H₂ (acetylene/ethyne). CaC₂ contains the carbide ion C₂²⁻. Acetylene is used for oxy-acetylene welding. CaC₂ is made: CaO + 3C → CaC₂ + CO (at ~2000°C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The heaviest naturally occurring element is:
questions.explanation: Heaviest naturally occurring element by atomic mass: Uranium (U, A = 238). Osmium has the highest density (22.59 g/cm³) but U has higher mass. Plutonium is heavier but not considered 'naturally occurring' in significant amounts.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The property used in the Van Arkel method of purification is:
questions.optionB: Formation and thermal decomposition of volatile iodide (TiI₄, ZrI₄, etc.)
questions.explanation: Van Arkel-De Boer method: Crude Ti + 2I₂(g) ⇌ TiI₄(g) at 500°C. Pure Ti deposits on hot tungsten wire (1400°C) by decomposition: TiI₄ → Ti + 2I₂. Iodine is recycled. Used for Ti, Zr, Hf.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The pH of a 0.1 M NaOH solution is:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Aqua regia is a mixture of:
questions.optionB: HCl and HNO₃ in 3:1 ratio (by volume)
questions.explanation: Aqua regia = 3 parts conc. HCl + 1 part conc. HNO₃. Dissolves noble metals (Au, Pt): Au + HNO₃ + 4HCl → H[AuCl₄] + NO + 2H₂O. The Cl⁻ complexes Au³⁺, driving dissolution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The half-life of C-14 used in radiocarbon dating is approximately:
questions.explanation: C-14 (¹⁴C) half-life = 5730 years. Used to date organic material up to ~50,000 years old. ¹⁴C is formed in atmosphere: ¹⁴N + n → ¹⁴C + p. Decays: ¹⁴C → ¹⁴N + β⁻.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Xenon was considered a noble gas incapable of forming compounds until:
questions.explanation: In 1962, Neil Bartlett prepared the first noble gas compound XePtF₆. He noticed O₂⁺PtF₆⁻ was stable and since IE of Xe (1170 kJ/mol) ≈ IE of O₂, Xe should also react. This overturned the 'noble gas don't react' dogma.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The gas responsible for the smell of fish is:
questions.explanation: The fishy smell is due to trimethylamine (CH₃)₃N and other amines produced during decomposition of proteins in fish. Squeezing lemon (acid) converts volatile amines to non-volatile ammonium salts.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The most electronegative element is:
questions.explanation: Fluorine (F) has the highest electronegativity (3.98 on Pauling scale), followed by O (3.44), Cl (3.16), N (3.04). Electronegativity increases across periods and up groups in periodic table.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Iodoform test is positive for:
questions.optionB: Acetaldehyde (ethanal) and compounds oxidizable to acetaldehyde (ethanol)
questions.explanation: Iodoform test (I₂/NaOH): positive for CH₃CHO, CH₃COCH₃ (ketone with CH₃CO group), and CH₃CH₂OH (ethanol). Yellow CHI₃ precipitate (iodoform) with antiseptic smell. Not methanol, not benzaldehyde.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The element with the highest first ionization energy (among main group elements) is:
questions.explanation: He has the highest first IE (2372 kJ/mol) due to smallest size and no shielding. Among reactive elements (non-noble), F is highest. Among period 2 elements excluding noble gases: N > O due to half-filled 2p stability.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Nichrome is used for making electric heating elements because it has:
questions.optionB: High electrical resistivity and high oxidation resistance at elevated temperatures
questions.explanation: Nichrome (Ni-Cr alloy): electrical resistivity ~110 μΩ·cm (high), oxidation-resistant due to Cr₂O₃ layer, melting point ~1400°C. These properties make it ideal for heating elements.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The formula of haematite (major iron ore) is:
questions.explanation: Haematite = Fe₂O₃ (iron(III) oxide). Most important iron ore (contains Fe³⁺). Also: Magnetite Fe₃O₄ = FeO·Fe₂O₃ (both Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺). Siderite FeCO₃, Pyrite FeS₂ (fool's gold, not used directly).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Concentrated HNO₃ makes iron passive because:
questions.optionA: Fe dissolves completely in conc. HNO₃
questions.optionB: A thin Fe₂O₃/Fe₃O₄ oxide layer forms, preventing further reaction
questions.optionC: HNO₃ reacts with Fe to form FeN
questions.explanation: Iron (and Al, Cr) becomes passive in concentrated HNO₃ due to formation of a thin, adherent iron oxide layer (Fe₃O₄) that resists further attack. Dilute HNO₃ does react with iron normally.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The formula of potassium permanganate is:
questions.explanation: Potassium permanganate = KMnO₄. Mn is in +7 oxidation state (highest). Used as oxidizing agent in acidic, neutral, and basic media. Potassium manganate = K₂MnO₄ (Mn in +6).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The percentage of iron in pig iron (cast iron from blast furnace) is approximately:
questions.optionB: 95-96% Fe (rest: C 3-4%, Si, Mn, S, P)
questions.explanation: Pig iron (cast iron): ~95-96% Fe with ~3-4% carbon + Si, Mn, S, P impurities. It is brittle due to high C content. Steel has C reduced to 0.2-2%.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Duralumin (aircraft alloy) is composed of:
questions.explanation: Duralumin: Al (95%) + Cu (4%) + Mg (0.5%) + Mn (0.5%). Lightweight and strong alloy used in aircraft structures. The Cu/Mg combination with heat treatment causes precipitation hardening.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The colour of dilute solution of KMnO₄ (permanganate) is:
questions.explanation: KMnO₄ solutions are purple/violet (due to charge transfer in MnO₄⁻). K₂MnO₄ solutions are green (Mn⁶⁺). MnO₂ is black/brown. Mn²⁺ solutions are pale pink/nearly colorless.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product of electrolysis of molten AlCl₃ at the cathode is:
questions.explanation: At cathode of molten AlCl₃: Al³⁺ + 3e⁻ → Al. At anode: 2Cl⁻ → Cl₂. In the Hall-Héroult process, Al₂O₃ (not AlCl₃) is used; Al is produced at cathode.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The element used in making photovoltaic solar cells is primarily:
questions.explanation: Most commercial solar cells use high-purity crystalline or amorphous silicon (Si). Si is doped with P (n-type) and B (p-type) to create the p-n junction that converts light to electricity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The slag formed in the blast furnace is:
questions.explanation: In blast furnace: CaO (from CaCO₃ decomposition) + SiO₂ (silica gangue) → CaSiO₃ (calcium silicate slag). Slag floats on molten iron and is tapped separately. Used in cement manufacturing.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The coinage metals (Group 11) are:
questions.explanation: Coinage metals (Group 11, d¹⁰s¹ configuration): Cu (copper), Ag (silver), Au (gold). All have full d subshell. Low reactivity (noble character), lustrous, good conductors, historically used as coins.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: When silver nitrate (AgNO₃) solution is added to sodium chloride solution, the observation is:
questions.optionB: White precipitate of AgCl (insoluble in HNO₃, soluble in NH₃)
questions.explanation: Ag⁺ + Cl⁻ → AgCl↓ (white, curdy precipitate). AgCl is insoluble in HNO₃ but dissolves in NH₃: AgCl + 2NH₃ → [Ag(NH₃)₂]⁺ + Cl⁻. Used as qualitative test for Cl⁻.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The metal that exists as liquid at room temperature is:
questions.explanation: Two liquid metals at room temperature (25°C, 1 atm): Mercury (Hg, mp -39°C) and Gallium (Ga, mp 29.76°C, liquid just above room temp). Cesium melts at 28.5°C (also close to room temp).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Rusting of iron is an example of:
questions.explanation: Rusting mechanism (electrochemical): Anode (Fe): Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻. Cathode (C or less reactive): O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻. Fe²⁺ + 2OH⁻ → Fe(OH)₂ → Fe₂O₃·xH₂O (rust). Requires both O₂ and H₂O.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The process of cyanide leaching is used in extraction of:
questions.explanation: Cyanide process: 4Au + 8NaCN + O₂ + 2H₂O → 4Na[Au(CN)₂] + 4NaOH. Gold dissolves as cyanide complex. Then: 2Na[Au(CN)₂] + Zn → Na₂[Zn(CN)₄] + 2Au. Gold is recovered by displacement.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The element that can displace hydrogen from steam but not from cold water is:
questions.explanation: Iron reacts with steam: 3Fe + 4H₂O(steam) → Fe₃O₄ + 4H₂↑. Iron is too unreactive to displace H₂ from cold water. Na and Ca react vigorously with cold water.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Invar (a low-expansion alloy) is composed of:
questions.explanation: Invar: Fe-Ni alloy with ~36% Ni. Has extremely low coefficient of thermal expansion (0.8 × 10⁻⁶/°C). Used in precision instruments, surveying tapes, pendulums, and scientific instruments where dimensional stability is critical.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The colour of cobalt chloride paper changes from blue to pink in presence of:
questions.explanation: Anhydrous CoCl₂ is blue. Hydrated CoCl₂·6H₂O is pink. Cobalt chloride paper: dipped in CoCl₂, dried (blue). Contact with water: CoCl₂ + 6H₂O → CoCl₂·6H₂O (pink). Used as humidity/water indicator.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction used in the Bessemer process for steel making is:
questions.optionB: Oxidation of impurities (C, Si, P, S) by blowing O₂/air through molten pig iron
questions.explanation: Bessemer process: Air or O₂ blown through molten pig iron. Impurities (C, Si, Mn) are oxidized: C + O₂ → CO₂; Si + O₂ → SiO₂. Exothermic process requires no external fuel. Temperature controlled by duration.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The most abundant metal in the Earth's crust is:
questions.explanation: Most abundant metals in Earth's crust: Al (8%) > Fe (5%) > Ca (3.6%) > Na (2.8%). Most abundant element overall is O (46%). Silicon (28%) is most abundant metalloid. Al is used extensively due to its abundance.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The metal that reacts with nitrogen gas directly at room temperature to form a nitride is:
questions.explanation: Lithium reacts with N₂ at room temperature: 6Li + N₂ → 2Li₃N (lithium nitride). Mg reacts with N₂ but requires burning. Na forms Na₃N only at high temperature and not easily. Li is unique among Group 1.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The metal that is liquid at room temperature and highly toxic is:
questions.explanation: Mercury (Hg): liquid metal at room temperature (mp -38.8°C). Highly toxic — vapor, inhalation, and contact cause neurological damage (Minamata disease). Used in thermometers, barometers, dental amalgam (being phased out).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Magnalium (alloy of Al + Mg) is used in:
questions.optionB: Light weight aircraft and automobile parts
questions.explanation: Magnalium: Al (95%) + Mg (5%). Very light, strong, and corrosion-resistant. Used in aircraft parts, cars, balance wheels of fine instruments. Also called 'magnalium alloy' in pyrotechnics.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The metal that exhibits the phenomenon of 'creep' (slow deformation under stress at high temperatures) most severely is:
questions.explanation: Creep is significant when temperature > 0.4 × melting point (in Kelvin). Lead (mp 327°C = 600K), so creep is significant at T > 0.4 × 600K = 240K = -33°C, meaning lead creeps at room temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The oxide of nitrogen used as an anaesthetic ('laughing gas') is:
questions.explanation: Nitrous oxide (N₂O) = 'laughing gas'. Properties: sweet smell, non-toxic at low concentrations, analgesic and anaesthetic effects. Used in dentistry and surgery. Also used in whipped cream dispensers.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The process by which magnesium metal is extracted commercially is:
questions.explanation: Dow process: electrolysis of molten MgCl₂ from seawater. MgCl₂ is produced by: Mg(OH)₂ + 2HCl → MgCl₂ + 2H₂O. Electrolysis: cathode: Mg²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Mg; anode: 2Cl⁻ → Cl₂.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Potassium superoxide (KO₂) reacts with CO₂ to produce:
questions.explanation: 4KO₂ + 2CO₂ → 2K₂CO₃ + 3O₂. This reaction is used in self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA) and space applications — absorbs CO₂ and releases O₂.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The chemical symbol W for tungsten comes from:
questions.explanation: Tungsten's symbol W comes from 'Wolfram' (German name for the element). Tungsten has the highest melting point (3422°C) and highest boiling point of all metals. Used in light bulb filaments.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The nitrogen cycle is important because:
questions.optionA: N₂ is directly used by plants as a nutrient
questions.optionB: Atmospheric N₂ is fixed into bioavailable forms (NH₃, NO₃⁻) by microbes and returned to N₂
questions.optionC: Nitrogen gas is a food source for animals
questions.optionD: Nitrogen prevents O₂ from accumulating in air
questions.explanation: Nitrogen cycle: N₂ → NH₃ (nitrogen fixation by Rhizobium/Azotobacter) → NO₃⁻ (nitrification) → plant absorption → denitrification → N₂. Plants can't use N₂ directly; need fixed forms.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The gas produced when any halide is treated with concentrated H₂SO₄ and it turns blue litmus red is:
questions.explanation: NaCl + H₂SO₄(conc.) → NaHSO₄ + HCl↑. HCl gas fumes, acidic, turns blue litmus red, gives white fumes with NH₃. For HBr and HI, conc. H₂SO₄ oxidizes them (HBr → Br₂, HI → I₂).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The noble gas used in discharge tubes to give an orange-red glow is:
questions.explanation: Neon signs: Neon gas in discharge tubes gives characteristic orange-red glow. Different gases/mercury vapor give different colors. Despite the name, 'neon signs' with other colors use Ar, Hg vapor, or fluorescent coatings.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound boric acid (H₃BO₃) is:
questions.optionB: A monobasic Lewis acid (accepts OH⁻, gives up H⁺ as water)
questions.explanation: Boric acid is monobasic Lewis acid: B(OH)₃ + H₂O → [B(OH)₄]⁻ + H⁺. It doesn't donate a proton directly but accepts OH⁻. Ka ≈ 5.8 × 10⁻¹⁰, weak acid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Calcium hypochlorite Ca(OCl)₂ is used as:
questions.optionB: Bleaching powder / disinfectant for swimming pools
questions.explanation: Ca(OCl)₂ (bleaching powder): used to bleach textiles and paper, and to disinfect swimming pools and drinking water. It releases HOCl (hypochlorous acid) which has oxidizing/bleaching action.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product formed when fluorine reacts with water is:
questions.explanation: 2F₂ + 2H₂O → 4HF + O₂. Fluorine is so oxidizing it oxidizes water (unlike other halogens which are reduced by water). F₂ may also produce OF₂ or H₂O₂ as minor products.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: White phosphorus is:
questions.optionA: Tetrahedral P₄ molecules, very reactive, stored under water
questions.optionB: A polymer of P atoms, red/brown color
questions.optionC: The most stable allotrope of phosphorus
questions.explanation: White phosphorus: P₄ tetrahedra with 60° P-P-P bond angles (strained). Very reactive, spontaneously ignites in air (stored under water). Highly toxic. Glows in dark (chemiluminescence). MP = 44°C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The element boron (B) is classified as a:
questions.explanation: Boron (B) is a metalloid/semimetal. It has some metallic properties (semiconductor) and some non-metallic properties. Located on the staircase boundary in the periodic table. Used in nuclear reactors and semiconductors.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The test used to detect presence of nitrate ion (NO₃⁻) in solution is:
questions.optionB: Brown ring test (FeSO₄ + conc. H₂SO₄ → brown [Fe(NO)(H₂O)₅]²⁺ ring)
questions.explanation: Brown ring test for NO₃⁻: Add FeSO₄ solution to test solution. Then slowly pour conc. H₂SO₄ down the side of the tube. A brown ring at the interface = positive test for NO₃⁻ → forms [Fe(NO)(H₂O)₅]²⁺.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Dry cell batteries lose charge over time even when not used because:
questions.optionB: NH₄Cl electrolyte slowly corrodes the Zn case (self-discharge)
questions.explanation: Self-discharge in dry cells: NH₄Cl electrolyte slowly reacts with Zn anode even without a circuit. The Zn-NH₄Cl-MnO₂ system has a small spontaneous reaction. Refrigeration slows self-discharge.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The acid that fumigates and is used to etch glass is:
questions.explanation: HF (hydrofluoric acid) etches glass: SiO₂ + 4HF → SiF₄ + 2H₂O. Used for etching glass, glass frosting. Despite being weak in terms of Ka, HF is extremely corrosive to tissue and skin. Fumes are toxic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of water with excess sodium metal gives:
questions.explanation: 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂↑. Sodium reacts vigorously (exothermically) with cold water, skimming across the surface, possibly catching fire. The H₂ gas may ignite.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound used to protect against radiation in X-ray rooms and nuclear reactors is:
questions.optionB: Lead (Pb) due to high density and atomic number
questions.explanation: Lead shielding: high density (11.3 g/cm³) and high atomic number (Z=82) make Pb effective at absorbing gamma rays and X-rays. Lead aprons in medical X-ray, lead-lined walls in X-ray rooms, lead casks for radioactive materials.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound responsible for the smell of rotten eggs is:
questions.explanation: H₂S (hydrogen sulphide) has the characteristic smell of rotten eggs. Produced by decomposition of sulphur-containing organic matter. Very toxic (more toxic than HCN at high concentrations). Detectable at <1 ppm.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Downs cell is used to produce:
questions.explanation: Down's cell: electrolysis of molten NaCl (mixed with CaCl₂ to lower mp to 600°C). Cathode: 2Na⁺ + 2e⁻ → 2Na(l). Anode: 2Cl⁻ → Cl₂(g). A diaphragm separates Na from Cl₂.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: When copper(II) hydroxide dissolves in excess ammonia, the solution becomes:
questions.explanation: Cu(OH)₂ + 4NH₃(aq) → [Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺ + 2OH⁻. The deep blue tetraamminecopper(II) complex ion is formed (Cu²⁺ with 4 NH₃ ligands). This is Schweitzer's reagent (dissolves cellulose).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The formula of baking soda is:
questions.explanation: Baking soda = NaHCO₃ (sodium hydrogen carbonate/bicarbonate). Decomposes on heating: 2NaHCO₃ → Na₂CO₃ + CO₂ + H₂O. CO₂ makes baked goods rise. Also used as antacid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The metal used as anode in the chlor-alkali process is:
questions.explanation: Chlor-alkali process (electrolysis of NaCl brine): Anode (graphite or Ti/RuO₂ DSA): 2Cl⁻ → Cl₂. Cathode: 2H₂O + 2e⁻ → H₂ + 2OH⁻. Products: Cl₂, H₂, NaOH. Used to make PVC (Cl₂) and bleach.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Castner-Kellner process is used to manufacture:
questions.optionB: NaOH (caustic soda) by electrolysis of brine using mercury cathode
questions.explanation: Castner-Kellner process (mercury cell): NaCl(aq) electrolysis. Hg cathode: Na⁺ + e⁻ → Na(Hg) (sodium amalgam). Na amalgam + H₂O → NaOH + Hg + H₂. Anode: Cl⁻ → Cl₂. Produces high-purity NaOH.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The main raw material for manufacturing aluminium is:
questions.optionB: Bauxite ore (Al₂O₃·2H₂O), purified to Al₂O₃ by Bayer process
questions.explanation: Aluminium production: Bauxite (Al₂O₃·2H₂O, with Fe₂O₃/SiO₂ impurities) → Bayer process (NaOH leaches Al₂O₃ selectively) → pure Al₂O₃ (alumina) → Hall-Héroult electrolysis → Al metal.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The term 'ore' means:
questions.optionB: A mineral from which a metal can be profitably extracted
questions.explanation: Ore: a naturally occurring mineral deposit from which a metal (or other substance) can be extracted profitably. Not all minerals are ores — an ore must have sufficient metal content for economic extraction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The metal that burns in nitrogen to form a nitride but does not react with water is:
questions.explanation: Magnesium burns in N₂: 3Mg + N₂ → Mg₃N₂. Mg doesn't react with cold water (MgO passivation) but reacts with steam. Note: Mg burned in air reacts with both O₂ and N₂ simultaneously.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: A complex ion with the formula [Fe(CN)₆]³⁻ is:
questions.optionB: Hexacyanoferrate(III) — used in potassium salt K₃[Fe(CN)₆]
questions.explanation: [Fe(CN)₆]³⁻ = hexacyanoferrate(III). Fe is in +3 state (3 negative charge from CN⁻ gives -6, and -3 total for complex, so Fe = +3). K₃[Fe(CN)₆] = potassium hexacyanoferrate(III), used to test for Fe²⁺.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Coke (carbon) is used as a reducing agent in metallurgy because:
questions.optionB: Carbon has a higher affinity for oxygen than many metals at high temperatures
questions.optionC: Carbon doesn't react with metal oxides
questions.optionD: Carbon lowers the melting point of metals
questions.explanation: Ellingham diagram: at high temperatures (>700°C for Fe, >900°C for Zn), the ΔG of C → CO₂/CO becomes more negative than metal oxide formation, meaning C can reduce those metal oxides thermodynamically.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The phenomenon where higher period transition metals have similar atomic radii to lower period ones due to lanthanide contraction is observed between:
questions.explanation: Lanthanide contraction: 4f electrons provide poor shielding → 5d elements are nearly same size as 4d. Thus: Zr (4d) ≈ Hf (5d), Mo (4d) ≈ W (5d), Nb ≈ Ta. This makes separation of 4d/5d metals very difficult.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The silver halide most sensitive to light (used in fast photographic films) is:
questions.explanation: AgBr is most widely used in photography due to its appropriate light sensitivity, grain size control, and speed. AgI is used in fast films. AgCl is used in slow, fine-grain films. AgF is too soluble.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The alloy of gold used in jewelry in Nepal is typically:
questions.explanation: 22 carat gold (hallmark 916, = 22/24 = 91.67% purity) is traditionally used in Nepali/South Asian jewelry. Pure 24K gold is too soft for jewelry. 22K adds small amounts of Cu, Ag, or Zn for hardness.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Calcium is obtained industrially by:
