questions.explanation: Calcium metal: electrolysis of molten CaCl₂. Cathode: Ca²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Ca. Anode: 2Cl⁻ → Cl₂. Carbon reduction won't work well for CaO (very stable oxide). Calcium is a strong reducing agent itself.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Bromine is the only element that is:
questions.optionA: A liquid metal at room temperature
questions.optionB: A non-metallic element that is liquid at room temperature (Br₂)
questions.optionD: A gaseous halogen at room temperature
questions.explanation: At 25°C: Br₂ is a dark reddish-brown liquid (mp -7°C, bp 59°C). Mercury is the only liquid metal. All other halogens at 25°C: F₂ and Cl₂ are gases, I₂ is solid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Anaesthetic gas dinitrogen oxide (N₂O) is prepared by:
questions.explanation: NH₄NO₃ → N₂O + 2H₂O (at 230°C). NH₄NO₃ undergoes internal redox: N in NH₄⁺ is -3, in NO₃⁻ is +5. Both oxidize/reduce to N₂O where N = +1. Heating above 300°C causes explosion.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Ozone is used to purify drinking water because it:
questions.optionB: Oxidizes and kills microorganisms without leaving toxic residue (unlike Cl₂)
questions.explanation: Ozone (O₃) for water treatment: strong oxidizing agent, kills bacteria and viruses, decomposes to harmless O₂. No residual disinfectant taste/smell. Used in Europe. Cl₂ leaves residuals but is cheaper.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The pungent smell of onions and garlic is due to:
questions.optionB: Sulphur-containing volatile organic compounds (allicin, diallyl sulfides)
questions.explanation: Onions/garlic smell: volatile sulphur compounds. Onions: propanethial-S-oxide (eye irritant), propyl disulfide. Garlic: allicin (diallyl thiosulfinate), diallyl disulfide. All contain S-S or C-S bonds.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The volume of one mole of any ideal gas at STP (0°C, 1 atm) is:
questions.explanation: Molar volume at STP (0°C = 273.15 K, 1 atm = 101.325 kPa): V = nRT/P = 1 × 0.08206 × 273.15/1 = 22.4 L. At standard conditions (25°C, 100 kPa, IUPAC definition), molar volume = 24.8 L.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The correct order of boiling point of halogens is:
questions.explanation: Boiling points: F₂ (-188°C) < Cl₂ (-35°C) < Br₂ (59°C) < I₂ (184°C). Increases down the group due to increasing molecular mass and London dispersion forces (more electrons → stronger induced dipole).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The pollutant responsible for photochemical smog is primarily:
questions.optionB: NOₓ and VOCs which react with sunlight to form ozone and peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN)
questions.explanation: Photochemical smog: NOₓ (from car engines) + volatile organic compounds (VOCs, from fuel) + sunlight → O₃ (ground level), PAN (peroxyacetyl nitrate), formaldehyde. Causes eye irritation, respiratory problems.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The inert nature of nitrogen (N₂) is due to:
questions.optionB: Triple bond (N≡N) with very high bond dissociation energy (945 kJ/mol)
questions.explanation: N₂ is chemically inert at room temperature due to the very strong N≡N triple bond (945 kJ/mol, highest among diatomic molecules). Reactions occur only at high T, high P, or with biological catalysts.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reagent used to identify primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols (Lucas test) is:
questions.explanation: Lucas test: conc. HCl + anhydrous ZnCl₂. Tertiary alcohol: immediate cloudiness (SN1, instant reaction). Secondary alcohol: cloudiness in 5 min. Primary alcohol: no cloudiness at room temperature. Distinguishes 1°, 2°, 3° alcohols.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The molecule that has an unpaired electron and is paramagnetic among oxides of nitrogen is:
questions.explanation: NO₂ has 17 electrons (odd electron molecule). One unpaired electron → paramagnetic (brownish color, reactive). At low temperature, 2NO₂ ⇌ N₂O₄ (colorless, diamagnetic, 18 electrons).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Diborane (B₂H₆) is unusual because:
questions.optionD: Diborane has the same structure as ethane
questions.explanation: Diborane B₂H₆: has 4 terminal B-H bonds and 2 three-center two-electron (3c2e) B-H-B bridging bonds ('banana bonds'). These are electron-deficient bonds (one bond, shared by 3 atoms, but only 2 electrons).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The chemical name of baking soda is:
questions.explanation: Baking soda = NaHCO₃ = sodium hydrogen carbonate (or sodium bicarbonate). Used in cooking (releases CO₂ to leaven bread), as antacid, and in fire extinguishers (reacts with acid to produce CO₂).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The allotrope of oxygen used as an antiseptic by decomposing to release nascent oxygen is:
questions.explanation: Ozone (O₃) releases nascent oxygen: O₃ → O₂ + [O]. [O] is a powerful oxidizing/bactericidal agent. Used to sterilize water, dental equipment, and in bleaching. Also occurs naturally in stratosphere (UV shield).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: What is the geometry of SO₄²⁻ ion?
questions.explanation: SO₄²⁻: S has 4 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs. Electron geometry = tetrahedral. S uses sp³ hybridization (expanded octet: S can use d orbitals). Bond angle = 109.5°, same as CH₄.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The method used to collect gases that react with water (e.g., HCl, NH₃) is:
questions.optionC: Displacement of water (not suitable for HCl or NH₃)
questions.explanation: HCl (dense, reactive with water): collected by downward displacement of air (heavier than air). NH₃ (light, reactive with water): collected by upward displacement of air (lighter). Displacement of water can't be used for water-soluble gases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Phosgene (COCl₂) is:
questions.optionB: A highly toxic gas used as chemical weapon in WWI and industrial intermediate
questions.explanation: Phosgene (COCl₂): colorless, highly toxic gas (smells of freshly cut hay). Used as chemical weapon in WWI. Now used industrially to make polycarbonates (via reaction with bisphenol A) and isocyanates for polyurethanes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Iodine solution turns starch blue-black. Heating this blue-black mixture results in:
questions.optionB: Disappearance of color (but returns on cooling)
questions.explanation: Starch-I₂ complex (blue-black): the I₂ is trapped inside the amylose helix. On heating, the helix uncoils and I₂ is released → color disappears. On cooling, helix reforms, I₂ is re-trapped → blue-black returns. Reversible.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Oxalic acid (H₂C₂O₄) reacts with KMnO₄ in acidic conditions. The product from oxalic acid is:
questions.explanation: 5H₂C₂O₄ + 2KMnO₄ + 3H₂SO₄ → 10CO₂ + 2MnSO₄ + K₂SO₄ + 8H₂O. Oxalic acid (C in +3 state) is oxidized to CO₂ (C in +4). KMnO₄ is reduced: Mn⁷⁺ → Mn²⁺. Used in titrations.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The industrial process for making nitric acid from ammonia is:
questions.optionA: Direct synthesis from N₂ and H₂O
questions.optionB: Ostwald process: NH₃ + O₂ (Pt/Rh catalyst) → NO → NO₂ → HNO₃
questions.explanation: Ostwald process: (1) 4NH₃ + 5O₂ → 4NO + 6H₂O (Pt-Rh catalyst, 900°C). (2) 2NO + O₂ → 2NO₂. (3) 4NO₂ + O₂ + 2H₂O → 4HNO₃. Produces ~60-70% HNO₃; concentrated by distillation with H₂SO₄.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: When Cl₂ gas is passed through NaOH solution, the products are:
questions.explanation: Cl₂ + 2NaOH (cold, dilute) → NaCl + NaOCl + H₂O. Hot concentrated: 3Cl₂ + 6NaOH → 5NaCl + NaClO₃ + 3H₂O. The difference is temperature and concentration.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which reaction shows amphoteric behavior of aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃)?
questions.optionA: Al₂O₃ + NaOH → NaAlO₂ + H₂O AND Al₂O₃ + H₂SO₄ → Al₂(SO₄)₃ + H₂O
questions.optionC: Al₂O₃ is a basic oxide only
questions.optionD: Al₂O₃ doesn't react with either acid or base
questions.explanation: Al₂O₃ is amphoteric: (1) With acid: Al₂O₃ + 3H₂SO₄ → Al₂(SO₄)₃ + 3H₂O (acts as base). (2) With base: Al₂O₃ + 2NaOH → 2NaAlO₂ + H₂O (acts as acid). Aluminium itself is also amphoteric.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The common name for calcium hydroxide used in agriculture to neutralize soil acidity is:
questions.explanation: Slaked lime = Ca(OH)₂. Used in agriculture to neutralize acidic soils: Ca(OH)₂ + 2H⁺ → Ca²⁺ + 2H₂O. CaO (quicklime) and CaCO₃ (limestone/chalk) are also used but Ca(OH)₂ acts faster.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reagent used to test for the presence of CO₂ gas is:
questions.optionB: Lime water Ca(OH)₂ (turns milky white as CaCO₃ forms)
questions.explanation: CO₂ + Ca(OH)₂ → CaCO₃↓ (milky) + H₂O. CaCO₃ turns lime water milky white. If excess CO₂ passed: CaCO₃ + CO₂ + H₂O → Ca(HCO₃)₂ (soluble) → milkiness disappears.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Tear gas (CS gas) used in riot control contains:
questions.explanation: CS gas: 2-chlorobenzalmalononitrile (C₁₀H₅ClN₂). Causes extreme eye and respiratory irritation. OC (oleoresin capsicum, pepper spray) is another tear gas. These are incapacitating agents, not lethal chemical weapons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The noble gas used in deep-sea diving mixtures (instead of N₂) to avoid nitrogen narcosis is:
questions.explanation: Heliox (He + O₂): used for deep diving. Helium is less narcotic and less soluble in blood/fat than N₂, avoiding nitrogen narcosis (raptures of the deep) at great depths. Trimix (He + N₂ + O₂) for very deep diving.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describes the structure of graphite?
questions.optionA: Tetrahedral sp³ carbon atoms in 3D network
questions.optionB: Sp² carbon layers with delocalized π electrons, layers held by van der Waals forces
questions.optionD: Fused rings in a single plane without delocalized electrons
questions.explanation: Graphite structure: sp² carbon in hexagonal planar layers. Each C has 3 σ bonds + 1 delocalized π electron (gives metallic conductivity). Layers held by weak van der Waals forces (makes it slippery, used as lubricant). Cleaves parallel to layers.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The hybridization of carbon in ethyne (HC≡CH) is:
questions.explanation: Ethyne (acetylene, HC≡CH): each carbon is sp hybridized. Two sp orbitals form σ bonds (C-H and C-C). Two remaining unhybridized p orbitals on each C form two π bonds (triple bond = 1σ + 2π).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following is a correct IUPAC name?
questions.explanation: 2-methylpropane (isobutane, C₄H₁₀): correct IUPAC name. Choose longest chain (propane = 3C), methyl branch at C-2. Option B is wrong (not lowest locant); C and D would be renamed.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The isomers that are non-superimposable mirror images of each other are called:
questions.explanation: Enantiomers: stereoisomers that are non-superimposable mirror images (like left and right hands). Require a chiral center (asymmetric carbon with 4 different groups). They rotate plane-polarized light in opposite directions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The IUPAC name of CH₃-CH₂-CH₂-CH₂-OH is:
questions.explanation: CH₃CH₂CH₂CH₂OH: 4-carbon chain with OH at C-1. IUPAC: 1-butanol. Number from end nearest -OH group. 'n-butyl alcohol' is common name (not IUPAC). '4-hydroxybutane' is wrong numbering.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Methane is obtained from the reaction of aluminium carbide with water:
questions.explanation: Al₄C₃ + 12H₂O → 4Al(OH)₃ + 3CH₄. Aluminium carbide (Al₄C₃) contains the C⁴⁻ (methanide) ion which is protonated by water to give methane (CH₄). This is called 'methanide carbide'.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product of the reaction of propene (CH₃CH=CH₂) with HBr according to Markovnikov's rule is:
questions.explanation: Markovnikov's rule: H adds to the carbon with more H (=CH₂), Br adds to the carbon with fewer H (=CH-). CH₃CH=CH₂ + HBr → CH₃CHBrCH₃ (2-bromopropane). Markovnikov product is more stable carbocation intermediate.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Wurtz reaction is used to prepare:
questions.optionB: Higher alkanes by coupling two alkyl halides with Na metal
questions.explanation: Wurtz reaction: 2RX + 2Na → R-R + 2NaX. Couples two alkyl halide molecules (same or different) to give a higher alkane. Limitations: can only efficiently make symmetric alkanes; with different halides, mixture forms.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The general formula of alkenes is:
questions.explanation: Alkenes (CₙH₂ₙ, n ≥ 2): one C=C double bond gives one degree of unsaturation. Alkanes: CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. Alkynes: CₙH₂ₙ₋₂. Benzene: C₆H₆ (CₙHₙ). Each ring or double bond reduces 2H from alkane formula.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of ethylene with hot concentrated H₂SO₄ followed by dilution with water gives:
questions.explanation: CH₂=CH₂ + H₂SO₄(conc.) → CH₃-CH₂-OSO₃H (ethyl hydrogen sulphate). Then + H₂O → CH₃CH₂OH (ethanol) + H₂SO₄. Indirect hydration of ethylene to give ethanol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Benzene undergoes electrophilic substitution rather than addition because:
questions.optionA: Benzene is too stable for any reaction
questions.optionB: Addition would destroy the aromatic π system (stable resonance), substitution preserves it
questions.explanation: Benzene's 6 delocalized π electrons (aromaticity) provide extra stability (resonance energy ~150 kJ/mol). Addition would disrupt this aromaticity. Substitution preserves the aromatic ring and is thermodynamically favored.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction used to convert alkene to diol (glycol) using cold dilute KMnO₄ is called:
questions.explanation: Cold dilute KMnO₄ (Baeyer's reagent, purple): oxidizes C=C to form 1,2-diol (glycol): CH₂=CH₂ + KMnO₄ → HOCH₂CH₂OH + MnO₂. KMnO₄ decolorizes (purple → brown MnO₂). Test for unsaturation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The systematic name for acetone is:
questions.explanation: Acetone (CH₃COCH₃): IUPAC = propan-2-one. 3-carbon chain (propan-), ketone carbonyl at C-2 (-2-one). Simplest ketone. Common name: acetone or dimethyl ketone.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The cracking of large petroleum hydrocarbons gives:
questions.optionB: Smaller alkanes and alkenes (including ethylene, propylene)
questions.explanation: Cracking (thermal or catalytic): breaks C-C bonds in large alkanes (petroleum fractions) → smaller alkanes + alkenes. C₁₀H₂₂ → C₄H₈ + C₆H₁₄ (example). Provides feedstock for plastics (ethylene → polyethylene).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The degree of unsaturation (DBE) in a molecular formula C₆H₆ is:
questions.explanation: DBE = (2C + 2 - H)/2 = (2×6 + 2 - 6)/2 = (12 + 2 - 6)/2 = 8/2 = 4. Benzene: 1 ring (1 DBE) + 3 double bonds (3 DBE) = 4 DBE. Each ring or double bond = 1 DBE; triple bond = 2 DBE.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product of addition of Br₂ to ethylene is:
questions.explanation: CH₂=CH₂ + Br₂ → BrCH₂CH₂Br (1,2-dibromoethane). Addition across the double bond. The Br₂/CCl₄ test: brown bromine water decolorized by alkene = positive test for unsaturation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Saponification is the process of:
questions.optionA: Making esters from alcohols and acids
questions.optionB: Hydrolysis of esters (especially fats/oils) with NaOH/KOH to give soaps (fatty acid salts) and glycerol
questions.explanation: Saponification: alkaline hydrolysis of fats/oils: fat (triglyceride) + NaOH → soap (Na salt of fatty acid) + glycerol. RCOOR' + NaOH → RCOONa + R'OH. Soap works by emulsifying oils in water.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The IUPAC name of CH₃COOH is:
questions.explanation: CH₃COOH: 2-carbon chain with -COOH at end. IUPAC: ethanoic acid (ethan- = 2C, -oic acid = carboxylic acid). Common name: acetic acid (or vinegar). Methanoic acid = HCOOH (formic acid, 1C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of propyne (CH₃C≡CH) with HBr follows Markovnikov's rule in the first step to give:
questions.explanation: CH₃C≡CH + HBr → CH₃CBr=CH₂ (2-bromopropene, Markovnikov). H adds to terminal carbon (≡CH, more H), Br to internal carbon. Second addition: CH₃CBr=CH₂ + HBr → CH₃CBr₂CH₃ (2,2-dibromopropane).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Friedel-Crafts alkylation of benzene uses:
questions.explanation: Friedel-Crafts alkylation: C₆H₆ + RCl (AlCl₃ catalyst) → C₆H₅R + HCl. AlCl₃ (Lewis acid) generates carbocation R⁺ from RCl. R⁺ acts as electrophile attacking benzene ring. Introduces alkyl group on benzene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following undergoes E2 (bimolecular elimination) reaction most readily?
questions.optionC: Tertiary alkyl halide with strong base
questions.explanation: E2 favored by: tertiary substrate (generates most stable alkene product) + strong bulky base (e.g., t-BuOK). Tertiary C has the most β-H atoms and gives most stable (Zaitsev product) trisubstituted alkene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The common name of ethanol is:
questions.explanation: Ethanol (C₂H₅OH = CH₃CH₂OH): common name = ethyl alcohol. The 'alcohol' in beverages. Methanol (CH₃OH) = methyl alcohol (wood spirit, poisonous). Isopropanol = 2-propanol (rubbing alcohol).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the reaction of toluene with Cl₂ (in the presence of UV light), the reaction is:
questions.optionB: Free radical substitution on the -CH₃ group (giving benzyl chloride)
questions.explanation: Toluene + Cl₂ under UV light (hν): free radical halogenation on the -CH₃ side chain → C₆H₅CH₂Cl (benzyl chloride). With AlCl₃ catalyst (ionic): ring substitution (o- and p-chlorotoluene). UV light drives radical mechanism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Kolbe's electrolytic process is used to prepare:
questions.explanation: Kolbe's synthesis: electrolysis of concentrated aqueous solution of sodium carboxylate. Anode: 2RCOO⁻ → R-R + 2CO₂ + 2e⁻. Example: 2CH₃COO⁻ → C₂H₆ + 2CO₂. Couples two alkyl groups.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The number of π bonds in benzene (C₆H₆) is:
questions.optionB: 3 delocalized π bonds (one delocalized π system)
questions.explanation: Benzene: 6 sp² carbons, each contributes 1 p orbital → 6 p orbitals overlap to form 1 delocalized π system (with 3 pairs of electrons = 6 π electrons). Often drawn as 3 double bonds (Kekulé) but actually 1 delocalized ring.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: What is the functional group in carboxylic acids?
questions.explanation: Carboxylic acids contain -COOH (carboxyl group) = carbonyl (C=O) + hydroxyl (O-H) on same carbon. General formula: R-COOH. Examples: formic acid (HCOOH), acetic acid (CH₃COOH). Stronger acids than phenol due to resonance stabilization of COO⁻.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The hydration of ethylene to ethanol is an example of:
questions.explanation: CH₂=CH₂ + H₂O (H₃PO₄ catalyst, steam, 300°C, 70 atm) → CH₃CH₂OH. This is an addition reaction (H and OH add across the double bond). Industrial method for ethanol production.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Hückel's rule states that an aromatic compound must have:
questions.optionB: 4n + 2 π electrons (where n = 0, 1, 2, ...) in a planar cyclic conjugated system
questions.explanation: Hückel's rule: aromatic if planar, cyclic, fully conjugated (sp² system) with 4n+2 π electrons: 2 (n=0), 6 (n=1), 10 (n=2), 14, etc. Benzene: 6 π e⁻ (n=1). Cyclopentadienyl anion: 6 π e⁻. Cyclooctatetraene: 8 π e⁻ (antiaromatic).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The nucleophilic substitution reaction of alkyl halides (SN2) is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Inversion of configuration (Walden inversion) at the chiral center
questions.explanation: SN2: backside attack by nucleophile → Walden inversion (like umbrella turning inside out). If substrate is chiral, configuration inverts (R → S or S → R). Bimolecular: rate = k[substrate][nucleophile].

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The process of converting vegetable oils to solid fats (ghee/margarine) is:
questions.explanation: Hardening of oils: vegetable oil (unsaturated C=C double bonds) + H₂ (Ni catalyst, 150-200°C) → saturated fat (solid). Converts liquid polyunsaturated oils to solid margarines. All C=C bonds are saturated with H.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of an alkene with O₃ followed by Zn/H₂O is called:
questions.explanation: Ozonolysis: alkene + O₃ → molozonide → ozonide. Reductive workup (Zn/H₂O): gives aldehydes/ketones. Oxidative workup (H₂O₂): gives carboxylic acids. Used to determine position of double bonds.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound formed by the dehydration of two molecules of ethanol is:
questions.explanation: 2CH₃CH₂OH (H₂SO₄, 140°C) → CH₃CH₂-O-CH₂CH₃ + H₂O. Diethyl ether (ethoxyethane). At higher temperature (170°C): CH₃CH₂OH → CH₂=CH₂ + H₂O (elimination gives ethylene).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The physical state of methane (CH₄) at room temperature is:
questions.explanation: Methane (CH₄): bp = -161.5°C, mp = -182°C. Gas at room temperature (25°C). Weak van der Waals forces only. Low molecular weight (16 g/mol). Main component of natural gas.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of sodium acetate with NaOH and CaO (soda-lime) upon heating gives:
questions.explanation: CH₃COONa + NaOH → CH₄ + Na₂CO₃ (heated with CaO, soda-lime). Decarboxylation of carboxylate salt produces alkane. CaO absorbs CO₂ and H₂O, shifting equilibrium. General: RCOONa + NaOH → R-H + Na₂CO₃.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In electrophilic aromatic substitution, NO₂ group is a:
questions.optionA: Ortho/para director and activates the ring
questions.optionB: Meta director and deactivates the ring
questions.optionC: Ortho/para director and deactivates the ring
questions.explanation: NO₂ is meta director (meta deactivating group). NO₂ withdraws electrons from ring (EWG) by induction and resonance (-M effect). Deactivates ring toward EAS. Meta products form because meta position is least destabilized.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of ethyl acetate with NaOH is called:
questions.explanation: CH₃COOC₂H₅ + NaOH → CH₃COONa + C₂H₅OH. Saponification = alkaline hydrolysis of ester. Irreversible (forms sodium carboxylate salt). Acid hydrolysis is reversible (esterification equilibrium).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product of Friedel-Crafts acylation of benzene with CH₃COCl/AlCl₃ is:
questions.explanation: Friedel-Crafts acylation: C₆H₆ + CH₃COCl (AlCl₃) → C₆H₅COCH₃ + HCl. Acetophenone = methyl phenyl ketone. AlCl₃ generates acylium ion CH₃CO⁺ (electrophile). No carbocation rearrangement (advantage over alkylation).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The heat of combustion is always negative (exothermic) for:
questions.optionB: All organic compounds (combustion is always exothermic)
questions.explanation: Combustion of organic compounds: always exothermic (ΔH < 0) because C-H, C-C, C=C bonds are broken and stronger C=O (CO₂) and O-H (H₂O) bonds are formed. Energy released as heat and light.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The boiling point order for isomers of C₅H₁₂ (pentane) is:
questions.explanation: n-pentane (bp 36°C) > isopentane (bp 28°C) > neopentane (bp 9.5°C). Branching decreases bp: less surface area → weaker London dispersion forces → lower bp. n-pentane (unbranched) has highest surface area and bp.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of acetylene (ethyne) with H₂O in the presence of H₂SO₄ and HgSO₄ gives:
questions.explanation: HC≡CH + H₂O (H₂SO₄, HgSO₄ catalyst) → [CH₂=CHOH] (vinyl alcohol, enol) → CH₃CHO (acetaldehyde, keto form). Markovnikov addition: vinyl alcohol rearranges to more stable keto form (keto-enol tautomerism).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Fehling's test is used to distinguish between:
questions.optionA: Aldehyde and ketone (only aldehydes reduce Fehling's, except aromatic)
questions.explanation: Fehling's test: blue Cu²⁺ complex reduced by aldehydes to red Cu₂O precipitate. Aliphatic aldehydes: positive (RCHO + Cu²⁺ → RCOOˉ + Cu₂O↓). Ketones (except α-hydroxy): negative. Aromatic aldehydes: negative.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The stereoisomers of 2-butene (cis and trans) are called:
questions.explanation: cis-2-butene and trans-2-butene are geometric isomers (diastereomers). They have same connectivity but different spatial arrangement. Not mirror images (not enantiomers). cis: same groups on same side. trans: same groups on opposite sides.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Petroleum is mainly composed of:
questions.optionB: A mixture of alkanes, cycloalkanes, and aromatics
questions.explanation: Petroleum (crude oil): complex mixture of hydrocarbons: mainly alkanes (paraffins), cycloalkanes (naphthenes), and aromatics (benzene-type). Refined by fractional distillation into: gasoline, kerosene, diesel, lubricating oil, bitumen, etc.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of propene with HI gives 2-iodopropane as the major product. This follows:
questions.optionB: Markovnikov's rule (H adds to more substituted carbon)
questions.explanation: CH₃CH=CH₂ + HI → CH₃CHICH₃ (2-iodopropane, major). H adds to =CH₂ (more H), I⁻ attacks the more stable secondary carbocation (CH₃CH⁺CH₃ > CH₃CH₂CH₂⁺). Markovnikov product.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The correct IUPAC name for the compound CH₂=CHCH₂CH₃ is:
questions.explanation: CH₂=CH-CH₂-CH₃: longest chain with double bond = 4C (but-); double bond starts at C-1 (= but-1-ene). IUPAC 2013: locant closest to double bond: but-1-ene. Locant before functional group suffix in current IUPAC.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following correctly represents a tertiary (3°) carbon?
questions.optionC: (CH₃)₃CH — the central C bonded to 3 other C atoms
questions.explanation: Classification: primary C (1°) = bonded to 1C; secondary C (2°) = bonded to 2C; tertiary C (3°) = bonded to 3C; quaternary C (4°) = bonded to 4C. In (CH₃)₃CH (isobutane): central C is 3° (bonded to 3 CH₃ carbons).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction: alkene + H₂ → alkane (with Ni/Pt/Pd catalyst) is called:
questions.explanation: Catalytic hydrogenation: adds H₂ across C=C double bond: CH₂=CH₂ + H₂ → CH₃CH₃ (Ni catalyst, 150°C). Also: C≡C + H₂ → C=C (partial, Lindlar catalyst) or C-C (excess H₂). Reduces degree of unsaturation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound 2-methylpropan-2-ol has how many α-hydrogens?
questions.explanation: 2-methylpropan-2-ol = (CH₃)₃COH (tertiary alcohol). The carbon bearing -OH has no H atoms (C bonded to 3 CH₃ and 1 OH). Therefore, zero α-hydrogens. This means it cannot be oxidized by common oxidizing agents and doesn't undergo aldol condensation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The IUPAC suffix for carboxylic acid is:
questions.explanation: IUPAC suffixes: -ol (alcohol), -al (aldehyde), -one (ketone), -oic acid (carboxylic acid), -amine (amine), -ether (ether/alkoxy). Example: CH₃COOH = ethanoic acid (ethan- + -oic acid).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of benzene with chlorine in the presence of Fe or FeCl₃ catalyst gives:
questions.explanation: C₆H₆ + Cl₂ (FeCl₃ catalyst) → C₆H₅Cl + HCl. Electrophilic substitution. FeCl₃ acts as Lewis acid, polarizes Cl₂ to Cl⁺δ-Cl⁻ FeCl₃. Cl⁺δ acts as electrophile attacking benzene ring.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The type of reaction in which a larger organic molecule breaks into smaller molecules by adding water is:
questions.explanation: Hydrolysis: addition of H₂O causes bond cleavage in a molecule. Examples: ester hydrolysis (RCOOR' + H₂O → RCOOH + R'OH), amide hydrolysis, protein hydrolysis (to amino acids), carbohydrate hydrolysis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Ethanol can be distinguished from diethyl ether by:
questions.optionB: Ethanol reacts with Na metal releasing H₂; ether doesn't
questions.optionD: Both react the same way with all reagents
questions.explanation: 2C₂H₅OH + 2Na → 2C₂H₅ONa + H₂↑. Ethanol has -OH (reactive with Na). Diethyl ether (C₂H₅OC₂H₅) has no -OH and doesn't react with Na. Also: ethanol has characteristic -OH infrared absorption (~3300 cm⁻¹).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The nitration of benzene uses:
questions.optionB: A nitrating mixture of conc. HNO₃ + conc. H₂SO₄ (generates NO₂⁺ electrophile)
questions.explanation: Nitration: HNO₃ + H₂SO₄ (conc.) → H₂NO₃⁺ + HSO₄⁻; H₂NO₃⁺ → NO₂⁺ + H₂O. NO₂⁺ (nitronium ion) is the electrophile: C₆H₆ + NO₂⁺ → C₆H₅NO₂ + H⁺.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The number of structural isomers of C₄H₁₀ (butane) is:
questions.explanation: C₄H₁₀ has 2 structural isomers: (1) n-butane: CH₃CH₂CH₂CH₃ (straight chain). (2) Isobutane (2-methylpropane): (CH₃)₃CH (branched). Both have same molecular formula, different connectivity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The catalyst used in the industrial synthesis of methanol from CO and H₂ (syngas) is:
questions.explanation: CO + 2H₂ → CH₃OH (methanol). Catalyst: Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ at 200-300°C, 50-100 atm. Syngas (CO + H₂) from natural gas reforming. Methanol used for MTBE, formaldehyde, fuel.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of calcium carbide with water is used to prepare:
questions.explanation: CaC₂ + 2H₂O → Ca(OH)₂ + HC≡CH↑ (ethyne/acetylene). Used in oxyacetylene welding (HC≡CH burns at ~3500°C with O₂). CaC₂ prepared: CaO + 3C → CaC₂ + CO at ~2000°C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Keto-enol tautomerism is an equilibrium between:
questions.optionB: Keto form (C=O) and enol form (C-OH with adjacent C=C)
questions.optionC: Constitutional isomers with different carbon skeletons
questions.explanation: Keto-enol tautomerism: CH₃COCH₃ ⇌ CH₂=C(OH)CH₃ (enol, ~1%). Interconversion via proton transfer. Acetaldehyde: 94% keto, 6% enol. β-diketones (acetylacetone) have >80% enol. Not true isomerism — can interconvert.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product of dehydration of cyclohexanol (in H₂SO₄) is:
questions.explanation: Cyclohexanol + H₂SO₄ (conc., 170°C) → cyclohexene + H₂O. Elimination (dehydration): -OH leaves with adjacent H. Cyclohexene has one C=C double bond (ring remains intact).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction to convert an alkyl halide to alkene using alcoholic KOH is:
questions.explanation: R-CH₂CH₂-X + KOH (alc.) → R-CH=CH₂ + KX + H₂O. Elimination: alcoholic KOH acts as base, removes β-H. HX is eliminated. Aqueous KOH: substitution (OH⁻ replaces X⁻, SN2).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The boiling point of ethanol (78°C) is much higher than dimethyl ether (−24°C) despite similar molecular mass because:
questions.optionB: Ethanol has intermolecular hydrogen bonding (O-H···O), ether lacks it
questions.explanation: Ethanol (C₂H₅OH): strong hydrogen bonds (O-H···O, 21 kJ/mol) → high bp 78°C. Dimethyl ether (CH₃OCH₃): similar MW (46 vs 46), only dipole-dipole + van der Waals → bp -24°C. H-bonding greatly raises bp.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Kolbe-Schmitt reaction is used to prepare:
questions.optionB: Salicylic acid (2-hydroxybenzoic acid) from sodium phenoxide
questions.explanation: Kolbe-Schmitt: C₆H₅ONa + CO₂ (140°C, 4-7 atm) → sodium salicylate → H⁺ → salicylic acid (2-OH-C₆H₄COOH). Salicylic acid used to make aspirin (acetylation) and as food preservative.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following alkanes has the highest boiling point?
questions.explanation: Boiling points increase with molecular mass (more electrons → stronger London dispersion forces): CH₄ (-161°C) < C₂H₆ (-89°C) < C₃H₈ (-42°C) < C₄H₁₀ (-1°C). n-Butane has highest bp among these four.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Cannizzaro reaction is characteristic of:
questions.explanation: Cannizzaro reaction: 2RCHO (no α-H) + NaOH → RCH₂OH + RCOONa. One aldehyde is oxidized to carboxylate, another is reduced to alcohol. Example: 2HCHO + NaOH → CH₃OH + HCOONa.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of propanone with HCN gives:
questions.explanation: (CH₃)₂CO + HCN → (CH₃)₂C(OH)CN (acetone cyanohydrin = 2-hydroxy-2-methylpropanenitrile). Nucleophilic addition: CN⁻ attacks carbonyl carbon (electrophilic), then protonation. Used to make α-hydroxy acids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The functional group isomers of alcohols (ROH) are:
questions.optionB: Ethers (R-O-R', same molecular formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂O but different connectivity)
questions.explanation: Functional group isomers: same molecular formula but different functional groups. Alcohols (ROH) and ethers (R-O-R') are functional group isomers with formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂O. Example: C₂H₆O: ethanol (CH₃CH₂OH) and dimethyl ether (CH₃OCH₃).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound responsible for the smell of vinegar is:
questions.explanation: Vinegar: 4-8% aqueous acetic acid (ethanoic acid, CH₃COOH). Produced by oxidation of ethanol by Acetobacter bacteria: CH₃CH₂OH + O₂ → CH₃COOH + H₂O. Used as food preservative and condiment.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The monomer of natural rubber (polyisoprene) is:
questions.explanation: Natural rubber: cis-polyisoprene = polymer of isoprene (CH₂=C(CH₃)-CH=CH₂, 2-methylbuta-1,3-diene). Vulcanization (S₈) cross-links chains → increases strength. Gutta-percha = trans-polyisoprene (hard).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Halogenation of alkanes in the presence of UV light proceeds by:
questions.optionB: Free radical chain mechanism (initiation, propagation, termination)
questions.explanation: Free radical halogenation: (1) Initiation: X₂ + hν → 2X•. (2) Propagation: X• + RH → HX + R•; R• + X₂ → RX + X•. (3) Termination: X• + X• → X₂, R• + R• → R₂, R• + X• → RX. Chain mechanism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The conversion of a primary alcohol to a carboxylic acid (direct one-step oxidation equivalent) is done by:
questions.optionC: KMnO₄ in acidic, neutral, or basic conditions (strongest oxidizing agent)
questions.explanation: KMnO₄ (acidified) oxidizes primary alcohol → carboxylic acid: R-CH₂OH → RCOOH. PCC (pyridinium chlorochromate) stops at aldehyde: RCHO. K₂Cr₂O₇ also oxidizes to carboxylic acid. Control of oxidation level depends on reagent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound formed when acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO) reacts with HCN is:
questions.explanation: CH₃CHO + HCN → CH₃CH(OH)CN (lactonitrile, α-hydroxypropionitrile). Nucleophilic addition of CN⁻ to aldehyde carbonyl. Hydrolysis of lactonitrile → lactic acid (2-hydroxypropanoic acid).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Tollens' test uses:
questions.optionB: Ag⁺ in NH₃ solution (ammoniacal silver nitrate), gives silver mirror
questions.explanation: Tollens' reagent: [Ag(NH₃)₂]⁺ (silver-ammonia complex). RCHO + 2[Ag(NH₃)₂]⁺ + 2OH⁻ → RCO₂⁻ + 2Ag(s) + 4NH₃ + H₂O. Silver mirror formed. Positive for aldehydes (except aromatic, which may be slow). Negative for ketones.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The structure of the most stable conformation of cyclohexane is:
questions.optionB: Chair conformation (all bonds staggered, no ring strain)
questions.explanation: Cyclohexane chair: all C-H bonds are staggered (no torsional strain), all bond angles ≈ 109.5° (no angle strain), no transannular interactions. Most stable conformation. Boat and twist-boat are higher energy due to eclipsing/flagpole interactions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound with the molecular formula C₂H₄O₂ that is an acid is:
questions.explanation: C₂H₄O₂: acetic acid (CH₃COOH) is the simplest carboxylic acid with this formula. Methyl formate (HCOOCH₃) has same formula but is an ester. Glycolaldehyde (HOCH₂CHO) has same formula but is a hydroxy aldehyde.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The heat of hydrogenation of benzene is lower than expected for a cyclic triene because:
questions.optionB: Benzene has extra resonance stability (delocalization energy ~150 kJ/mol)
questions.explanation: Expected ΔH of hydrogenation for 3 isolated double bonds: 3 × (-120 kJ/mol) = -360 kJ/mol. Observed for benzene: -209 kJ/mol. Difference = 360 - 209 = 151 kJ/mol = resonance energy (delocalization stabilization).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The term 'Zaitsev's rule' states that in elimination reactions, the major product is:
questions.optionB: The most substituted alkene (thermodynamically more stable)
questions.optionC: The product with most H atoms
questions.explanation: Zaitsev's rule (Saytzeff's rule): in E2 elimination with small bases (EtO⁻, OH⁻), the major product is the more substituted (more stable) alkene. Example: 2-bromobutane + KOH → mainly 2-butene (disubstituted) over 1-butene (monosubstituted).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: When methanol is used as fuel (in methanol fuel cells or direct combustion), the combustion reaction is:
questions.explanation: 2CH₃OH + 3O₂ → 2CO₂ + 4H₂O. Complete combustion of methanol. Methanol (CH₃OH) burns with a blue flame. Used as racing fuel and in methanol fuel cells (direct methanol fuel cell, DMFC).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of ethylene (CH₂=CH₂) with cold, dilute alkaline KMnO₄ (Baeyer's reagent) gives:
questions.explanation: Cold dil. KMnO₄: CH₂=CH₂ + KMnO₄ + H₂O → HOCH₂CH₂OH (ethylene glycol) + MnO₂. Anti-addition of two OH groups (both syn to C=C face). KMnO₄ turns from purple to brown (MnO₂). Used as test for alkene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Grignard reagent is:
questions.optionA: An organic halide dissolved in ether
questions.optionB: Organomagnesium halide (RMgX) — a powerful nucleophile/carbanion equivalent
questions.explanation: Grignard reagent: RMgX (R = alkyl/aryl; X = Cl, Br, I). Prepared: RX + Mg → RMgX (in dry ether). Strong nucleophile (R⁻ character). Reacts with carbonyl compounds to form alcohols, with CO₂ → carboxylic acids, with H₂O → alkane.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following is a primary alcohol?
questions.explanation: 1-propanol (CH₃CH₂CH₂OH): -OH on primary C (bonded to only 1 other C). Secondary alcohol: -OH on 2° C (2-butanol, cyclohexanol). Tertiary alcohol: -OH on 3° C (t-butanol). Classification based on carbon bearing -OH.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Polymerization of ethylene to polyethylene involves:
questions.explanation: Polyethylene: n CH₂=CH₂ → (-CH₂-CH₂-)ₙ. Addition polymerization: double bond opens, chains add without loss of any atoms. No byproduct. Two types: LDPE (free radical, high P) and HDPE (Ziegler-Natta catalyst).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reagent that converts a ketone to an alkane (complete deoxygenation) is:
questions.explanation: Clemmensen: RCOR' + Zn(Hg) + HCl → RCH₂R' (in acidic conditions). Wolff-Kishner: RCOR' + N₂H₄ + KOH → RCH₂R' + N₂ (in basic conditions, Δ). Both reduce C=O to -CH₂- completely.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound formed when acetyl chloride (CH₃COCl) reacts with excess of ethanol is:
questions.explanation: CH₃COCl + C₂H₅OH → CH₃COOC₂H₅ + HCl. Nucleophilic acyl substitution: ethanol (nucleophile) replaces Cl⁻. Ethyl acetate (common solvent). Acid chlorides are more reactive than esters or anhydrides.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The mechanism of dehydration of alcohols to alkenes by H₂SO₄ follows:
questions.optionB: E1 mechanism (via carbocation intermediate for tertiary) or E2 for primary
questions.explanation: Dehydration with H₂SO₄ (conc.): (1) ROH + H⁺ → R-OH₂⁺. (2) R-OH₂⁺ → carbocation R⁺ + H₂O. (3) R⁺ → alkene + H⁺. E1 mechanism. Order of reactivity: 3° > 2° > 1°. H₂SO₄ is proton source and catalyst.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Carbon tetrachloride (CCl₄) has which shape?
questions.optionB: Tetrahedral (sp³, no lone pairs, 4 C-Cl bonds)
questions.explanation: CCl₄: C has 4 bonding pairs, no lone pairs. sp³ hybridization. Tetrahedral shape with bond angles 109.5°. Non-polar (dipole moments of 4 C-Cl bonds cancel by symmetry). Used as solvent (toxic, ozone-depleting).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The acidic strength of carboxylic acids (RCOOH) increases with:
questions.optionA: Increasing size of alkyl group R
questions.optionB: Electron-withdrawing substituents (EWG) like -Cl, -F near the carboxyl group
questions.optionD: Adding more methyl groups to R
questions.explanation: EWG (like Cl, F, NO₂) near COOH stabilize the carboxylate anion RCOO⁻ by inductive effect → increase acid strength. Example: ClCH₂COOH (Ka = 1.36×10⁻³) > CH₃COOH (Ka = 1.8×10⁻⁵). Inductive effect decreases with distance.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction that converts benzene to cyclohexane is:
questions.optionC: Catalytic hydrogenation with Pt/Ni at high temperature
questions.explanation: C₆H₆ + 3H₂ (Pt or Ni, 200°C) → C₆H₁₂ (cyclohexane). Requires 3 moles of H₂ (benzene has 3 degrees of unsaturation). High temperature and pressure needed due to aromaticity stability of benzene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The major product of monochlorination of propane (CH₃CH₂CH₃) is:
questions.explanation: Propane + Cl₂ (hν): 6 equivalent primary H atoms but 2 secondary H. Chlorine selectivity: 2°H reacts ~3.5× faster than 1°H. Probability: 1-chloropropane: (6 × 1)/(6×1 + 2×3.5) = 6/13 = 46%. 2-chloropropane: (2×3.5)/13 = 54% (major).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound that exhibits resonance and delocalization of electrons, making it planar, is:
questions.explanation: Benzene: 6 sp² carbons, all bond angles 120°, planar. 6 p orbitals (one from each C) perpendicular to plane → delocalized π electron cloud (aromatic sextet). Resonance structures (Kekulé) interconvert. Electron delocalization = resonance stabilization.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of methane with steam at high temperature (steam reforming) gives:
questions.explanation: CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ (syngas, steam reforming, 700-1000°C, Ni catalyst). Industrial reaction for H₂ production. Syngas is feedstock for Haber process (N₂ + H₂ → NH₃), Fischer-Tropsch synthesis, and methanol synthesis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: An alkyl group derived from removing one H from a carbon of propane is:
questions.explanation: Propyl: from propane (C₃H₈). Two propyl groups: n-propyl (CH₃CH₂CH₂-, H removed from C-1) and isopropyl ((CH₃)₂CH-, H removed from C-2). The C₃ alkyl groups.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product of heating acetic acid (CH₃COOH) with phosphorus pentachloride (PCl₅) is:
questions.explanation: CH₃COOH + PCl₅ → CH₃COCl + POCl₃ + HCl. PCl₅ converts -OH of carboxylic acid to -Cl, giving acid chloride (acyl chloride). This is a standard way to prepare acid chlorides from carboxylic acids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of propene with water in presence of H₃PO₄ catalyst (direct hydration) gives:
questions.explanation: CH₃CH=CH₂ + H₂O (H₃PO₄, 300°C) → CH₃CHOH-CH₃ (2-propanol). Markovnikov addition: H⁺ adds to =CH₂, OH to =CH-. 2-propanol (isopropanol) is used as rubbing alcohol and solvent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The functional group isomers corresponding to molecular formula C₃H₆O are:
questions.explanation: C₃H₆O (DBE = 1): (1) Propanal (CH₃CH₂CHO) — aldehyde. (2) Propan-2-one/acetone (CH₃COCH₃) — ketone. (3) Allyl alcohol (CH₂=CHCH₂OH) — vinyl alcohol. (4) Cyclopropanol. (5) Methyloxirane (propylene oxide). Multiple isomers!

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Baeyer strain theory explains ring strain in cycloalkanes using:
questions.optionB: Deviation of interior bond angles from the ideal sp³ angle of 109.5°
questions.explanation: Baeyer strain: Small rings: cyclopropane (60°, strain = 49.5°), cyclobutane (90°, strain = 19.5°), cyclopentane (108°, strain = 1.5°), cyclohexane (111°≈ 109.5°, minimal strain). Larger deviation → more strain → less stable.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which compound shows both acidic and basic character (amphoteric) among amino acids?
questions.optionB: Amino acids (H₂N-CHR-COOH) exist as zwitterions, act as both acid and base
questions.explanation: Amino acids: H₂N-CHR-COOH. -NH₂ is basic (accepts H⁺); -COOH is acidic (donates H⁺). In neutral solution, they form zwitterion: ⁺H₃N-CHR-COO⁻. This amphoteric character makes them ideal biological buffers.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The organic compound that acts as an antiseptic when diluted is:
questions.explanation: Phenol (carbolic acid): first antiseptic used by Joseph Lister (1865) for surgical sterilization. Dilute phenol solution kills bacteria by denaturing proteins. Pure phenol is caustic (burns skin). Now replaced by milder antiseptics.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The IUPAC name of isobutylene is:
questions.explanation: Isobutylene (CH₂=C(CH₃)₂): IUPAC = 2-methylprop-1-ene. Longest chain with double bond = 3C (prop-), double bond at C-1, methyl branch at C-2. Used to make isooctane (antiknock), butyl rubber.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) is prepared by the acetylation of:
questions.optionB: Salicylic acid (2-hydroxybenzoic acid) with acetic anhydride
questions.explanation: Aspirin synthesis: Salicylic acid + (CH₃CO)₂O → Aspirin (2-acetoxybenzoic acid) + CH₃COOH. The -OH of salicylic acid is acetylated. Aspirin: analgesic, antipyretic, anti-inflammatory, antiplatelet.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Reppe reaction involves polymerization of acetylene (HC≡CH) to give:
questions.explanation: Reppe's synthesis: 4HC≡CH (Ni catalyst) → C₈H₈ (cyclooctatetraene, COT). COT has 8 π electrons (antiaromatic per Hückel), tub-shaped, non-planar. Separate from true Reppe reactions involving acetylene + CO, H₂O.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of ethyl amine (C₂H₅NH₂) with HCl gives:
questions.explanation: C₂H₅NH₂ + HCl → C₂H₅NH₃⁺Cl⁻ (ethylammonium chloride). Amine (base) accepts H⁺ from HCl to form alkylammonium salt. Reversible with NaOH: C₂H₅NH₃⁺ + NaOH → C₂H₅NH₂ + NaCl + H₂O.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The simplest aromatic hydrocarbon (arene) is:
questions.explanation: Benzene (C₆H₆): simplest aromatic hydrocarbon. Cyclic, planar, conjugated with 6 π electrons (Hückel: 4×1+2 = 6). Discovered by Faraday (1825). Structure (delocalized) proposed by Kekulé (1865). Parent compound of aromatic series.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction used to prepare pure acetylene (ethyne) in the laboratory is:
questions.explanation: Laboratory preparation of acetylene: CaC₂ + 2H₂O → Ca(OH)₂ + HC≡CH↑. The reaction is vigorous. Pure acetylene is collected over water. The gas is used in oxy-acetylene welding (combustion in O₂ at ~3200°C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The acidic nature of terminal alkynes (RC≡CH) is due to:
questions.optionA: The presence of the triple bond
questions.optionB: sp hybridized C-H has more s-character → shorter, stronger bond → more acidic H
questions.optionD: The terminal H being a halide equivalent
questions.explanation: Terminal alkynes (pKa ~25): sp C has more s-character (50%) → electrons held closer to C → C-H bond more electronegative carbon → easier to remove H⁺. Compare: alkane (sp³, 25% s, pKa ~50), alkene (sp², 33% s, pKa ~44), alkyne (sp, 50% s, pKa ~25).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: When acetylene is passed through ammoniacal silver nitrate (Tollens'), the product is:
questions.explanation: HC≡CH + 2[Ag(NH₃)₂]OH → AgC≡CAg↓ (silver acetylide, white/yellow explosive) + 2NH₃ + 2H₂O. This is a test for terminal alkynes. Warning: dry silver acetylide is shock-sensitive explosive!

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The lindlar catalyst is used for:
questions.optionA: Complete hydrogenation of alkyne to alkane
questions.optionB: Partial hydrogenation of alkyne to cis-alkene (syn addition of H₂)
questions.explanation: Lindlar catalyst: Pd on CaCO₃, poisoned with Pb(OAc)₂ and quinoline. Selective: RC≡CR' + H₂ → cis-RCH=CHR' (syn addition, less reactive Pd stops at alkene). Na/liq. NH₃ (Birch reduction) gives trans-alkene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The IUPAC name of CH₃C≡CCH₃ is:
questions.explanation: CH₃C≡CCH₃: 4-carbon chain with triple bond between C-2 and C-3. IUPAC: but-2-yne (or 2-butyne). Number chain to give lowest locant to triple bond. Compare: HC≡C-CH₂CH₃ = but-1-yne.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Nucleophilic aromatic substitution (SNAr) is favored in aromatic rings containing:
questions.explanation: SNAr (addition-elimination): EWG at ortho/para stabilize the anionic Meisenheimer complex (transition state) by delocalizing negative charge. Example: 2,4-dinitrochlorobenzene + NaOH → 2,4-dinitrophenol (fast SNAr due to 2 NO₂ groups).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The major product when bromobenzene (C₆H₅Br) reacts with sodium amide (NaNH₂) in liquid NH₃ is:
questions.optionB: Aniline (via benzyne intermediate, mixture of isomers)
questions.explanation: C₆H₅Br + NaNH₂ (liq. NH₃) → C₆H₄ (benzyne) + NaBr + H → aniline (C₆H₅NH₂). Elimination-addition via benzyne (strained triple bond intermediate). Mixture of aniline isomers if substituted ring used.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Chloroform (CHCl₃) is prepared by the reaction of:
questions.optionB: Ethanol or acetone with Cl₂ in NaOH (haloform reaction)
questions.explanation: Haloform reaction: CH₃CHO or CH₃COCH₃ + 3Cl₂ (NaOH) → CCl₃CHO/CCl₃COCH₃ → CHCl₃ + RCOONa. Or: C₂H₅OH + I₂/NaOH → CHI₃ (iodoform, yellow precipitate, iodoform test). Chloroform was early anesthetic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reactivity of aryl halides toward nucleophilic substitution is much lower than alkyl halides because:
questions.optionB: C-X bond has partial double bond character due to resonance with ring π electrons (ring → C-X delocalization)
questions.explanation: Aryl C-X: Cl lone pairs conjugate with benzene ring → partial double bond C-X character → shorter, stronger bond (C-Cl in C₆H₅Cl = 1.69 Å vs. C-Cl in CH₃Cl = 1.77 Å). Also, sp² C less electrophilic. Very resistant to SN2.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound DDT (dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane) is a:
questions.optionB: Persistent organochlorine insecticide that bioaccumulates in food chains
questions.explanation: DDT (CCl₃CHCl-C₆H₄Cl)₂: very effective insecticide (malaria control), but extremely persistent in environment, not biodegradable, bioaccumulates in fatty tissues up the food chain (biomagnification). Now banned in most countries.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Benzene diazonium chloride (C₆H₅N₂Cl) is formed by the reaction of:
questions.explanation: Diazotization: C₆H₅NH₂ + NaNO₂ + 2HCl (0-5°C) → C₆H₅N₂⁺Cl⁻ + NaCl + 2H₂O. Must keep temperature <5°C to prevent diazonium salt decomposition. Diazonium salts are key intermediates for azo dyes, phenol, etc.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Sandmeyer reaction converts diazonium salt to:
questions.optionB: Aryl halide (ArCl, ArBr, ArCN) using Cu/CuX catalyst
questions.explanation: Sandmeyer reaction: ArN₂⁺ + CuCl → ArCl + N₂ (or CuBr → ArBr, CuCN → ArCN). Converts diazonium salts to aryl halides or nitriles. Key synthetic tool. Gattermann (Cu powder instead of CuX) is similar.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Methyl orange is prepared from diazonium salt by:
questions.explanation: Azo coupling: C₆H₅N₂⁺ + (CH₃)₂N-C₆H₄ → (CH₃)₂N-C₆H₄-N=N-C₆H₄SO₃Na (methyl orange). Azo dyes (-N=N- chromophore). Coupling with activated aromatic compounds (phenols, anilines) at ortho/para positions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of benzyl chloride (C₆H₅CH₂Cl) with NaOH(aq) proceeds by:
questions.optionB: SN2 (benzylic C is primary but activated by resonance)
questions.explanation: Benzyl chloride (C₆H₅CH₂Cl) + NaOH → C₆H₅CH₂OH + NaCl. Benzyl position is reactive via SN1 (forms stable benzyl carbocation C₆H₅CH₂⁺ by resonance) or SN2. Much more reactive than simple primary alkyl chloride.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Gabriel synthesis is used to prepare:
questions.optionB: Pure primary amines (1°) from alkyl halides using potassium phthalimide
questions.explanation: Gabriel synthesis: phthalimide + KOH → K-phthalimide. K-phthalimide + RX → N-alkyl phthalimide. Hydrolysis (H₂NNH₂ or H₃O⁺) → RNH₂ (primary amine) + phthalhydrazide. Advantage: gives only 1° amines (no over-alkylation).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound that gives a white precipitate with AgNO₃ without any catalyst in cold conditions is:
questions.explanation: AgNO₃ test for organic halides: benzyl chloride and allyl chloride give immediate white precipitate (AgCl) in cold without catalyst. They form stable carbocations. Alkyl chlorides react slowly (warm needed). Vinyl/aryl halides: no reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of aniline (C₆H₅NH₂) with excess of methyl iodide (CH₃I) gives:
questions.explanation: Exhaustive methylation of aniline: C₆H₅NH₂ → C₆H₅NHCH₃ → C₆H₅N(CH₃)₂ → C₆H₅N⁺(CH₃)₃ I⁻ (trimethylanilinium iodide). Each methylation uses CH₃I. Quaternary ammonium salt formed.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Chloroform stored in air oxidizes to:
questions.explanation: 2CHCl₃ + O₂ → 2COCl₂ + 2HCl. Chloroform slowly oxidizes in light/air to phosgene (COCl₂) — an extremely toxic gas. Therefore, chloroform is stored in dark bottles with a small amount of ethanol (which neutralizes phosgene).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound formed when benzene is reacted with conc. HNO₃ and conc. H₂SO₄ at 50°C is:
questions.explanation: C₆H₆ + HNO₃ (conc.) + H₂SO₄ (conc.) at 50°C → C₆H₅NO₂ + H₂O. Nitration (EAS). NO₂⁺ electrophile attacks ring. At higher temperature and more HNO₃: further nitration → 1,3-dinitrobenzene, 1,3,5-trinitrobenzene (TNB).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The sulphonation of benzene reverses upon steam treatment because:
questions.optionA: The sulphonic acid group is unstable
questions.optionB: Sulphonation is reversible; steam removes -SO₃H group
questions.explanation: C₆H₆ + SO₃ (oleum) → C₆H₅SO₃H (benzenesulphonic acid). Reverse: C₆H₅SO₃H + H₂O (steam) → C₆H₆ + H₂SO₄. Unlike nitration (irreversible). Sulphonation is used to temporarily block a ring position, then removed.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The structure of TNT (trinitrotoluene) is:
questions.optionB: Toluene with NO₂ groups at 2, 4, 6 positions
questions.explanation: TNT = 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene: toluene with -NO₂ at ortho (2,6) and para (4) positions. An explosive: rapid exothermic decomposition releasing N₂, CO, CO₂. Made by stepwise nitration of toluene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of a diazonium salt with water (warm) gives:
questions.explanation: Diazonium hydrolysis: C₆H₅N₂⁺ + H₂O (warm) → C₆H₅OH + N₂ + H⁺. Used to prepare phenol. N₂ gas is lost (driving force). Kupfer reaction (heating with H₂O or H₃PO₄) also gives phenol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound with IUPAC name 1-bromo-2-chloroethane has the structural formula:
questions.explanation: 1-bromo-2-chloroethane: 2-carbon chain; Br at C-1, Cl at C-2. Structure: Br-CH₂-CH₂-Cl. Number chain: lowest locants to substituents. Not the same as chlorobromomethane (CH₂BrCl, 1 carbon).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following is an electrophile?
questions.explanation: Electrophile: electron-pair acceptor (Lewis acid). BF₃: B has only 6 electrons, electron-deficient, accepts electron pair from nucleophile. NH₃, H₂O, OH⁻ are nucleophiles (donate electron pairs). NO₂⁺, Br⁺, H⁺, carbocations are also electrophiles.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Birch reduction of benzene gives:
questions.explanation: Birch reduction: aromatic + Na or Li metal in liq. NH₃ + alcohol (proton source) → partial reduction. Benzene → 1,4-cyclohexadiene. Substituted benzenes: EWG substituents remain on sp³ C; EDG substituents remain on sp² C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Carbon disulphide (CS₂) reacts with alkali to give:
questions.explanation: CS₂ + 3NaOH → Na₂CS₃ + NaOH → (products depend on conditions). CS₂ + 6NaOH → 2Na₂S + Na₂CO₃ + 3H₂O. CS₂ is a good solvent for sulfur, phosphorus, and in viscose rayon production (forms xanthate with cellulose).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product formed when vinyl chloride (CH₂=CHCl) polymerizes is:
questions.explanation: n CH₂=CHCl → (-CH₂-CHCl-)ₙ (PVC, polyvinyl chloride). Addition polymerization. PVC: used for pipes, packaging, flooring, wires. Rigid PVC (no plasticizer) for pipes; flexible PVC (with plasticizers like phthalates) for flooring.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of hydrogen cyanide with an aldehyde (R-CHO) or ketone (R-CO-R') is an example of:
questions.optionB: Nucleophilic addition (CN⁻ is the nucleophile attacking C=O)
questions.explanation: Nucleophilic addition to C=O: CN⁻ (nucleophile) attacks electrophilic carbonyl carbon, forming alkoxide, then protonation by HCN → cyanohydrin. C=O is polarized (Cδ⁺-Oδ⁻) → C is electrophilic → attacked by nucleophiles.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The degree of unsaturation in naphthalene (C₁₀H₈) is:
questions.explanation: Naphthalene C₁₀H₈: DBE = (2×10 + 2 - 8)/2 = (22-8)/2 = 14/2 = 7. Two fused benzene rings (2 rings = 2 DBE) + 5 double bonds (but delocalized). Total DBE = 7 ✓ (2 rings × 1 + 5 localized π = 7).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Hofmann degradation (Hofmann rearrangement) converts:
questions.optionB: Primary amide (RCONH₂) to primary amine (RNH₂) with loss of CO (via isocyanate)
questions.explanation: Hofmann rearrangement: RCONH₂ + Br₂ + 2NaOH → RNH₂ + Na₂CO₃ + 2NaBr. One carbon is lost (CO₂). Mechanism: N-bromamide → isocyanate (RNCO) → rearrangement → amine. Product has one fewer carbon than amide.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of nitrobenzene with Fe and HCl (or Sn/HCl) gives:
questions.explanation: C₆H₅NO₂ + 3Fe + 7HCl → C₆H₅NH₂·HCl + 3FeCl₂ + 2H₂O. Then: C₆H₅NH₂·HCl + NaOH → aniline + NaCl + H₂O. Industrial: Fe/HCl or electrolytic reduction. Aniline is precursor to dyes, drugs, pesticides.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound formed when aniline is treated with excess Cl₂ water is:
questions.explanation: Aniline + Cl₂(water) → 2,4,6-trichloroaniline↓ (white precipitate). -NH₂ is highly activating (ortho/para), so all three active positions react with Cl₂. No AlCl₃ needed. This is the deactivation test for -NH₂ group.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The difference between arenes and aliphatic hydrocarbons is:
questions.optionA: Arenes contain only C and H
questions.optionB: Arenes contain benzene ring with delocalized 4n+2 π electrons (aromatic system)
questions.optionD: Aliphatic = chain, aromatic = ring (only difference is ring vs. chain)
questions.explanation: Arenes (aromatic): contain benzene ring with delocalized π electrons (Hückel: 4n+2), sp² C, planar. Aliphatic: open chain (or non-aromatic cyclic). The key distinction is aromaticity (special stability, prefers substitution not addition).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The preparation of ethyl bromide from ethanol involves reaction with:
questions.explanation: CH₃CH₂OH + HBr → CH₃CH₂Br + H₂O. Also: 3C₂H₅OH + PBr₃ → 3C₂H₅Br + H₃PO₃. HBr can be generated in situ: NaBr + H₂SO₄(conc.). For secondary/tertiary: simple HX addition. For primary: PBr₃ gives better yield.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Reimer-Tiemann reaction is used to prepare:
questions.explanation: Reimer-Tiemann: phenol + CHCl₃ + NaOH → salicylaldehyde (2-OH-C₆H₄-CHO, major) + 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde (minor). CHCl₃/NaOH generates dichlorocarbene (:CCl₂), which acts as electrophile attacking o-position of phenol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: When chlorobenzene reacts with Mg in dry ether, the product is:
questions.explanation: C₆H₅Cl + Mg (dry ether) → C₆H₅MgCl (phenyl magnesium chloride). Aryl Grignard reagents are prepared similarly to alkyl ones. Used as nucleophiles: react with CO₂ → C₆H₅COOH (benzoic acid), with aldehydes → secondary alcohols, etc.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound formed by the reaction of benzene with conc. H₂SO₄ at 80°C is:
questions.explanation: Sulphonation: C₆H₆ + H₂SO₄ (or SO₃) → C₆H₅SO₃H + H₂O. The electrophile is SO₃ (from oleum) or HSO₄⁻ (from H₂SO₄). Sulphonic acid group (-SO₃H) is meta-directing (if second substitution occurs).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The 1H NMR signal of aromatic protons (ArH) appears at:
questions.optionC: δ 7-8 ppm (downfield due to aromatic ring current)
questions.explanation: Aromatic H NMR chemical shifts: δ 7-8 ppm (benzene: 7.27 ppm). Aromatic ring current causes increased deshielding: ring current creates induced magnetic field that deshields protons in the plane of the ring. Aldehydic H: 9-10 ppm.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product of exhaustive methylation of a cyclic amine (Hofmann exhaustive methylation/Hofmann elimination) is:
questions.optionB: An open-chain alkene (by Hofmann elimination from quaternary ammonium hydroxide)
questions.explanation: Hofmann exhaustive methylation: cyclic amine → exhaustive CH₃I → quaternary ammonium salt → AgOH → quaternary ammonium hydroxide → heat → alkene + trimethylamine + H₂O. Used to determine ring size of cyclic amines (alkaloid structure elucidation).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The acidity of phenol (C₆H₅OH) compared to cyclohexanol is explained by:
questions.optionB: Phenoxide anion (C₆H₅O⁻) is stabilized by resonance delocalization into the ring
questions.optionC: Phenol is a stronger acid due to benzene ring electronegativity
questions.explanation: Phenol (pKa 10): more acidic than cyclohexanol (pKa ~16). C₆H₅O⁻ (phenoxide) is stabilized by resonance — negative charge delocalized into ring (5 resonance structures). Cyclohexanol's anion can't delocalize, so it's less stable → cyclohexanol is weaker acid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The dehydration of 2-methylcyclohexanol by H₂SO₄ mainly gives:
questions.explanation: 2-methylcyclohexanol dehydration: 1-methylcyclohexene (more substituted, trisubstituted = Zaitsev product) is major. Zaitsev's rule predicts more substituted alkene. 3-methylcyclohexene (less substituted) and methylenecyclohexane are minor.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Menshutkin reaction is used to prepare:
questions.optionB: Quaternary ammonium salts from tertiary amines and alkyl halides
questions.explanation: Menshutkin reaction: R₃N + R'X → R₃NR'⁺X⁻ (quaternary ammonium salt). Tertiary amine acts as nucleophile attacking alkyl halide (SN2). Quaternary ammonium salts used as phase-transfer catalysts, antiseptics (cetrimide), and fabric softeners.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Trichloromethane (chloroform, CHCl₃) can be detected by:
questions.optionB: Lassaigne's test for halogens (Na fusion → NaCl, AgCl precipitate) + carbylamine test
questions.explanation: Detection of CHCl₃: (1) Lassaigne's test (Na fusion → NaCl → white AgCl precipitate). (2) Isocyanide/carbylamine test: CHCl₃ + RNH₂ + KOH → RNC (carbylamine, foul smelling gas) — positive for CHCl₃ (haloform test for -CHX₃ group).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The boiling point of halogenoalkanes (alkyl halides, R-X) increases in the order:
questions.explanation: Boiling points of alkyl halides: RF < RCl < RBr < RI (with same alkyl group). Increases down the halogen group: more electrons → stronger London dispersion forces → higher bp. F is smallest, weakest dispersion forces.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of propan-2-ol with excess HI is:
questions.explanation: Propan-2-ol + HI → 2-iodopropane (major, no rearrangement needed). The secondary carbocation formed is already stable. I⁻ attacks the secondary carbocation directly to give 2-iodopropane. No rearrangement needed here.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The number of isomers of dichlorobenzene (C₆H₄Cl₂) is:
questions.explanation: Dichlorobenzene isomers: (1) ortho (1,2-): two Cl on adjacent carbons. (2) meta (1,3-): Cl separated by one C. (3) para (1,4-): Cl on opposite C. Total: 3 isomers. Easily separated by their different melting points (o: -17°C, m: 24.7°C, p: 53.1°C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The test to distinguish between primary, secondary, and tertiary alkyl halides using alcoholic silver nitrate is based on:
questions.optionB: Rate of AgCl precipitate formation (3° > 2° >> 1°, via SN1 carbocation stability)
questions.explanation: AgNO₃ in EtOH test: SN1 mechanism (forms carbocation). Rate 3° > 2° > 1° (based on carbocation stability). 3° alkyl halide: immediate precipitate. 2°: precipitate on warming. 1°: no precipitate or very slow even on heating.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The monomer of Teflon (PTFE, polytetrafluoroethylene) is:
questions.explanation: PTFE (Teflon): n CF₂=CF₂ → (-CF₂-CF₂-)ₙ. Addition polymerization. Properties: very low friction, chemical inertness, heat resistance (mp 327°C), non-stick. Used in cookware coatings, pipes, seals.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The mechanism of electrophilic substitution in benzene involves:
questions.optionA: Direct displacement of H by electrophile in one step
questions.optionB: Formation of sigma complex (arenium ion/Wheland intermediate), then deprotonation
questions.explanation: EAS mechanism: (1) E⁺ attacks benzene π system → sigma complex (Wheland intermediate, arenium ion, carbocation, sp³ at attack site). (2) H⁺ lost (base removes) → rearomatization. Slow step = step 1 (attack). Two-step mechanism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Iodoform (CHI₃) has the structure:
questions.optionB: CH: iodine on all 3 bonds of a methane with one remaining H (CHI₃)
questions.optionC: A yellow solid with antiseptic properties
questions.optionD: Both B and C are correct
questions.explanation: Iodoform (CHI₃): triiodomethane. Structure: H-C(I)₃ — carbon bonded to 1 H and 3 I atoms. Yellow solid (mp 123°C) with distinctive antiseptic smell. Used historically as antiseptic. Iodoform test distinguishes CH₃CO- compounds.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The strongest nucleophile among the following is:
questions.explanation: Nucleophilicity in polar protic solvents: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ > F⁻. Larger, more polarizable I⁻ is better nucleophile (dispersion interactions with the electrophile). In polar aprotic solvents (DMF, DMSO): F⁻ is strongest (no solvation, charge-to-size is important).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Cyclohexane undergoes ring-flip (ring inversion) between:
questions.optionB: Two equivalent chair conformations (axial and equatorial H swap)
questions.explanation: Cyclohexane ring flip: chair₁ (with certain H equatorial) ⇌ chair₂ (same H now axial). In monosubstituted cyclohexane: equatorial conformation more stable (substituent equatorial). Large groups strongly prefer equatorial (less 1,3-diaxial interactions).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: A compound C₃H₇Cl has how many structural isomers?
questions.explanation: C₃H₇Cl (monochloropropane): (1) 1-chloropropane: CH₃CH₂CH₂Cl. (2) 2-chloropropane: CH₃CHClCH₃. Only 2 structural isomers. (Note: no branch possible with 3C chain giving only 2 positions for Cl: C-1 or C-2).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of acetylene with HCl gives vinyl chloride. This is used to make:
questions.explanation: HC≡CH + HCl → CH₂=CHCl (vinyl chloride, by Markovnikov). n CH₂=CHCl → PVC (polyvinyl chloride). PVC is one of the most widely produced synthetic polymers. Used for pipes, packaging, electrical wire insulation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product of the reaction of aniline with benzoyl chloride (C₆H₅COCl) is:
questions.explanation: C₆H₅NH₂ + C₆H₅COCl → C₆H₅CONHC₆H₅ (benzanilide) + HCl. Schotten-Baumann reaction. The amine nitrogen attacks the carbonyl of acid chloride. This is amide bond formation (N-acylation).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The phthalate esters are used as:
questions.optionB: Plasticizers in PVC to make it flexible
questions.explanation: Phthalate esters (di-n-butyl phthalate, dioctyl phthalate = DOP): used as plasticizers in PVC. They insert between PVC chains, reducing intermolecular forces → more flexible plastic. Some are endocrine disruptors (health concerns).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound formed when ethyl iodide reacts with dry silver oxide (Ag₂O) is:
questions.explanation: 2C₂H₅I + Ag₂O (dry) → C₂H₅OC₂H₅ + 2AgI. Diethyl ether formed. Silver oxide acts as a weak base in anhydrous conditions, forming the ether. Wet (moist) Ag₂O + RX → ROH (alcohol).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Balz-Schiemann reaction converts diazonium salt to:
questions.optionB: Aryl fluoride (ArF) via ArN₂BF₄ salt decomposition
questions.explanation: Balz-Schiemann: ArN₂⁺ + HBF₄ → ArN₂⁺BF₄⁻ (stable) → dry heat → ArF + N₂ + BF₃. Used to introduce F into aromatic ring. Difficult by direct means since F₂ is too reactive. Only reliable way to make ArF via diazotization.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The relative reactivity of alkyl halides in SN2 reactions is:
questions.explanation: SN2 reactivity: methyl > 1° > 2° > 3°. Steric hindrance is critical: SN2 requires backside attack. Methyl (no steric hindrance) → fastest. 3° (3 alkyl groups block approach of nucleophile) → slowest/doesn't react. Inverse of SN1 reactivity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of benzaldehyde (C₆H₅CHO) with NaOH (Cannizzaro reaction) gives:
questions.optionB: Benzyl alcohol + sodium benzoate (benzaldehyde has no α-H)
questions.explanation: 2C₆H₅CHO + NaOH → C₆H₅CH₂OH + C₆H₅COONa. Benzaldehyde: no α-H → undergoes Cannizzaro. One benzaldehyde oxidized to benzoate, another reduced to benzyl alcohol. Hydride transfer mechanism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Phenol is more reactive than benzene in electrophilic aromatic substitution because:
questions.optionB: -OH donates electrons to ring by resonance (+M effect), activating ortho/para positions
questions.optionC: Phenol is liquid, benzene is liquid
questions.explanation: -OH on benzene: lone pairs on O are conjugated with ring (p-p resonance). This increases electron density at ortho and para positions → activates ring toward EAS. Phenol reacts with Br₂/H₂O without catalyst (benzene needs AlBr₃).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product when acetylene reacts with 2 moles of HBr is:
questions.explanation: HC≡CH + HBr (1 mol) → CH₂=CHBr (vinyl bromide, Markovnikov). CH₂=CHBr + HBr → CH₃CHBr₂ (1,1-dibromoethane, Markovnikov again). Two Br add to same C following Markovnikov's rule twice.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of diethyl ether (C₂H₅OC₂H₅) with HI gives:
questions.optionB: Ethyl iodide + ethanol (then ethyl iodide from ethanol with HI if excess)
questions.explanation: C₂H₅OC₂H₅ + HI → C₂H₅OH + C₂H₅I. One ether cleavage: I⁻ attacks one carbon, giving ethyl iodide and ethanol. With excess HI: C₂H₅OH + HI → C₂H₅I + H₂O. Final: 2 C₂H₅I + H₂O from one ether molecule.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Picric acid (2,4,6-trinitrophenol) is more acidic than phenol because:
questions.optionA: It has three -NO₂ groups that are electron-withdrawing
questions.optionB: Three -NO₂ groups further stabilize the picrate anion by delocalization
questions.optionC: Picric acid is a phenol with NO₂ groups making it a stronger acid
questions.explanation: Picric acid: EWG (-NO₂) at 2,4,6 positions. (1) Inductive withdrawal of electrons. (2) Resonance: negative charge of picrate anion is delocalized into 3 -NO₂ groups (extra stabilization). All these factors increase acidity over phenol (pKa 10 → picric acid pKa 0.3).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The formation of coloured azo compounds when diazonium salts couple with β-naphthol is used in:
questions.optionB: Dye industry (azo dyes, e.g. Para Red, Sudan dyes)
questions.explanation: Azo coupling: ArN₂⁺ + β-naphthol → brilliant red/orange azo dye. Para Red is the first azo dye made this way (1878). Azo dyes (-N=N- chromophore) constitute >50% of all synthetic dyes. Used in textiles, food (tartrazine), paper.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of Grignard reagent (CH₃MgBr) with formaldehyde (HCHO) gives, after hydrolysis:
questions.explanation: CH₃MgBr + HCHO → CH₃CH₂OMgBr → H₃O⁺ → CH₃CH₂OH (ethanol). Grignard + HCHO → 1° alcohol. + RCHO → 2° alcohol. + RCOR' → 3° alcohol. + CO₂ → carboxylic acid (one more C). This extends carbon chain.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The elimination of HX from a vicinal dihalide (1,2-dihalide) produces:
questions.optionB: Alkyne (if 2 HX are eliminated from vicinal dihalide)
questions.optionD: Both C and then B (if second elimination occurs)
questions.explanation: Vicinal dihalide (RCHXCHxR'): 1 equiv. KOH(alc.) → alkene (HX eliminated). 2 equiv. sodamide (NaNH₂): → alkyne (2 HX eliminated). Alkyne preparation: RCHBrCHBrR' + 2NaNH₂ → RC≡CR' + 2NaBr + 2NH₃.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The carbonyl group (C=O) is:
questions.optionB: Electrophilic at carbon (Cδ⁺-Oδ⁻ due to high O electronegativity) and nucleophilic at oxygen
questions.explanation: C=O bond is polar: Oδ⁻-Cδ⁺. The carbonyl carbon is electrophilic (positive) → attacked by nucleophiles. The oxygen is nucleophilic (negative, lone pairs) → attacks electrophiles (protons, Lewis acids). Both nucleophilic and electrophilic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Fries rearrangement converts a phenol ester to:
questions.optionB: Hydroxy ketone (ortho or para-hydroxyacetophenone) with AlCl₃
questions.explanation: Fries rearrangement: Ar-O-COR (phenol ester) + AlCl₃ → o-hydroxyaryl ketone (major at high T) or p-hydroxyaryl ketone (major at low T). Intramolecular acyl migration from O to ring carbon. e.g., Phenyl acetate → o-hydroxyacetophenone.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The action of an acid (HBr) on styrene (C₆H₅CH=CH₂) according to Markovnikov's rule gives:
questions.explanation: Styrene + HBr: Markovnikov — H adds to =CH₂ (more H), Br to =CH- (fewer H, gives benzylic carbocation C₆H₅CH⁺CH₃ which is stabilized by resonance with phenyl). Product: C₆H₅CHBrCH₃ (1-phenylethyl bromide).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Bouveault-Blanc reduction uses:
questions.optionB: Na metal + ethanol to reduce ester to alcohol
questions.explanation: Bouveault-Blanc reduction: ester + Na + ethanol → primary alcohol. 2RCOOR' + 4Na + 4EtOH → 2RCH₂OH + 2R'OH + 4NaOEt. Historical method before LiAlH₄ was available. Used to reduce unsaturated fatty acid esters.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The condensation of acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO) with itself in the presence of dilute NaOH is called:
questions.explanation: Aldol condensation: 2CH₃CHO + NaOH(dil.) → CH₃CH(OH)CH₂CHO (3-hydroxybutanal, 'aldol'). The α-carbon of one molecule attacks the carbonyl of another. On heating, aldol dehydrates to but-2-enal (crotonaldehyde).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Ritter reaction involves the reaction of:
questions.optionB: Carbocation (from alkene or alcohol) with nitrile to give an amide
questions.explanation: Ritter reaction: alkene/alkene + H₂SO₄ → carbocation; carbocation + RCN → imine (nitrilium ion) → water attack → amide. Used to make N-alkyl amides. Example: isobutylene + H₂SO₄ + CH₃CN → N-tert-butylacetamide.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound that does NOT react with metallic sodium is:
questions.explanation: Sodium reacts with compounds having active H (O-H, N-H bonds): alcohols, carboxylic acids, phenols, primary/secondary amines, terminal alkynes. Ethers have no active H → no reaction with Na. 2ROH + 2Na → 2RONa + H₂↑.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of sodium propanoate (CH₃CH₂COONa) with soda lime gives:
questions.explanation: CH₃CH₂COONa + NaOH(CaO, heat) → C₂H₆ + Na₂CO₃. Decarboxylation: removes COO group from carboxylate. Product is alkane with one fewer carbon. Soda lime = NaOH + CaO.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The IUPAC name of CH₃CH₂CHO is:
questions.explanation: CH₃CH₂CHO: 3-carbon chain with -CHO at end. IUPAC: propanal. The -CHO carbon is always C-1 in IUPAC (it gets lowest possible locant). Common name: propionaldehyde.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The test to identify the aldehyde group (-CHO) in the presence of ketone group (C=O):
questions.optionB: Tollens' test (silver mirror) or Fehling's test (Cu₂O precipitate)
questions.explanation: Aldehydes are reducing sugars (have easily oxidizable C-H in C=O): give Tollens' (Ag mirror) and Fehling's (red Cu₂O). Ketones (no C-H on carbonyl C) don't reduce these reagents. Exception: alpha-hydroxy ketones (fructose) give Fehling's.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product when propan-2-ol is oxidized with acidified K₂Cr₂O₇ is:
questions.explanation: Propan-2-ol (secondary alcohol) + K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄ → propanone (CH₃COCH₃). Secondary alcohols oxidize to ketones. Primary alcohols → aldehydes (mild oxidant) or carboxylic acids (strong oxidant). Tertiary alcohols don't oxidize easily.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Sodium ethoxide (C₂H₅ONa) in ethanol is used in the:
questions.optionB: Williamson synthesis of ethers (reacts with primary alkyl halide)
questions.explanation: Williamson synthesis: RONa + R'X → R-O-R' + NaX. Example: C₂H₅ONa + C₂H₅Br → C₂H₅OC₂H₅ + NaBr. Works best with primary R'X (SN2). Tertiary R'X → elimination with alkoxide base. Best for making asymmetric ethers.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound diethyl ether (C₂H₅OC₂H₅) is used as:
questions.optionB: A solvent and formerly as an inhalation anesthetic
questions.explanation: Diethyl ether: good solvent for organic reactions (dissolves Grignard reagents, organic compounds). Formerly used as inhalation anesthetic (explosive, flammable, causes nausea). Replaced by safer anesthetics (halothane, isoflurane).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product of reaction of formaldehyde with excess Grignard reagent (CH₃MgBr) and subsequent hydrolysis:
questions.optionB: Ethanol (1° alcohol, one C added to 1C formaldehyde)
questions.explanation: CH₃MgBr + HCHO → C₂H₅OMgBr → H₃O⁺ → C₂H₅OH (ethanol). Formaldehyde (HCHO) + RMgBr → primary alcohol with R+1 carbons. Grignard with HCHO always gives 1° alcohol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Adipic acid (hexanedioic acid, HOOC-(CH₂)₄-COOH) reacts with hexamethylenediamine to form:
