questions.explanation: Nylon-6,6: adipic acid (6C diacid) + hexamethylenediamine (6C diamine) → condensation polymerization (amide bond = -CONH- formed, water released). First commercial synthetic fiber (DuPont, 1935). Used for parachutes, stockings, ropes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound formed when methyl alcohol reacts with sodium metal:
questions.explanation: 2CH₃OH + 2Na → 2CH₃ONa + H₂↑. Sodium methoxide (methylate) formation. Alkoxides are strong bases/nucleophiles used in organic synthesis. Compare: ROH + Na → RONa + ½H₂ (general reaction for alcohols with Na).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The percentage of carbon in glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) is approximately:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: When excess acetylene reacts with HCN in the presence of a catalyst, the product formed is:
questions.explanation: HC≡CH + HCN (Cu₂Cl₂ catalyst) → CH₂=CHCN (acrylonitrile, vinyl cyanide). Markovnikov addition. Acrylonitrile polymerizes to acrylic fiber (PAN, polyacrylonitrile): used in Orlon, Acrilan, Dralon.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reagent that converts a primary alcohol to an aldehyde (stopping at aldehyde without further oxidation) is:
questions.explanation: For 1° alcohol to aldehyde (selective): PCC (RCH₂OH → RCHO, stops at aldehyde), or Swern oxidation (oxalyl chloride/DMSO/Et₃N). K₂Cr₂O₇ and KMnO₄ over-oxidize to carboxylic acid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The hydrocarbon that reacts with Br₂ in carbon tetrachloride WITHOUT UV light or catalyst is:
questions.explanation: Alkenes react with Br₂ in CCl₄ (no light, no catalyst) via electrophilic addition: CH₂=CH₂ + Br₂ → BrCH₂CH₂Br (immediate decolorization). Alkanes need UV light for free radical halogenation. Benzene needs Lewis acid catalyst.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reagent used to prepare phenol from benzene diazonium chloride is:
questions.optionB: Warm water (or dilute acid) — hydrolysis releases phenol
questions.explanation: C₆H₅N₂⁺Cl⁻ + H₂O (warm) → C₆H₅OH + N₂↑ + HCl. Diazonium salt hydrolysis. The diazonium group is an excellent leaving group (N₂ gas released). This is the main industrial route from aniline → phenol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Neopentane (2,2-dimethylpropane) has the structure:
questions.optionB: C(CH₃)₄ — central carbon bonded to 4 methyl groups
questions.explanation: Neopentane = 2,2-dimethylpropane: C(CH₃)₄. Central C is quaternary (bonded to 4 other C atoms). Highest degree of branching among C₅ isomers → lowest boiling point (9.5°C) due to least surface area and weakest van der Waals forces.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Wacker process converts ethylene to:
questions.optionB: Acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO) using PdCl₂/CuCl₂ catalyst in O₂
questions.explanation: Wacker oxidation: CH₂=CH₂ + ½O₂ (PdCl₂/CuCl₂ catalyst) → CH₃CHO. Industrial process. Pd²⁺ is reduced to Pd⁰, which is reoxidized to Pd²⁺ by CuCl₂; Cu⁺ reoxidized by O₂. Catalytic cycle.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Dettol (commercial antiseptic) contains:
questions.explanation: Dettol: 4.8% chloroxylenol (a chlorinated phenol) + pine oil + isopropyl alcohol. Chloroxylenol disrupts bacterial cell membranes. Less irritating than pure phenol. Used for skin antisepsis and surface disinfection.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The number of structural isomers of pentane (C₅H₁₂) is:
questions.explanation: C₅H₁₂ has 3 structural isomers: (1) n-pentane (unbranched, bp 36°C). (2) Isopentane (2-methylbutane, one branch, bp 28°C). (3) Neopentane (2,2-dimethylpropane, most branched, bp 9.5°C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of formic acid (HCOOH) with conc. H₂SO₄ gives:
questions.optionB: CO + H₂O (dehydration by H₂SO₄ as dehydrating agent)
questions.explanation: HCOOH + H₂SO₄(conc.) → CO + H₂O. Concentrated H₂SO₄ is a dehydrating agent. HCOOH = H₂O + CO (dehydration). This is how CO is prepared in the lab. Different from other carboxylic acids which don't dehydrate this way.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound that reacts with both acids and bases (amphoteric amino acid in ionic form) is:
questions.explanation: Glycine zwitterion (⁺H₃N-CH₂-COO⁻): acts as acid (⁺NH₃ → NH₂, donates H⁺) or as base (COO⁻ → COOH, accepts H⁺). This amphoteric character is fundamental to protein chemistry and biological buffer systems.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The carbohydrate that gives a positive Fehling's test is:
questions.optionB: Fructose (and all reducing sugars with free hemiacetal/aldehyde group)
questions.explanation: Reducing sugars (positive Fehling's): glucose, fructose, maltose, lactose — have free aldehyde or ketone group that reduces Cu²⁺. Non-reducing: sucrose (no free hemiacetal — glycosidic bond between anomeric carbons of both rings). Starch/cellulose = very slow/negative.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Bakelite is a polymer made from:
questions.explanation: Bakelite: phenol-formaldehyde resin (condensation polymerization). Phenol + HCHO → cross-linked polymer. Properties: thermosetting (hardens permanently), electrical insulator, heat resistant. Used in electrical switches, billiard balls, telephone cases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Perkin condensation involves the reaction of:
questions.optionB: Aromatic aldehyde + acetic anhydride with sodium acetate → α,β-unsaturated acid
questions.explanation: Perkin reaction: ArCHO + (CH₃CO)₂O + CH₃COONa → ArCH=CHCOOH + CH₃COOH. Produces cinnamic acid-type compounds (α,β-unsaturated acids). Example: benzaldehyde + acetic anhydride/NaOAc → cinnamic acid (C₆H₅CH=CHCOOH).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The monomer of Dacron (polyethylene terephthalate, PET) is:
questions.explanation: PET (Dacron, Terylene): terephthalic acid + ethylene glycol → condensation polyester (ester bonds, -H₂O). Used for polyester fiber (Dacron), PET bottles, packaging. Linear polyester.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound that distinguishes alkene from alkane when Br₂ in CCl₄ is used is:
questions.optionB: Alkene immediately decolorizes reddish-brown Br₂ solution in CCl₄ (alkane does not)
questions.explanation: Br₂/CCl₄ test (or Br₂ water): alkene → immediate decolorization (reddish-brown → colorless) by addition: R₂C=CR'₂ + Br₂ → R₂CBr-CBrR'₂. Alkane: no reaction in CCl₄ at room temp (needs UV light for radical halogenation).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The main use of acetylene (ethyne) in industry is:
questions.optionB: Oxy-acetylene welding/cutting, and as chemical feedstock (vinyl chloride, acrylonitrile)
questions.explanation: Acetylene (HC≡CH) industrial uses: (1) Oxy-acetylene welding (burns at ~3200°C with O₂). (2) Chemical feedstock: HC≡CH + HCl → vinyl chloride (→PVC); + HCN → acrylonitrile; + CH₃COOH → vinyl acetate; trimerization → benzene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The primary structure of a protein refers to:
questions.optionB: The sequence of amino acids linked by peptide bonds
questions.explanation: Protein structure levels: (1) Primary = amino acid sequence (peptide bonds). (2) Secondary = α-helix, β-sheet (hydrogen bonds). (3) Tertiary = 3D folding (disulfide bridges, van der Waals, H-bonds). (4) Quaternary = multiple subunit arrangement.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Hydrolysis of a fat (triglyceride) with NaOH gives:
questions.optionB: Soap (sodium salt of fatty acids) + glycerol
questions.explanation: Saponification: fat (glyceryl triester) + 3NaOH → glycerol + 3RCOONa (soap). Soap molecules have hydrophilic -COO⁻ head and hydrophobic R (alkyl) tail → forms micelles → emulsifies oils in water.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound formed when aniline is treated with acetyl chloride (CH₃COCl) is:
questions.explanation: C₆H₅NH₂ + CH₃COCl → C₆H₅NHCOCH₃ (acetanilide, N-phenylacetamide) + HCl. Acetylation of amine (N-acylation). Acetanilide is an analgesic (historical, now replaced). Used to protect -NH₂ in synthesis (less reactive).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The common name of 2-hydroxypropanoic acid is:
questions.explanation: 2-hydroxypropanoic acid = lactic acid (CH₃CH(OH)COOH). Found in sour milk (produced by Lactobacillus from glucose). Also produced in muscles during anaerobic respiration. Has a chiral center — L-lactic acid in biology.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The complete combustion of n-octane in petrol gives:
questions.explanation: Complete combustion of octane: 2C₈H₁₈ + 25O₂ → 16CO₂ + 18H₂O. Highly exothermic (ΔH = -5471 kJ/mol). Octane number measures antiknock resistance in petrol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of a primary amine with chloroform (CHCl₃) and KOH gives a foul-smelling product. This test is called:
questions.explanation: Carbylamine test: RNH₂ + CHCl₃ + 3KOH → RNC (isocyanide/carbylamine) + 3KCl + 3H₂O. Isocyanide has a characteristic foul smell. Positive only for primary amines (1°). Secondary (R₂NH) and tertiary (R₃N) amines give negative test.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The IUPAC name for glycerol (the alcohol component of fats) is:
questions.explanation: Glycerol: HOCH₂CH(OH)CH₂OH. IUPAC: propane-1,2,3-triol. 3-carbon chain with 3 OH groups. Also called glycerin. Component of fats (esterified with 3 fatty acids to form triglyceride).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of acetic acid with ethanol in the presence of H₂SO₄ catalyst is an example of:
questions.explanation: CH₃COOH + C₂H₅OH ⇌ CH₃COOC₂H₅ + H₂O. Fischer esterification: acid-catalyzed, reversible. H₂SO₄ catalyzes and also acts as dehydrating agent (removes water, shifts equilibrium to products). Ethyl acetate is the product.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The hardening of fat by hydrogenation uses which catalyst?
questions.explanation: Hydrogenation of oils (hardening): vegetable oil + H₂ (Ni catalyst, 150-200°C) → saturated solid fat (margarine). C=C double bonds in fatty acid chains are saturated with H. Ni is the most common catalyst for this industrial process.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The polarity of an amide bond (peptide bond) compared to a simple C-N bond is:
questions.optionB: Amide bond has partial double bond character due to N lone pair resonance with C=O
questions.optionD: Amide bond has no polar character
questions.explanation: Amide bond: N lone pairs conjugate with C=O → -C(=O)-N- ↔ -C(-O⁻)=N⁺-. Partial double bond character (bond rotation hindered). This is why peptide bonds are planar and contributes to protein secondary structure (β-sheets, α-helices via H-bonding).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: When methyl amine (CH₃NH₂) is dissolved in water, it forms a:
questions.optionB: Basic solution (pKb ≈ 3.36, stronger base than NH₃)
questions.explanation: CH₃NH₂ + H₂O ⇌ CH₃NH₃⁺ + OH⁻. Methylamine is a stronger base than NH₃ (pKb 3.36 vs. 4.74). Alkyl groups donate electrons to N (+I effect) → more available for protonation. Solution is basic (pH > 7).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound that can exist as cis and trans isomers is:
questions.explanation: Geometric (cis-trans) isomerism requires: C=C bond + each C of C=C must have two DIFFERENT groups. 2-butene: each C has CH₃ and H (different groups). cis: CH₃ same side. trans: CH₃ opposite sides. Ethene (2×H on each C) and isobutylene (2×CH₃ on one C) don't show cis-trans isomerism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: What is the product when aniline reacts with HNO₂ (NaNO₂/HCl) at 0-5°C?
questions.explanation: Diazotization: C₆H₅NH₂ + NaNO₂ + 2HCl → C₆H₅N₂⁺Cl⁻ + NaCl + 2H₂O at 0-5°C. Below 5°C: stable diazonium salt. Above 5°C: decomposes to phenol (hydrolyzes) and then tar. Temperature control is critical.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The test for aromatic amine (like aniline) using FeCl₃ solution gives:
questions.optionB: Violet/blue color (phenols give this, but aniline gives a different color)
questions.optionC: No significant color with FeCl₃ alone (unlike phenols)
questions.explanation: FeCl₃ test: gives colored complexes mainly with phenols (violet/blue) due to phenolate-Fe complexes. Aromatic amines (aniline) may give slight color but not as characteristic. This distinguishes phenol (-OH) from aniline (-NH₂).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In peptide bond formation:
questions.optionA: -COOH group of one amino acid reacts with -NH₂ of another, releasing NH₃
questions.optionB: -COOH of one amino acid reacts with -NH₂ of another, releasing H₂O to form -CO-NH- (amide/peptide bond)
questions.explanation: Peptide bond formation: -COOH + H₂N- → -CO-NH- + H₂O. This amide (peptide) bond links amino acids in polypeptides. Polypeptide chains form proteins. Hydrolysis of peptide bonds (by acid, base, or protease enzymes) gives amino acids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reactivity order of acyl derivatives toward nucleophilic acyl substitution is:
questions.explanation: Reactivity toward nucleophilic substitution at C=O: acid chloride (most reactive) > anhydride > ester > amide (least reactive). Better leaving group = more reactive. Cl⁻ better than RCO₂⁻ better than RO⁻ better than NH₂⁻.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Rayon (artificial silk) is made from:
questions.explanation: Rayon (viscose rayon): cellulose (from wood pulp) is dissolved as cellulose xanthate (with CS₂/NaOH) then regenerated by extrusion into acid bath → regenerated cellulose fibers. Semi-synthetic (chemically processed natural polymer).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction used to synthesize an aldehyde from a carboxylic acid via its acid chloride is:
questions.explanation: Rosenmund reduction: RCOX + H₂ (Pd/BaSO₄ catalyst, poisoned with quinoline/thiourea) → RCHO + HX. The Pd catalyst is deliberately poisoned to prevent over-reduction (RCHO → RCH₂OH). Converts acid chloride to aldehyde selectively.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Wittig reaction converts:
questions.optionB: Aldehyde/ketone + phosphonium ylide → alkene (C=C) and triphenylphosphine oxide
questions.explanation: Wittig: R₂C=O + R'₂C=PPh₃ → R₂C=CR'₂ + Ph₃P=O. Converts C=O to C=C. The phosphonium ylide (Ph₃P=CHR') is prepared from alkyl halide + Ph₃P + n-BuLi. Highly useful for synthesizing alkenes with defined geometry.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of ammonia with excess methyl iodide gives:
questions.optionB: Quaternary ammonium salt (CH₃)₄N⁺I⁻ after exhaustive methylation
questions.explanation: Exhaustive methylation: NH₃ + CH₃I → CH₃NH₂·HI → (CH₃)₂NH·HI → (CH₃)₃N·HI → (CH₃)₄N⁺I⁻. Each step requires CH₃I. Hofmann method for amine synthesis (methylation of NH₃ gives mixture; Gabriel synthesis gives pure 1° amines).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Baeyer-Villiger oxidation converts:
questions.optionB: Ketone to ester (or aldehyde to carboxylic acid)
questions.explanation: Baeyer-Villiger: ketone + peracid (mCPBA, H₂O₂/H₂SO₄) → ester by insertion of O between C and C=O. Cyclohexanone → ε-caprolactone (useful for Nylon-6 precursor). RCOCR' → RCOOCR' (O inserts adjacent to more substituted carbon).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) belongs to the class of:
questions.explanation: Glucose: monosaccharide (single sugar unit), hexose (6C), aldose (aldehyde group). D-glucose = dextrose = blood sugar. Key energy source. Exists as pyranose ring (Fischer cyclization) in solution. α-D-glucose and β-D-glucose are anomers.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Sucrose (table sugar, C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁) is a disaccharide of:
questions.explanation: Sucrose = α-D-glucopyranosyl-β-D-fructofuranoside. Glucose (C1) + fructose (C2) linked glycosidically (α,β-1→2). Non-reducing sugar (both anomeric carbons blocked). Hydrolysis by invertase/sucrase → 'invert sugar' (glucose + fructose).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Hydrolysis of starch gives:
questions.explanation: Starch: polymer of α-D-glucose (amylose = linear α-1,4; amylopectin = branched α-1,6). Complete hydrolysis (H₂SO₄ or amylase enzyme) → glucose. Partial hydrolysis: dextrin, maltose. Cellulose: β-1,4 glucose polymer → can't be digested by humans.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Knoevenagel condensation involves the reaction of:
questions.optionB: Aldehyde + active methylene compound (malonate, acetoacetate) in the presence of base
questions.explanation: Knoevenagel condensation: ArCHO + CH₂(COOR)₂ (amine catalyst) → ArCH=C(COOR)₂ + H₂O. Active methylene compound (flanked by two EWG) reacts with aldehyde. Used in synthesis of cinnamic acid derivatives, coumarins.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product of the Clemmensen reduction of cyclohexanone is:
questions.explanation: Cyclohexanone + Zn(Hg)/conc. HCl → cyclohexane. Clemmensen reduction: C=O → CH₂ (complete deoxygenation). Works in acidic conditions. Wolff-Kishner (N₂H₄/KOH) gives same product in basic conditions. Ring size preserved.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of acetone with NH₂OH gives:
questions.explanation: (CH₃)₂CO + H₂NOH → (CH₃)₂C=NOH (acetoxime) + H₂O. Reaction with hydroxylamine forms oxime (-C=N-OH). With hydrazine (H₂NNH₂): hydrazone (-C=N-NH₂). With semicarbazide: semicarbazone. Used for characterization (sharp melting points).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of cis-2-butene with Br₂ gives:
questions.explanation: Electrophilic addition of Br₂ to cis-2-butene is anti (bromonium ion intermediate). cis-2-butene → anti addition → meso-2,3-dibromobutane (has plane of symmetry). trans-2-butene → anti addition → (±)-2,3-dibromobutane (chiral, racemic mixture).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the Diels-Alder reaction, the diene must be in:
questions.optionB: Cis (s-cis) conformation (both double bonds on same side of single bond)
questions.explanation: Diels-Alder: [4+2] cycloaddition. Diene must be in s-cis conformation (C1=C2-C3=C4 where single bond C2-C3 has both double bonds on same side) for the terminal carbons to overlap with dienophile π system. Locked s-cis dienes (cyclopentadiene) are very reactive.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The organic compound used in the manufacture of nylon-6 is:
questions.explanation: Nylon-6: ring-opening polymerization of caprolactam (ε-caprolactam, 7-membered ring lactam). Initiator opens ring, chain grows: n caprolactam → (-NH-(CH₂)₅-CO-)ₙ. Nylon-6,6: two different monomers (adipic acid + hexamethylenediamine).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reagent 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine (2,4-DNP) is used to test for:
questions.explanation: 2,4-DNP test: RCHO or RCOR' + 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine → yellow/orange precipitate (2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazone). Positive for both aldehydes AND ketones. Derivatives have sharp melting points for identification.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The ester formed when ethanol reacts with formic acid (HCOOH) is:
questions.explanation: HCOOH + C₂H₅OH ⇌ HCOOC₂H₅ (ethyl formate) + H₂O. Formate ester has characteristic fruit-like smell. HCOOC₂H₅ (ethyl formate) vs. CH₃COOC₂H₅ (ethyl acetate, acetic acid + ethanol).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Mannich reaction produces:
questions.optionB: β-amino carbonyl compound (Mannich base) from HCHO + amine + carbonyl with α-H
questions.explanation: Mannich reaction: R-CO-CH₂R' + HCHO + R₂NH → R-CO-CHR'-CH₂NR₂ (Mannich base) + H₂O. Important in synthesis of pharmaceuticals (β-amino carbonyl compounds). Example in aspirin, lidocaine synthesis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product of treating methyl orange with HCl is a change in:
questions.optionB: Color from orange to red (protonation changes the chromophore structure)
questions.explanation: Methyl orange is an acid-base indicator: orange in neutral (pH ~4-pH~7 range) to red in acid (pH < 3.1). Protonation of the azo nitrogen changes the resonance structure of the chromophore → different color absorption.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound formed by the reaction of ethylene with oxygen over silver catalyst is:
questions.explanation: 2CH₂=CH₂ + O₂ (Ag catalyst, 250°C) → 2-ethylene oxide (oxirane). Industrial process. Ethylene oxide is highly reactive (strained 3-membered ring), used to make ethylene glycol (antifreeze), detergents, and polyethylene glycol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The type of polymer in which monomers of same type are linked is:
questions.explanation: Homopolymer: all repeating units are same monomer. Example: polyethylene (-CH₂CH₂-)ₙ from ethylene. Co-polymer: two or more different monomers. Example: SBR rubber (styrene-butadiene rubber). ABS plastic (acrylonitrile-butadiene-styrene).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The oil of wintergreen (methyl salicylate) is the ester formed from:
questions.explanation: Methyl salicylate (oil of wintergreen): salicylic acid (2-hydroxybenzoic acid) + CH₃OH → methyl 2-hydroxybenzoate + H₂O. Used as liniment (topical analgesic) and flavoring. Characteristic wintergreen smell.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of acetaldehyde with excess ethanol and HCl gives:
questions.explanation: CH₃CHO + 2C₂H₅OH (H⁺ catalyst) → CH₃CH(OC₂H₅)₂ (acetal, diethyl acetal) + H₂O. Acetal formation: two steps: (1) hemiacetal (-OH, -OEt on same C) then (2) second alcohol replaces OH. Acetals are stable to base but hydrolyze in acid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The enzyme that catalyzes hydrolysis of proteins is called:
questions.explanation: Enzymes: amylase (starch hydrolysis), lipase (fat hydrolysis), protease/peptidase (protein hydrolysis), sucrase/invertase (sucrose hydrolysis). Stomach pepsin and intestinal trypsin are proteases that break peptide bonds.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The element present in all organic compounds is:
questions.explanation: Organic chemistry is fundamentally the chemistry of carbon compounds. Carbon is present in ALL organic compounds by definition. H is present in almost all (except CCl₄, CF₄ — which are technically organic). N and O may or may not be present.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of benzene with ozone (O₃) followed by hydrolysis is called:
questions.explanation: Benzene ozonolysis: C₆H₆ + 3O₃ → [three ozonides] → glyoxal (OHC-CHO) × 3 on hydrolysis. Historically used by Kekulé to confirm benzene structure. Ring opens to give three 2C aldehydic fragments. Not used synthetically (explosive ozonides).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: When ethanol is passed over heated copper (Cu) at 300°C, the product is:
questions.explanation: C₂H₅OH + CuO (300°C) → CH₃CHO + Cu + H₂O. Cu is oxidized to CuO by air, then CuO oxidizes ethanol to acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO), Cu reduced back. Net dehydrogenation: C₂H₅OH → CH₃CHO + H₂.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Nylon is a polyamide because:
questions.optionA: It contains N in the ring
questions.optionB: The repeating units are linked by amide (-CO-NH-) bonds
questions.optionC: It is made from amines only
questions.optionD: It contains only N and C
questions.explanation: Nylon (polyamide): -NH-(CH₂)ₙ-CO- repeating units linked by amide bonds (-CO-NH-). Nylon-6 (one monomer) and Nylon-6,6 (two monomers) are polyamides. Silk and wool are natural polyamides (proteins).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The most stable free radical is:
questions.explanation: Stability of free radicals: tert (3°) > sec (2°) > primary (1°) > methyl. Hyperconjugation and inductive effects from alkyl groups stabilize the odd electron by electron donation. Similar to carbocation stability order.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Proteins are hydrolyzed by:
questions.optionB: Acid or base at high temperature, or proteolytic enzymes (pepsin, trypsin)
questions.explanation: Protein hydrolysis: acid (6 M HCl, 110°C, 24h) or base (NaOH) breaks all peptide bonds → mixture of amino acids. Enzymatic: pepsin (stomach), trypsin/chymotrypsin (small intestine) cleave specific peptide bonds selectively.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The isoelectric point (pI) of an amino acid is the pH at which:
questions.optionA: The amino acid is fully protonated
questions.optionB: The amino acid has zero net charge (zwitterion form)
questions.optionC: The amino acid is fully deprotonated
questions.explanation: Isoelectric point (pI): pH at which the amino acid has zero net charge (zwitterion, ⁺H₃N-CHR-COO⁻ predominates). pI = (pKa1 + pKa2)/2. At pI, amino acid has minimum solubility and doesn't migrate in electric field. Used in isoelectric focusing.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound that can be used as a biofuel (fermentation product) is:
questions.optionB: Ethanol (from fermentation of sugars by yeast)
questions.explanation: Bioethanol: C₆H₁₂O₆ (glucose) → 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂ (yeast, anaerobic fermentation). Mixed with petrol (E10 = 10% ethanol). Renewable fuel. Brazil: sugarcane ethanol (E100 flex-fuel cars). USA: corn ethanol for E85.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The polymer Kevlar (used in bulletproof vests) is a:
questions.explanation: Kevlar: poly(p-phenylene terephthalamide). Aramid polyamide: -NH-C₆H₄-NH-CO-C₆H₄-CO- repeating unit. Very stiff, high-strength fiber (hydrogen bonding between chains). 5× stronger than steel by weight. Used in bulletproof vests, tires, aerospace.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Benedict's reagent test is used to test for:
questions.optionB: Reducing sugars (glucose, fructose, lactose, maltose) — gives brick-red Cu₂O precipitate
questions.explanation: Benedict's test: Cu²⁺ (alkaline citrate solution) reduced by reducing sugars (aldose or ketose) → Cu₂O (brick-red precipitate). Semiquantitative: green (trace), yellow (low), orange, red (high glucose). Used to detect diabetes (glucose in urine).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of amine with nitrous acid (HNO₂) at room temperature:
questions.optionC: Tertiary → no characteristic reaction (just salt formation with HNO₂)
questions.optionD: All of the above are correct for respective amine types
questions.explanation: With HNO₂: 1° ArNH₂ → stable diazonium ArN₂⁺. 1° aliphatic RNH₂ → unstable diazonium → N₂ + alcohol. 2° amine (R₂NH) → N-nitrosamine (R₂N-NO, yellow oily). 3° amine → no specific reaction. Key test to distinguish amine types.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound known as TNT has the systematic name:
questions.explanation: TNT = 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene: toluene with -NO₂ at C-2, C-4, C-6. Pale yellow solid, explosive. Used in artillery shells, bombs. Less sensitive to shock than RDX (safer to handle). About 1 kilogram of TNT releases ~4.2 MJ.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following is a condensation polymer?
questions.optionC: Nylon-6,6 (formed with release of H₂O as byproduct)
questions.explanation: Condensation polymers: formed by condensation reactions (lose small molecule like H₂O, HCl). Examples: Nylon (amide bonds, -H₂O), polyester (ester bonds, -H₂O), Bakelite (phenol-formaldehyde, -H₂O). Addition polymers (PE, PVC, PS): no byproduct.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The drug paracetamol (acetaminophen) is chemically:
questions.explanation: Paracetamol (acetaminophen): 4'-hydroxyacetanilide = HO-C₆H₄-NHCOCH₃ (4-acetamidophenol). Analgesic and antipyretic (not anti-inflammatory). Safe in recommended doses. Overdose causes liver toxicity (hepatotoxicity via NAPQI formation).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The relationship between starch and cellulose is:
questions.optionB: Both are polymers of glucose but differ in glycosidic linkage (α-1,4 vs β-1,4)
questions.optionC: Starch is from plants, cellulose from animals
questions.optionD: Cellulose is soluble, starch is insoluble
questions.explanation: Starch: α-D-glucose linked by α-1,4 (amylose) and α-1,6 (amylopectin) glycosidic bonds. Cellulose: β-D-glucose linked by β-1,4 bonds. The β-linkage creates a linear, rigid structure (H-bonding between chains) → structural material. α-linkage in starch is more coiled (storage).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of aniline with conc. H₂SO₄ at 180°C gives:
questions.explanation: Sulphonation of aniline: C₆H₅NH₂ + H₂SO₄(conc.) → (heat 180°C) → 4-H₂N-C₆H₄-SO₃H (sulphanilic acid). At 180°C, intramolecular rearrangement of initially formed aniline sulphate → sulphanilic acid (para product). Used in azo dyes, sulpha drugs.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The IUPAC name of diethyl ether (C₂H₅OC₂H₅) is:
questions.explanation: Diethyl ether: IUPAC = ethoxyethane (larger alkyl group = parent ethane; smaller = ethoxy prefix). Also acceptable: 1-ethoxyethane. For unsymmetrical ethers: smaller group becomes alkoxy prefix. CH₃OC₂H₅ = methoxyethane (or ethoxymethane).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Schiemann reaction is used to prepare:
questions.explanation: Schiemann (Balz-Schiemann) reaction: ArN₂⁺Cl⁻ + HBF₄ → ArN₂⁺BF₄⁻ (diazonium tetrafluoroborate) → dry heat → ArF + N₂ + BF₃. One of few reliable methods to introduce F into aromatic ring.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The acid-base indicator phenolphthalein is colorless in acid and:
questions.explanation: Phenolphthalein: colorless in acid (pH < 8.2), pink/magenta in base (pH 8.2-12). Above pH 12: colorless again (trisodium salt). pH range 8.2-10. Structural change: lactone form (colorless) ⇌ open quinone form (pink) at high pH.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction between a Grignard reagent (RMgX) and CO₂ followed by hydrolysis gives:
questions.explanation: RMgX + CO₂ → RCOOMgX → H₃O⁺ → RCOOH. Grignard reacts with CO₂ (nucleophilic addition to C=O of CO₂), then hydrolysis gives carboxylic acid. This adds one carbon to the chain. CH₃MgBr + CO₂ → CH₃COOH (acetic acid).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The mechanism for the conversion of alkyl halide to alcohol using NaOH(aq) is predominantly:
questions.optionB: SN1 for tertiary; SN2 for primary
questions.explanation: RX + NaOH(aq) → ROH + NaX. Mechanism: primary RX → SN2 (OH⁻ attacks backside, one step). Tertiary RX → SN1 (ionizes to carbocation, then OH⁻ attacks). Secondary → both SN1 and SN2 compete. Condition: aqueous NaOH = substitution; alcoholic KOH = elimination.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Reformatsky reaction uses:
questions.optionA: Zn metal + α-bromo ester to form organozinc reagent, which reacts with carbonyl compounds
questions.explanation: Reformatsky reaction: Zn + BrCH₂COOR (α-bromo ester) → organozinc compound → reacts with aldehyde/ketone → β-hydroxy ester. Milder than Grignard (Zn less reactive). Useful when functional group sensitivity is an issue.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The conversion of acetaldehyde to acetic acid using KMnO₄ is an example of:
questions.explanation: CH₃CHO + [O] → CH₃COOH. Aldehydes are oxidized to carboxylic acids (they have C-H on carbonyl carbon which is oxidized). KMnO₄, K₂Cr₂O₇, AgNO₃(NH₃), Cu(OH)₂ can all oxidize aldehydes. Ketones (no C-H on carbonyl) are resistant to mild oxidation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Succinic acid (butanedioic acid) undergoes intramolecular dehydration to form:
questions.explanation: Succinic acid [HOOC(CH₂)₂COOH] heated → succinic anhydride (5-membered cyclic anhydride) + H₂O. Favorable entropy for 5-membered ring. Phthalic acid (1,2-benzene dicarboxylic acid) similarly forms phthalic anhydride.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound formed when butanone (CH₃COC₂H₅) undergoes aldol condensation with NaOH is:
questions.optionB: Complex mixture (two different α-H positions give multiple products)
questions.explanation: Butanone has two types of α-H (next to CH₃ and CH₂). NaOH-catalyzed aldol condensation gives a complex mixture of products due to self-condensation at different α-positions. Mixed aldol with another carbonyl compound also possible. Not as clean as acetaldehyde aldol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound used as antifreeze in automobile radiators is:
questions.explanation: Ethylene glycol (HOCH₂CH₂OH): used as automotive antifreeze (lowers freezing point of water when mixed, -37°C at 50% solution). Also used to make PET plastic. Toxic if ingested. Propylene glycol (less toxic) used in food applications.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Strecker synthesis is used to prepare:
questions.explanation: Strecker synthesis: RCHO + NH₃ + HCN → RCH(NH₂)CN (α-aminonitrile) → hydrolysis → RCH(NH₂)COOH (α-amino acid). Historically important; glycine from formaldehyde, alanine from acetaldehyde. Racemic amino acids formed.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The most common method of industrial production of benzene is:
questions.optionB: Catalytic reforming of petroleum (cyclohexane → benzene by aromatization)
questions.explanation: Industrial benzene: catalytic reforming of petroleum naphtha fraction. Cyclohexane + Pt catalyst → benzene + 3H₂ (aromatization/dehydrogenation). Coal tar (destructive distillation of coal) was the historical source. Toluene → benzene by hydrodealkylation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Ether linkage (-O-) in diethyl ether makes it:
questions.optionB: Relatively inert (doesn't react with Na, dilute acid, dilute base under normal conditions)
questions.explanation: Ethers are relatively inert: no reactive H, O lone pairs weakly basic. Don't react with: Na, dilute HCl, dilute NaOH, KMnO₄. Do react with: HI (cleavage), Lewis acids (complex formation), O₂ slowly (peroxide formation). Used as solvents for Grignard reactions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product formed when propionic acid (propanoic acid) reacts with SOCl₂ is:
questions.explanation: RCOOH + SOCl₂ → RCOCl + SO₂ + HCl. SOCl₂ converts -OH of carboxylic acid to -Cl, giving acid chloride (acyl chloride). Byproducts (SO₂, HCl) are gases, making workup easy. Alternative: PCl₃ or PCl₅.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The biopolymer DNA is composed of:
questions.optionA: Amino acids linked by peptide bonds
questions.optionB: Nucleotides linked by phosphodiester bonds (sugar-phosphate backbone with bases)
questions.optionC: Glucose units linked by glycosidic bonds
questions.explanation: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid): polymer of deoxyribonucleotides. Each nucleotide = deoxyribose sugar + phosphate + nitrogenous base (A, T, G, C). Nucleotides linked by 3'-5' phosphodiester bonds (between 3' OH of one sugar and 5' phosphate of next). Double helix (Watson-Crick model).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of ethylene with H₂SO₄ (conc.) at room temp gives:
questions.explanation: CH₂=CH₂ + H₂SO₄(conc.) → CH₃CH₂OSO₃H (ethyl hydrogen sulphate). Then: CH₃CH₂OSO₃H + H₂O → CH₃CH₂OH + H₂SO₄. This indirect hydration (at 100°C with H₂O) gives ethanol industrially.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The boiling point of carboxylic acids is higher than alcohols of similar molecular weight because:
questions.optionB: Carboxylic acids form dimers through two hydrogen bonds (R-COOH dimer, MW effectively doubled)
questions.explanation: Carboxylic acids: form dimers via two O-H···O=C H-bonds (ring dimer). This effectively doubles the MW → much higher bp than expected. Acetic acid (MW 60, bp 118°C) vs. propanol (MW 60, bp 97°C). The dimer is stable in liquid and gas phase.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound formed when salicylic acid is treated with acetic anhydride is:
questions.explanation: Salicylic acid + (CH₃CO)₂O → CH₃CO-O-C₆H₄-COOH (aspirin, 2-acetoxybenzoic acid) + CH₃COOH. Acetylation of phenolic -OH. Acetic anhydride is preferred over acetic acid (faster, goes to completion). Aspirin synthesis by Felix Hoffmann (Bayer), 1897.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Peptide bond angle and planarity are due to:
questions.optionB: Partial double bond character (resonance: N lone pair delocalized into C=O)
questions.explanation: Peptide bond O=C-N: N's lone pair conjugates with C=O → partial C-N double bond → restricted rotation (rotation barrier ~88 kJ/mol). Peptide bond is planar (atoms in same plane) and rigid. This planarity is fundamental to protein secondary structure (α-helix, β-sheet).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The test used to detect the presence of nitrogen in an organic compound is:
questions.optionA: Lassaigne's test (Na fusion → NaCN → Fe(CN)₆³⁻ → Prussian blue with FeSO₄)
questions.explanation: Lassaigne's test (sodium fusion test): organic compound + Na → NaCN (if N present). NaCN + FeSO₄ → K₃[Fe(CN)₆] → Fe₄[Fe(CN)₆]₃ (Prussian blue) on acidification with HCl. Prussian blue = positive for N. Used as qualitative detection of N, S, halogens.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Cyclohexane cannot undergo addition reactions (like alkenes) because:
questions.optionB: Cyclohexane has only C-C single bonds (σ bonds) and no π bonds to react
questions.explanation: Cyclohexane (C₆H₁₂): cycloalkane with all C-C σ single bonds. No π electrons available for addition reactions. Unlike cyclohexene (has C=C) or benzene (aromatic π system). Cyclohexane undergoes only free radical substitution (like alkanes) in UV light.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following pairs represents an aldehyde and its corresponding primary alcohol?
questions.explanation: Propanal (CH₃CH₂CHO) is the aldehyde corresponding to propan-1-ol (CH₃CH₂CH₂OH). Oxidation of propan-1-ol → propanal (then propanoic acid with stronger oxidant). Reduction of propanal → propan-1-ol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Haloform reaction occurs with methyl ketones (CH₃CO-R) to give:
questions.optionD: All of the above depending on halogen used
questions.explanation: Haloform reaction with X₂/NaOH on CH₃COR: (1) 3X₂ + 3NaOH → trihalomethyl ketone → (2) CX₃⁻ leaves (carbanion) → CHX₃ (haloform). All three halogens work: Cl₂ → CHCl₃, Br₂ → CHBr₃, I₂ → CHI₃ (yellow, iodoform test).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) is an example of:
questions.optionB: Water-soluble vitamin and a good reducing agent/antioxidant
questions.explanation: Vitamin C (L-ascorbic acid, C₆H₈O₆): water-soluble vitamin. Antioxidant (reducing agent): easily oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid. Essential for collagen synthesis. Deficiency → scurvy. Found in citrus fruits, bell peppers. Cannot be synthesized by humans.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound used in the synthesis of penicillin (a β-lactam antibiotic) contains:
questions.optionB: A β-lactam ring (4-membered ring amide) fused to thiazolidine
questions.explanation: Penicillin structure: β-lactam ring (4-membered cyclic amide, strained) fused to a 5-membered thiazolidine ring. The strained β-lactam is the pharmacophore: inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis (cross-linking of peptidoglycan). Bacteria produce β-lactamase to resist.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The difference between thermoplastic and thermosetting polymers is:
questions.optionA: Thermoplastics melt and re-mold on heating; thermosets do not
questions.explanation: Thermoplastics (PE, PVC, nylon): linear/branched chains, soften/melt on heating (can remold), solidify on cooling (recyclable). Thermosets (Bakelite, epoxy, vulcanized rubber): cross-linked 3D network, harden permanently on heating (cannot remold, not recyclable).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Ziegler-Natta catalyst for polymerization of alkenes is:
questions.explanation: Ziegler-Natta catalyst: TiCl₄ + AlEt₃. Coordinates and inserts alkene monomers at Ti site. Produces stereoregular (isotactic, syndiotactic) polymers. Nobel Prize (1963). HDPE (high-density, linear PE) made this way. Also used for polypropylene (isotactic, crystalline).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound responsible for the flavor of vanilla is:
questions.explanation: Vanillin (4-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzaldehyde): the primary component of vanilla bean extract. Commercially synthesized from guaiacol (from eugenol or lignin). Used as flavoring in food, fragrance in cosmetics. First synthesized (1874) by Tiemann and Haarmann.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of diazomethane (CH₂N₂) with carboxylic acid gives:
questions.explanation: RCOOH + CH₂N₂ → RCOOCH₃ + N₂. Diazomethane is an excellent methylating agent; reaction is fast, selective, and mild. Also: inserts into C-H bonds, reacts with ketones to give ring expansion (Arndt-Eistert reaction). N₂ loss drives the reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The sulpha drugs (antibiotics) are derivatives of:
questions.explanation: Sulpha drugs: sulphanilamide derivatives (H₂N-C₆H₄-SO₂-NHR). First class of antibiotics (prontosil, 1932). Act by competitive inhibition of dihydropteroate synthase (bacteria need PABA for folate; sulpha drugs mimic PABA). Examples: sulfamethoxazole, sulfadiazine.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of sodium phenoxide (C₆H₅ONa) with methyl iodide gives:
questions.explanation: C₆H₅ONa + CH₃I → C₆H₅OCH₃ (anisole, methoxybenzene) + NaI. Williamson synthesis of aryl-alkyl ether. Cannot do it in reverse (C₆H₅I + CH₃ONa) because aryl halides resist SN2. Anisole: used as solvent and in perfumes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Beckmann rearrangement converts:
questions.explanation: Beckmann rearrangement: ketoxime (R-CO-R' → oxime → lactam). R-C(=NOH)-R' + H⁺ → → cyclohexanone oxime → ε-caprolactam (used for Nylon-6!). Industrially important. The group anti to -OH migrates.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product when acetylene (C₂H₂) reacts with acetic acid (CH₃COOH) in presence of Hg(II) catalyst is:
questions.explanation: HC≡CH + CH₃COOH (Hg²⁺ catalyst) → CH₂=CH-OOCCH₃ (vinyl acetate). Vinyl acetate polymerizes to PVA (polyvinyl acetate, used in adhesives) or PVAc. Can be hydrolyzed to PVA (polyvinyl alcohol) which is water-soluble.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Camphor is a terpene ketone. Terpenes are organic compounds built from:
questions.explanation: Terpenes: built from isoprene (C₅H₈) units. Monoterpenes = 2 isoprene units (C₁₀): limonene, menthol, camphor. Sesquiterpenes = 3 C₅ (C₁₅). Diterpenes = 4 C₅ (C₂₀): retinol (vitamin A). Triterpenes = 6 C₅: steroids, cholesterol framework.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound used as a standard in bomb calorimetry is:
questions.explanation: Benzoic acid (C₆H₅COOH) is used as primary standard for calibrating bomb calorimeters: precise, stable, easy to purify, well-characterized ΔH°c = -3226.7 kJ/mol. The heat of combustion of unknowns is determined relative to benzoic acid standard.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The 'Oleum' (fuming H₂SO₄) is used in:
questions.optionB: Industrial organic sulphonation, making dyes, drugs, detergents
questions.explanation: Oleum (SO₃ in H₂SO₄): very strong electrophile SO₃ (stronger sulphonation agent than H₂SO₄). Used for sulphonation of benzene rings in detergent manufacture (linear alkylbenzene sulphonates, LAS), dye synthesis (anthraquinone dyes), and drug synthesis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of benzene with conc. H₂SO₄ and conc. HNO₃ forms nitrobenzene, which on reduction gives aniline. The reagent for reduction is:
questions.optionB: Fe + HCl (or Sn + HCl, or Zn + HCl)
questions.explanation: C₆H₅NO₂ + 3Fe + 7HCl → C₆H₅NH₂·HCl + 3FeCl₂ + 2H₂O. Fe in acidic medium (Fe/HCl or Fe/H₂O) is the classical industrial/laboratory method. Then base (NaOH) liberates free aniline from the salt.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Aldehyde functional group can be detected by all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Lucas test uses ZnCl₂ + conc. HCl to test alcohols (distinguishes 1°, 2°, 3°). It does NOT test for aldehydes. Aldehydes test: Tollens' ✓, Fehling's ✓, Benedict's ✓, 2,4-DNP ✓ (but 2,4-DNP is positive for ketones too). Schiff's test also detects aldehydes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound formed by the reaction of benzaldehyde with acetaldehyde in NaOH is:
questions.explanation: Crossed (mixed) aldol: C₆H₅CHO (no α-H, can't be enolized) + CH₃CHO (has α-H, enolizes) + NaOH → C₆H₅CH=CHCHO (cinnamaldehyde, trans) + H₂O. Benzaldehyde with one α-H compound (acetaldehyde) gives selective product.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The test that distinguishes a primary amine from a secondary amine using HNO₂ (at room temperature) is:
questions.optionB: Secondary gives N-nitrosamine (yellow oil); primary gives unstable diazonium (→ N₂ + alcohol)
questions.explanation: HNO₂ + aliphatic 1° amine → unstable alkyldiazonium salt → N₂ evolved rapidly + alcohol/alkene (effervescence). 2° amine + HNO₂ → stable N-nitroso compound (R₂N-NO, yellow oily, carcinogenic). 3° amines → no significant reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Claisen condensation involves:
questions.optionB: Two ester molecules (or one ester with one ketone) in base → β-ketoester
questions.explanation: Claisen condensation: 2CH₃COOC₂H₅ + NaOC₂H₅ → CH₃COCH₂COOC₂H₅ (ethyl acetoacetate) + C₂H₅OH. The α-H of one ester is deprotonated; the resulting carbanion attacks the carbonyl of the second ester, releasing ethoxide. Forms β-keto ester.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Grignard reagent reacts with water to give:
questions.explanation: RMgX + H₂O → R-H + Mg(OH)X. Grignard reagents are destroyed by water (and alcohols, acids). The very basic R⁻ equivalent extracts H from water. This is why Grignard reactions must be carried out in anhydrous conditions (dry ether). CH₃MgBr + H₂O → CH₄ + Mg(OH)Br.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The order of basicity among aromatic amines in water is:
questions.optionB: Triphenylamine < diphenylamine < aniline (phenyl groups decrease lone pair availability)
questions.explanation: Basicity decreases with more phenyl groups: each Ph withdraws electrons from N by resonance (-M), reducing lone pair availability for protonation. Triphenylamine (pKa of conjugate acid ≈ -5, essentially non-basic) < diphenylamine (pKa 0.8) < aniline (pKa 4.6).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction that shows electrophilic addition to alkene with anti-Markovnikov selectivity using peroxide is:
questions.explanation: Radical addition of HBr (peroxide/UV): R• + HBr → RH + Br•. Br• adds to =CH₂ end (less substituted, less steric). Result: anti-Markovnikov (Br goes to =CH₂, H to =CH-). Only HBr shows this (HCl and HI don't because of bond strength/radical stability reasons).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The final product of complete hydrolysis of a tripeptide Ala-Gly-Val is:
questions.optionB: Three amino acids: alanine + glycine + valine
questions.explanation: Ala-Gly-Val: tripeptide of three amino acids linked by 2 peptide bonds. Complete hydrolysis (acid/base/enzyme) breaks both peptide bonds → 3 individual amino acids: L-alanine + glycine + L-valine. Hydrolysis of peptides is the reverse of condensation polymerization.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The halogenation of alkanes at room temperature requires:
questions.optionB: UV light or high temperature (for free radical initiation)
questions.explanation: Halogenation of alkanes (free radical mechanism): initiation requires UV light (hν) to homolytically cleave X₂ → 2X•. Without UV, Cl₂/Br₂ don't react with alkanes at room temperature. The reaction is a chain process: initiation → propagation → termination.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In organic chemistry, an electrophile is a species that:
questions.optionB: Accepts an electron pair (Lewis acid, electron-deficient)
questions.explanation: Electrophile (electros = electron, philos = lover): accepts electron pair from nucleophile. Lewis acid = electron pair acceptor. Examples: H⁺, NO₂⁺, Br⁺, carbocations (R⁺), BF₃, AlCl₃. Nucleophile = electron pair donor (Lewis base).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The conversion of glucose to ethanol by yeast involves:
questions.explanation: Fermentation: C₆H₁₂O₆ + yeast enzymes (at 25-35°C, anaerobic) → 2CH₃CH₂OH + 2CO₂. Zymase (enzyme complex) in yeast. This is the basis of wine, beer, bioethanol production. CO₂ is a byproduct (used for carbonation).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The degree of polymerization (DP) is:
questions.optionA: The number of carbon atoms in the monomer
questions.optionB: The number of repeating units in a polymer chain
questions.explanation: Degree of polymerization (DP or n): number of monomer units (repeating units) in a polymer chain. MW polymer = DP × MW monomer. For polyethylene with DP = 10,000: MW = 10,000 × 28 = 280,000 g/mol. High DP = high molecular weight = high-strength polymer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Acetylene trimerizes in presence of activated charcoal at 600°C to give:
questions.explanation: 3HC≡CH (activated C, 600°C) → C₆H₆ (benzene). Cyclotrimerization. Bergmann (1866): first synthesis of benzene from acetylene. Reppe reaction (with Ni catalyst at 60°C) gives cyclooctatetraene (tetramerization of 4 molecules of acetylene).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The IUPAC name of isopropyl alcohol is:
questions.explanation: Isopropyl alcohol (IPA) = (CH₃)₂CHOH. IUPAC: propan-2-ol. OH on C-2 of propane. Common uses: rubbing alcohol, antiseptic (70% IPA), solvent. More effective antiseptic than ethanol for some bacteria.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The ester ethyl acetate (CH₃COOC₂H₅) is formed from:
questions.explanation: CH₃COOH + C₂H₅OH ⇌ CH₃COOC₂H₅ + H₂O. Fischer esterification with H₂SO₄ catalyst. Ethyl acetate: excellent solvent for paints, lacquers, varnishes, nail polish remover. Fruity smell. BP 77°C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The strongest acid among the following is:
questions.explanation: Acid strength: benzoic acid (pKa 4.2) > acetic acid (pKa 4.75) > phenol (pKa 10) > ethanol (pKa 16). Smaller pKa = stronger acid. Benzoic acid is stronger than acetic acid because the phenyl group withdraws electrons slightly (inductive) stabilizing benzoate anion.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The formation of polyurethane involves the reaction of:
questions.explanation: Polyurethane: diisocyanate (O=C=N-R-N=C=O) + diol (HO-R'-OH) → polyurethane (-NH-CO-O-R'-O-CO-NH-R-)ₙ. No byproduct. Used for foam cushions, insulation, shoe soles, adhesives, coatings.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound formed by the self-condensation of formaldehyde in NaOH (Cannizzaro) is:
questions.optionB: Formaldehyde is special: gives methanol + formate (sodium formate)
questions.explanation: Formaldehyde Cannizzaro: 2HCHO + NaOH → CH₃OH + HCOONa. Formaldehyde (no α-H) undergoes Cannizzaro. One HCHO oxidized to formate, another reduced to methanol. Both products are industrially important.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound that turns moist starch-iodide paper blue is:
questions.explanation: Cl₂ oxidizes I⁻ in starch-iodide paper: Cl₂ + 2KI → 2KCl + I₂. I₂ reacts with starch → blue-black color. Used to test for Cl₂ (and other oxidizing halogens: Br₂, O₃). SO₂ and CO₂ don't oxidize I⁻.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the E/Z nomenclature system for geometric isomers, Z means:
questions.optionA: Zusammen (German: together) — higher priority groups on same side
questions.optionB: Entgegen (German: opposite) — higher priority groups on opposite sides
questions.explanation: E/Z system (CIP rules): Z (Zusammen = together) = higher priority groups on SAME side. E (Entgegen = opposite) = higher priority groups on OPPOSITE sides. Determined by Cahn-Ingold-Prelog priority rules (higher atomic number = higher priority).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Stearic acid (octadecanoic acid, C₁₇H₃₅COOH) is:
questions.optionB: A saturated fatty acid (no C=C) with 18 carbons
questions.explanation: Stearic acid: C₁₈:0 (18 carbons, 0 double bonds). Saturated fatty acid. Found in animal fats (beef, cocoa butter). Solid at room temperature. Compare: oleic acid (C₁₈:1, monounsaturated), linoleic acid (C₁₈:2, polyunsaturated, essential).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The specific rotation [α] of a compound is measured using:
questions.optionB: Polarimeter (plane-polarized light through solution of chiral compound)
questions.explanation: Optical rotation measured by polarimeter: plane-polarized light passes through chiral solution. Enantiomers rotate light equally but in opposite directions: (+) or d (dextrorotatory, clockwise), (-) or l (levorotatory, counterclockwise). [α] = observed angle / (concentration × path length).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which organic reaction forms a new carbon-carbon bond?
questions.optionB: Aldol condensation (enolate adds to carbonyl of another molecule)
questions.optionD: Esterification and saponification are reverse reactions
questions.explanation: C-C bond forming reactions: Aldol condensation ✓, Grignard reaction ✓, Wittig ✓, Diels-Alder ✓, Friedel-Crafts ✓, Claisen ✓. Esterification forms C-O bonds. Saponification breaks ester (hydrolysis). C-C bond formation is key in organic synthesis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound that is both an aldehyde and a ketone is:
questions.optionB: Glucose (has aldehyde at C-1) and fructose (has ketone at C-2)
questions.explanation: A compound with both aldehyde (-CHO) and ketone (C=O) groups: 2-oxopropanal (methylglyoxal: CH₃-CO-CHO), glyoxal (OHC-CHO, both aldehyde). Note: glyoxal has 2 aldehyde groups. Glucose has only aldehyde. Fructose has only ketone.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction between an amine and an acid to form an amide is called:
questions.explanation: R-COOH + H₂N-R' → R-CO-NH-R' + H₂O. Condensation reaction (loses water). Amide (peptide) bond forms between -COOH and -NH₂. Requires activation (acid chloride, anhydride) or high temperature for direct condensation. Key reaction in protein synthesis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reducing sugar that gives a positive iodine test (starch test) is:
questions.optionC: Starch (amylose gives blue-black with I₂, not a sugar reduction test)
questions.explanation: Iodine test (I₂/KI solution): STARCH gives blue-black color (amylose helix traps I₂). Regular reducing sugars (glucose, fructose) don't give blue color with I₂. The starch test and reducing sugar tests are different tests.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The mechanism of SN1 reaction involves:
questions.optionA: Backside attack by nucleophile in one step
questions.optionB: Ionization to carbocation (rate-determining step), then nucleophilic attack
questions.explanation: SN1 mechanism: (1) Rate-determining step: R₃CX → R₃C⁺ + X⁻ (ionization, slow). (2) R₃C⁺ + Nu⁻ → R₃CNu (fast). Unimolecular: rate = k[substrate] only. Carbocation is planar → attack from both faces → racemization. Favored for 3° substrates, polar protic solvents.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Diels-Alder reaction is a:
questions.explanation: Diels-Alder reaction: diene (4π, s-cis) + dienophile (2π, electron-poor, with EWG) → cyclohexene (cyclic product). [4+2] pericyclic cycloaddition. Concerted (no intermediate), stereospecific (syn addition), forms two new C-C bonds. Nobel Prize (Diels and Alder, 1950).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The name of the reaction: RNH₂ + R'COCl → R'CONHR + HCl is:
questions.explanation: N-acylation: amine reacts with acid chloride (or anhydride) at the nitrogen → amide bond formed. Product: RCONHR' (secondary amide). HCl is released (use base like pyridine to neutralize). Key reaction for protecting -NH₂ groups.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The property of benzene by which it is more stable than expected (from 3 localized double bonds) is called:
questions.explanation: Resonance energy (delocalization energy) of benzene: ≈ 150 kJ/mol. Extra stability from π electron delocalization over all 6 carbons. Measured by difference between heat of hydrogenation of 3 isolated C=C (theoretical) and actual benzene. Makes benzene prefer substitution over addition.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound that is both acidic and phenolic is:
questions.optionB: Salicylic acid (2-hydroxybenzoic acid, has both -OH phenol and -COOH)
questions.explanation: Salicylic acid: has both -OH (phenolic) and -COOH (carboxylic acid) groups. pKa of -COOH ≈ 3.0 (stronger than benzoic acid due to intramolecular H-bonding of anion). Used in aspirin synthesis, food preservative (sodium benzoate), and as a keratolytic agent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In photosynthesis, CO₂ and H₂O are converted to:
questions.optionB: Glucose and O₂ (6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂)
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + hν (chlorophyll) → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants use light energy to fix CO₂ into carbohydrates. Reverse of combustion/respiration. Essential for life on Earth (produces O₂, fixes carbon, base of food chains).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The refractive index of a compound is related to:
questions.optionB: Its optical properties and polarizability of electrons
questions.optionD: Only inorganic compounds have refractive index
questions.explanation: Refractive index (n): n = speed of light in vacuum / speed of light in medium. Depends on electron polarizability of the substance. Organic compounds with more π electrons (aromatics) have higher refractive indices. Used in identification of liquids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of dimethylamine ((CH₃)₂NH) with acetyl chloride (CH₃COCl) gives:
questions.explanation: (CH₃)₂NH + CH₃COCl → CH₃CON(CH₃)₂ + HCl. N-acylation of secondary amine with acid chloride. Product: N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMA), a common aprotic polar solvent. Schotten-Baumann reaction with base to neutralize HCl.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The main structural difference between saturated and unsaturated fatty acids is:
questions.optionB: Unsaturated fatty acids have one or more C=C double bonds in the hydrocarbon chain
questions.explanation: Saturated fatty acids (SFA): no C=C double bonds. Example: palmitic (C16:0), stearic (C18:0). Solid at room temperature (animal fats). Unsaturated: one or more C=C bonds. Monounsaturated (MUFA): oleic (C18:1). Polyunsaturated (PUFA): linoleic (C18:2, ω-6). Generally liquid (oils).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Maillard reaction (browning during cooking) involves:
questions.optionB: Reaction between reducing sugars and amino acids (non-enzymatic browning)
questions.explanation: Maillard reaction: reducing sugar (e.g., glucose) + amino acid (e.g., asparagine) → complex mixture of flavor/color compounds (at high temperature). Creates brown color and complex flavors in bread crust, seared meat, coffee, chocolate. Not the same as caramelization (pure sugar browning).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound used to preserve food by inhibiting bacterial growth is:
questions.optionD: All of the above are food preservatives
questions.explanation: Food preservatives: (1) Sodium benzoate (E211): inhibits mold/yeast in acidic foods. (2) Acetic acid/vinegar: acidic conditions inhibit bacteria. (3) Citric acid: lowers pH. (4) Sorbic acid/potassium sorbate. (5) Propionic acid (in bread). All work by lowering pH or directly inhibiting microbial enzymes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The color of iodine solution in organic solvents (CCl₄) is:
questions.optionB: Violet/purple (I₂ solvated by organic solvent without forming I₃⁻)
questions.explanation: I₂ in nonpolar solvents (CCl₄, hexane): violet/purple color (same as I₂ gas). In water: brown/orange (I₃⁻ formed: I₂ + I⁻ → I₃⁻). The color difference helps identify I₂ in different phases (used in solvent extraction demonstrations).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of benzene with SO₃ (oleum) is an example of:
questions.explanation: Benzene + SO₃ → C₆H₅SO₃H (benzenesulphonic acid). SO₃ is the electrophile (S is electron-deficient). EAS mechanism: sigma complex → deprotonation → sulphonic acid. Ring aromaticity preserved (substitution, not addition).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Sommelet reaction converts benzyl halides to:
questions.explanation: Sommelet reaction: ArCH₂X + hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) → Ar-CHO. HMTA is hydrolyzed in the reaction. Used to synthesize aromatic aldehydes from benzyl halides. Alternative to Gattermann-Koch reaction for ArCHO synthesis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: When toluene is treated with KMnO₄ in acidic conditions, the product is:
questions.explanation: Toluene + KMnO₄ (acidic, Δ) → benzoic acid (PhCOOH). KMnO₄ oxidizes the side chain -CH₃ all the way to -COOH. No matter how long the side chain, product is always ArCOOH with benzoic acid if only one chain. Benzaldehyde would require mild oxidant.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The vitamin that is synthesized in human skin on exposure to UV radiation is:
questions.optionB: Vitamin D (calciferol, from 7-dehydrocholesterol by UV photolysis)
questions.explanation: Vitamin D synthesis: UV light (290-315 nm) converts 7-dehydrocholesterol (in skin) to previtamin D₃ → vitamin D₃ (cholecalciferol). Activated in liver and kidney. Deficiency: rickets (children), osteomalacia (adults). Steroid-derived vitamin.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Among the following, the compound with lowest boiling point is:
questions.explanation: For straight-chain alkanes, boiling point increases with molecular weight (more electrons → stronger London dispersion forces). n-butane (C₄) < n-pentane (C₅) < n-hexane (C₆) < n-heptane (C₇). BPs: n-C₄H₁₀: -1°C, n-C₅H₁₂: 36°C, n-C₆H₁₄: 69°C, n-C₇H₁₆: 98°C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product of the reaction of acetyl chloride with water is:
questions.explanation: CH₃COCl + H₂O → CH₃COOH + HCl. Nucleophilic acyl substitution: H₂O attacks electrophilic carbonyl C, Cl⁻ leaves. Very fast, vigorous reaction (fuming in moist air). Acid chlorides are the most reactive acyl compounds toward hydrolysis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound used as tear gas in riot control (CS gas) with functional group:
questions.optionB: A nitrile and a halide (2-chlorobenzalmalononitrile contains CN groups)
questions.explanation: CS gas (2-chlorobenzalmalononitrile, C₁₀H₅ClN₂): contains a benzene ring with Cl and two CN groups. The CN groups and the alkene provide extreme lachrymatory (tear-inducing), burning sensation. Much more potent than older CN gas (chloroacetophenone).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Rubber undergoes vulcanization by treatment with:
questions.explanation: Vulcanization (Goodyear, 1839): natural rubber + S₈ (1-3%) at 150°C → cross-linked rubber. S forms bridges between polyisoprene chains → less sticky, more elastic, stronger, temperature-resistant. More S (20-30%) → hard rubber (ebonite).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The electrophile responsible for nitration of benzene is:
questions.optionB: NO₂⁺ (nitronium ion, generated from HNO₃ + H₂SO₄)
questions.explanation: HNO₃ + H₂SO₄ → H₂NO₃⁺ + HSO₄⁻; H₂NO₃⁺ → NO₂⁺ + H₂O. Nitronium ion (NO₂⁺): the active electrophile in nitration. Linear geometry (sp N). Attacks π electron cloud of benzene ring. Evidence: Raman spectroscopy of HNO₃/H₂SO₄ mixture shows NO₂⁺ peaks.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Peroxide (anti-Markovnikov) addition of HBr to propene gives:
questions.explanation: Anti-Markovnikov addition (free radical, peroxide): Br• adds to the terminal =CH₂ carbon (less substituted, less steric hindrance for Br•). H• then adds to internal carbon. Product: CH₃CH₂CH₂Br (1-bromopropane), opposite to Markovnikov (which gives 2-bromopropane).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Michaelis-Menten equation describes:
questions.explanation: Michaelis-Menten: v = Vmax[S]/(Km + [S]). Describes enzyme catalysis: v = reaction rate, Vmax = maximum rate at substrate saturation, [S] = substrate concentration, Km = Michaelis constant (substrate concentration at half-Vmax). Essential equation in biochemistry for enzyme characterization.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Markovnikov rule applies to the addition of unsymmetrical reagents (like HX) to:
questions.explanation: Markovnikov's rule: for unsymmetrical reagent (HX) adding to unsymmetrical alkene (RCH=CH₂): H adds to the carbon with MORE H atoms (forms more stable carbocation intermediate). Rule applies to HX, H₂O, H₂SO₄ addition to alkenes (also to alkynes).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The dehydrohalogenation of 2-chlorobutane with KOH/alcohol gives mainly:
questions.explanation: 2-chlorobutane + KOH/alcohol (E2): Zaitsev's rule → major product is most substituted alkene = 2-butene (disubstituted, more stable). 1-butene (monosubstituted) is minor product. With bulky base (t-BuOK) → Hofmann product (1-butene) via anti-Zaitsev.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The detergent action of soap depends on:
questions.optionB: Micelle formation (hydrophobic tails toward center, hydrophilic heads outside in water)
questions.explanation: Soap micelles: hydrophilic -COO⁻ head (outward, with water), hydrophobic hydrocarbon tail (inward, away from water). Oil/grease trapped inside micelle. Soap acts as emulsifier. Hard water (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) precipitates soap as insoluble scum (Ca/Mg fatty acid salts).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Carborundum (SiC) is commonly used as an abrasive. Among organic compounds, the hardest natural substance is:
questions.explanation: Diamond: hardest naturally occurring substance (10 on Mohs scale). All sp³ C atoms in tetrahedral 3D network. Each C bonded to 4 other C with strong σ bonds. Used as cutting/drilling tools, jewelry, thermal conductor. Graphite is very soft (lubricant).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction that forms an ether from two different alkoxides and alkyl halide is called:
questions.explanation: Williamson synthesis: R'ONa + RX → R'-O-R + NaX. The alkoxide (nucleophile) displaces halide via SN2. Best with primary RX (no E2 competition). For aryl-alkyl ethers: ArONa + R-X (ArX + R-ONa doesn't work as ArX resists SN2).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The degree of unsaturation (DBE) of aspirin (C₉H₈O₄) is:
questions.explanation: Aspirin C₉H₈O₄: DBE = (2×9 + 2 - 8)/2 = (18+2-8)/2 = 12/2 = 6. Benzene ring (4 DBE) + ester C=O (1 DBE) + carboxylic acid C=O (1 DBE) = 6 total DBE ✓.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Phenyl isocyanide (C₆H₅NC) is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Extremely foul smell; its formation is the basis of the carbylamine test
questions.explanation: Isocyanides (R-NC, also called isonitriles or carbyl amines): extremely foul-smelling (nauseating). Formed in carbylamine test: RNH₂ + CHCl₃ + KOH → RNC + 3KCl + 3H₂O. The foul smell is qualitative evidence of isocyanide formation. Test is positive ONLY for primary amines.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In condensation polymerization, the degree of polymerization increases with:
questions.optionB: Increase in conversion (high extent of reaction, >99% conversion needed for high MW)
questions.explanation: Carothers equation: DP = 1/(1-p) where p = fraction of functional groups reacted (conversion). At p = 0.99: DP = 100. At p = 0.999: DP = 1000. Condensation polymerization needs very high conversion for high MW. Byproduct (water) must be removed to drive equilibrium forward.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound formed when phenol reacts with sodium hydroxide is:
questions.explanation: C₆H₅OH + NaOH → C₆H₅ONa + H₂O. Phenol is a weak acid (pKa 10) — reacts with NaOH (strong base) to form sodium phenoxide (phenolate sodium). Sodium phenoxide is used in Williamson synthesis, Kolbe reaction, and Reimer-Tiemann reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The percentage of nitrogen in urea (NH₂CONH₂) is:
questions.explanation: Urea = CO(NH₂)₂. Molecular formula: CH₄N₂O. MW = 12 + 4 + 28 + 16 = 60 g/mol. N content = 28/60 × 100 = 46.67% ≈ 46.7%. Highest nitrogen content of any common fertilizer, making it cost-effective for agriculture.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The number of sigma (σ) bonds in ethylene (CH₂=CH₂) is:
questions.explanation: Ethylene CH₂=CH₂: σ bonds: C-C (1) + each C has 2 C-H bonds (total 4 C-H bonds) = 5 σ bonds total. Plus 1 π bond. Total bonds: 6 (5σ + 1π). Compare ethane: 7 σ bonds (1 C-C + 6 C-H). Ethyne: 3 σ bonds (1 C-C + 2 C-H) + 2 π bonds.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following is a nucleophile?
questions.explanation: Nucleophile: electron pair donor (Lewis base). NH₃ has lone pair on N → donates to electrophiles. BF₃, AlCl₃, H⁺ are electrophiles (electron pair acceptors). Other nucleophiles: OH⁻, CN⁻, halides (X⁻), H₂O, R-O-R (oxygen lone pairs), RS⁻, carbanions (R⁻).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of propanone with Grignard reagent (CH₃MgBr) gives, after hydrolysis:
questions.explanation: CH₃COCH₃ + CH₃MgBr → (CH₃)₃COMgBr → H₃O⁺ → (CH₃)₃COH (t-butanol, 2-methylpropan-2-ol). Ketone + RMgX → tertiary alcohol. Propanone + CH₃MgBr adds another CH₃ → (CH₃)₃C-OH.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of ethylene oxide with H₂O gives:
questions.explanation: Ethylene oxide + H₂O (acid or base catalyst) → HOCH₂CH₂OH (ethylene glycol). Ring opening of the strained 3-membered ring epoxide. Industrial production of ethylene glycol: ethylene → ethylene oxide (Ag cat.) → + H₂O → glycol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The chemical name of aspirin is:
questions.explanation: Aspirin: 2-acetoxybenzoic acid (or acetylsalicylic acid). Structure: benzene ring with -COOH at C-1 and -OOCCH₃ (acetate ester) at C-2. Compare: paracetamol = 4-acetamidophenol; methyl salicylate = oil of wintergreen.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The oxidation of methanol by CrO₃ or K₂Cr₂O₇ gives:
questions.optionD: Both B and C depending on conditions
questions.explanation: CH₃OH + mild oxidant (limited, PCC) → HCHO (formaldehyde). CH₃OH + strong oxidant (excess K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄) → HCOOH (formic acid, then CO₂ + H₂O). Primary alcohol: 1° → aldehyde (mild) → carboxylic acid (strong). Methanol is special: aldehyde = HCHO, acid = HCOOH.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The monomer unit of cellulose is:
questions.optionB: β-D-glucose linked β-1→4 (inverted glycosidic linkage every other glucose)
questions.explanation: Cellulose: unbranched polymer of β-D-glucose linked by β-1,4-glycosidic bonds. Alternating β-glucose units give linear, ribbon-like chains that hydrogen bond to each other → crystalline structure (cotton, wood). Humans lack cellulase enzyme to digest cellulose.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product of treating aniline with acetic anhydride is:
questions.explanation: C₆H₅NH₂ + (CH₃CO)₂O → C₆H₅NHCOCH₃ (acetanilide) + CH₃COOH. Milder than acetyl chloride (no HCl produced). Acetanilide: used in early analgesics; less reactive than aniline (protects -NH₂ in synthesis). Acetylation of -NH₂ makes it a less reactive (deactivated) substituent for EAS.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Biuret test is used to detect:
questions.optionB: Proteins (and peptides with ≥2 peptide bonds) — blue Cu²⁺ → purple/violet with proteins
questions.explanation: Biuret test: protein + NaOH + CuSO₄(dil.) → purple/violet color. Cu²⁺ coordinates with N and O of peptide bonds in alkaline solution → colored complex. Requires at least 2 peptide bonds (-CO-NH-). Dipeptides barely react; polypeptides give strong positive. Named after biuret (H₂NCONHCONH₂) which gives same reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The IUPAC suffix for a nitrile compound (RC≡N) is:
questions.explanation: Nitriles (R-C≡N): IUPAC suffix '-nitrile' (C of CN counted in main chain) or '-carbonitrile' (CN is a substituent on longest chain). Examples: CH₃CN = ethanenitrile (acetonitrile); C₆H₅CN = benzonitrile (benzenecarbonitrile). Nitriles are reduced to amines or hydrolyzed to carboxylic acids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The saponification value of a fat is:
questions.optionA: The amount of fat in grams
questions.optionB: mg KOH needed to saponify 1 g of fat (indicates average molecular weight of fatty acid)
questions.explanation: Saponification value (SV): mg KOH needed to completely saponify 1 g of fat. High SV = low MW fatty acids (shorter chains). Low SV = high MW fatty acids. SV of coconut oil ≈ 255, castor oil ≈ 180, beef tallow ≈ 195. Used in soap/fat quality analysis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In an SN1 reaction, the intermediate is:
questions.explanation: SN1: involves carbocation intermediate. Step 1 (slow, RDS): R-X → R⁺ + X⁻ (carbocation formed). Step 2 (fast): R⁺ + Nu⁻ → R-Nu. Carbocation is planar (sp² hybridized) → nucleophile attacks from both faces → racemization of chiral center.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The iodine value (iodine number) of a fat measures:
questions.optionB: Degree of unsaturation (g I₂ absorbed per 100 g fat)
questions.explanation: Iodine value: g of I₂ absorbed by 100 g fat. I₂ adds across C=C double bonds. High IV = more unsaturated fat (e.g., linseed oil IV ≈ 180). Low IV = more saturated (e.g., coconut oil IV ≈ 10, beef tallow ≈ 40). Used in fat quality analysis for food and paint industry.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The element that forms the backbone of all organic molecules is:
questions.explanation: Carbon is unique: (1) Tetravalency (forms 4 bonds). (2) Catenation (C-C bonds, forms chains and rings). (3) Multiple bonding (C=C, C≡C). (4) Small size (strong bonds). These properties allow carbon to form millions of diverse compounds — the basis of organic chemistry.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of phenol with formaldehyde (HCHO) and NaOH gives:
questions.explanation: Phenol + HCHO (NaOH catalyst) → resol (prepolymer) → Bakelite (thermoset) by condensation polymerization. Cross-linked 3D polymer (methylene bridges link phenol rings). First synthetic plastic (Bakelmann, 1907). Cannot melt (thermoset). Used in electrical insulators.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound formed by dehydration of two molecules of acetic acid (CH₃COOH) is:
questions.explanation: 2CH₃COOH → (CH₃CO)₂O + H₂O (dehydration). Acetic anhydride: used to acetylate compounds (make acetamides, acetate esters). Reacts rapidly with amines and alcohols. More reactive than acetic acid but less than acetyl chloride. Used in aspirin synthesis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The primary structure of DNA is held together by:
questions.optionB: Covalent phosphodiester bonds between sugar-phosphate backbone
questions.explanation: DNA primary structure: covalent 3'-5' phosphodiester bonds between the 3'-OH of one deoxyribose and the 5'-phosphate of the next. The base sequence (A,T,G,C) constitutes genetic information. Double helix: two strands held by hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs (A-T, G-C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction used to identify a double bond in an organic compound using cold dilute KMnO₄ (Baeyer's reagent) shows:
questions.optionA: Decolorization of KMnO₄ from purple to brown MnO₂
questions.explanation: Baeyer's test: cold, dilute KMnO₄ (purple) is decolorized by alkene → brown MnO₂ precipitate forms. 3R-CH=CH-R' + 2KMnO₄ + 4H₂O → 3R-CH(OH)-CH(OH)-R' + 2MnO₂ + 2KOH. Purple → brown = positive for C=C (or C≡C, or other reducing organic groups).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The main product of catalytic cracking of n-octadecane (C₁₈H₃₈) at 500°C is:
questions.optionB: Mixture of smaller alkanes and alkenes (C₃-C₁₀ range, for petrol)
questions.explanation: Catalytic cracking of large alkanes: C₁₈H₃₈ → C₃H₆ + C₄H₈ + C₄H₁₀ + C₇H₁₄ + ... (complex mixture). Zeolite catalysts at 450-500°C. Goal: produce high-octane gasoline (C₄-C₁₀ range) and alkenes (ethylene, propylene) as chemical feedstocks.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Meisenheimer complex is an intermediate in:
questions.explanation: Meisenheimer complex: anionic sigma complex intermediate in SNAr. Ring carbon loses aromaticity at the site of nucleophilic attack. Stabilized by EWG (NO₂, CN) at ortho/para positions that delocalize the negative charge. The rate-determining step in SNAr.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Neoprene (polychloroprene) rubber is made from:
questions.explanation: Neoprene: addition polymer of chloroprene (2-chloro-1,3-butadiene). Properties: oil/chemical resistant, heat resistant, better than natural rubber for many applications. Used in wetsuits, gaskets, hoses. Introduced by DuPont (1930).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of benzaldehyde with NH₂OH gives:
questions.explanation: C₆H₅CHO + NH₂OH → C₆H₅CH=NOH (benzaldehyde oxime) + H₂O. Condensation of aldehyde with hydroxylamine (H₂NOH) → oxime. Two geometric isomers: syn (Z) and anti (E). Derivatives like oximes, hydrazones, semicarbazones have sharp melting points for compound identification.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Sabatier-Senderens reaction is:
questions.optionB: Catalytic reduction of organic compounds with H₂ over Ni (or Pd, Pt) catalyst
questions.optionC: Synthesis of carboxylic acid from ester
questions.optionD: Production of CO₂ from organic acids
questions.explanation: Sabatier-Senderens: catalytic hydrogenation using H₂ + Ni catalyst. Reduces C=C, C=O, C≡C, ArNO₂, etc. Nobel Prize (Sabatier, 1912). General heterogeneous catalysis: H₂ adsorbs on metal surface, adds to unsaturated bonds. Basis of oil hydrogenation (margarine).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The carbonyl compound that does NOT undergo nucleophilic addition easily is:
questions.explanation: Steric hindrance decreases nucleophilic addition to C=O: bulky groups block attack. Di-tert-butyl ketone: two large t-Bu groups make the carbonyl C very inaccessible → resists addition. Order of reactivity: HCHO > CH₃CHO > acetone > diisopropyl ketone > di-tert-butyl ketone.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The biologically active form of vitamin B₁₂ (cyanocobalamin) contains:
questions.optionB: Cobalt (Co) in a porphyrin-like corrin ring
questions.explanation: Vitamin B₁₂: only vitamin containing cobalt. Corrin ring (similar to porphyrin but not identical). Cobalt is in the center. Essential for DNA synthesis, nerve function. Deficiency → pernicious anemia, nerve damage. Only synthesized by microorganisms; found in animal products.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The organic compound used as a solvent in dry cleaning is:
