questions.explanation: Dry cleaning solvent: tetrachloroethylene (perchloroethylene, PCE, C₂Cl₄). Excellent solvent for fats and oils (cleans clothes without water). Non-flammable. Environmental concern (halogenated solvent, suspected carcinogen). Being replaced by CO₂ or hydrocarbon solvents.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The nitrogen-containing aromatic heterocycle present in DNA bases (adenine, guanine) is:
questions.explanation: Purines (adenine, guanine): bicyclic heterocycle = pyrimidine (6-membered ring with 2N) fused to imidazole (5-membered ring with 2N). 4 N atoms total. Pyrimidines (cytosine, thymine, uracil): single 6-membered ring with 2N atoms.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reagent used to determine the degree of unsaturation in fats (iodine value test) is:
questions.optionB: Wijs solution (ICl in acetic acid) or iodine monochloride solution
questions.explanation: Iodine value determination: Wijs reagent (ICl in CHCl₃/AcOH) or Hanus reagent (IBr) adds to C=C double bonds. Excess reagent back-titrated with Na₂S₂O₃. Calculation: IV = (moles ICl reacted × 254.8) / (grams fat × 100). Higher IV = more unsaturation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Kjeldahl method is used to determine:
questions.optionB: Nitrogen content of organic compounds (via NH₃ distillation and titration)
questions.explanation: Kjeldahl method: organic compound digested with H₂SO₄ (catalyst) → converts all N to NH₄⁺. Then NaOH added → NH₃ distilled → collected in HCl (or H₃BO₃) → back-titrated. Widely used for protein N in food analysis. %protein = %N × 6.25 (average factor).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Dumas method measures:
questions.optionA: Nitrogen in organic compounds (by combustion to N₂, measured volumetrically)
questions.explanation: Dumas method: organic compound + CuO (excess) → CO₂ + H₂O + N₂ (at high temp). N₂ collected over KOH (absorbs CO₂) → volume measured → N% calculated. Alternative to Kjeldahl. Used for all types of N compounds (including nitro, azo compounds that Kjeldahl may underestimate).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Carius method is used to determine:
questions.optionC: Halogens and sulphur in organic compounds
questions.explanation: Carius method: organic compound heated with fuming HNO₃ (in sealed tube/carius tube). C → CO₂, H → H₂O, halogens → AgX (weighed as precipitate with AgNO₃), S → H₂SO₄ → BaSO₄. Used for quantitative determination of halogens and sulfur.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The maximum number of structural isomers for C₃H₇Br is:
questions.explanation: C₃H₇Br: propane chain with Br. (1) 1-bromopropane: Br on C-1. (2) 2-bromopropane: Br on C-2. Only 2 structural isomers (no branching possible for 3-C chain giving additional positional isomers for monobromo substitution).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Nitrobenzene is reduced to aniline by iron filings in HCl because:
questions.optionA: Fe reacts with NO₂ group directly
questions.optionB: Fe acts as reducing agent in acidic medium; HCl provides H⁺ for the reduction
questions.optionC: HCl is the actual reducing agent
questions.explanation: Fe + 2HCl → FeCl₂ + H₂ (nascent H). Nascent H reduces NO₂ to NH₂: C₆H₅NO₂ + 6[H] → C₆H₅NH₂ + 2H₂O. Fe is oxidized (Fe → Fe²⁺), H⁺ provides protons. Alternatively: industrial process uses H₂/Cu catalyst.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The product formed when ether (R-O-R) is treated with HI is:
questions.optionB: Alkyl iodide + alcohol (and with excess HI, both alcohols → alkyl iodide)
questions.explanation: R-O-R' + HI → RI + R'OH (at low temp). With excess HI: R'OH + HI → R'I + H₂O. Overall: R-O-R' + 2HI → RI + R'I + H₂O. HI (strongest nucleophile HX) is most reactive for ether cleavage. For t-butyl ethers: SN1; for primary: SN2.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound that can act as both nucleophile and electrophile depending on conditions is:
questions.explanation: α,β-unsaturated carbonyl (e.g., CH₂=CHCHO): (1) 1,2-addition: Nu attacks C=O (carbonyl C, electrophile). (2) 1,4-addition (Michael addition): Nu attacks β-carbon (conjugate addition). Hard nucleophiles attack 1,2; soft nucleophiles prefer 1,4 (HSAB theory).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the phrase 'constitutional isomers', these isomers have:
questions.optionB: Same molecular formula but different connectivity (different order of bonds)
questions.optionC: Same connectivity but different spatial arrangement
questions.explanation: Constitutional isomers (structural isomers): same molecular formula, different structural formulas (different connectivity). Examples: C₂H₆O: ethanol (CH₃CH₂OH) vs. dimethyl ether (CH₃OCH₃). C₄H₁₀: n-butane vs. isobutane. Contrast: stereoisomers (same connectivity, different 3D arrangement).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which compound has the highest octane number?
questions.explanation: Octane scale: n-heptane = 0 (worst knocking), isooctane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane) = 100 (best antiknock). Branched and aromatic hydrocarbons have high octane numbers. Modern petrol (88-95 octane) is a blend to achieve target performance.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Clemmensen reduction is performed under:
questions.explanation: Clemmensen reduction: ketone/aldehyde + Zn(Hg)/conc. HCl → alkane (C=O → CH₂). Acidic aqueous conditions. Used when substrate is stable in acid. Complement: Wolff-Kishner (N₂H₄/KOH, basic conditions). Together, both provide C=O → CH₂ reduction under different conditions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Fürst-Plattner (diaxial opening) rule applies to:
questions.optionB: Ring opening of cyclohexene epoxides — nucleophile attacks to give diaxial trans product
questions.explanation: Fürst-Plattner (anti-diaxial) rule: cyclohexene oxide (epoxide) ring opening by nucleophile gives trans-diaxial product (both O and Nu in axial positions). Lowest energy transition state. Applies to conformationally constrained cyclic systems.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In organic nomenclature, the prefix for the substituent -CH₂CH₃ is:
questions.explanation: Alkyl groups (R-): methyl (CH₃-), ethyl (CH₃CH₂- or C₂H₅-), propyl (C₃H₇-), butyl (C₄H₉-). The prefix for -CH₂CH₃ (2 carbons) is 'ethyl'. Named by removing one H from the corresponding alkane: ethane → ethyl.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of carboxylic acid with SOCl₂ is used to prepare:
questions.explanation: RCOOH + SOCl₂ → RCOCl + SO₂ + HCl. SOCl₂ (thionyl chloride) converts -OH of RCOOH to -Cl. Gaseous byproducts (SO₂, HCl) make purification easy. Acid chlorides are highly reactive acylating agents. Alternatively: PCl₃ or PCl₅.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Ninhydrin test for amino acids gives:
questions.optionA: Blue color with all carboxylic acids
questions.optionB: Purple (Ruhemann's purple) color with α-amino acids; yellow with proline
questions.explanation: Ninhydrin test: ninhydrin (triketohydrindane) + α-amino acid → Ruhemann's purple (diketohydrindylidene-diketohydrindamine). Used for amino acid detection after paper/TLC chromatography. Proline (secondary amine) gives yellow-orange. Purple = primary amine + carbonyl = purple chromophore.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Fischer projection is used to represent:
questions.optionB: Spatial arrangement of chiral centers (stereochemistry) in cyclic compounds
questions.optionC: Stereochemistry of acyclic molecules with chiral centers (horizontal bonds toward viewer, vertical bonds away)
questions.explanation: Fischer projection: chiral center drawn as cross. Horizontal bonds: toward viewer (wedge bonds). Vertical bonds: away from viewer (dash bonds). Used for amino acids, sugars. Established by Emil Fischer for sugar stereochemistry. D/L nomenclature based on Fischer projection.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compound with the molecular formula C₃H₄O₂ that undergoes polymerization is:
questions.explanation: C₃H₄O₂: acrylic acid (CH₂=CHCOOH, DBE = 2: 1 C=C + 1 C=O). Addition polymerization of acrylic acid → poly(acrylic acid) (superabsorbent polymer in diapers after crosslinking). Also: methyl acrylate CH₂=CHCOOCH₃ (C₄H₆O₂).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following is the correct statement about the conformations of ethane?
questions.optionB: Staggered conformation is more stable by 12 kJ/mol (torsional strain in eclipsed)
questions.explanation: Ethane conformations: staggered (H-H dihedral 60°) = more stable; eclipsed (H-H dihedral 0°) = less stable by 12 kJ/mol. Torsional strain in eclipsed: electron cloud repulsion between eclipsed H atoms. Conversion between conformers is rapid (low barrier), equilibrium strongly favors staggered.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The nucleophilic addition to imines (Schiff bases, R₂C=NR') in mild acid gives:
questions.optionB: Amine (reductive amination product after NaBH₄/H₂ reduction)
questions.explanation: Reductive amination: RCHO + R'NH₂ → imine (Schiff base, R-CH=NR') → [NaBH₄ or H₂/Pd reduction] → R-CH₂-NHR' (secondary amine). Key route to secondary amines. Widely used in pharmaceutical synthesis. The imine is formed reversibly and the amine reduced irreversibly.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the Meerwein-Pondorf-Verley reduction:
questions.optionB: A ketone/aldehyde is reduced by aluminum isopropoxide (via hydride transfer from isopropanol)
questions.optionC: An alkene is reduced by H₂/Pd
questions.optionD: A carboxylic acid is reduced by LiAlH₄
questions.explanation: Meerwein-Ponndorf-Verley (MPV) reduction: R₂C=O + Al(OiPr)₃ → R₂CHOH + acetone. Mild, selective for C=O (leaves C=C intact). Aluminum alkoxide acts as hydride donor. Oppenauer oxidation is the reverse. Useful for sensitive substrates.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Schiff base formed between benzaldehyde and aniline is called:
questions.explanation: Benzaldehyde + aniline (condensation) → C₆H₅CH=N-C₆H₅ (N-benzylideneaniline, a Schiff base, imine) + H₂O. Schiff bases (imines, R₂C=NR') are formed from primary amines + carbonyl compounds. Used in metal complexes, organic synthesis, tests for carbonyl groups.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The last step in the Haber process involves:
questions.optionB: Separation of ammonia by liquefaction from unreacted N₂ and H₂ (recycled)
questions.explanation: Haber process: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ (Fe catalyst, 450-500°C, 150-300 atm, only ~15% conversion per pass). NH₃ is separated by cooling and compression (liquefy) from unreacted gases. Unreacted N₂/H₂ are recycled back to reactor. This recycling is essential for economic operation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the SN2 reaction, the nucleophile attacks from:
questions.optionA: The same side as the leaving group
questions.optionB: The opposite side of the leaving group
questions.optionD: The top face of the molecule
questions.explanation: SN2 involves backside attack, causing Walden inversion (inversion of configuration).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following best describes the SN1 reaction?
questions.explanation: SN1 is first-order, proceeds via a planar carbocation intermediate, giving racemization.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The E2 elimination reaction requires:
questions.optionA: Anti-periplanar arrangement of H and leaving group
questions.explanation: E2 is concerted and requires anti-periplanar (180°) geometry for H and LG.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Saytzeff's rule states that elimination preferentially gives:
questions.explanation: Saytzeff (Zaitsev) rule: the major product of elimination is the more substituted alkene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Hofmann elimination gives:
questions.explanation: Hofmann elimination (from quaternary ammonium salts) gives the least substituted alkene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which reaction converts a primary amide to a primary amine with one fewer carbon?
questions.explanation: Hofmann rearrangement: RCONH2 + Br2 + NaOH → RNH2 (loss of one carbon).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Beckmann rearrangement converts an oxime to:
questions.explanation: Beckmann rearrangement converts a ketoxime to an amide under acidic conditions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Curtius rearrangement involves the thermal decomposition of:
questions.explanation: Curtius: acyl azide (RCONs) → isocyanate (R-N=C=O) on heating, then hydrolysis gives amine.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Baeyer-Villiger oxidation converts a ketone to:
questions.explanation: Baeyer-Villiger oxidation uses peracids to insert an oxygen into C–C bond of ketone, giving ester.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In Baeyer-Villiger oxidation, which group migrates preferentially?
questions.explanation: Migration aptitude in B-V: tertiary > secondary > aryl > primary > methyl.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Wittig reaction converts a carbonyl compound to:
questions.explanation: Wittig: R2C=O + Ph3P=CR2 → alkene + Ph3P=O via an oxaphosphetane intermediate.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Stabilized ylides in Wittig reaction give predominantly:
questions.explanation: Stabilized ylides (e.g., Ph3P=CHCO2Et) give predominantly E-alkenes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which reaction forms a β-hydroxy carbonyl compound?
questions.explanation: Aldol reaction: enolate + carbonyl → β-hydroxy carbonyl (aldol product).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Claisen condensation produces:
questions.optionA: β-keto ester from two ester molecules
questions.explanation: Claisen condensation: 2 ester molecules → β-keto ester + ROH, catalyzed by alkoxide.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Dieckmann condensation is an intramolecular:
questions.explanation: Dieckmann condensation is an intramolecular Claisen condensation of diethyl esters to form cyclic β-keto esters.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Michael addition is the conjugate addition of a nucleophile to:
questions.explanation: Michael addition: soft nucleophile (Michael donor) adds 1,4 to α,β-unsaturated carbonyl (Michael acceptor).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Mannich reaction produces a:
questions.explanation: Mannich reaction: carbonyl compound + formaldehyde + amine → β-amino carbonyl (Mannich base).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Perkin reaction condenses an aromatic aldehyde with:
questions.optionA: Acetic anhydride in presence of sodium acetate
questions.optionD: Malonic ester in the presence of base
questions.explanation: Perkin reaction: ArCHO + (CH3CO)2O + CH3COONa → α,β-unsaturated arylacrylic acid (cinnamic acid from benzaldehyde).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Knoevenagel condensation involves the reaction of active methylene compounds with:
questions.explanation: Knoevenagel: active methylene compound (e.g., malonic acid) + ArCHO → α,β-unsaturated product.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Reformatsky reaction uses zinc to generate an organozinc enolate from:
questions.explanation: Reformatsky: α-bromo ester + Zn → organozinc enolate; reacts with aldehyde/ketone to give β-hydroxy ester.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Grignard reagent (RMgX) reacts with CO2 followed by hydrolysis to give:
questions.explanation: RMgX + CO2 → RCOOMgX → RCOOH after hydrolysis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Grignard reagent reacts with ethylene oxide to give (after hydrolysis):
questions.optionA: Primary alcohol with two more carbons
questions.explanation: RMgX + CH2–CH2(O) → R-CH2-CH2-OH (primary alcohol, chain extended by 2 carbons).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In IR spectroscopy, the carbonyl (C=O) stretching frequency typically appears at:
questions.explanation: C=O stretch: ~1700–1850 cm⁻¹ for aldehydes/ketones; acids ~1710, esters ~1735 cm⁻¹.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: A broad O–H stretch in the 2500–3300 cm⁻¹ region of IR spectrum indicates:
questions.explanation: Carboxylic acids show a broad O–H stretch from ~2500–3300 cm⁻¹ due to strong hydrogen bonding.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In ¹H NMR, the chemical shift (δ) of aldehydic proton (CHO) is approximately:
questions.explanation: Aldehydic proton appears far downfield at δ 9–10 ppm due to deshielding by C=O and anisotropy.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In ¹H NMR, splitting pattern of a proton with n equivalent neighboring protons follows:
questions.explanation: First-order NMR: n equivalent neighboring H's split the signal into (n+1) lines (n+1 rule).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In mass spectrometry, the molecular ion peak (M⁺) represents:
questions.optionB: The unfragmented molecule minus one electron
questions.explanation: M⁺ is the radical cation formed when the molecule loses one electron; its m/z equals the molecular mass.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The base peak in a mass spectrum corresponds to:
questions.explanation: The base peak is the most intense peak (100% relative abundance) in the mass spectrum.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following is an example of a pericyclic reaction?
questions.explanation: Pericyclic reactions occur through cyclic transition states; Diels-Alder is a [4+2] cycloaddition.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Diels-Alder reaction is a [4+2] cycloaddition between:
questions.explanation: Diels-Alder: conjugated diene (4π) + dienophile (2π) → cyclohexene adduct via concerted mechanism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The endo product in Diels-Alder reaction is kinetically preferred due to:
questions.explanation: Endo approach has secondary orbital interactions between diene and substituents on dienophile, stabilizing TS.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Sigmatropic rearrangements are classified by:
questions.explanation: Sigmatropic: [i,j] where i,j are positions of sigma bond migration in the π system.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Cope rearrangement involves a [3,3]-sigmatropic shift in:
questions.explanation: Cope rearrangement: 1,5-hexadiene undergoes [3,3]-sigmatropic shift to give another 1,5-diene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Claisen rearrangement is a [3,3]-sigmatropic shift of:
questions.explanation: Claisen rearrangement: allyl vinyl ether → γ,δ-unsaturated carbonyl via [3,3]-sigmatropic shift.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Fries rearrangement converts a phenolic ester to:
questions.explanation: Fries rearrangement: ArOCOR + AlCl3 → o- or p-hydroxyaryl ketone.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Wolff-Kishner reduction converts a carbonyl group to:
questions.explanation: Wolff-Kishner: carbonyl + hydrazine + KOH/heat → methylene + N2. Used for base-stable substrates.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Clemmensen reduction converts a carbonyl to methylene using:
questions.explanation: Clemmensen: carbonyl + Zn(Hg)/HCl → methylene. Acidic conditions; complementary to Wolff-Kishner.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Meerwein-Ponndorf-Verley (MPV) reduction uses:
questions.optionA: Aluminum alkoxide as hydride source via cyclic TS
questions.explanation: MPV reduction: Al(OiPr)3 transfers hydride from isopropoxide to carbonyl via 6-membered transition state.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Birch reduction of benzene uses:
questions.optionA: Na in liquid NH3 with alcohol
questions.explanation: Birch reduction: dissolved metal reduction (Na/NH3/ROH) gives 1,4-cyclohexadiene from benzene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Ozonolysis of an alkene followed by reductive workup (DMS or Zn) gives:
questions.explanation: Reductive ozonolysis: alkene → ozonide → aldehydes/ketones. Oxidative workup → carboxylic acids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Syn-dihydroxylation of alkenes is achieved using:
questions.explanation: OsO4 or cold dilute KMnO4 give cis-diols by syn addition of two OH groups.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: mCPBA (meta-chloroperoxybenzoic acid) converts an alkene to:
questions.explanation: mCPBA is a peracid that epoxidizes alkenes via butterfly transition state with retention of geometry.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Sharpless epoxidation uses titanium tartrate catalyst and is used for:
questions.explanation: Sharpless: Ti(OiPr)4 + tartrate ester + TBHP → chiral epoxide from allylic alcohol (Nobel Prize 2001).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: R and S configuration in stereochemistry is determined by:
questions.explanation: R/S absolute configuration is assigned by CIP rules: assign priorities, view from back of lowest priority.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: A meso compound is:
questions.optionA: Optically inactive despite having stereocenters due to internal plane of symmetry
questions.explanation: Meso compound: has stereocenters but internal mirror plane makes it achiral and optically inactive.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The maximum number of stereoisomers for a compound with n stereocenters is:
questions.explanation: Maximum stereoisomers = 2n (may be fewer if meso forms exist).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Enantiomers have identical physical properties EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Enantiomers differ only in the direction of rotation of plane-polarized light (+/−).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which reagent is used for the Swern oxidation of a primary alcohol to aldehyde?
questions.explanation: Swern oxidation uses (COCl)2/DMSO/Et3N; mild and selective for primary → aldehyde.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: PCC (pyridinium chlorochromate) selectively oxidizes:
questions.optionA: Primary alcohol to aldehyde (no over-oxidation to acid)
questions.optionC: Secondary alcohol to ketone then to acid
questions.explanation: PCC in CH2Cl2 oxidizes 1° alcohol → aldehyde and 2° alcohol → ketone, without over-oxidation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky (HVZ) reaction introduces halogen at the α-carbon of:
questions.explanation: HVZ: carboxylic acid + Br2 + P (phosphorus) → α-bromoacid via enol-like acyl bromide intermediate.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which heterocyclic compound is aromatic and has nitrogen in a 5-membered ring?
questions.explanation: Pyrrole is a 5-membered N-heterocycle; N lone pair part of π system making it aromatic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Pyrrole is a weaker base than pyridine because:
questions.optionA: N lone pair is delocalized into ring π system
questions.explanation: In pyrrole, N lone pair is part of the 6-electron aromatic system; not available for protonation → weak base.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Furan undergoes electrophilic aromatic substitution preferentially at position:
questions.explanation: In furan, electrophiles attack preferentially at α (C-2) because it gives more stable arenium ions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which compound is the parent heterocycle in DNA purine bases (adenine, guanine)?
questions.explanation: Purine is the bicyclic base: pyrimidine ring fused with imidazole ring. Adenine and guanine are purines.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which nucleobases in DNA are pyrimidines?
questions.explanation: DNA pyrimidines: cytosine (C) and thymine (T). Purines: adenine (A) and guanine (G).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The glycosidic bond in nucleotides is:
questions.optionA: N-glycosidic bond between base and sugar
questions.explanation: Nucleoside: nucleobase linked to ribose/deoxyribose by N-glycosidic bond.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In RNA, which base replaces thymine (T)?
questions.explanation: RNA contains uracil instead of thymine; both pair with adenine but uracil lacks the 5-methyl group.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The phosphodiester bonds in DNA link:
questions.optionA: 3′-OH of one nucleotide to 5′-phosphate of next
questions.explanation: Phosphodiester bond: 3′-OH + 5′-phosphate of adjacent nucleotide → 3′→5′ backbone.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Reducing sugars react with Tollens' reagent because they:
questions.explanation: Reducing sugars have a free hemiacetal (aldehyde equivalent) that reduces Ag+ in Tollens' reagent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Sucrose is a non-reducing sugar because:
questions.optionA: Glycosidic bond involves both anomeric carbons
questions.explanation: In sucrose, C1 of glucose and C2 of fructose are both involved in glycosidic bond, blocking ring opening.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Haworth projection of α-D-glucose shows the C1–OH in:
questions.explanation: In α-D-glucose Haworth projection, C1–OH is below the plane (trans to CH2OH group at C5).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The linkage in starch (amylose) between glucose units is:
questions.explanation: Amylose has linear chain of α-D-glucose linked by α-1,4-glycosidic bonds (forms helical structure).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Cellulose differs from starch in having:
questions.explanation: Cellulose: β-1,4 linkages → straight chains, strong H-bonds, indigestible by humans.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Chitin, found in insect exoskeletons, is a polysaccharide of:
questions.explanation: Chitin: β-1,4 linked N-acetyl-D-glucosamine; structural polymer similar to cellulose.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Lipids are characterized by being:
questions.optionA: Non-polar, hydrophobic, soluble in organic solvents
questions.explanation: Lipids are non-polar biomolecules soluble in organic solvents; include fats, oils, phospholipids, steroids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Saponification of a triglyceride with NaOH gives:
questions.explanation: Saponification: triglyceride + 3NaOH → glycerol + 3 sodium carboxylate (soap) salts.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The iodine number (value) of a fat measures:
questions.explanation: Iodine number: grams of I2 absorbed per 100g fat; higher value = more double bonds (more unsaturated).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Phospholipids are major components of cell membranes because they are:
questions.explanation: Phospholipids: polar phosphate head + nonpolar fatty acid tails → form bilayer membranes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Cholesterol belongs to the class of lipids called:
questions.explanation: Cholesterol is a steroid: four fused ring system (three cyclohexane + one cyclopentane).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Essential fatty acids must be obtained from diet because:
questions.optionA: Humans cannot synthesize them due to lack of certain desaturase enzymes
questions.optionB: They are too large to synthesize
questions.explanation: Humans lack Δ12 and Δ15 desaturases; cannot make linoleic (ω-6) or α-linolenic (ω-3) acids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The isoelectric point (pI) of an amino acid is the pH at which:
questions.optionA: The molecule has zero net charge (zwitterion form)
questions.explanation: pI = (pKa1 + pKa2)/2 for simple amino acids; at pI the molecule exists as zwitterion with zero net charge.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The primary structure of a protein refers to:
questions.optionA: Sequence of amino acids linked by peptide bonds
questions.explanation: Primary structure: specific amino acid sequence; encoded by gene; determines higher structure.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The secondary structure of proteins is stabilized primarily by:
questions.optionA: Hydrogen bonds between backbone N–H and C=O
questions.explanation: α-helices and β-sheets form by regular H-bonding between amide NH and carbonyl CO along backbone.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The tertiary structure of a protein is maintained by all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Peptide bonds determine primary structure, not tertiary. Tertiary structure involves disulfide bonds, H-bonds, ionic bonds, hydrophobic interactions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Denaturation of a protein:
questions.optionA: Destroys secondary and tertiary structure without breaking peptide bonds
questions.explanation: Denaturation disrupts non-covalent bonds (H-bonds, hydrophobic, ionic) unfolding the protein; primary structure preserved.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The ninhydrin test gives a purple color with:
questions.explanation: Ninhydrin reacts with primary amines (α-amino acids) to give Ruhemann's purple color.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The biuret test detects proteins with at least:
questions.explanation: Biuret reagent (Cu²⁺/NaOH) detects two or more peptide bonds → violet/purple complex.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Millon's test is used for detecting:
questions.explanation: Millon's test: Hg(NO3)2 + HNO3 gives red color with tyrosine due to phenolic –OH group.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Free radical chain polymerization involves initiation, propagation, and:
questions.explanation: Addition polymerization: initiation (radical generation) → propagation (chain growth) → termination (coupling/disproportionation).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: AIBN (azobisisobutyronitrile) is commonly used as a:
questions.explanation: AIBN decomposes on heating to give two isobutyronitrile radicals + N2; used as initiator.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Ziegler-Natta catalysts are used for:
questions.explanation: Ziegler-Natta (TiCl4/AlR3): coordinates and inserts alkenes with stereocontrol giving isotactic polymers.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Isotactic polypropylene has all methyl groups:
questions.optionA: On the same side of the backbone
questions.explanation: Isotactic: all substituents on same side (mm triads). Syndiotactic: alternating. Atactic: random.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Neoprene (polychloroprene) is a synthetic rubber made from:
questions.explanation: Neoprene: polymerization of chloroprene (2-chloro-1,3-butadiene); oil and flame resistant.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Natural rubber is the polymer of:
questions.explanation: Natural rubber = cis-1,4-polyisoprene; gutta-percha = trans-1,4-polyisoprene (harder, less elastic).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Vulcanization of rubber involves:
questions.optionA: Cross-linking polymer chains with sulfur bridges
questions.explanation: Vulcanization: heating rubber with sulfur creates S–S crosslinks, improving elasticity and strength.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) is formed by condensation polymerization of:
questions.explanation: PET: condensation of HOCH2CH2OH + HOOC-C6H4-COOH → polyester + H2O.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Polycarbonate (PC) is made from bisphenol A and:
questions.explanation: PC: bisphenol A + phosgene → polycarbonate; tough, transparent, high Tg.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is:
questions.optionA: Temperature at which amorphous polymer transitions from glassy to rubbery state
questions.explanation: Tg: amorphous polymers transition from rigid glassy to flexible rubbery state; important processing parameter.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Nylon-6 is formed by ring-opening polymerization of:
questions.explanation: Nylon-6: ring-opening polymerization of ε-caprolactam (7-membered lactam) → polyamide.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Kevlar is an aromatic polyamide with exceptional strength due to:
questions.optionA: Extensive H-bonding between chains and rigid aromatic rings
questions.explanation: Kevlar (poly-p-phenylene terephthalamide): rigid chains, interchain H-bonds → very high tensile strength.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Bakelite is a thermosetting polymer formed from:
questions.explanation: Bakelite: phenol + formaldehyde → 3D crosslinked thermoset (first synthetic plastic).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following polymers is biodegradable?
questions.explanation: PLA is biodegradable polyester from lactic acid; degrades by hydrolysis and microbial action.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Dacron is a trade name for:
questions.explanation: Dacron = PET fiber (polyester); Terylene in UK. Used in clothing and medical implants.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Teflon (PTFE) is notable for its:
questions.optionA: Extremely low coefficient of friction and chemical inertness
questions.explanation: PTFE: poly(tetrafluoroethylene); C–F bonds very strong; non-stick, chemically inert up to ~260°C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which reaction converts benzene to aniline industrially?
questions.explanation: Industrial aniline: benzene → nitrobenzene (HNO3/H2SO4) → aniline (Fe/HCl reduction).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Gabriel synthesis is used to prepare:
questions.explanation: Gabriel synthesis: phthalimide + RX → N-alkylphthalimide → primary amine RNH2 by hydrolysis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reaction of an amine with benzenesulfonyl chloride (Hinsberg test) forms:
questions.optionA: Sulfonamide soluble in NaOH only for primary amines
questions.explanation: Hinsberg test: 1° amine → sulfonamide with acidic NH, soluble in NaOH; 2° amine → insoluble sulfonamide; 3° → no reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The coupling reaction of a diazonium salt with a phenol gives:
questions.explanation: Coupling: ArN2+ + ArOH (base) → Ar–N=N–Ar–OH (azo compound); used in dye manufacture.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Sandmeyer reaction converts diazonium salt to aryl halide using:
questions.explanation: Sandmeyer: ArN2+ + CuCl → ArCl; or CuBr → ArBr; or CuCN → ArCN. Cu(I) catalyst required.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Balz-Schiemann reaction converts a diazonium salt to aryl fluoride using:
questions.optionA: HBF4 → diazonium tetrafluoroborate → ArF on heating
questions.explanation: Balz-Schiemann: ArN2+ + HBF4 → ArN2BF4 → ArF + N2 + BF3 on thermal decomposition.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Heck reaction is a palladium-catalyzed coupling between an aryl/vinyl halide and:
questions.explanation: Heck reaction (Nobel 2010): Pd-catalyzed coupling of ArX + alkene → arylalkene (vinyl-arene product).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Suzuki coupling uses a palladium catalyst to couple an aryl halide with:
questions.explanation: Suzuki-Miyaura: ArX + Ar'B(OH)2 + Pd/base → Ar–Ar' biaryl. One of most used cross-coupling reactions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Stille coupling uses organotin (organostannane) reagents. The general reaction is:
questions.explanation: Stille coupling: aryl halide + organostannane under Pd catalysis → C–C bond formation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Sonogashira coupling forms a C(sp)–C(sp2) bond between:
questions.optionA: Terminal alkyne and aryl/vinyl halide using Pd/Cu catalyst
questions.explanation: Sonogashira: R-C≡C-H + ArX + Pd/Cu → R-C≡C-Ar. Used to make internal alkynes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Olefin metathesis (Grubbs catalyst) involves:
questions.optionA: Exchange of alkylidene groups between two alkenes
questions.explanation: Olefin metathesis (Nobel 2005): two alkenes exchange alkylidene fragments via metallacyclobutane intermediate.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Pauson-Khand reaction converts an enyne to:
questions.explanation: Pauson-Khand: alkyne + alkene + CO (via Co2(CO)8) → bicyclic cyclopentenone.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The iodoform test is positive for compounds having:
questions.optionA: CH3CO– group (methyl ketone) or CH3CHOH group
questions.explanation: Iodoform test: I2/NaOH → CHI3 (yellow ppt) with methyl ketones (CH3COR) or secondary alcohols (CH3CHOHR).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Lucas test distinguishes between primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols using:
questions.explanation: Lucas reagent (ZnCl2/HCl): 3° alcohol reacts immediately (turbidity), 2° after heating, 1° no reaction at RT.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Fehling's solution gives brick-red precipitate with:
questions.explanation: Fehling's: Cu²⁺ (tartrate complex) reduced by aldehyde → Cu2O (brick-red precipitate).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Benedict's test is used clinically to detect:
questions.explanation: Benedict's reagent (Cu²⁺/citrate): reduces with glucose → orange/red Cu2O precipitate.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Tollen's reagent (silver mirror test) is used to detect:
questions.explanation: Tollen's: [Ag(NH3)2]+ + aldehyde → silver mirror + carboxylate. Ketones do not react.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The 2,4-DNP test identifies:
questions.optionA: Carbonyl compounds (aldehydes and ketones) giving yellow/orange precipitate
questions.explanation: 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine reacts with C=O to form 2,4-DNP derivative (yellow/orange crystalline precipitate).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Baeyer test (decolorization of KMnO4) detects:
questions.explanation: Purple KMnO4 decolorized by alkenes/alkynes via oxidation → brown MnO2 precipitate (Baeyer test).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Bromine water decolorization is used to test for:
questions.explanation: Br2 water decolorized by alkenes (addition), alkynes (addition), and phenols (substitution) — all rapidly.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which test is used to distinguish between primary and secondary alcohols?
questions.optionA: Jones oxidation: 1° gives acid (or aldehyde with PCC), 2° gives ketone
questions.explanation: Jones reagent (CrO3/H2SO4): 1° alcohol → carboxylic acid; 2° alcohol → ketone. PCC stops at aldehyde for 1°.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Lassaigne test in organic analysis uses sodium fusion to detect:
questions.optionA: N, S, and halogens (as NaCN, Na2S, NaX)
questions.explanation: Lassaigne (sodium fusion): converts N→NaCN, S→Na2S, halogens→NaX, then confirmed by specific tests.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The percentage of carbon is determined in organic compounds by:
questions.explanation: Liebig method: organic compound + O2 → CO2 + H2O; CO2 absorbed and weighed to calculate % C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Kjeldahl method determines the percentage of:
questions.explanation: Kjeldahl: compound + H2SO4 (conc.) → (NH4)2SO4 → distill NH3 → titrate with HCl → calculate %N.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Carius method is used to determine the percentage of:
questions.optionA: Halogens and sulfur in organic compounds
questions.explanation: Carius method: compound + fuming HNO3 in sealed tube → AgX or BaSO4 precipitate → weigh for % halogen or S.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Molecular formula is determined from empirical formula using:
questions.optionA: Molecular formula = n × empirical formula, where n = molar mass / empirical formula mass
questions.explanation: n = M(compound) / M(empirical formula); multiply empirical formula by n to get molecular formula.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The degree of unsaturation (DBE) for C6H6 is:
questions.explanation: DBE = (2×6 + 2 − 6)/2 = (14−6)/2 = 4. Benzene: 3 double bonds + 1 ring = 4.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Organic compounds with same molecular formula but different structural formulas are called:
questions.explanation: Constitutional isomers: same molecular formula, different connectivity of atoms.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Conformational isomers (conformers) interconvert by:
questions.explanation: Conformers differ by rotation around C–C single bond; they interconvert rapidly at room temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The most stable conformation of ethane is:
questions.explanation: Staggered conformation minimizes torsional strain; most stable. Eclipsed has maximum torsional strain.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The most stable conformation of n-butane is:
questions.explanation: Anti conformation of butane: two methyls at 180° (anti-periplanar) → minimum steric and torsional strain.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The most stable chair conformation of cyclohexane has substituents in:
questions.explanation: Equatorial substituents avoid 1,3-diaxial interactions; bulky groups strongly prefer equatorial.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The most stable form of trans-1,4-dimethylcyclohexane is:
questions.explanation: Trans-1,4-dimethylcyclohexane: in chair conformation, trans arrangement allows both groups equatorial.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In Fischer projection, a horizontal bond represents:
questions.explanation: Fischer projection: horizontal lines = bonds toward viewer; vertical lines = bonds away from viewer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Absolute configuration is determined experimentally by:
questions.explanation: X-ray crystallography (Bijvoet method) directly determines R/S configuration experimentally.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The specific rotation [α] of a compound depends on:
questions.optionA: Concentration, path length, solvent, temperature, and wavelength
questions.explanation: [α] = α / (c × l); c = concentration (g/mL), l = path length (dm). Measured at defined T and λ.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Racemic mixture (racemate) is:
questions.optionA: Equal mixture of enantiomers; optically inactive overall
questions.explanation: Racemate: 50:50 enantiomers; rotations cancel → [α] = 0; properties same as pure enantiomers except optical activity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Resolution of a racemic mixture involves:
questions.optionA: Converting to diastereomers using a chiral resolving agent then separating
questions.optionD: Column chromatography with achiral stationary phase
questions.explanation: Resolution: racemate + chiral resolving agent (e.g., tartaric acid) → diastereomeric salts → separate by crystallization.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Thalidomide is an example where chirality matters because:
questions.optionA: R-enantiomer is sedative; S-enantiomer is teratogenic
questions.optionD: It is a racemic mixture by design
questions.explanation: Thalidomide tragedy: R-isomer therapeutic (morning sickness), S-isomer caused birth defects (teratogen).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Transition state theory states that the rate of reaction depends on:
questions.optionA: Energy of the activated complex (transition state) relative to reactants
questions.explanation: Rate ∝ e^(-ΔG‡/RT); higher activation energy (ΔG‡) → slower reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Hammond postulate states that for an endothermic step, the transition state resembles:
questions.explanation: Hammond's postulate: endothermic step → late TS (product-like); exothermic step → early TS (reactant-like).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Inductive effect is:
questions.optionA: Permanent polarization of σ bonds transmitted through the chain
questions.explanation: Inductive effect: electron withdrawal/donation through σ bonds; decreases with distance. –I for EWG, +I for EDG.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Resonance (mesomeric) effect involves:
questions.optionA: Delocalization of π electrons or lone pairs through conjugated system
questions.optionC: Overlap of σ bonds with π bonds
questions.explanation: Resonance effect (+M or −M): lone pairs or π electrons delocalized into ring or double bond system.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Hyperconjugation involves:
questions.optionA: Overlap of σ C–H bonds with adjacent empty p orbital or π system
questions.explanation: Hyperconjugation: σ C–H electrons delocalize into adjacent π system/empty p orbital; stabilizes carbocations and radicals.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Carbocation stability order is:
questions.explanation: Tertiary carbocations most stable (hyperconjugation + inductive donation from 3 alkyl groups).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Carbanion stability increases with:
questions.optionA: Increasing s-character (sp > sp2 > sp3) and EWG substituents
questions.explanation: Carbanions more stable on sp carbon (more electronegative) and with EWG neighbors stabilizing negative charge.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Free radical stability order is:
questions.explanation: Free radical stability follows same trend as carbocation: 3° most stable (more hyperconjugation and hyperconjugation).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Markovnikov rule predicts that in electrophilic addition to an alkene, H adds to:
questions.optionA: The carbon bearing more hydrogen atoms
questions.optionC: The carbon bearing fewer hydrogen atoms
questions.explanation: Markovnikov: H adds to carbon with more H's; electrophile goes to less substituted carbon → more stable carbocation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Anti-Markovnikov addition is observed in HBr addition to alkenes in the presence of:
questions.explanation: Peroxide-initiated free radical addition: Br• adds to less substituted carbon → anti-Markovnikov product.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Hydroboration-oxidation of an alkene gives:
questions.explanation: Hydroboration (BH3) + oxidation (H2O2/NaOH): syn addition; B goes to less hindered carbon → anti-Markovnikov alcohol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Oxymercuration-demercuration of an alkene gives:
questions.explanation: Oxymercuration [Hg(OAc)2/H2O then NaBH4]: Markovnikov alcohol; proceeds via mercurinium ion without carbocation rearrangement.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Wacker oxidation converts an alkene (especially ethylene) to a carbonyl compound using:
questions.explanation: Wacker process: CH2=CH2 + O2 → CH3CHO via Pd(II)/Cu(II) catalyst. Industrial process for acetaldehyde.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The acetylene (ethyne) gives what product on hydration with HgSO4/H2SO4?
questions.explanation: HC≡CH + H2O (HgSO4/H2SO4) → CH2=CHOH (enol) → CH3CHO (acetaldehyde by keto-enol tautomerism).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Keto-enol tautomerism involves equilibrium between:
questions.explanation: Keto-enol: C=O (keto) ⇌ C(OH)=C (enol). Usually keto predominates; enol content varies with structure.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The enol content of 2,4-pentanedione (acetylacetone) is unusually high (~80%) because:
questions.optionA: Intramolecular H-bonding and resonance stabilize the enol form
questions.explanation: β-diketone: enol stabilized by intramolecular H-bond and conjugated enol-carbonyl system.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The acidity of terminal alkynes (pKa ~25) is greater than alkenes (pKa ~44) because:
questions.optionA: sp-hybridized C has greater s-character → closer to nucleus → better holds negative charge
questions.optionD: Inductive effect of triple bond carbon only
questions.explanation: sp carbon (50% s-character) vs sp3 (25% s-character): higher s-character → more electronegative → more acidic C–H.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following is the strongest acid?
questions.explanation: CF3COOH: three highly electronegative F atoms → strong –I effect → stabilize carboxylate anion → strongest acid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Phenol (pKa ~10) is more acidic than cyclohexanol (pKa ~16) because:
questions.optionA: Phenoxide ion is resonance-stabilized (negative charge delocalized in ring)
questions.explanation: Phenoxide: negative charge delocalized into aromatic ring via resonance → greatly stabilized anion → more acidic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: p-Nitrophenol is more acidic than phenol because:
questions.optionA: –NO2 group withdraws electrons by –I and –M effects, stabilizing phenoxide
questions.explanation: para-NO2: strong EWG by –I and resonance withdrawal → phenoxide anion better stabilized → lower pKa.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: p-Methylphenol (cresol) is less acidic than phenol because:
questions.optionA: Methyl group is +I, destabilizing the phenoxide anion
questions.explanation: Methyl is +I/+hyperconjugation: EDG increases electron density on phenoxide → less stable → less acidic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Trimethylamine is more basic than ammonia because:
questions.optionA: Methyl groups donate electrons (+I) increasing lone pair availability on N
questions.explanation: Alkyl groups: +I effect → increased electron density on N → more basic. But in water, steric/solvation effects complicate the trend.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Aniline is a much weaker base than cyclohexylamine because:
questions.optionA: Nitrogen lone pair delocalized into benzene ring (−M effect) → less available for protonation
questions.optionC: Benzene ring withdraws electrons by –I only
questions.optionD: Cyclohexylamine is more soluble in water
questions.explanation: Aniline: N lone pair in resonance with ring → decreased availability for protonation → pKaH ~4.6 vs ~10 for alkylamines.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Ortho effect in substituted benzoic acids: ortho substituents generally make the acid:
questions.optionA: Stronger (regardless of electronic nature) due to steric inhibition of resonance
questions.explanation: Ortho effect: bulky ortho substituent twists COOH out of plane → reduced resonance → COOH more like aliphatic acid → more acidic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Hammett equation (log k/k0 = σρ) relates reaction rate/equilibrium to:
questions.optionA: Electronic effects of substituents on reaction center
questions.explanation: Hammett: σ = substituent constant (electronic); ρ = reaction sensitivity. EWG (σ > 0), EDG (σ < 0).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: A reaction with positive ρ (rho) in Hammett analysis is favored by:
questions.explanation: Positive ρ: reaction builds negative charge at reaction center → favored by EWG (positive σ) → e.g., ester hydrolysis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which solvent is most polar aprotic?
questions.explanation: DMSO is a highly polar aprotic solvent; DMF, acetonitrile, acetone also polar aprotic. Favors SN2.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Polar protic solvents favor which reaction mechanism?
questions.explanation: Polar protic solvents (H2O, ROH): stabilize ions by H-bonding → favor SN1 (ionic intermediates).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The leaving group ability order in SN reactions is:
questions.optionA: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ > F⁻ (best leaving group = weakest base)
questions.explanation: Best leaving groups are weakest bases (most stable anions): I⁻ best, F⁻ worst leaving group in polar solvents.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In which reaction does a 1,2-hydride or alkyl shift (Wagner-Meerwein) occur?
questions.optionA: SN1 involving formation of a carbocation
questions.explanation: Carbocation (SN1) can rearrange by 1,2-hydride or 1,2-alkyl shift to give more stable carbocation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Neighbouring group participation (NGP) in SN reactions leads to:
questions.optionA: Retention of configuration (double inversion via cyclic intermediate)
questions.explanation: NGP: intramolecular nucleophile displaces LG (first inversion) → then external nucleophile attacks (second inversion) → net retention.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Acylium ion (RCO⁺) is the electrophile in:
questions.explanation: F-C acylation: RCOCl + AlCl3 → RCO+ (acylium ion) → attacks aromatic ring → aryl ketone.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The nitronium ion (NO2⁺) is the electrophile in:
questions.explanation: Nitration of benzene: HNO3 + H2SO4 → NO2+ (nitronium ion) → electrophile in EAS.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Sulfonation of benzene is reversible. The reverse reaction (desulfonation) is carried out by:
questions.explanation: Desulfonation: benzenesulfonic acid + H2O/H+ (steam distillation) → benzene + H2SO4.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Halogeno (halogenation) of benzene requires a Lewis acid catalyst because:
questions.optionA: Lewis acid activates halogen (X2) to form X+ or polarized complex (electrophile)
questions.explanation: FeBr3 or AlCl3 coordinates with Br2 → Br-Br weakened → Br+ generated (electrophile for EAS).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which directing group is a meta-director in EAS?
questions.explanation: –NO2 is strong EWG (–I and –M): deactivates ring and directs electrophile to meta position.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Halogen substituents on benzene are:
questions.explanation: Halogens: –I (deactivating) but lone pair donation (+M) → o/p directing. Overall deactivated vs benzene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Nucleophilic aromatic substitution (SNAr) requires:
questions.optionA: EWG ortho/para to leaving group to stabilize Meisenheimer complex
questions.explanation: SNAr: nucleophile adds to ring carbon bearing LG → Meisenheimer complex → stabilized by EWG (NO2) at o/p.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The benzyne mechanism is an elimination-addition mechanism for nucleophilic aromatic substitution on:
questions.optionA: Unactivated aryl halides under very harsh conditions (strong base/high T)
questions.explanation: Benzyne (aryne) mechanism: strong base removes ortho-H → benzyne triple bond intermediate → nucleophile adds.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Furan is less reactive than benzene in EAS because:
questions.optionA: Furan is more reactive than benzene; it undergoes EAS more easily
questions.optionB: Furan is aromatic but the oxygen lone pair is part of aromatic system
questions.optionD: Furan has no lone pairs on oxygen
questions.explanation: Furan is actually MORE reactive than benzene in EAS; oxygen lone pair activates the ring.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Pyridine is less reactive than benzene toward EAS because:
questions.optionA: Nitrogen is electronegative (–I and –M) → ring deactivated toward electrophiles
questions.optionC: Pyridine cannot undergo EAS at all
questions.explanation: Pyridine N: –I and –M effects deactivate ring and direct electrophile to β-position (C-3).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Indole undergoes EAS preferentially at:
questions.explanation: Indole: EAS at C-3 (most activated; 3-substituted indole product = most stable sigma complex).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Pictet-Spengler reaction is used to synthesize:
questions.explanation: Pictet-Spengler: aromatic amine + carbonyl → Schiff base → cyclizes to tetrahydroisoquinoline/β-carboline.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Paal-Knorr synthesis produces furan from:
questions.explanation: Paal-Knorr: 1,4-diketone + acid → furan; + amine → pyrrole; + P2S5 → thiophene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Hantzsch pyridine synthesis involves condensation of two molecules of β-ketoester with:
questions.explanation: Hantzsch: 2 β-ketoester + aldehyde + NH3 → 1,4-dihydropyridine → oxidize → pyridine.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Skraup synthesis is used to prepare:
questions.explanation: Skraup: aniline + glycerol + H2SO4 + oxidizing agent → quinoline.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Terpenes are classified by isoprene units. A sesquiterpene contains:
questions.explanation: Sesquiterpene = C15 = 3 isoprene (C5) units. Monoterpene = C10 (2), diterpene = C20 (4), triterpene = C30 (6).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The isoprene rule (biogenetic isoprene rule) states that terpene carbon skeletons are:
questions.optionA: Built from head-to-tail condensation of isoprene (C5) units
questions.explanation: Terpenes follow isoprene rule: C5 units joined head-to-tail via IPP and DMAPP by prenyl transferase.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Alkaloids are natural products that typically contain:
questions.optionA: Nitrogen in a heterocyclic ring; pharmacologically active
questions.explanation: Alkaloids: N-containing (usually basic) natural products; examples: morphine, quinine, caffeine, cocaine.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Morphine is classified as which type of alkaloid?
questions.explanation: Morphine belongs to the morphinan group of isoquinoline alkaloids; derived from tyrosine biosynthetically.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Strychnine and quinine are examples of:
questions.explanation: Strychnine (indole alkaloid from Strychnos nux-vomica); quinine (quinoline alkaloid from Cinchona bark).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Penicillin contains which key functional group in its β-lactam ring?
questions.explanation: Penicillin: β-lactam (4-membered cyclic amide) fused to thiazolidine ring. β-lactam inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) is synthesized by:
questions.optionA: Acetylation of salicylic acid with acetic anhydride
questions.explanation: Aspirin: salicylic acid + (CH3CO)2O → acetylsalicylic acid + CH3COOH.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Paracetamol (acetaminophen) is formed by:
questions.optionA: Acetylation of p-aminophenol with acetic anhydride
questions.explanation: Paracetamol: p-aminophenol + acetic anhydride → N-acetyl-p-aminophenol (paracetamol).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The ozone layer is damaged by CFCs through:
questions.optionA: Chlorine radicals (Cl•) from CFC photolysis catalytically destroying O3
questions.explanation: UV → CFCl3 → Cl• + CFCl2•; Cl• + O3 → ClO + O2; ClO + O → Cl• + O2 (catalytic cycle destroys O3).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The greenhouse effect is mainly caused by which organic compound?
questions.explanation: Greenhouse gases include CO2, CH4, N2O, H2O, and CFCs. CH4 is 25× more potent than CO2 per molecule.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Biodiesel is produced by:
questions.explanation: Biodiesel: vegetable oil/animal fat + methanol + NaOH → fatty acid methyl esters (FAME) + glycerol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Bioethanol is produced from carbohydrates by:
questions.explanation: Bioethanol: C6H12O6 → 2C2H5OH + 2CO2 (yeast fermentation); distillation gives ~95% ethanol azeotrope.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The cracking of petroleum produces:
questions.optionA: Smaller, more useful alkene and alkane fractions from large hydrocarbons
questions.explanation: Cracking: large alkanes → smaller alkanes + alkenes (e.g., ethylene, propylene) used as fuels and feedstocks.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Reforming of petroleum converts naphtha to:
questions.explanation: Catalytic reforming: naphtha (C6–C10 alkanes/cycloalkanes) → BTX aromatics + H2 using Pt catalyst.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Octane number measures:
questions.explanation: Octane number: 2,2,4-trimethylpentane (isooctane) = 100; n-heptane = 0. Higher octane = less knocking.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Haber process for ammonia synthesis is represented as:
questions.explanation: Haber process: N2 + 3H2 → 2NH3; exothermic, Le Chatelier favors high pressure; Fe catalyst used.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Contact process is used to manufacture:
questions.explanation: Contact process: SO2 + ½O2 → SO3 (V2O5 catalyst) → SO3 + H2SO4 → oleum → dilute to H2SO4.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Ostwald process manufactures:
questions.explanation: Ostwald: 4NH3 + 5O2 → 4NO + 6H2O (Pt/Rh); 2NO + O2 → 2NO2; 3NO2 + H2O → 2HNO3 + NO.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Solvay process is used for industrial manufacture of:
questions.explanation: Solvay: NaCl + NH3 + CO2 + H2O → NaHCO3 → Na2CO3 + CO2 + H2O.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Green chemistry principles aim to:
questions.optionA: Design chemical products and processes that minimize environmental impact and waste
questions.explanation: Green chemistry (Anastas & Warner, 1998): 12 principles including atom economy, safer solvents, renewable feedstocks.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Atom economy (E-factor) is a metric in green chemistry calculated as:
questions.optionA: (MW of desired product / MW of all products) × 100%
questions.optionC: Moles of product / moles of reactant
questions.optionD: Ratio of green to non-green steps
questions.explanation: Atom economy = (MW product / sum MW all products) × 100. High atom economy = less waste.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Dalton's atomic theory states that atoms of the same element are:
questions.optionA: Different in mass but same in chemical properties
questions.optionB: Identical in mass and chemical properties
questions.optionC: Different in both mass and chemical properties
questions.optionD: Same in mass but different in chemical properties
questions.explanation: According to Dalton's atomic theory, all atoms of a given element are identical in mass and chemical properties, while atoms of different elements have different properties.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which law states that matter can neither be created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction?
questions.explanation: The Law of Conservation of Mass (Lavoisier) states that total mass of reactants equals total mass of products in a chemical reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The law of definite proportions states that a chemical compound always contains elements combined in:
questions.explanation: Law of Definite Proportions (Proust): A pure chemical compound always contains the same elements combined in the same proportion by mass.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the law of multiple proportions, masses of one element combining with a fixed mass of another are in ratio of:
questions.explanation: Law of Multiple Proportions (Dalton): The masses of one element combining with a fixed mass of another element are in simple whole number ratios.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The value of Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's number (Nₐ) = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹. This is the number of particles (atoms, molecules, ions) in one mole of any substance.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: How many moles are present in 44 g of CO₂ (molar mass = 44 g/mol)?
questions.explanation: Moles = mass/molar mass = 44/44 = 1 mol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: What is the molar mass of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)?
questions.explanation: Molar mass of C₆H₁₂O₆ = 6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 72 + 12 + 96 = 180 g/mol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: A compound contains 40% carbon, 6.67% hydrogen, and 53.33% oxygen by mass. Its empirical formula is:
questions.explanation: Mole ratio C:H:O = 40/12 : 6.67/1 : 53.33/16 = 3.33:6.67:3.33 = 1:2:1. Empirical formula = CH₂O.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The empirical formula of a compound is CH₂O and its molar mass is 180 g/mol. The molecular formula is:
questions.explanation: Empirical formula mass CH₂O = 12+2+16 = 30. n = 180/30 = 6. Molecular formula = C₆H₁₂O₆.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The percentage of oxygen in water (H₂O) is approximately:
questions.explanation: Molar mass of H₂O = 18. Mass of O = 16. Percentage = (16/18) × 100 = 88.9%.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the reaction N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃, if 28 g N₂ and 3 g H₂ are mixed, which is the limiting reagent?
questions.explanation: 1 mol N₂ (28g) requires 3 mol H₂ (6g). Only 3g (1.5 mol) H₂ is available. H₂ is the limiting reagent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The equivalent mass of sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) in an acid-base reaction is:
questions.explanation: Equivalent mass = Molar mass / n-factor. For H₂SO₄ (diprotic), n-factor = 2. Equivalent mass = 98/2 = 49 g/eq.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: A solution has 9.8 g of H₂SO₄ dissolved in 1 L. Its normality is:
questions.explanation: Equivalents = 9.8/49 = 0.2. Normality = equivalents/volume(L) = 0.2/1 = 0.2 N.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Molarity of a solution containing 58.5 g NaCl (MW = 58.5) in 500 mL solution is:
questions.explanation: Moles of NaCl = 58.5/58.5 = 1 mol. Volume = 0.5 L. Molarity = 1/0.5 = 2 M.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Vapour density of a gas is 16. Its molecular mass is:
questions.explanation: Molecular mass = 2 × vapour density = 2 × 16 = 32 g/mol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Gay-Lussac's law of gaseous volumes states that gases combine in volumes in ratio of:
questions.explanation: Gay-Lussac's Law: Gases combine in simple whole number ratios by volume under the same conditions of temperature and pressure.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The number of atoms in 1 mole of nitrogen gas (N₂) is:
questions.explanation: 1 mol N₂ = 6.022×10²³ molecules. Each molecule has 2 N atoms. Total = 2 × 6.022×10²³ = 12.044×10²³ atoms.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: A solution contains 2 mol solute and 8 mol solvent. The mole fraction of solute is:
questions.explanation: Mole fraction of solute = 2/(2+8) = 2/10 = 0.2.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The volume occupied by 1 mole of any ideal gas at STP is:
questions.explanation: At STP (0°C, 1 atm), 1 mole of ideal gas occupies 22.4 L (molar volume).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Avogadro's hypothesis states that equal volumes of gases at same T and P contain equal number of:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's hypothesis: Equal volumes of all gases at the same T and P contain equal numbers of molecules.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The n-factor of KMnO₄ in acidic medium (Mn⁷⁺ → Mn²⁺) is:
questions.explanation: In KMnO₄, Mn = +7. In acidic medium, Mn → +2. Change in oxidation state = 7-2 = 5. n-factor = 5.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: How many grams of NaOH (MW = 40) are needed to prepare 500 mL of 2 M solution?
questions.explanation: Moles = 2 × 0.5 = 1 mol. Mass = 1 × 40 = 40 g.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In a reaction, 2 mol of A produces 3 mol of B. If 5 mol A is used, moles of B produced are:
questions.explanation: 2 mol A → 3 mol B. So 5 mol A → (3/2) × 5 = 7.5 mol B.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: If 8 g of product is obtained when theoretical yield is 10 g, percentage yield is:
questions.explanation: Percentage yield = (actual/theoretical) × 100 = (8/10) × 100 = 80%.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Equivalent mass = Molar mass divided by:
questions.explanation: Equivalent mass = Molar mass / n-factor. The n-factor depends on electrons transferred, protons donated/accepted, etc.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The number of molecules in 2 g of H₂ (molar mass = 2 g/mol) is:
questions.explanation: Moles of H₂ = 2/2 = 1 mol. Molecules = 1 × 6.022×10²³ = 6.022×10²³.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Molality (m) is defined as moles of solute per:
questions.explanation: Molality = moles of solute / mass of solvent in kg. Unlike molarity, molality is temperature-independent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The law of reciprocal proportions is also known as:
questions.optionB: Law of equivalence or Richter's law
questions.explanation: The Law of Reciprocal Proportions (Richter/Wenzel) relates how elements combine with each other through their proportions with a third element.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In combustion analysis, carbon content is determined by measuring the mass of:
questions.explanation: In combustion analysis, all carbon converts to CO₂. Carbon % is calculated from mass of CO₂ collected.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: 28 g N₂ and 6 g H₂ react: N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃. Maximum mass of NH₃ formed is:
questions.explanation: 1 mol N₂ (28g) reacts with 3 mol H₂ (6g) — exact stoichiometric amounts. 1 mol N₂ → 2 mol NH₃ (34g).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Rutherford's gold foil experiment concluded that the positive charge and most of the atom's mass is in:
questions.optionA: A diffuse positive cloud throughout the atom
questions.explanation: Large-angle alpha particle scattering proved the nucleus is tiny (~10⁻¹⁵ m), dense, and contains all positive charge and most of the atom's mass.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Bohr's second postulate states that angular momentum of an electron in an allowed orbit is:
questions.explanation: Bohr's second postulate: mvr = nh/2π, where n = 1, 2, 3... Angular momentum is quantized.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Lyman series of hydrogen spectrum lies in:
questions.explanation: Lyman series (electron transitions to n=1) lies in UV region. Balmer (n=2) is visible; Paschen (n=3) is infrared.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Balmer series of hydrogen corresponds to electron transitions ending at:
questions.explanation: Balmer series: electrons fall from n=3,4,5... to n=2. These transitions produce lines in the visible region.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Energy of electron in nth Bohr orbit of hydrogen atom is:
questions.explanation: Eₙ = -13.6/n² eV. For ground state (n=1): E = -13.6 eV. Negative sign means electron is bound.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle states:
questions.optionA: Energy and mass cannot be measured simultaneously
questions.optionB: Position and momentum of a particle cannot both be precisely determined simultaneously
questions.optionC: Spin and charge of electron are uncertain
questions.optionD: Atomic number and mass number are related
questions.explanation: Δx·Δp ≥ h/4π. The more precisely position is known, the less precisely momentum is known, and vice versa.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The de Broglie wavelength of a particle of mass m moving with velocity v is:
questions.explanation: de Broglie equation: λ = h/p = h/mv, where h = 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The principal quantum number (n) determines:
questions.optionC: Energy level and size of the orbital
questions.explanation: Principal quantum number n (1, 2, 3...) specifies the main energy level (shell) and average electron-nucleus distance.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The azimuthal quantum number l for a d orbital is:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The number of orbitals in the d-subshell is:
questions.explanation: Number of orbitals = 2l + 1. For d (l=2): 2(2)+1 = 5 orbitals (dxy, dyz, dxz, dx²-y², dz²).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Pauli exclusion principle states that:
questions.optionA: No two electrons can have the same principal quantum number
questions.optionB: No two electrons in the same atom can have all four quantum numbers identical
questions.optionC: Electrons fill orbitals singly before pairing
questions.explanation: Pauli Exclusion Principle: No two electrons in an atom can have identical sets of all four quantum numbers (n, l, mₗ, mₛ). Max 2 electrons per orbital with opposite spins.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Hund's rule states that electrons fill degenerate orbitals by:
questions.optionA: Pairing in the same orbital first
questions.optionB: Occupying each orbital singly with parallel spins before pairing
questions.explanation: Hund's Rule: Electrons fill degenerate orbitals singly with parallel spins before any pairing. This minimizes electron-electron repulsion and gives maximum multiplicity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The electronic configuration of Fe (Z=26) is:
questions.explanation: Fe (Z=26): [Ar](18e) + 8 more electrons: 4s² (2) + 3d⁶ (6). Configuration: [Ar] 3d⁶ 4s².

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Copper (Cu, Z=29) has anomalous configuration [Ar] 3d¹⁰ 4s¹ because:
questions.optionB: Completely filled d¹⁰ has extra stability
questions.optionC: Energy difference between 3d and 4s is zero
questions.explanation: Completely filled (d¹⁰) and half-filled (d⁵) subshells have extra stability due to symmetrical charge distribution and maximum exchange energy.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Maximum number of electrons in the 3rd shell (n=3) is:
questions.explanation: Maximum electrons = 2n². For n=3: 2(9) = 18. Third shell has 3s (2), 3p (6), 3d (10) subshells.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Correct filling order according to Aufbau principle is:
questions.explanation: Aufbau order follows (n+l) rule: for 4s, n+l=4; for 3d, n+l=5. Lower (n+l) fills first, so 4s fills before 3d.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: An orbital is best described as:
questions.optionA: A fixed circular path for electrons
questions.optionB: A 3D region where probability of finding electron is maximum (>90%)
questions.optionC: The exact position of an electron
questions.optionD: A flat plane in which electrons move
questions.explanation: An orbital is a three-dimensional probability region around the nucleus. It's defined by a boundary surface containing ~90% probability of finding the electron.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The shape of a p orbital is:
questions.explanation: p orbitals have a dumbbell (two-lobe) shape. There are three p orbitals (px, py, pz) oriented along the three axes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Cathode rays are deflected by:
questions.explanation: Cathode rays are negatively charged electrons. They deflect toward positive plate in electric field and in magnetic field according to Lorentz force law.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The neutron was discovered by:
questions.explanation: James Chadwick (1932) discovered the neutron by bombarding beryllium with alpha particles, detecting uncharged radiation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Atoms with the same mass number but different atomic numbers are called:
questions.explanation: Isobars: same A, different Z. Example: ⁴⁰Ca (Z=20) and ⁴⁰Ar (Z=18).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Atoms with the same atomic number but different mass numbers are:
questions.explanation: Isotopes: same Z (same element), different A (different neutron number). Example: ¹²C and ¹⁴C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The photoelectric effect demonstrated the:
questions.explanation: Photoelectric effect (Einstein, 1905): electrons ejected only above threshold frequency, not threshold intensity, proving light consists of discrete photons with E = hν.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Work function in photoelectric effect is:
questions.optionB: Minimum energy needed to eject an electron from metal surface
questions.explanation: Work function (φ): minimum energy to remove electron from metal. KE of emitted electron = hν - φ. Emission only occurs when hν > φ.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the hydrogen atom, the radius of nth Bohr orbit is proportional to:
questions.explanation: rₙ = n²a₀, where a₀ = 0.529 Å. Radius grows as n², so n=2 orbit is 4× n=1, and n=3 is 9× n=1.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The spin quantum number (ms) can have values:
questions.explanation: Spin quantum number ms = +1/2 (spin up, ↑) or -1/2 (spin down, ↓). Two electrons in same orbital must have opposite spins.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Chromium (Cr, Z=24) has [Ar] 3d⁵ 4s¹ configuration because:
questions.explanation: Half-filled (d⁵) subshell has extra stability due to symmetrical distribution and maximum exchange energy. Cr promotes one 4s electron to achieve [Ar]3d⁵4s¹.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Number of radial nodes in a 3s orbital is:
questions.explanation: Radial nodes = n - l - 1. For 3s (n=3, l=0): 3-0-1 = 2 radial nodes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Planck's quantum theory states energy is emitted in discrete packets called:
questions.explanation: Planck (1900): Energy is emitted/absorbed in discrete quanta. Energy per quantum E = hν (h = Planck's constant, ν = frequency).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Paschen series of hydrogen spectrum lies in:
questions.explanation: Paschen series (transitions to n=3 from n=4,5,6...) lies in near-infrared region. Brackett (n=4) and Pfund (n=5) are also infrared.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The modern periodic law states properties of elements are periodic functions of:
questions.explanation: Modern Periodic Law (Moseley, 1913): Properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers, not atomic mass.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Atomic radius decreases across a period because:
questions.optionC: Nuclear charge increases while electrons are added to same shell
questions.explanation: Across a period, Zeff increases (more protons) but electrons enter the same shell. Increased nuclear attraction pulls electrons closer, decreasing atomic radius.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Ionization energy generally increases across a period because:
questions.optionB: Nuclear charge increases and atomic radius decreases
questions.explanation: Across a period, Zeff increases and atomic size decreases, making electrons harder to remove. IE generally increases left to right (with some anomalies).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which element has the highest electronegativity?
questions.explanation: Fluorine has the highest electronegativity (4.0 on Pauling scale) due to very small size and high nuclear charge.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Down a group, ionization energy:
questions.optionA: Increases due to increasing nuclear charge
questions.optionB: Decreases due to increasing atomic size and shielding
questions.explanation: Down a group, atomic size and shielding increase, reducing Zeff on valence electrons. This makes electron removal easier, so IE decreases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Elements in the same group have the same:
questions.explanation: Same group = same number of valence electrons → similar chemical properties.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The s-block elements occupy Groups:
questions.explanation: s-block elements: Groups 1 (alkali metals) and 2 (alkaline earth metals). Valence electrons in s-orbitals.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Diagonal relationships in periodic table exist between:
questions.optionB: Li and Mg, Be and Al
