questions.explanation: Diagonal relationship: Li resembles Mg; Be resembles Al; B resembles Si. These pairs have similar charge densities and electronegativities.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Among isoelectronic species Na⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺, F⁻ (all with 10 electrons), largest ionic radius belongs to:
questions.explanation: F⁻ has 9 protons for 10 electrons (weakest nuclear attraction) → largest radius. Al³⁺ has 13 protons → smallest.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: First ionization energy of O is less than N because:
questions.explanation: N has half-filled 2p³ (extra stable). O has 2p⁴ with one paired electron suffering extra repulsion — easier to remove. IE(N) > IE(O).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Transition elements (d-block) characteristically show:
questions.optionC: Variable oxidation states and colored compounds
questions.explanation: Transition metals show variable oxidation states (both 3d and 4s electrons available) and form colored compounds due to d-d transitions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The shielding effect reduces the effective nuclear charge experienced by:
questions.explanation: Inner (core) electrons partially shield outer valence electrons from full nuclear charge, reducing Zeff felt by valence electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Noble gases have nearly zero electron affinity because:
questions.optionB: Their outermost shell is completely filled
questions.explanation: Noble gases have completely filled s²p⁶ valence shell. There is no tendency to gain electrons; additional electrons must enter the next higher shell.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Electron affinity is the energy change when:
questions.optionA: A gaseous atom loses an electron
questions.optionB: A gaseous atom gains an electron to form a gaseous anion
questions.optionD: Two atoms form a covalent bond
questions.explanation: Electron affinity: X(g) + e⁻ → X⁻(g) + energy. It is the energy released when a gaseous atom gains an electron.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Lanthanides and actinides are placed separately because:
questions.optionB: Their differentiating electron enters the f-subshell
questions.explanation: Lanthanides (4f, Z=58-71) and actinides (5f, Z=90-103) are f-block elements placed separately to keep the periodic table compact.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Number of elements in Period 4 of the periodic table is:
questions.explanation: Period 4 has 18 elements (K to Kr): 2 s-block + 10 d-block (first transition series) + 6 p-block = 18.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Among Li, Na, K, Rb (Group 1), which has the smallest atomic radius?
questions.explanation: Going down Group 1, new shells are added. Li (n=2) is smallest; Rb (n=5) is largest.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Metallic character in a group:
questions.explanation: Down a group, atomic size increases and IE decreases, making electron loss easier. Metallic character increases down a group.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Mendeleev arranged elements in order of increasing:
questions.explanation: Mendeleev's periodic table (1869) arranged elements by increasing atomic mass. He left gaps for undiscovered elements and successfully predicted their properties.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The period number in the periodic table corresponds to:
questions.optionB: Highest principal quantum number (n) of outermost electrons
questions.explanation: Period number = highest n of valence electrons. Period 3 elements have outermost electrons in n=3 shell.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Elements with high IE and high EA are typically:
questions.explanation: Non-metals have high IE (hard to lose electrons) and high EA (tend to gain electrons). Located upper-right in periodic table.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which property increases from Na to Cl across Period 3?
questions.explanation: Across Period 3: atomic radius decreases, metallic character decreases, electronegativity increases (Na: 0.93 → Cl: 3.16).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The second ionization energy of Na is much higher than first because:
questions.optionA: Na has only one valence electron
questions.optionB: Second electron removed from the filled inner 2p⁶ shell
questions.optionD: Second electron is in a d orbital
questions.explanation: After losing 3s¹ (low IE), the second electron must be removed from inner 2p⁶ with much higher Zeff — very large energy needed.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Isoelectronic species have the same:
questions.explanation: Isoelectronic = same number of electrons. Example: N³⁻, O²⁻, F⁻, Ne, Na⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺ all have 10 electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Electronegativity is the ability of an atom to:
questions.optionB: Attract shared electrons in a covalent bond
questions.optionC: Gain electrons to form an anion
questions.explanation: Electronegativity (Pauling) measures an atom's power to attract shared electron pair in a covalent bond. Fluorine (4.0) is most electronegative.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The p-block elements belong to Groups:
questions.explanation: p-block elements (Groups 13-18) have valence electrons in p-orbitals. Includes metals (Sn, Pb), metalloids (Si), non-metals (C, N, O, F), and noble gases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Effective nuclear charge Z* is:
questions.explanation: Z* = Z - σ (Slater), where σ is shielding constant. Z* determines how strongly valence electrons are attracted by the nucleus.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Anomalous properties of first element of each group are due to:
questions.optionB: Very small size and high charge density
questions.explanation: First elements (Li, Be, B, N, O, F) have very small radii and high charge densities, giving them unique properties compared to other group members.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Elements with atomic numbers 58–71 are called:
questions.explanation: Lanthanides (Z=58-71) are 4f-block elements (rare earths). Actinides (Z=90-103) are 5f-block elements.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following is a metalloid?
questions.explanation: Metalloids: B, Si, Ge, As, Sb, Te, Po. They have intermediate properties. Silicon is the most important semiconductor.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Among halogens, which has the most negative electron affinity?
questions.explanation: Cl has most negative electron affinity (-348 kJ/mol). F has slightly less due to its very small size causing high e⁻-e⁻ repulsion when gaining an electron.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Atomic radius of noble gases is measured as:
questions.explanation: Noble gases don't bond, so their size is measured as Van der Waals radius (half the distance between adjacent non-bonded atoms of same element).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Halogens belong to which group?
questions.explanation: Halogens: Group 17 (F, Cl, Br, I, At). They have 7 valence electrons (ns²np⁵) and are the most reactive non-metals.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The modern periodic table has:
questions.explanation: Modern periodic table: 7 horizontal periods and 18 vertical groups. Currently contains 118 confirmed elements.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Ionization energy dip from Be to B occurs because:
questions.optionB: B has 2p¹ which is higher energy and easier to remove than Be's 2s²
questions.optionD: Nuclear charge of B is insufficient
questions.explanation: Be has filled 2s² (more stable). B has 2p¹ electron at higher energy level, easier to remove. IE(Be) > IE(B) despite B having higher Z.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: An element with configuration 2, 8, 8, 1 belongs to:
questions.explanation: 4 shells → Period 4; 1 valence electron → Group 1. Total Z = 2+8+8+1 = 19 = Potassium (K).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: A cation is __ in size compared to its parent atom:
questions.explanation: Cations are smaller: removing electrons reduces e⁻-e⁻ repulsion, and remaining electrons are more tightly held by the same nuclear charge.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: An anion is __ in size compared to its parent atom:
questions.explanation: Anions are larger: gaining electrons increases e⁻-e⁻ repulsion with no change in nuclear charge, so the electron cloud expands.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Alkali metals have low ionization energies because they have:
questions.optionB: One loosely held valence electron with large atomic radius
questions.explanation: Alkali metals have one ns¹ valence electron far from nucleus with significant shielding. The electron is easily removed, giving very low first IE.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which pair of elements disproved Mendeleev's atomic mass-based classification?
questions.explanation: Te (atomic mass 127.6) appears before I (126.9) in atomic mass order, but Mendeleev placed I after Te based on properties. Moseley resolved this by showing Te (Z=52) < I (Z=53).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following gas laws states that at constant temperature, volume of a gas is inversely proportional to pressure?
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law (1662): At constant T, PV = constant (P ∝ 1/V). Doubling pressure halves volume.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Charles's law states that at constant pressure, volume of a gas is:
questions.explanation: Charles's Law: V ∝ T (at constant P), where T is in Kelvin. V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The ideal gas equation is:
questions.explanation: Ideal gas law: PV = nRT, where P = pressure, V = volume, n = moles, R = 8.314 J/mol·K, T = absolute temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The value of universal gas constant R is:
questions.optionC: Both A and B are correct
questions.explanation: R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ = 0.0821 L·atm·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ = 1.987 cal·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹. All are the same constant in different units.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Dalton's law of partial pressures states that the total pressure of a gas mixture is:
questions.optionB: Sum of partial pressures of all gases
questions.optionD: Equal to the largest partial pressure
questions.explanation: Dalton's Law: Ptotal = P₁ + P₂ + P₃ + ... Each gas exerts its pressure independently as if it alone occupied the container.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Graham's law of diffusion states that the rate of diffusion of a gas is:
questions.optionA: Directly proportional to its molecular mass
questions.optionB: Inversely proportional to the square root of its molecular mass
questions.explanation: Graham's Law: rate ∝ 1/√M. Lighter gases diffuse faster. r₁/r₂ = √(M₂/M₁).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Real gases deviate from ideal behavior most at:
questions.explanation: Real gases deviate most from ideal behavior at low temperature (molecules slow down, attractions matter more) and high pressure (molecules are close, volume effects important).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Van der Waals equation for real gases is:
questions.explanation: Van der Waals equation: (P + an²/V²)(V - nb) = nRT. 'a' corrects for intermolecular attractions; 'b' corrects for molecular volume.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The kinetic theory of gases assumes that gas molecules:
questions.optionA: Have significant volume and strong attractions
questions.optionB: Are point masses with no intermolecular forces between them
questions.explanation: Kinetic theory assumptions: molecules are point masses (negligible volume), no intermolecular attractions, elastic collisions, random motion, average KE ∝ absolute temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: According to kinetic theory, the average kinetic energy of gas molecules is:
questions.explanation: Average KE = (3/2)kT, where k = Boltzmann's constant and T = absolute temperature. Average KE is directly proportional to absolute temperature (in Kelvin).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Surface tension of a liquid is caused by:
questions.optionB: Unbalanced cohesive forces on surface molecules
questions.explanation: Surface tension arises because molecules at the surface experience net inward cohesive forces (unbalanced), while interior molecules are surrounded from all sides. This creates a tendency to minimize surface area.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Viscosity of a liquid:
questions.optionD: First increases then decreases with temperature
questions.explanation: Viscosity decreases as temperature increases because higher thermal energy overcomes intermolecular forces that resist flow. Water at 100°C flows more easily than at 20°C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Hydrogen bonding is strongest in:
questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonding requires H bonded to highly electronegative, small atom (F, O, N). HF has the strongest hydrogen bonds because F is the most electronegative element with smallest size.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The boiling point of a liquid is the temperature at which:
questions.explanation: A liquid boils when its vapour pressure equals the external (atmospheric) pressure. At this point, bubbles of vapour form throughout the liquid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Critical temperature of a gas is:
questions.optionB: Temperature above which gas cannot be liquefied by pressure alone
questions.optionC: Temperature at which gas has maximum kinetic energy
questions.optionD: Temperature at which gas behaves ideally
questions.explanation: Critical temperature (Tc): the temperature above which a gas cannot be liquefied, regardless of how much pressure is applied. CO₂: Tc = 31.1°C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Crystalline solids differ from amorphous solids in that crystalline solids have:
questions.explanation: Crystalline solids have regular, periodic (long-range ordered) arrangement of atoms/ions/molecules. Amorphous solids (glass, rubber) have random arrangement with no long-range order.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In a face-centred cubic (FCC) unit cell, the number of atoms per unit cell is:
questions.explanation: FCC: 8 corner atoms × (1/8) + 6 face atoms × (1/2) = 1 + 3 = 4 atoms per unit cell.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In a body-centred cubic (BCC) unit cell, the number of atoms per unit cell is:
questions.explanation: BCC: 8 corner atoms × (1/8) + 1 body-centre atom = 1 + 1 = 2 atoms per unit cell.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The coordination number in a simple cubic structure is:
questions.explanation: In simple cubic: each atom touches 6 nearest neighbours (top, bottom, left, right, front, back). Coordination number = 6.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Gay-Lussac's pressure-temperature law states that at constant volume, pressure of a gas is:
questions.explanation: Gay-Lussac's Law (pressure law): at constant V, P/T = constant, or P ∝ T (absolute temperature). Heating a sealed container increases pressure.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The root mean square (rms) speed of gas molecules is:
questions.explanation: Root mean square speed: u_rms = √(3RT/M), where R = gas constant, T = temperature (K), M = molar mass (kg/mol).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The compressibility factor Z for an ideal gas is:
questions.explanation: Compressibility factor Z = PV/nRT. For an ideal gas, PV = nRT, so Z = 1. Real gases have Z ≠ 1 (Z > 1 at high P, Z < 1 at moderate P).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: A gas occupies 2 L at 300 K. At constant pressure, the volume at 600 K is:
questions.explanation: Charles's Law: V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂. V₂ = V₁ × T₂/T₁ = 2 × 600/300 = 4 L.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: At STP, 5.6 L of gas corresponds to how many moles?
questions.explanation: Moles = volume/molar volume = 5.6/22.4 = 0.25 mol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In a closed-packed crystal structure (FCC/HCP), the coordination number is:
questions.explanation: In FCC (cubic close-packed) and HCP (hexagonal close-packed) structures, each atom is surrounded by 12 nearest neighbours. Coordination number = 12.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The pressure of a gas at constant volume is doubled. The temperature of the gas (initially at 300 K) becomes:
questions.explanation: Gay-Lussac's Law: P/T = constant. If P doubles: T₂ = T₁ × P₂/P₁ = 300 × 2 = 600 K.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Vapour pressure of a liquid increases with:
questions.explanation: Vapour pressure increases with temperature because more molecules gain enough energy to escape to the vapour phase. Adding non-volatile solute decreases vapour pressure (Raoult's Law).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which type of solid has the highest melting point?
questions.explanation: Network covalent solids (diamond, SiO₂, SiC) have extremely high melting points because covalent bonds must be broken throughout the 3D network. Diamond melts above 3500°C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The efficiency of packing in a simple cubic unit cell is approximately:
questions.explanation: Simple cubic packing efficiency = (1 atom × 4/3 πr³) / a³ = π/6 ≈ 52.4%. FCC has 74%, BCC has 68%.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The mean free path of a gas molecule is:
questions.optionB: The average distance travelled between two successive collisions
questions.optionC: The distance from wall to wall in a container
questions.explanation: Mean free path (λ): average distance a gas molecule travels between two successive collisions. It increases with decreasing pressure and increases with higher temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Oxidation is defined in terms of electron transfer as:
questions.explanation: Oxidation = Loss of electrons (OIL - Oxidation Is Loss). The oxidized species loses electrons to the oxidizing agent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Reduction is defined as:
questions.explanation: Reduction = Gain of electrons (RIG - Reduction Is Gain). The reduced species gains electrons from the reducing agent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The oxidation number of oxygen in H₂O₂ is:
questions.explanation: In H₂O₂: H = +1, so 2(+1) + 2x = 0. x = -1. Each oxygen has oxidation number -1 (not -2 as in most compounds).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The oxidation number of Mn in KMnO₄ is:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The oxidation number of Cr in K₂Cr₂O₇ is:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In a galvanic cell, oxidation occurs at the:
questions.explanation: In a galvanic (voltaic) cell: oxidation occurs at the anode (negative electrode); reduction occurs at the cathode (positive electrode). Mnemonic: An Ox (Anode Oxidation), Red Cat (Reduction Cathode).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The reducing agent in the reaction is the substance that:
questions.explanation: The reducing agent donates electrons (gets oxidized itself). It reduces the other species. Example: in Fe + CuSO₄ → FeSO₄ + Cu, Fe is the reducing agent (Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: What is the oxidation number of S in SO₄²⁻?

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the reaction Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂, the substance oxidized is:
questions.explanation: Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ (Zn loses electrons = oxidized). H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂ (H⁺ gains electrons = reduced). Zn is oxidized; H⁺ is reduced.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Disproportionation reaction involves:
questions.optionA: One element being both oxidized and reduced simultaneously
questions.explanation: Disproportionation: one element simultaneously undergoes both oxidation and reduction. Example: 2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂ (oxygen in H₂O₂ goes to -2 in H₂O (reduced) and 0 in O₂ (oxidized)).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The oxidation number of nitrogen in NH₃ is:
questions.explanation: In NH₃: N + 3(+1) = 0. N = -3. Nitrogen is in its lowest common oxidation state (-3) in ammonia.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following is the strongest oxidizing agent?
questions.explanation: F₂ is the strongest oxidizing agent. Fluorine has the highest electronegativity and the strongest tendency to gain electrons (F₂ + 2e⁻ → 2F⁻, E° = +2.87 V).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Balancing redox reactions in acidic medium requires adding:
questions.optionB: H₂O to balance oxygen, H⁺ to balance hydrogen
questions.explanation: In acidic medium: add H₂O to balance O atoms, then H⁺ to balance H atoms, then electrons to balance charge.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The oxidation number of oxygen in OF₂ is:
questions.explanation: In OF₂: F = -1 (always). So O + 2(-1) = 0. O = +2. Oxygen is +2 only when bonded to fluorine (F is more electronegative).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The number of electrons gained by 1 mol of MnO₄⁻ in acidic medium (MnO₄⁻ → Mn²⁺) is:
questions.explanation: Mn goes from +7 to +2: gain of 5 electrons. So 1 mol MnO₄⁻ gains 5 mol electrons (n-factor = 5).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: For a reversible reaction at equilibrium, the rate of forward reaction:
questions.optionB: Equals the rate of reverse reaction
questions.optionC: Is greater than the rate of reverse reaction
questions.optionD: Is less than the rate of reverse reaction
questions.explanation: At chemical equilibrium, the rate of forward reaction equals the rate of reverse reaction. Concentrations remain constant (not zero).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The equilibrium constant Kc for the reaction N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ is:
questions.explanation: Kc = [products]^(stoich. coeff) / [reactants]^(stoich. coeff) = [NH₃]²/([N₂][H₂]³).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: If Kc >> 1 for a reaction, it means:
questions.explanation: Kc >> 1 (Kc > 10³): equilibrium strongly favours products (high product concentration). Kc << 1: equilibrium favours reactants.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Le Chatelier's principle states that if a system at equilibrium is disturbed, the system will:
questions.optionC: Shift in the direction that reduces the disturbance
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium, when disturbed, shifts in the direction that counteracts the disturbance to re-establish equilibrium.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: For the reaction N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g) + heat, increasing pressure will:
questions.explanation: Increasing pressure shifts equilibrium toward fewer moles of gas. Left side: 1+3=4 mol gas. Right side: 2 mol gas. So equilibrium shifts right.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The relationship between Kp and Kc is:
questions.explanation: Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn, where Δn = moles of gaseous products - moles of gaseous reactants, R = 0.0821 L·atm/mol·K, T in Kelvin.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The degree of dissociation (α) for a reaction is defined as:
questions.optionA: Fraction of molecules that remain unreacted
questions.optionB: Fraction of molecules that have dissociated at equilibrium
questions.optionC: Total number of molecules at equilibrium
questions.optionD: Number of moles of products formed
questions.explanation: Degree of dissociation α = (moles dissociated) / (initial moles). α ranges from 0 (no dissociation) to 1 (complete dissociation).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: For the equilibrium: PCl₅(g) ⇌ PCl₃(g) + Cl₂(g), Kp = 1 atm. At equilibrium, adding inert gas at constant volume will:
questions.optionC: Have no effect on equilibrium position
questions.explanation: Adding inert gas at constant volume does not change partial pressures of reactants/products, so equilibrium position does not shift. Kp remains unchanged.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: A catalyst increases the rate of reaction by:
questions.explanation: A catalyst provides an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It speeds up both forward and reverse reactions equally, reaching equilibrium faster but NOT changing Kc.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: For the reaction: H₂(g) + I₂(g) ⇌ 2HI(g), Δn = 0. Which statement is correct?
questions.explanation: Since Δn = moles of gaseous products - reactants = 2 - 2 = 0. Kp = Kc(RT)⁰ = Kc × 1 = Kc.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: For an exothermic reversible reaction, increasing temperature will:
questions.optionA: Increase Kc and shift equilibrium right
questions.optionB: Decrease Kc and shift equilibrium left
questions.optionD: Increase rate of reverse reaction only
questions.explanation: For exothermic reaction, heat is a product. Increasing T shifts equilibrium left (Le Chatelier) and decreases Kc (Van't Hoff equation). Kc = A·e^(-ΔH/RT).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Reaction quotient Q is used to predict:
questions.optionB: The direction a reaction will proceed to reach equilibrium
questions.optionC: The activation energy of a reaction
questions.explanation: Q < Kc: reaction proceeds right (forward). Q > Kc: reaction proceeds left (reverse). Q = Kc: system is at equilibrium.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Haber process for ammonia synthesis uses the equilibrium: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃. The conditions used are high pressure and moderate temperature (450°C) because:
questions.optionA: High T favours NH₃; high P makes it faster
questions.optionB: High P increases yield (fewer gas moles on right); moderate T balances yield and rate
questions.optionD: High P and T both increase equilibrium constant
questions.explanation: High pressure favours NH₃ (shifts toward fewer moles of gas: 4→2). Very low T gives high equilibrium yield but very slow rate. 450°C is a compromise for adequate rate and reasonable yield.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Homogeneous equilibrium involves:
questions.optionA: Reactants and products in different phases
questions.optionB: Reactants and products in the same phase
questions.explanation: Homogeneous equilibrium: all species (reactants and products) are in the same phase (all gas or all aqueous solution). Heterogeneous equilibrium involves multiple phases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the equilibrium expression for CaCO₃(s) ⇌ CaO(s) + CO₂(g), the Kc expression is:
questions.explanation: Concentrations of pure solids are constant (activity = 1) and omitted from Kc expression. Only [CO₂] appears: Kc = [CO₂].

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: According to Arrhenius theory, an acid is a substance that:
questions.optionC: Produces H⁺ ions in aqueous solution
questions.explanation: Arrhenius acid: produces H⁺ (or H₃O⁺) ions in aqueous solution. Example: HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻. Arrhenius base: produces OH⁻ ions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: According to Brønsted-Lowry theory, an acid is:
questions.explanation: Brønsted-Lowry acid: a proton (H⁺) donor. Base: proton acceptor. Example: HCl (acid) + H₂O (base) → H₃O⁺ + Cl⁻.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The pH of a solution with [H⁺] = 10⁻⁴ M is:
questions.explanation: pH = -log[H⁺] = -log(10⁻⁴) = 4. pH < 7: acidic; pH = 7: neutral; pH > 7: basic (at 25°C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: For a weak acid HA ⇌ H⁺ + A⁻ with Ka = 10⁻⁵, the acid is:
questions.explanation: Ka = 10⁻⁵ is small, indicating partial dissociation. Ka > 1: strong acid; Ka << 1 (e.g., 10⁻⁵): weak acid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The ionic product of water (Kw) at 25°C is:
questions.explanation: Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 10⁻⁷ × 10⁻⁷ = 10⁻¹⁴ mol²/L² at 25°C. In neutral water, [H⁺] = [OH⁻] = 10⁻⁷ M.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: A buffer solution resists change in pH when:
questions.optionA: Large amounts of acid or base are added
questions.optionB: Small amounts of acid or base are added
questions.explanation: Buffer solutions resist pH change when small amounts of acid or base are added. They consist of a weak acid + its conjugate base (or weak base + conjugate acid).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation for a buffer is:
questions.explanation: Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]) = pKa + log([salt]/[acid]).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Hydrolysis of a salt of strong acid and weak base produces a:
questions.explanation: Salt of strong acid + weak base (e.g., NH₄Cl): NH₄⁺ hydrolyzes → NH₃ + H⁺. The solution is acidic (pH < 7).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The solubility product Ksp of AgCl = 1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰. Its molar solubility is:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Lewis acid is defined as:
questions.explanation: Lewis acid: electron pair acceptor. Lewis base: electron pair donor. Example: BF₃ (Lewis acid) + NH₃ (Lewis base) → F₃B←NH₃.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: For an amphoteric substance, it can act as:
questions.optionC: Both an acid and a base
questions.explanation: Amphoteric substances can act as both acid and base. Examples: water (H₂O), amino acids, Al(OH)₃. Water: H₂O acts as acid (donates H⁺) or base (accepts H⁺).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The common ion effect states that addition of a common ion to a sparingly soluble salt:
questions.explanation: Common ion effect: adding a common ion (already present in the equilibrium) shifts equilibrium left, decreasing solubility of the sparingly soluble salt. Ksp remains constant.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: pOH of a solution is 4. Its pH at 25°C is:
questions.explanation: At 25°C: pH + pOH = 14. pH = 14 - pOH = 14 - 4 = 10. This is an alkaline solution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: A salt that undergoes anionic hydrolysis to produce a basic solution is formed from:
questions.explanation: Salt of weak acid + strong base (e.g., CH₃COONa): CH₃COO⁻ + H₂O ⇌ CH₃COOH + OH⁻. The anion hydrolyzes producing OH⁻, giving basic solution (pH > 7).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The degree of hydrolysis of a salt depends on:
questions.optionB: Temperature and the Ka or Kb of the weak acid/base
questions.explanation: Degree of hydrolysis depends on: (1) Ka of weak acid (or Kb of weak base) - weaker acids/bases hydrolyze more; (2) temperature (increases with T); (3) concentration (increases with dilution).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In electrolysis, reduction occurs at the:
questions.explanation: In electrolysis (and galvanic cells): reduction always at cathode, oxidation always at anode. In electrolysis, cathode is connected to negative terminal of battery.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Faraday's first law of electrolysis states that the mass deposited is:
questions.optionA: Proportional to the square of current
questions.optionB: Proportional to the quantity of electricity (charge) passed
questions.explanation: Faraday's First Law: mass deposited (m) ∝ quantity of electricity (Q = It). m = ZIt = ZQ, where Z = electrochemical equivalent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: One Faraday of electricity is equivalent to:
questions.explanation: 1 Faraday = 96,500 C ≈ 96,500 A·s. It represents the charge carried by 1 mole of electrons (Nₐ × e = 6.022×10²³ × 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ ≈ 96,500 C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The EMF of a galvanic cell is related to ΔG by:
questions.explanation: ΔG = -nFE, where n = moles of electrons transferred, F = Faraday constant (96,500 C/mol), E = cell EMF. Spontaneous reactions have ΔG < 0, so E > 0.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Nernst equation for electrode potential is:
questions.explanation: Nernst equation: E = E° - (RT/nF) ln Q. At 25°C: E = E° - (0.0592/n) log Q. It relates electrode potential to concentration.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The standard hydrogen electrode (SHE) has a standard electrode potential of:
questions.explanation: SHE (Standard Hydrogen Electrode): H⁺(1M) + e⁻ → ½H₂(1 atm) has E° = 0.00 V by convention. All other electrode potentials are measured relative to SHE.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Kohlrausch's law states that at infinite dilution, the molar conductivity of an electrolyte equals:
questions.optionC: Difference of cationic and anionic conductivities
questions.explanation: Kohlrausch's Law: λ°m = λ⁺ + λ⁻. At infinite dilution, molar conductivity equals the sum of limiting molar conductivities of the individual ions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Specific conductance (κ) of an electrolyte increases with:
questions.optionC: Increasing concentration (up to a point for strong electrolytes)
questions.explanation: For strong electrolytes, κ increases with concentration (more ions present). However, molar conductivity (Λm = κ×1000/c) decreases with concentration due to interionic interactions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the electrolysis of dilute H₂SO₄ solution, at the cathode:
questions.explanation: At cathode (reduction): 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂(g). At anode (oxidation): 2H₂O → O₂ + 4H⁺ + 4e⁻.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Faraday's second law of electrolysis states that masses of different substances deposited by same quantity of electricity are:
questions.optionD: Inversely proportional to their atomic masses
questions.explanation: Faraday's Second Law: For the same quantity of electricity, masses of different substances deposited are in ratio of their equivalent masses (Molar mass/n-factor).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The rate of a chemical reaction is defined as:
questions.optionB: Change in concentration of reactant or product per unit time
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = -Δ[reactant]/Δt = +Δ[product]/Δt. It has units of mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The rate constant k in the Arrhenius equation depends on:
questions.explanation: Arrhenius equation: k = A·e^(-Ea/RT), where A = frequency factor, Ea = activation energy, R = gas constant, T = temperature. k depends on T and Ea.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: A first-order reaction has the characteristic that:
questions.optionC: Rate is proportional to reactant concentration; half-life is constant
questions.optionD: Rate is proportional to concentration squared
questions.explanation: First-order reaction: rate = k[A]. Half-life t₁/₂ = 0.693/k (constant, independent of initial concentration). Unit of k = s⁻¹.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The activation energy (Ea) can be determined from the slope of the plot:
questions.explanation: From Arrhenius equation: ln k = ln A - Ea/RT. Plot of ln k vs 1/T gives a straight line with slope = -Ea/R. Ea = -slope × R.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The order of reaction with rate = k[A]²[B] is:
questions.explanation: Order = sum of all exponents in rate law = 2 + 1 = 3. It is a third-order reaction (second order in A, first order in B).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: For a zero-order reaction, the rate is:
questions.optionD: Proportional to square root of concentration
questions.explanation: Zero-order reaction: rate = k (constant), independent of reactant concentration. The graph of [A] vs time is a straight line with slope = -k.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The molecularity of a reaction refers to:
questions.optionA: The number of moles of reactants
questions.optionB: The number of molecules/ions colliding in the rate-determining step
questions.explanation: Molecularity: number of reacting species (molecules, atoms, ions) in the elementary step (especially rate-determining step). Always a whole number: 1 (unimolecular), 2 (bimolecular), 3 (termolecular).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Increasing temperature increases reaction rate mainly because:
questions.optionB: More molecules have energy ≥ activation energy
questions.explanation: At higher T, more molecules have kinetic energy ≥ activation energy (Ea). This increases the fraction of effective collisions. As a rough rule, rate doubles for every 10°C rise (Q₁₀ ≈ 2).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: For a first-order reaction, if initial concentration is 0.1 M and k = 0.693 min⁻¹, the half-life is:
questions.explanation: t₁/₂ = 0.693/k = 0.693/0.693 = 1 min. Half-life of first-order reaction is independent of initial concentration.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: A covalent bond is formed by:
questions.optionA: Transfer of electrons from one atom to another
questions.optionB: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.optionC: Electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions
questions.explanation: Covalent bond: formed by sharing of electron pair(s) between two atoms. Each atom contributes one electron to each shared pair (or two in coordinate bond).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The VSEPR theory predicts that the shape of H₂O is:
questions.explanation: H₂O: 2 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs around O. VSEPR: lone pairs repel more than bonding pairs. Shape is bent with H-O-H angle of ~104.5° (less than tetrahedral 109.5°).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: According to VBT (Valence Bond Theory), sigma (σ) bonds are formed by:
questions.explanation: σ bond: formed by head-on (axial/frontal) overlap of orbitals along the internuclear axis. π bond: formed by lateral (sideways) overlap of p-orbitals above and below the axis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In sp³ hybridization, the bond angle is approximately:
questions.explanation: sp³ hybridization: 4 equivalent sp³ orbitals arranged tetrahedrally with bond angle 109.5°. Example: CH₄ (methane), NH₃ (pyramidal), H₂O (bent).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The bond angle in BF₃ (sp² hybridized, trigonal planar) is:
questions.explanation: sp² hybridization: 3 equivalent orbitals in a plane with 120° angles. BF₃ is trigonal planar with F-B-F = 120°. Also: SO₃, C₂H₄ (around each C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Ionic bonds are formed between:
questions.optionB: A metal and a non-metal with large electronegativity difference
questions.explanation: Ionic bond: formed by electron transfer from metal (low IE) to non-metal (high EA), when electronegativity difference is typically >1.7. Example: NaCl, MgO.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The number of moles of CO₂ produced when 12 g of carbon (atomic mass 12) is burned completely is:
questions.explanation: C + O₂ → CO₂. Moles of C = 12/12 = 1 mol. 1 mol C produces 1 mol CO₂.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: What volume of O₂ at STP is required to burn 4 g of hydrogen gas?
questions.explanation: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O. Moles of H₂ = 4/2 = 2 mol. Moles of O₂ = 1 mol. Volume = 22.4 L at STP.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The normality of a 2 M H₃PO₄ solution (when all three protons react) is:
questions.explanation: H₃PO₄ has n-factor = 3 (triprotic). Normality = Molarity × n-factor = 2 × 3 = 6 N.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: What mass of CaCO₃ (MW = 100) must be heated to obtain 11.2 L of CO₂ at STP?
questions.explanation: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂. 11.2 L CO₂ = 0.5 mol. Moles CaCO₃ = 0.5 mol. Mass = 0.5 × 100 = 50 g.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The percentage of nitrogen in ammonium sulphate [(NH₄)₂SO₄, MW = 132] is approximately:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In 500 mL of 0.1 M NaCl solution, the number of Cl⁻ ions is:
questions.explanation: Moles NaCl = 0.1 × 0.5 = 0.05 mol. Each NaCl gives 1 Cl⁻. Cl⁻ ions = 0.05 × 6.022×10²³ = 3.011×10²².

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The molarity of pure water (density = 1 g/mL, MW = 18) is:
questions.explanation: 1 L of water = 1000 g. Moles = 1000/18 = 55.5 mol. Molarity = 55.5 mol/L = 55.5 M.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The empirical formula of benzene (C₆H₆) is:
questions.explanation: Ratio C:H in C₆H₆ = 6:6 = 1:1. Simplest formula = CH. Empirical formula of benzene = CH (actual molecular formula is C₆H₆).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: A gas has vapour density 22. Its molar mass is:
questions.explanation: Molar mass = 2 × vapour density = 2 × 22 = 44 g/mol. This corresponds to CO₂ or C₃H₈.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: If 5 g of a substance contains 0.125 mol, its molar mass is:
questions.explanation: Molar mass = mass/moles = 5/0.125 = 40 g/mol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The energy required to remove an electron from the ground state of a hydrogen atom is:
questions.explanation: Ionization energy of H from ground state (n=1): E = 13.6/1² = 13.6 eV. This is the binding energy of the electron in ground state.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The wavelength of the first line of Balmer series (Hα line) is approximately:
questions.explanation: First Balmer line (n=3→n=2): λ ≈ 656 nm, in red region of visible spectrum. This is the H-alpha (Hα) line.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The total number of electrons in the p-subshells of nitrogen (N, Z=7) is:
questions.explanation: N: 1s² 2s² 2p³. The 2p subshell has 3 electrons (one in each of 2px, 2py, 2pz by Hund's rule).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: An electron in the 2p subshell has which set of quantum numbers?
questions.explanation: 2p: n=2 (second shell), l=1 (p subshell, l=1). ml can be -1, 0, or +1. Valid: n=2, l=1, ml=0 (or ±1).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The number of angular nodes in a 3p orbital is:
questions.explanation: Angular nodes = l. For 3p orbital (l=1): 1 angular node. Radial nodes = n-l-1 = 3-1-1 = 1. Total nodes = n-1 = 2.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following quantum number sets is NOT valid?
questions.explanation: For l=2, ml can range from -2 to +2. ml=3 is NOT valid. This set violates the quantum number rule.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The spectrum of hydrogen is a line spectrum (not continuous) because:
questions.optionB: Electrons can only exist in specific energy levels
questions.explanation: Electrons exist in discrete energy levels. Emission occurs only when electrons fall from higher to lower specific levels, releasing photons of specific energies → specific wavelengths → line spectrum.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Dual nature of matter (wave-particle duality) was demonstrated for electrons by:
questions.optionB: Davisson and Germer electron diffraction experiment
questions.explanation: Davisson and Germer (1927) observed electron diffraction by nickel crystal, confirming de Broglie's wave nature of electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The mass of an electron is approximately:
questions.explanation: Mass of electron = 9.109 × 10⁻³¹ kg ≈ 1/1837 amu. Mass of proton = 1.67 × 10⁻²⁷ kg. Electron is ~1836 times lighter than proton.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The charge-to-mass ratio (e/m) was determined for electrons by:
questions.explanation: J.J. Thomson (1897) determined e/m ratio of cathode rays = 1.76 × 10¹¹ C/kg. Millikan later measured the charge e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The maximum number of electrons in the f-subshell is:
questions.explanation: f subshell has 7 orbitals (l=3, m_l = -3 to +3). Each holds 2 electrons. Maximum = 7 × 2 = 14 electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the photoelectric effect, the kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons:
questions.optionA: Increases with increasing intensity of light
questions.optionB: Increases with increasing frequency of incident light
questions.explanation: KE = hν - φ. KE increases with increasing frequency (ν) of incident light, not intensity. Increasing intensity increases number of electrons, not their KE.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Bohr's model of the atom failed to explain:
questions.optionB: Fine structure of spectral lines and spectra of multi-electron atoms
questions.optionD: The stability of circular electron orbits
questions.explanation: Bohr's model succeeds for hydrogen and hydrogen-like ions but fails to explain: fine structure (Zeeman/Stark effects), spectra of multi-electron atoms, and the wave nature of electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The specific charge (e/m) of a proton compared to an electron is:
questions.explanation: e/m for electron ≈ 1.76 × 10¹¹ C/kg; for proton ≈ 9.58 × 10⁷ C/kg. Since proton mass >> electron mass and charges are equal, proton's e/m is much smaller.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Rydberg constant for hydrogen is approximately:
questions.explanation: Rydberg constant R_H = 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹. Used in: 1/λ = R_H(1/n₁² - 1/n₂²) to calculate wavelengths of hydrogen spectral lines.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The element with the highest first ionization energy among all elements is:
questions.explanation: Helium has the highest first ionization energy (2372 kJ/mol) among all elements, because it has a filled 1s² shell with the strongest nuclear attraction relative to electron shielding.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following has the largest atomic radius?
questions.explanation: Going down Group 14: C < Si < Ge < Sn < Pb. Atomic radius increases as new electron shells are added. Sn (n=5) is largest among the options.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The property that describes the ease with which an atom in a molecule can be oxidized (electron density transferred away) is:
questions.explanation: Electronegativity determines how strongly an atom pulls electron density in bonds. Highly electronegative atoms (F, O) pull electron density away from less electronegative atoms, effectively oxidizing them.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Among the alkali metals, which is most reactive toward water?
questions.explanation: Reactivity of alkali metals with water increases down the group: Li < Na < K < Rb < Cs. Cs is most reactive (lowest IE, largest radius), reacting explosively with water.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The oxidation state of chlorine in HClO₄ (perchloric acid) is:
questions.explanation: In HClO₄: H = +1, O = -2. +1 + Cl + 4(-2) = 0. Cl = +7. Cl shows +7 oxidation state in perchloric acid and perchlorates.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Paramagnetism of transition metals is due to:
questions.explanation: Paramagnetism: substances with unpaired electrons are weakly attracted to magnetic fields. Transition metals have unpaired electrons in d-orbitals, making many of them paramagnetic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which group of the periodic table contains only non-metals?
questions.explanation: Group 17 (halogens: F, Cl, Br, I, At) are all non-metals. Group 1 has H (non-metal), Li-Cs (metals). Group 14 has C, Si (non-metals/metalloids) and Ge, Sn, Pb (metalloids/metals).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The property of an element to exist in more than one physical form in the same phase is called:
questions.explanation: Allotropy: an element existing in two or more different forms in the same physical state. Example: carbon (diamond, graphite, fullerene); phosphorus (white, red, black); oxygen (O₂, O₃).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The first ionization energy of Be is greater than that of B because:
questions.optionB: Be has completely filled 2s² which is more stable than B's 2p¹
questions.explanation: Be (1s²2s²) has a completely filled 2s subshell — more stable. B (1s²2s²2p¹) has one electron in higher-energy 2p orbital, which is easier to remove. Hence IE(Be) > IE(B).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following has the smallest size?
questions.explanation: Mg²⁺ has the smallest size: it has 12 protons holding only 10 electrons (same as Na⁺ which has 11 protons with 10 electrons, but Mg²⁺ has stronger nuclear attraction).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Lanthanide contraction refers to:
questions.optionA: Increase in atomic radius along lanthanides
questions.optionB: Decrease in atomic and ionic radii along lanthanides
questions.explanation: Lanthanide contraction: atomic and ionic radii decrease along the lanthanide series (Ce to Lu) because the 4f electrons provide poor shielding. This makes the 5th and 6th period transition metals nearly equal in size.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The second electron affinity (addition of second electron) of oxygen is:
questions.explanation: Second electron affinity of O (O⁻ + e⁻ → O²⁻) is positive (+798 kJ/mol) because adding an electron to an already negative ion requires overcoming electron-electron repulsion.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Across a period, the non-metallic character:
questions.explanation: Across a period, Zeff increases → higher tendency to gain electrons → increasing non-metallic character. Na (metal) → Cl (non-metal) → Ar (noble gas) in Period 3.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The correct order of ionic radii for isoelectronic ions N³⁻, O²⁻, F⁻, Na⁺, Mg²⁺ is:
questions.explanation: All have 10 electrons. More protons = stronger nuclear pull = smaller radius. Z: N(7) < O(8) < F(9) < Na(11) < Mg(12). So N³⁻ is largest, Mg²⁺ smallest.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which element is an exception to the octet rule in its ground state electronic configuration?
questions.explanation: Sulfur in SF₆ has 12 electrons around it (expanded octet), violating the octet rule. d-orbitals of 3rd period and beyond elements allow this. H violates the octet rule by having only 2 electrons (duet).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: At constant temperature, if the pressure of a gas is doubled, its volume becomes:
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: PV = constant. If P doubles, V is halved (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂ → 2P₁V₂ = P₁V₁ → V₂ = V₁/2).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Absolute zero (0 K) is the temperature at which:
questions.explanation: At absolute zero (0 K = -273.15°C), the kinetic energy of molecules theoretically becomes zero (molecular motion ceases). In practice, quantum effects prevent this.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The van der Waals constant 'a' represents:
questions.explanation: In van der Waals equation, 'a' corrects for intermolecular attractions (reduces observed pressure below ideal). 'b' corrects for finite molecular volume (reduces available volume).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: At what temperature does a gas at 27°C need to be heated for its volume to triple at constant pressure?
questions.explanation: Charles's Law: V/T = constant. T₁ = 300 K. For V₂ = 3V₁: T₂ = 3T₁ = 900 K = 627°C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In a simple cubic crystal, the packing efficiency is lowest because:
questions.optionB: There are large void spaces between atoms (52% efficiency)
questions.optionD: It has the most atoms per unit cell
questions.explanation: Simple cubic has only 52% packing efficiency — the lowest of all cubic structures. The atoms touch only along edges, leaving large void spaces. Most metals prefer FCC or BCC.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The temperature above which a substance can only exist as gas (cannot be liquefied by pressure) is:
questions.explanation: Critical temperature (Tc): above this, gas cannot be liquefied by pressure alone. Below Tc, compression can liquefy the gas. For CO₂, Tc = 31.1°C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The unit cell that contains atoms only at the corners is:
questions.explanation: Simple cubic (SC): atoms only at 8 corners. BCC: corners + 1 body-centre. FCC: corners + 6 face-centres. Atoms per SC unit cell = 8 × (1/8) = 1.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which has stronger intermolecular forces — H₂O or H₂S?
questions.optionB: H₂O, because of stronger hydrogen bonding
questions.optionD: H₂S, because of higher molecular mass
questions.explanation: H₂O has strong hydrogen bonds (O-H...O) because O is highly electronegative and small. H₂S only has weaker van der Waals forces. Hence H₂O has much higher boiling point (100°C vs -60°C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The pressure of a gas in a container is due to:
questions.optionB: Collisions of gas molecules with container walls
questions.explanation: Gas pressure is caused by continuous collisions of gas molecules with the container walls. Force per unit area from these collisions gives pressure (P = F/A).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The rate of diffusion of NH₃ compared to HCl (given M(NH₃)=17, M(HCl)=36.5) is:
questions.explanation: Graham's Law: r(NH₃)/r(HCl) = √(36.5/17) = √2.15 ≈ 1.47. NH₃ diffuses 1.47 times faster (lighter molecule).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The oxidation number of sulfur in Na₂S₂O₃ (sodium thiosulfate) is:
questions.explanation: In Na₂S₂O₃: 2(+1) + 2S + 3(-2) = 0. 2S = 4. S = +2. (Actually, one S is +5 and one is -1, average = +2, or calculated directly as above).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the reaction: MnO₄⁻ + Fe²⁺ → Mn²⁺ + Fe³⁺ (acidic medium), the oxidizing agent is:
questions.explanation: MnO₄⁻ is reduced (Mn: +7 → +2), so MnO₄⁻ is the oxidizing agent. Fe²⁺ is oxidized (Fe: +2 → +3), so Fe²⁺ is the reducing agent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The oxidation number of carbon in CO₃²⁻ is:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The change in oxidation number of Cr in the reaction: K₂Cr₂O₇ + ... → CrCl₃ is:
questions.explanation: In K₂Cr₂O₇, Cr = +6. In CrCl₃, Cr = +3. Cr changes from +6 to +3 (gain of 3 electrons = reduction). K₂Cr₂O₇ acts as oxidizing agent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the electrolytic refining of copper, the impure copper acts as:
questions.explanation: In electrolytic refining: impure metal = anode (dissolves oxidatively), pure metal = cathode (deposits by reduction), electrolyte = metal salt solution. Impurities fall as 'anode mud'.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The process of removing oxygen from a compound is called:
questions.explanation: Reduction can be defined as: (1) gain of electrons, (2) loss of oxygen, (3) gain of hydrogen. Removing oxygen from a compound is a form of reduction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Identify the element that is oxidized in: 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl
questions.optionC: Both Na and Cl are oxidized
questions.explanation: Na → Na⁺ + e⁻ (oxidation, loses electrons). Cl₂ + 2e⁻ → 2Cl⁻ (reduction, gains electrons). Na is oxidized; Cl₂ is reduced.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Identify the substance that acts as both oxidizing and reducing agent (autodilution or disproportionation) in:  Cl₂ + NaOH → NaCl + NaClO + H₂O
questions.explanation: In this reaction, Cl₂ undergoes disproportionation: one Cl atom is reduced from 0 to -1 (in NaCl) and another is oxidized from 0 to +1 (in NaClO). Cl₂ acts as both oxidizing and reducing agent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The strongest reducing agent among the alkali metals is:
questions.optionA: Li (due to most negative reduction potential E° = -3.05 V)
questions.explanation: Li has the most negative standard reduction potential (E° = -3.05 V), making it the strongest reducing agent among alkali metals. Despite being smallest, Li's high hydration enthalpy makes it most easily oxidized in aqueous solution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The term 'oxidation state' and 'valence' of an element:
questions.optionB: Are often different — valence is always positive
questions.optionC: Are the same only for non-metals
questions.optionD: Are both always zero for elements
questions.explanation: Oxidation state can be positive, negative, or zero. Valence is the combining capacity (always positive or zero). Example: N in NH₃ has valence 3, oxidation state -3.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: At equilibrium, the equilibrium constant Kc:
questions.explanation: Kc depends only on temperature. Changing concentrations, pressure, or adding a catalyst shifts the position of equilibrium but does NOT change Kc.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: For the reaction: 2SO₂ + O₂ ⇌ 2SO₃, Kc = 100 M⁻¹. The equilibrium favours:
questions.explanation: Kc = 100 >> 1, so at equilibrium products are favoured. This is the basis of the Contact Process for H₂SO₄ manufacture.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: For the dissolution equilibrium: CO₂(g) ⇌ CO₂(aq), increasing pressure:
questions.explanation: Increasing pressure shifts equilibrium right, causing more CO₂ to dissolve (Henry's Law). This is why carbonated beverages are bottled under high CO₂ pressure.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The unit of Kc for the reaction: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ is:
questions.explanation: Kc = [NH₃]²/([N₂][H₂]³) = (mol/L)²/((mol/L)(mol/L)³) = (mol²/L²)/(mol⁴/L⁴) = L²/mol² = L² mol⁻².

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The equilibrium constant Kc for the reverse reaction is:
questions.optionA: Same as Kc for forward reaction
questions.explanation: If the forward reaction has Kc = K, then the reverse reaction has Kc = 1/K. When a reaction is reversed, the equilibrium constant is inverted.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Ka of acetic acid (CH₃COOH) is 1.8 × 10⁻⁵. The pH of 0.1 M acetic acid solution is approximately:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The aqueous solution of which salt will be neutral (pH ≈ 7)?
questions.explanation: NaCl: salt of strong acid (HCl) and strong base (NaOH). No hydrolysis occurs. pH = 7 (neutral). NH₄Cl: acidic. Na₂CO₃: basic. CH₃COONa: basic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The indicator used for strong acid-strong base titration is:
questions.optionC: Both A and B are suitable
questions.explanation: For strong acid-strong base titration, the pH changes sharply near the equivalence point (covering pH 3-11). Both methyl orange and phenolphthalein are suitable indicators.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The Ksp of BaSO₄ is 1.1 × 10⁻¹⁰. Adding excess Ba²⁺ to a BaSO₄ solution will:
questions.optionB: Decrease solubility of BaSO₄ (common ion effect)
questions.explanation: Adding Ba²⁺ (common ion) to BaSO₄ equilibrium shifts it left, decreasing [SO₄²⁻] and reducing BaSO₄ solubility. This is the common ion effect.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Buffer capacity is maximum when:
questions.explanation: Maximum buffer capacity: when [HA] = [A⁻], the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation gives pH = pKa. At this point, equal amounts of acid and conjugate base are present to neutralize added base or acid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In a Daniel cell (Zn-Cu cell), the overall reaction is:
questions.explanation: Daniell cell: Anode: Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ (oxidation). Cathode: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (reduction). Net: Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The EMF of the Daniell cell (Zn|Zn²⁺||Cu²⁺|Cu) given E°(Zn²⁺/Zn) = -0.76 V and E°(Cu²⁺/Cu) = +0.34 V is:
questions.explanation: E°cell = E°cathode - E°anode = E°(Cu²⁺/Cu) - E°(Zn²⁺/Zn) = 0.34 - (-0.76) = 1.10 V.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The process of coating an iron object with zinc to prevent rusting is called:
questions.explanation: Galvanization: coating iron with zinc to prevent rusting. Zinc acts as a sacrificial anode (more reactive than iron). Even if Zn coating breaks, Zn oxidizes preferentially protecting Fe.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In electrolysis of molten NaCl, the product at cathode is:
questions.explanation: Molten NaCl electrolysis: Cathode: Na⁺ + e⁻ → Na(l). Anode: 2Cl⁻ → Cl₂(g) + 2e⁻. This is the industrial method (Down's process) for producing sodium metal.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The unit of the rate constant k for a second-order reaction is:
questions.explanation: For a second-order reaction: rate = k[A]². k = rate/[A]² = (mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹)/(mol L⁻¹)² = L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The temperature coefficient (Q₁₀) of most reactions is approximately:
questions.explanation: Q₁₀ ≈ 2 for most biochemical and chemical reactions, meaning the rate approximately doubles for every 10°C rise in temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Pseudo-first order reaction is a reaction that:
questions.optionB: Appears to be first-order because one reactant is in large excess (concentration constant)
questions.optionD: Has a rate constant of one
questions.explanation: Pseudo-first order: A second-order reaction where one reactant is in huge excess (constant concentration), making it appear first-order. Example: acid hydrolysis of ester in excess water.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the rate expression Rate = k[A]^m[B]^n, the order with respect to A is m and with respect to B is n. The overall order is:
questions.explanation: Overall order of reaction = sum of all exponents in the rate expression = m + n.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The hybridization of carbon in CO₂ is:
questions.explanation: CO₂: O=C=O (two double bonds, no lone pairs on C). Two bonding regions → sp hybridization → linear geometry (180°).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The shape of NH₃ according to VSEPR theory is:
questions.explanation: NH₃: 3 bonding pairs + 1 lone pair around N. Electronic geometry: tetrahedral. Molecular shape: trigonal pyramidal (lone pair not counted in molecular shape). H-N-H angle ≈ 107°.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In a coordinate (dative) covalent bond, both electrons are contributed by:
questions.explanation: Coordinate (dative) bond: both electrons of the shared pair come from one atom (Lewis base/donor). Example: NH₃ + H⁺ → NH₄⁺ (N donates lone pair to H⁺).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following has the shortest bond length?
questions.explanation: Bond length decreases as bond order increases: C-C (154 pm) > C=C (134 pm) > C≡C (120 pm). Triple bonds have highest bond order (3) and shortest length.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The hybridization of phosphorus in PCl₅ is:
questions.explanation: PCl₅: 5 bonding pairs, no lone pairs on P. sp³d hybridization → trigonal bipyramidal geometry (90° and 120° bond angles).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Resonance structures of a molecule are:
questions.optionC: Different representations of same molecule with electrons in different positions
questions.explanation: Resonance structures: different Lewis structures for the same molecule, differing only in electron (not atom) positions. The real structure is a hybrid (average) of all resonance forms. Example: benzene, ozone.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The bond angle in CH₄ is 109.5°. In NH₃ it is ~107° and in H₂O it is ~104.5°. This trend is due to:
questions.optionB: Lone pair-bond pair repulsion being greater than bond pair-bond pair repulsion
questions.explanation: Lone pair (LP) repels more than bonding pair (BP): LP-LP > LP-BP > BP-BP. NH₃ has 1 LP (107°), H₂O has 2 LP (104.5°), CH₄ has 0 LP (109.5°). More LPs → smaller bond angle.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: According to Molecular Orbital Theory (MOT), which of the following is paramagnetic?
questions.explanation: O₂ MOT configuration: (σ1s)²(σ*1s)²(σ2s)²(σ*2s)²(σ2p)²(π2p)⁴(π*2p)² — two unpaired electrons in π* orbitals → paramagnetic. This is a famous success of MOT (VBT predicts diamagnetic O₂, incorrectly).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The bond order in O₂ molecule according to MOT is:
questions.explanation: O₂ MO configuration: bonding electrons = 8, antibonding = 4. Bond order = (8-4)/2 = 2. O₂ has a double bond and two unpaired electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: London dispersion forces are present in:
questions.explanation: London (dispersion) forces arise from temporary instantaneous dipoles and exist in ALL atoms and molecules. They are the only intermolecular forces in noble gases and non-polar molecules.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The electronegativity difference required for a bond to be considered predominantly ionic is generally:
questions.explanation: Electronegativity difference > 1.7 generally indicates ionic character > 50%. The bond is considered predominantly ionic. For ΔEN < 0.5: nonpolar covalent; 0.5-1.7: polar covalent; > 1.7: ionic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The bond order in N₂ molecule is:
questions.explanation: N₂ has a triple bond (N≡N): 1 σ bond + 2 π bonds. Bond order = 3. This gives N₂ a very short bond length (110 pm) and high bond dissociation energy (945 kJ/mol).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: For a reaction, activation energy Ea = 0. This means:
questions.optionB: Reaction rate is independent of temperature
questions.optionC: Reaction is spontaneous at all temperatures
questions.explanation: Arrhenius equation: k = A·e^(-Ea/RT). If Ea = 0: k = A·e⁰ = A = constant. The rate constant (and reaction rate) is independent of temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The integrated rate law for a first-order reaction A → products is:
questions.explanation: First-order integrated rate law: ln[A] = ln[A]₀ - kt, or [A] = [A]₀·e^(-kt). Plot of ln[A] vs time gives straight line with slope = -k.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: A reaction is found to be second order when concentrations of both A and B are doubled, the rate becomes:
questions.explanation: Rate = k[A][B] (second order: first in A, first in B). Doubling both: New rate = k(2[A])(2[B]) = 4k[A][B] = 4 × original rate.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The half-life of a second-order reaction depends on:
questions.optionC: Both rate constant and initial concentration
questions.explanation: Second-order half-life: t₁/₂ = 1/(k[A]₀). Unlike first-order (constant t₁/₂ = 0.693/k), second-order half-life depends on both k and initial concentration [A]₀.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The standard cell potential for a spontaneous electrochemical cell must be:
questions.explanation: For a spontaneous cell: ΔG = -nFE° < 0, which requires E°cell > 0. A positive E° indicates the cell reaction is spontaneous under standard conditions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Molar conductivity (Λm) of a strong electrolyte varies with concentration (c) as:
questions.explanation: Debye-Hückel-Onsager equation: Λm = Λ°m - K√c (for strong electrolytes). Plot of Λm vs √c is linear with intercept = Λ°m (molar conductivity at infinite dilution).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: During the electrolysis of aqueous NaCl (brine), the product at anode is:
questions.explanation: Aqueous NaCl electrolysis (chlor-alkali process): Cathode: 2H₂O + 2e⁻ → H₂ + 2OH⁻. Anode: 2Cl⁻ → Cl₂ + 2e⁻. Cl₂ is preferred over O₂ at anode due to overpotential.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Dipole moment of a molecule depends on:
questions.optionC: Both bond polarity and molecular geometry
questions.explanation: Dipole moment (μ) depends on both bond polarity (magnitude of partial charges) and molecular geometry (direction and cancellation of bond dipoles). CO₂ is linear, so bond dipoles cancel: μ = 0. H₂O is bent: μ ≠ 0.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: The VSEPR predicted shape of SF₆ is:
questions.explanation: SF₆: 6 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs on S (sp³d² hybridization) → octahedral geometry with F-S-F = 90°.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In metallic bonding, the metallic properties (electrical conductivity, malleability) are due to:
questions.explanation: Metallic bond: metal cations are surrounded by a 'sea' of delocalized electrons. Free electrons move under electric potential (conductivity), and allow atom planes to slide without breaking (malleability, ductility).
